topics.title: Introduction, Example Applications of RL, Elements of Reinforcement Learning, Finite Markov Decision Processes: The Agent-Environment Interface, Examples - Pick-and-Place Robot, Recycling Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction, Example Applications of RL, Elements of Reinforcement Learning, Finite Markov Decision Processes: The Agent-Environment Interface, Examples - Pick-and-Place Robot, Recycling Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Reinforcement Learning (RL)

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a computational framework and a branch of machine learning concerned with how an autonomous **agent** ought to take sequential **actions** in a dynamic **environment** in order to maximize a cumulative numerical **reward** signal. Unlike supervised learning, RL does not rely on labeled input-output pairs; instead, the agent learns *from its own experience* by interacting with the environment and observing the consequences of its actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RL is the third paradigm of Machine Learning (alongside Supervised and Unsupervised Learning). It is fundamentally a **closed-loop, sequential decision-making** problem, distinguished by the explicit trial-and-error search and delayed reward mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Sutton & Barto Framework, adopted by KTU):**
> *Reinforcement Learning is the science of learning optimal or near-optimal decision-making policies by an agent that interacts with a stochastic environment through actions and receives scalar rewards, with the goal of maximizing the expected cumulative reward over time.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **toddler learning to walk**. No one hands the toddler a dataset of `(image, correct_joint_angle)` pairs. Instead, the toddler:
1. Tries random leg movements (**exploration**),
2. Falls down and feels pain (**negative reward**),
3. Stumbles forward a few steps and hears a parent cheer (**positive reward**),
4. Gradually refines which muscle sequences lead to stable walking (**policy improvement**).

A robotic arm learning to grasp objects behaves identically — it nudges its joints, observes whether the gripper closes successfully (a positive reward) or knocks the object over (a negative reward), and progressively builds an internal **policy** mapping perceived states to actions.

| Concept | Toddler Analogy | Robotic Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Agent | The child | The robot controller |
| Environment | The room + gravity | The workspace + physics simulator |
| State | Body posture | Joint angles, camera pixels |
| Action | Muscle contractions | Motor torques |
| Reward | Praise / pain | Score function (e.g., +1 for grasp) |
| Policy | Neural reflexes | Mapping $\pi(a \mid s)$ |

---

## 1.2 Example Applications of RL in Robotics

KTU 2024 emphasizes robotics-centric applications. The following are high-yield examples:

1. **Industrial Manipulators (Pick-and-Place):** RL teaches a 6-DOF arm to pick objects from a conveyor and place them in bins, adapting to varying object shapes and lighting.
2. **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR):** Warehouse robots (e.g., Kiva/Amazon) learn collision-free navigation and shelf-picking policies in dynamic human environments.
3. **Quadrupedal/Bipedal Locomotion:** Robots like Boston Dynamics' Spot or MIT's Mini-Cheetah learn stable gaits via RL controllers.
4. **Autonomous Driving:** Lane-keeping, merging, and overtaking policies are learned through simulated RL before deployment.
5. **Drone Swarm Coordination:** Multi-agent RL for collaborative aerial coverage and formation flying.
6. **Soft Robotics:** Compliant grippers learn delicate object manipulation (e.g., handling fruit without bruising).
7. **Surgical Robotics:** Da Vinci-style systems learn suturing and tissue manipulation from expert demonstrations refined by RL.
8. **Recycling Robot (Sutton & Barto Classic):** A canonical example used in KTU to illustrate episodic vs. continuing tasks, finite state sets, and battery management.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for "applications," always pair the application with **(a) the agent, (b) the state, (c) the action, and (d) the reward**. Examiners award marks for explicitly identifying these four elements.

---

## 1.3 Elements of Reinforcement Learning

The KTU 2024 syllabus enumerates the **six core elements** that constitute any RL system. Memorize these in order.

### 1.3.1 Policy ($\pi$)

A **policy** defines the agent's behavior. It is a mapping from perceived states to actions.

$$\pi : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{A} \quad \text{or more generally} \quad \pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$$

A policy can be **deterministic** ($a = \pi(s)$) or **stochastic** (a probability distribution).

### 1.3.2 Reward Signal ($R_t$)

A scalar real number received from the environment at each time step. It defines the **goal**. The agent's sole objective is to maximize the **expected cumulative reward**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reward hypothesis states that *all goals can be described by the maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (called reward).*

### 1.3.3 Value Function ($V_\pi(s)$, $Q_\pi(s,a)$)

The value of a state is the **expected cumulative reward** achievable from that state under a given policy.

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

where $G_t$ is the **return** and $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the **discount factor**.

### 1.3.4 Model (Optional)

An internal representation of the environment's dynamics, i.e., the transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward function $R(s,a)$. Methods using models are **model-based RL**; those without are **model-free RL**.

### 1.3.5 Agent

The decision-making entity (e.g., a neural network controller on a robot).

### 1.3.6 Environment

Everything outside the agent that the agent cannot arbitrarily change (e.g., physics simulator, sensors, actuators, the physical world).

---

## 1.4 The Agent–Environment Interface

The **Agent–Environment Interface (AEI)** is the formal boundary that separates what the agent can control from what it cannot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The agent and environment interact at each of a sequence of discrete time steps $t = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. At each time step $t$, the agent receives a numerical **reward** $R_{t+1} \in \mathcal{R} \subset \mathbb{R}$ and a representation of the environment's **state** $S_{t+1} \in \mathcal{S}$, and selects an **action** $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$. In turn, the environment transitions to a new state $S_{t+1}$ and emits the next reward $R_{t+1}$.

The **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is the mathematical formalization of this interface. The tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ captures:
- $\mathcal{S}$ — finite set of states.
- $\mathcal{A}$ — finite set of actions.
- $P(s' \mid s, a) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$ — transition probability.
- $R(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ — expected reward.
- $\gamma$ — discount factor.

The system is called **finite MDP** when $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{A}$ are both finite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Property

A state signal possesses the **Markov property** if it retains *all relevant information from history* to predict the future, given the present action.

$$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Markov matters in KTU exams:** The Markov property is the **mathematical justification** for why an agent can ignore history and base its decisions only on the current state. If violated, the formal MDP framework breaks down.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Robot Localization:** A Markov belief state $b(s)$ summarizing all past sensor readings enables optimal Bayes-filter localization.
- **Inventory Control:** Stock levels (Markovian) vs. raw sales history (non-Markovian) determine whether classical RL or RNN-augmented RL must be used.

---

## 2.2 Episodic vs. Continuing Tasks

| Property | Episodic Task | Continuing Task |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Termination | Has a terminal state (e.g., robot drops object) | Runs indefinitely (e.g., process control) |
| Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ |
| Implication for $V_\pi$ | Finite sum, well-defined | Requires $\gamma < 1$ for convergence |
| KTU Example | Pick-and-Place episode | Recycling Robot (battery monitor) |

---

## 2.3 Return, Discount, and Optimization Objective

The **return** is the cumulative discounted reward:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

The **state-value function** measures expected return from state $s$ following policy $\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

The **action-value function** measures expected return after taking action $a$ in state $s$ under $\pi$:

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the high-yield formulas examiners expect to see verbatim in KTU 2024 ESE answers. Memorize the symbols and their units.**

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Range / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $G_t$ | Return | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Scalar (real-valued) |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | Weight on future rewards | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ (dimensionless) |
| $V_\pi(s)$ | State value | $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Real-valued |
| $Q_\pi(s,a)$ | Action value | $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Real-valued |
| $P(s' \mid s,a)$ | Transition | $\Pr(S_{t+1}=s' \mid S_t=s, A_t=a)$ | $[0,1]$ |
| $R(s,a)$ | Expected reward | $\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Real-valued |
| $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Stochastic policy | $\Pr(A_t=a \mid S_t=s)$ | $[0,1]$, $\sum_a \pi = 1$ |
| $r$ | Stochastic reward | $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ (sample) | Real-valued |
| $T$ | Horizon (episodic) | Number of steps to terminal | Positive integer |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate | Step-size in updates | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using $\vert$ inside table rows** — the KTU 2024 PDF/HTML renderer can misinterpret vertical bars in markdown tables. Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in math contexts.

---

## 2.5 Bellman Equations (Foundational)

The **Bellman Expectation Equation** recursively decomposes the value function:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a') \right]$$

The relationship between $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) Q_\pi(s,a)$$

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A 14-mark question frequently asks you to "derive the Bellman equation for $V_\pi$." The derivation begins by writing $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$, then taking the expectation. This is the single most exam-relevant step in Module 1.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility Summary

- **Industrial Automation:** Pick-and-place RL policies reduce cycle time by 30–50% in bin-picking benchmarks.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** State-value estimates form the basis of decision-making at unsignalized intersections.
- **Process Control:** Discounted MDPs model chemical reactors where the long-term cost of catalyst damage dominates short-term gains.
- **Healthcare Robotics:** Value functions encode surgeon preferences in tele-operated suturing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the State-Value Function (Bellman Expectation)

**Goal:** Express $V_\pi(s)$ recursively in terms of $V_\pi(s')$.

### Step 1 — Start from the definition

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ G_t \mid S_t = s \big]$$

### Step 2 — Use the recursive decomposition of the return

By definition, the return is:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots$$

Factor out the first term and rewrite the rest recursively:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \big( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \ldots \big) = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the recursive form into the expectation

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \big]$$

### Step 4 — Apply linearity of expectation

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \big] + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \big]$$

### Step 5 — Condition on the next state and action

Using the law of total expectation:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \big]$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the inner expectation as the value function

By definition, $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V_\pi(s')$.

### Step 7 — Final recursive form (Bellman Equation)

$$\boxed{\,V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Action-Value Function (Bellman Equation for $Q_\pi$)

**Goal:** Express $Q_\pi(s,a)$ recursively.

### Step 1 — Definition

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \big]$$

### Step 2 — Use the recursive return formula

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \big]$$

### Step 3 — Condition on next state and next reward

$$Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \big]$$

### Step 4 — Condition on next action

The next expectation is over $a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')$:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a')$$

### Step 5 — Final recursive form

$$\boxed{\,Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a') \right]\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compare Pick-and-Place vs. Recycling Robot

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **gold** for KTU exams. Examiners love asking comparative questions framed as "Differentiate between a Pick-and-Place Robot and a Recycling Robot under the RL framework." Below is the complete decomposition.

| Element | Pick-and-Place Robot | Recycling Robot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **State $\mathcal{S}$** | Joint angles $\theta$, gripper state (open/closed), object pose $(x,y,\phi)$, vision features | Battery level (high/low), bin status (empty/full), can position |
| **Action $\mathcal{A}$** | 6-DOF arm torques or waypoints; gripper open/close | Wait, search, pick, place, recharge |
| **Reward $R$** | $+1$ on successful grasp-place; $-1$ on drop/failure; $0$ otherwise | $+1$ per can recycled; small penalty for searching with low battery |
| **Episode** | Episodic — terminates on success or timeout | Continuing — runs indefinitely; sometimes modeled episodically |
| **Transition $P$** | Stochastic (object slip, camera noise) | Stochastic (battery drain, can arrival) |
| **Discount $\gamma$** | $\gamma \approx 0.9$ — task is short-horizon | $\gamma \approx 0.95$ — must value long-term battery survival |
| **Policy Type** | Often deterministic for industrial accuracy | Stochastic due to uncertainty in can locations |
| **Value Function** | $V(s)$ reflects probability of successful pick | $V(s)$ heavily weights battery level |
| **Markov Property** | Holds if state includes full object pose | Holds if battery level is observed exactly |
| **Optimal Behavior** | Minimize cycle time | Maximize recycling rate while never running out of power |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Recycling Robot Transition Probabilities

A recycling robot has states: `high` (battery high), `low` (battery low). Actions: `search` (for cans), `wait`, `recharge`. Suppose from `high` doing `search`:
- With probability $\alpha$ the robot finds a can and stays in `high`.
- With probability $1-\alpha$ the robot finds nothing and the battery drains to `low`.

From `low` doing `search`:
- With probability $\beta$ a can is found and battery stays `low`.
- With probability $1-\beta$ the battery is critically low and robot must `rescue` (forced recharge) with large negative reward $r_{\text{rescue}}$.

### Compute $V_\pi(\text{high})$ under the search-from-high policy

$$V_\pi(\text{high}) = r_{\text{search}} + \gamma \big[ \alpha V_\pi(\text{high}) + (1-\alpha) V_\pi(\text{low}) \big]$$

### Compute $V_\pi(\text{low})$ under the search-from-low policy

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) = r_{\text{search}} + \gamma \big[ \beta V_\pi(\text{low}) + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}} \big]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Solve for $V_\pi(\text{low})$ first** since it does not depend on $V_\pi(\text{high})$:
> 
> $$V_\pi(\text{low}) = \frac{r_{\text{search}} + \gamma (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}}}{1 - \gamma \beta}$$
> 
> Then substitute into the first equation to obtain $V_\pi(\text{high})$. This algebraic technique (substitution) is the exact pattern KTU examiners use to award 7 of 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agent–Environment Interface (MDP Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop interaction formalized in the finite MDP framework. The agent and environment exchange state, action, and reward signals at every discrete time step.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AGENT["AGENT POLICY PI"]
        nodeA[Action Selector A_t]
        nodeB[State Value Estimator V_pi]
        nodeC[Q Value Estimator Q_pi]
    end
    subgraph ENV["ENVIRONMENT"]
        nodeD[Dynamics p s prime r given s a]
        nodeE[Reward Generator R_t+1]
        nodeF[Next State S_t+1]
    end
    nodeA -->|action A_t| nodeD
    nodeD -->|next state S_t+1| nodeF
    nodeD -->|reward r| nodeE
    nodeF -->|observation S_t+1| nodeA
    nodeE -->|reward R_t+1| nodeB
    nodeB -->|value V| nodeA
    nodeC -->|action value Q| nodeA
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagram for the exam:**
> - The arrow **from the agent to the environment** carries the action $A_t$.
> - The arrows **from the environment to the agent** carry the next state $S_{t+1}$ and reward $R_{t+1}$.
> - Internally, the agent updates its policy $\pi$ using $V_\pi$ or $Q_\pi$ estimates.

---

## 4.2 Pick-and-Place Robot — RL Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAMERA["VISION MODULE"]
        nodeP1[RGB D Camera]
        nodeP2[Object Pose Estimator]
    end
    subgraph STATEF["STATE CONSTRUCTOR"]
        nodeP3[State Vector s joint angles gripper object pose]
    end
    subgraph POLF["POLICY NETWORK"]
        nodeP4[Deep Neural Network pi a given s]
    end
    subgraph ACTF["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        nodeP5[Joint Torques Tau 1 to Tau 6]
        nodeP6[Gripper Open Close]
    end
    subgraph REWF["REWARD FUNCTION"]
        nodeP7[+1 on successful place]
        nodeP8[minus 1 on drop]
        nodeP9[0 otherwise]
    end
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5
    nodeP4 --> nodeP6
    nodeP5 --> nodeP1
    nodeP6 --> nodeP1
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7
    nodeP5 --> nodeP8
    nodeP5 --> nodeP9
```

---

## 4.3 Recycling Robot — Decision Tree (State–Action Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeQ0{State: Battery HIGH or LOW}
    nodeQ0 -->|HIGH| nodeQ1{Action Choice}
    nodeQ0 -->|LOW| nodeQ3{Action Choice}
    nodeQ1 -->|search| nodeQ2a[With prob alpha, find can, stay HIGH]
    nodeQ1 -->|search| nodeQ2b[With prob 1 minus alpha, no can, battery drops to LOW]
    nodeQ1 -->|wait| nodeQ2c[Stay in HIGH, no reward]
    nodeQ3 -->|search| nodeQ4a[With prob beta, find can, stay LOW]
    nodeQ3 -->|search| nodeQ4b[With prob 1 minus beta, battery critical, forced rescue]
    nodeQ3 -->|recharge| nodeQ4c[Return to HIGH, negative reward r_rescue]
    nodeQ3 -->|wait| nodeQ4d[Stay in LOW, no reward]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RL-Controlled Robotic System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        layerA1[Proprioceptive Sensors: Encoders IMU Force Torque]
        layerA2[Exteroceptive Sensors: LIDAR RGB Cameras]
        layerA3[State Estimator: Bayes Filter Kalman Filter]
    end
    subgraph RL_CORE["RL POLICY CORE"]
        layerB1[Actor Network pi a given s]
        layerB2[Critic Network Q pi s a]
        layerB3[Replay Buffer]
        layerB4[Reward Computer]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL["LOW LEVEL CONTROL"]
        layerC1[Trajectory Planner]
        layerC2[Inverse Dynamics]
        layerC3[Motor Drivers]
    end
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL ROBOT"]
        layerD1[Joint Actuators]
        layerD2[End Effector]
        layerD3[Workspace Environment]
    end
    layerA1 --> layerA3
    layerA2 --> layerA3
    layerA3 --> layerB1
    layerA3 --> layerB2
    layerB4 --> layerB1
    layerB2 --> layerB1
    layerB3 --> layerB1
    layerB3 --> layerB2
    layerB1 --> layerC1
    layerC1 --> layerC2
    layerC2 --> layerC3
    layerC3 --> layerD1
    layerD1 --> layerD2
    layerD2 --> layerD3
    layerD3 --> layerA1
    layerD3 --> layerA2
    layerD3 --> layerB4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram is exam-critical:** It connects the *abstract MDP* (agent, state, action, reward) to the *physical robotic system* (sensors, actuators, workspace). KTU examiners frequently give 14-mark questions that ask you to map a real robot onto the abstract RL tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ — this architecture is your reference template.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
**Define the term "policy" in Reinforcement Learning. Differentiate between a deterministic and a stochastic policy with one example each from a robotic context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **policy** $\pi$ is the agent's behavioral strategy — a mapping from the perceived state of the environment to the action to be taken. It is the central concept of RL because it alone is sufficient to determine the agent's behavior.

- **Deterministic policy:** $\pi(s) = a$ — for each state, a single specific action is chosen. *Example:* A pick-and-place arm moves to $(x,y,z) = (0.3, 0.2, 0.1)$ whenever the camera detects a red object.
- **Stochastic policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ — for each state, a probability distribution over actions is output. *Example:* A recycling robot searches for cans with probability $0.7$ and waits with probability $0.3$ when battery is high, enabling exploration.

Stochastic policies are essential for exploration and are mandatory in partially observable or adversarial environments. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model Paper)]
**List any six elements of a Reinforcement Learning system and write the formal Markov property equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six elements are: **(1)** policy, **(2)** reward signal, **(3)** value function, **(4)** model of the environment (optional), **(5)** agent, **(6)** environment. **(1.5 marks)**

The **Markov property** requires that the future state depends only on the current state and action, not on the history:

$$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots, S_0, A_0) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

**(1.5 marks)**

---

## Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — MDP & Recycling Robot

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define a finite Markov Decision Process. For a recycling robot, identify the state set $\mathcal{S}$, action set $\mathcal{A}$, and reward function $R$ clearly. Explain why the state must be **Markov**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For the recycling robot, derive the Bellman expectation equation for $V_\pi(\text{low})$ under the policy of searching. Assume: with probability $\beta$ a can is found (reward $r_{\text{search}}$), and with probability $1-\beta$ the robot is forced into a "rescue" state with reward $r_{\text{rescue}}$. Solve for $V_\pi(\text{low})$ in closed form.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Part Solution *(7 marks)*

A **finite Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ where: $\mathcal{S}$ is a finite set of states; $\mathcal{A}$ is a finite set of actions; $P(s' \mid s,a)$ is the transition probability; $R(s,a)$ is the expected reward; and $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the discount factor. **(2 marks)**

**Recycling Robot Specification:**
- $\mathcal{S} = \{ \text{high}, \text{low} \}$ — battery level states. **(1 mark)**
- $\mathcal{A} = \{ \text{search}, \text{wait}, \text{recharge} \}$ — available actions. **(1 mark)**
- $R$: $+r_{\text{search}}$ when a can is found, $r_{\text{rescue}} \ll 0$ when rescue occurs, $0$ for `wait`. **(1 mark)**

**Why the state must be Markov:** If the state is defined only by current battery level, the Markov property is satisfied because the future battery level depends *only* on the current level and the chosen action (search drains, recharge restores, wait does nothing). No additional history is needed to predict the next state. If, however, we mistakenly defined the state as "is the robot currently moving?" without including the battery level, the state would not be Markov because the next transition depends on hidden battery information. **(2 marks)**

#### (b) Part Solution *(7 marks)*

From the state `low` taking action `search`:

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = \text{low}, A_t = \text{search} \big]$$

Condition on the two outcomes:

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) = \beta \big[ r_{\text{search}} + \gamma V_\pi(\text{low}) \big] + (1-\beta) \big[ r_{\text{rescue}} + \gamma V_\pi(\text{low}) \big]$$

Note: the rescue state is treated as terminal (resets to high, so the future value contribution is captured by re-entry into the high state — for this derivation we approximate $V_\pi(\text{rescue}) \approx 0$ for the *immediate* return component after rescue, leading to the simpler form):

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) = \beta \, r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}} + \gamma \big[ \beta V_\pi(\text{low}) + (1-\beta) V_\pi(\text{low}) \big]$$

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) = \beta \, r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}} + \gamma V_\pi(\text{low})$$

**Solving algebraically:**

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) - \gamma V_\pi(\text{low}) = \beta \, r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}}$$

$$V_\pi(\text{low}) (1 - \gamma) = \beta \, r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_\pi(\text{low}) = \frac{\beta \, r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}}}{1 - \gamma}\,}$$

**(7 marks)** — *Full marks awarded for: correct formulation (3), algebraic manipulation (2), final closed-form solution (2).*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Pick-and-Place Robot

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Model a **Pick-and-Place Robot** as a finite MDP. Define the agent, environment, state space, action space, and a suitable reward function. Justify why a **discount factor** $\gamma < 1$ is required in this scenario.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Derive the Bellman equation for the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ for the pick-and-place robot using the recursive decomposition of the return. Show clearly the role of the policy $\pi$ in your derivation.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Part Solution *(7 marks)*

**Agent–Environment Modeling of a Pick-and-Place Robot:**

| Element | Definition | Robot-Specific Realization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Agent | Decision-making controller | The on-board RL policy (often a neural network) |
| Environment | External world + physics | Conveyor belt, object dynamics, vision system |
| State $s \in \mathcal{S}$ | Numerical description of the world | $s = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_6, g, x_o, y_o, \phi_o, I)$ — joint angles, gripper open/closed, object pose, image features |
| Action $a \in \mathcal{A}$ | Choice available to the agent | 6-DOF velocity commands or waypoint deltas; gripper toggle |
| Reward $R$ | Scalar objective | $+10$ for successful pick-and-place, $-5$ for drop, $-1$ per time step (encourages speed) |
| Discount $\gamma$ | Future-reward weight | $\gamma = 0.95$ balances speed and success |

**(4 marks for tabular specification)**

**Justification for $\gamma < 1$:** The discount factor $\gamma < 1$ ensures: **(i)** the infinite-horizon return $G_t = \sum \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ converges to a finite value even if rewards are non-zero every step; **(ii)** the agent prefers immediate rewards, encouraging time-efficient manipulation; **(iii)** mathematically, the value function is a *contraction*, guaranteeing a unique fixed point for the Bellman operator. **(3 marks)**

#### (b) Part Solution *(7 marks)*

**Derivation of the Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ G_t \mid S_t = s \big]$$

**Step 2 — Recursive expansion of $G_t$:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the expectation:**

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \big]$$

**Step 4 — Condition on the action $A_t = a$ via the policy $\pi$:**

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \big]$$

**Step 5 — Condition on the next state and reward via the transition kernel $p(s', r \mid s, a)$:**

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \big]$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V_\pi(s')$:**

$$\boxed{\,V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]\,}$$

**Role of the policy $\pi$:** The policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ weights the contribution of each possible action in state $s$. A stochastic policy assigns non-zero probability to multiple actions, smoothing the value estimate. A deterministic policy concentrates all weight on a single $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$. **(7 marks)** — *2 marks for each of the 3 critical steps, 1 mark for stating the role of $\pi$.*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 1 ESE questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the policy $\pi$ inside the outer sum.** A common error is writing the Bellman equation as $V_\pi(s) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ *without* summing over $a$ weighted by $\pi(a \mid s)$. The policy is **mandatory** — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the reward $R$ with the value $V$.** $R$ is an *immediate, single-step* quantity. $V$ is an *expected cumulative* quantity. Mixing these is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Omitting the assumption that $\gamma < 1$.** A derivation of $V_\pi$ that ignores convergence conditions loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Vague definitions of state/action for robots.** Saying "the state is the environment" earns zero marks. Always write the explicit tuple form: $s = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_6, g, I)$.
> 5. **Skipping the Markov justification.** Any question asking "model X as an MDP" requires a one-line justification of why the state is Markov. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Writing $\pi : s \mapsto a$ as if it is a function from integers to integers.** State and action *sets* must be declared (e.g., $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **RL is the third ML paradigm** — closed-loop, trial-and-error, delayed reward.
- [x] **Agent–Environment Interface** is formalized by the finite MDP tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$.
- [x] **Six elements of RL:** policy, reward, value function, model (optional), agent, environment.
- [x] **Markov Property:** future $\perp$ history given present state and action.
- [x] **Return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- [x] **Discount factor $\gamma$** is dimensionless and lies in $[0,1]$.
- [x] **State-value function $V_\pi(s)$** = expected return from $s$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Action-value function $Q_\pi(s,a)$** = expected return from $s$ after taking $a$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Bellman Equation (state value):** $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$.
- [x] **Bellman Equation (action value):** $Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s,a) [r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s',a')]$.
- [x] **Episodic tasks** terminate; **continuing tasks** require $\gamma < 1$.
- [x] **Pick-and-Place Robot:** episodic, $\gamma \approx 0.9$, high-dimensional state, deterministic policy common.
- [x] **Recycling Robot:** 2-state MDP (high/low battery), $\gamma \approx 0.95$, illustrates forced transitions and rescue reward.
- [x] **Closed-form value for recycling low state:** $V_\pi(\text{low}) = \frac{\beta r_{\text{search}} + (1-\beta) r_{\text{rescue}}}{1-\gamma}$.
- [x] **Always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid$** in LaTeX within tables to avoid markdown conflicts.
- [x] **Mermaid node IDs must be alphanumeric** and **labels must be plain text** (no bold/italic tags).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Goals and Rewards, Returns and Episodes, Example - Pole Balancing, Unified Notation for Episodic and Continuing Tasks, Policies and Value Functions, Example - Grid World, Optimal Policies and Optimal Value Functions, Bellman Optimality Equation, Example - Bellman Optimality Equations for the Recycling Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — Foundations of the Markov Decision Process (MDP) Framework

This module consolidates the **agent–environment formalism**, the **reward hypothesis**, the notion of **return**, the **policy/value-function machinery**, and the **Bellman optimality principle** that underpins every modern RL algorithm (Dynamic Programming, MC, TD, Q-learning, etc.). The discussion below follows the canonical formalism used by KTU for the PERAT747 syllabus.

---

## 1.1 Goals and Rewards — The Reward Hypothesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reward Hypothesis (Sutton & Barto):** *All goals can be described by the maximisation of the expected cumulative scalar reward signal received from the environment.*

In RL, **every objective — navigating a maze, balancing a pole, grasping an object, winning a chess game — is re-expressed as the maximisation of a single scalar number called the reward $R_t \in \mathbb{R}$**.

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S_t$ | State at time $t$ | $s \in \mathcal{S}$ |
| $A_t$ | Action at time $t$ | $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ |
| $R_{t+1}$ | Reward received after $A_t$ | $r \in \mathcal{R} \subset \mathbb{R}$ |
| $G_t$ | Return (cumulative reward) from $t$ | scalar |

The reward $R_{t+1}$ is **stochastic**, drawn from the environment's transition kernel:

$$p(s', r \mid s, a) \;=\; \Pr\{ S_{t+1}=s', \; R_{t+1}=r \mid S_t=s, \; A_t=a\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Thermostat Analogy:** Imagine a room whose temperature is the "state". A thermostat is the "agent". Every minute it chooses "heat on" or "heat off" (action) and observes the new temperature (next state) and a comfort score (reward: +1 if $21^\circ\!C$, −10 if $<18^\circ\!C$). The thermostat does **not** know the *formula* for comfort; it simply learns actions that historically produced high cumulative comfort. The reward signal is the **only feedback channel** that communicates the goal.

**Why rewards (not goals) directly?** Specifying a goal like *"stand upright"* or *"reach the goal cell"* requires pre-computing success. Specifying the **reward** $r=+1$ for standing and $r=0$ otherwise (or $r=-1$ per timestep the pole is upright, etc.) lets the agent learn the goal **implicitly** through interaction.

---

## 1.2 Returns and Episodes — Formal Definition

The **return** $G_t$ is the cumulative discounted reward the agent seeks to maximise:

$$G_t \;\doteq\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1}$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the **discount factor**.

| $\gamma$ value | Behaviour | KTU use-case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\gamma = 0$ | Myopic (only $R_{t+1}$ matters) | Reactive control |
| $\gamma \to 1$ | Far-sighted (all future rewards matter) | Long-horizon planning |
| $\gamma = 0.9$, $0.99$ | Standard engineering default | Robotics & games |

**Episodic tasks:** The interaction breaks into sub-sequences called **episodes**, each ending in a special **terminal state**. $T$ denotes the terminal timestep.

$$G_t \;\doteq\; \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1}\, R_{k}$$

**Continuing tasks:** The interaction goes on forever with no terminal state; the infinite-horizon discounted sum applies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discounting is not optional in robotics:** Without $\gamma<1$, returns may diverge in stochastic, continuing tasks, and the agent becomes indifferent between receiving +1 now and +1 in a million steps — mathematically valid but practically useless.

### Example — Pole Balancing

> [!NOTE]
> **Pole-Balancing / Cart-Pole (Barto, Sutton & Anderson, 1983)** is the canonical episodic RL benchmark.

* **State:** $s = (x, \dot{x}, \theta, \dot{\theta})$ — cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, pole angular velocity.
* **Actions:** $a \in \{ \text{push left}, \text{push right} \}$.
* **Reward:** $r = +1$ for every timestep the pole remains upright (and cart within track).
* **Episode termination:** $\vert \theta \vert > 12^\circ$ or $\vert x \vert > 2.4$ m (the pole falls / cart derails).
* **Objective:** **Maximise $G_0 = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} \gamma^{k} \cdot 1 \approx T$** ⇒ maximise episode length.

The pole-balancing example is *episodic with "until-failure"* — there is no natural terminal reward; the episode ends when the **failure condition** is met, and the implicit goal is to delay failure.

---

## 1.3 Unified Notation for Episodic and Continuing Tasks

KTU expects a **single set of equations** that works for both task types. Define:

$$G_t \;\doteq\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1}, \qquad \gamma^{k}=0 \text{ for } k \geq T-t$$

Equivalently: **treat the terminal state as absorbing with $R=0$ thereafter**. This is the **absorbing-state convention** used universally in KTU RL modules.

---

## 1.4 Policies and Value Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Policy $\pi$:** A mapping from states to a probability distribution over actions.
> $$\pi(a \mid s) \;\doteq\; \Pr\{ A_t = a \mid S_t = s\}$$

**State-Value Function** $v_\pi(s)$ — expected return when starting in $s$ and following $\pi$:

$$v_\pi(s) \;\doteq\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\, G_t \mid S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\, \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t=s\,\right]$$

**Action-Value Function** $q_\pi(s,a)$ — expected return when starting in $s$, taking action $a$, then following $\pi$:

$$q_\pi(s,a) \;\doteq\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\, G_t \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a\,\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — GPS Navigation:** $v_\pi(s)$ is "how good is this city?" (e.g., Kochi). $q_\pi(s,a)$ is "how good is taking *Highway 47* out of Kochi?" — it captures the value of the *commitment* to a specific action.

The relationship:

$$v_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s)\; q_\pi(s,a)$$

---

## 1.5 Example — Grid World (4×4 illustrative MDP)

|  ⬜ |  ⬜ |  ⬜ | **+1** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
|  ⬜ |  ✖ (wall) |  ⬜ | **−1** |
|  ⬜ |  ⬜ |  ⬜ |  ⬜ |
|  ⬜ |  ⬜ |  ⬜ |  ⬜ |

* **Start:** $s_0 = (1,1)$ (bottom-left).
* **Actions:** Up, Down, Left, Right. With probability $0.8$ move intended direction; with probability $0.2$ slip sideways.
* **Rewards:** $+1$ at $(4,4)$, $-1$ at $(4,3)$, $0$ elsewhere.
* **Terminal:** entering $(4,4)$ or $(4,3)$.
* **$\gamma = 0.9$.**

For *any* fixed policy $\pi$, $v_\pi(s)$ is computed by solving the **Bellman expectation equation** (see §2/§3). The grid-world value landscape typically shows higher values in cells "close" to +1, gradient decaying outward.

---

## 1.6 Optimal Policies and Optimal Value Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Policy $\pi_*$:** A policy that is **at least as good as every other policy in every state**.
> $$\pi_* \;\geq\; \pi \quad \text{for all } \pi \; \text{iff} \; v_{\pi_*}(s) \geq v_\pi(s) \; \forall s$$

**Optimal state-value function:**

$$v_*(s) \;\doteq\; \max_{\pi} v_\pi(s)$$

**Optimal action-value function:**

$$q_*(s,a) \;\doteq\; \max_{\pi} q_\pi(s,a)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key existence theorem (KTU-high-yield):** For any finite MDP, **there exists at least one deterministic optimal policy**, and *all* optimal policies share the same $v_*$ and $q_*$.

---

## 1.7 Bellman Optimality Equation — Top-Level Statement

The Bellman optimality equation expresses the fact that the value of a state under an optimal policy equals the **expected return of the best action**:

$$v_*(s) \;=\; \max_{a}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, v_*(S_{t+1}) \;\big|\; S_t=s,\, A_t=a\,\right]$$

In expanded (kernel) form:

$$v_*(s) \;=\; \max_{a}\; \sum_{s', r}\, p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_*(s') \,\bigr]$$

For action-values:

$$q_*(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r}\, p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} q_*(s',a') \,\bigr]$$

These are **non-linear** (because of the $\max$ operator) and form the fixed-point equations that all value-iteration, policy-iteration, Q-learning and SARSA algorithms ultimately try to solve.

---

## 1.8 Example — Bellman Optimality Equations for the Recycling Robot

> [!NOTE]
> **Recycling Robot (Sutton & Barto Example 3.3)** — the KTU benchmark for teaching finite-state MDPs.

**States:**
* **high** — battery high (robust enough to search for cans).
* **low** — battery low (must be cautious).
* **wait** — robot is *waiting* in a station (cannot search).

**Actions and transitions:**

| From | Action | Resulting State | Reward |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| high | search | high with prob. $\alpha$; low with prob. $1-\alpha$ | $r_{\text{search}}$ |
| low | search | low with prob. $\beta$; **dead** with prob. $1-\beta$ | $r_{\text{search}}$ |
| high | wait | high | $r_{\text{wait}}$ |
| low | wait | low | $r_{\text{wait}}$ |
| low | recharge | high | $0$ |
| wait | wait | wait | $r_{\text{wait}}$ |

with $0 < \beta \leq \alpha < 1$ and $r_{\text{search}} > r_{\text{wait}} > 0$.

The Bellman optimality equations (one per state) are derived and solved in §3.4. KTU examiners frequently ask for these equations as a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agent–Environment Loop — Operational Logic

The RL problem is formalised as a discrete-time stochastic control process:

1. At time $t$, the agent observes state $S_t = s$.
2. The agent samples action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$.
3. The environment, governed by $p$, samples $(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1})$.
4. The agent receives reward $R_{t+1}$ and observes $S_{t+1}$.
5. **Repeat** until terminal or $\infty$.

The *only* channel through which the agent learns the goal is $R_{t+1}$; the *only* channel through which it observes the world is $S_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary between agent and environment:** Anything the agent *cannot* change arbitrarily is part of the environment (motors, sensors, reward computation, even the simulator). KTU expects this exact demarcation.

## 2.2 Why Discounting $\gamma$? — Three Engineering Reasons

1. **Mathematical convergence:** $\sum \gamma^{k} R_{\max}$ converges iff $\gamma < 1$.
2. **Animal/human behaviour analogy:** uncertainty about the future implies immediate reward is preferred.
3. **Avoidance of infinite loops:** without discounting, an agent could prefer wandering forever to receiving +1 now.

## 2.3 Episodic vs Continuing Tasks — Boundary Conditions

| Feature | Episodic | Continuing |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Terminal state | Yes ($T$ finite) | None |
| Return | $\sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k$ | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ |
| Example | Pole-balancing, chess | Process control, portfolio mgmt |
| Notation trick | Absorbing terminal state makes equations identical |

## 2.4 Properties of $v_\pi$ and $q_\pi$

* **Recursive (self-consistent):** every value function satisfies its own Bellman **expectation** equation.
* **Unique fixed point:** for a finite MDP with $\gamma<1$, the contraction mapping theorem guarantees a unique $v_\pi$ for each $\pi$.
* **Linear:** $v_\pi$ and $q_\pi$ satisfy *linear* systems (no $\max$), whereas $v_*, q_*$ satisfy *non-linear* ones.

## 2.5 Optimal Policy Theorems — KTU High-Yield

* **Existence:** at least one optimal policy $\pi_*$ exists.
* **Determinism:** there exists a *deterministic* optimal policy.
* **Greedy wrt $v_*$:** $\pi_*$ is the greedy policy w.r.t. $v_*$ — i.e. $\pi_*(s) = \arg\max_a q_*(s,a)$.
* **Uniqueness of optimal value functions:** $v_*$ and $q_*$ are unique even if multiple optimal policies exist.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation you are expected to reproduce in the KTU ESE for Module 1**. Memorise symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Equation | Domain / Boundary |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Markov / transition kernel | $p(s',r \mid s,a)$ | $\sum_{s',r} p = 1$ |
| 2 | Policy | $\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr(A_t=a \mid S_t=s)$ | $\sum_a \pi = 1$ |
| 3 | Discounted return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ (continuing) |
| 4 | Episodic return | $G_t = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k$ | $T$ finite |
| 5 | State-value | $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s]$ | finite MDP, $\gamma<1$ |
| 6 | Action-value | $q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | same |
| 7 | $v_\pi \!\leftrightarrow\! q_\pi$ | $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\, q_\pi(s,a)$ | expectation over policy |
| 8 | $q_\pi \!\leftrightarrow\! v_\pi$ | $q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s')]$ | expectation over env. |
| 9 | Bellman expectation (V) | $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s')]$ | recursive form |
| 10 | Bellman expectation (Q) | $q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma \sum_{a'}\pi(a' \mid s')\, q_\pi(s',a')]$ | recursive form |
| 11 | Optimal value | $v_*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s)$ | unique |
| 12 | Bellman optimality (V) | $v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma\, v_*(s')]$ | non-linear |
| 13 | Bellman optimality (Q) | $q_*(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q_*(s',a')]$ | non-linear |
| 14 | Recycling — high/search | $v_*(\text{high}) = \max\{\, v_{\text{search}}(\text{high}),\, r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}) \,\}$ | with $v_{\text{search}}(\text{high}) = r_{\text{search}} + \gamma[\alpha\, v_*(\text{high}) + (1-\alpha)\, v_*(\text{low})]$ |
| 15 | Recycling — low/search | $v_*(\text{low}) = \max\{\, v_{\text{search}}(\text{low}),\, -3 + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}),\, r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{low}) \,\}$ | $-3$ = death penalty (S&B convention) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Robotic manipulation:** rewards encode grasp success, collision penalties, energy cost.
* **Autonomous navigation:** rewards encode time-to-goal, lane-keeping, fuel.
* **Process control (continuing tasks):** rewards encode yield, downtime.
* **Recommendation systems:** discounted cumulative clicks / watch-time.
* **Algorithmic trading:** discounted P&L as reward, $\gamma \approx 0.95$–$0.99$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are intentionally **exhaustive** — every algebraic transition is stated, every substitution identified, every expectation justified. KTU examiners award partial marks for *showing the recursion*, not merely the final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Form of $G_t$ (Why $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}$)

We begin from the definition:

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots$$

Factor $\gamma$ out of every term except the first:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \bigl( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \cdots \bigr) \\
&= R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \underbrace{\bigl( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \cdots \bigr)}_{= G_{t+1}} \\
\therefore \quad G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical interpretation:** The return from time $t$ equals the immediate reward plus a *discounted* continuation return from $t+1$. This recursive identity is the **mathematical heart** of every Bellman equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation for $v_\pi$

Starting from the definition of $v_\pi(s)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &\doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_t \mid S_t = s \,\bigr] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t = s \,\bigr] \quad &&\text{(by §3.1)} \\
&= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s \,\bigr] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_{t+1} \mid S_t=s \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Now condition on the action $A_t = a$ and use the law of iterated expectations:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\, \bigl[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \,\bigr] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\, \bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s') \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

The last equality uses $v_\pi(s') = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s']$, which is valid because under policy $\pi$ the *future* depends only on the future state (Markov property).

**Final Bellman expectation equation for $v_\pi$:**

$$\boxed{\; v_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s') \,\bigr] \;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation for $q_\pi$

By analogous reasoning, conditioning on the *next* state and reward:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_\pi(s,a) &\doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_t \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a \,\bigr] \\
&= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a,\; S_{t+1}=s',\; R_{t+1}=r \,\bigr] \\
&= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \,\bigr] \\
&= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s') \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Now substitute $v_\pi(s') = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, q_\pi(s', a')$:

$$\boxed{\; q_\pi(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, q_\pi(s', a') \,\bigr] \;}$$

---

## 3.4 Bellman Optimality Equation — Derivation

Starting from the definition $v_*(s) = \max_a q_*(s,a)$ and $q_*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[R + \gamma v_*(S') \mid s,a]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_*(s) &= \max_a q_*(s,a) \\
&= \max_a \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, v_*(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a \,\bigr] \\
&= \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_*(s') \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman optimality equation for $v_*$** — note the $\max$ operator makes it **non-linear** (unlike the Bellman expectation equation which is linear in $v_\pi$).

Similarly for $q_*$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_*(s,a) &= \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} q_*(S_{t+1}, a') \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a \,\bigr] \\
&= \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} q_*(s', a') \,\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The system $\{v_*(s)\}$ has a **unique solution** because the operator $T(v) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma v(s')]$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\ell_\infty$ (Banach fixed-point theorem).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Bellman Optimality Equations for the Recycling Robot

We construct the equations **one state at a time**, exactly as the KTU examiner expects.

### 3.5.1 State **high**

From `high`, the agent can either `search` or `wait`.

**Action `search` from `high`:**

$$q_*(\text{high}, \text{search}) \;=\; r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\, \bigl[\, \alpha\, v_*(\text{high}) + (1-\alpha)\, v_*(\text{low}) \,\bigr]$$

Reasoning: with probability $\alpha$ the robot stays at `high` (reward $r_{\text{search}}$), with probability $1-\alpha$ the battery drops and the next state is `low`. No terminal state is reached, so no special reward handling.

**Action `wait` from `high`:**

$$q_*(\text{high}, \text{wait}) \;=\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high})$$

**Optimal Bellman equation for `high`:**

$$v_*(\text{high}) \;=\; \max\!\Big\{\, q_*(\text{high}, \text{search}),\; q_*(\text{high}, \text{wait}) \,\Big\}$$

---

### 3.5.2 State **low**

From `low`, the agent can `search`, `wait`, or `recharge`. Following Sutton & Barto, the "dead" outcome (battery fully depleted while searching) is modelled with reward $-3$ and no continuation:

**Action `search` from `low`:**

$$q_*(\text{low}, \text{search}) \;=\; r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\, \bigl[\, \beta\, v_*(\text{low}) + (1-\beta)\,(-3) \,\bigr]$$

**Action `wait` from `low`:**

$$q_*(\text{low}, \text{wait}) \;=\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{low})$$

**Action `recharge` from `low`:**

$$q_*(\text{low}, \text{recharge}) \;=\; 0 + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high})$$

**Optimal Bellman equation for `low`:**

$$v_*(\text{low}) \;=\; \max\!\Big\{\, q_*(\text{low}, \text{search}),\; q_*(\text{low}, \text{wait}),\; q_*(\text{low}, \text{recharge}) \,\Big\}$$

---

### 3.5.3 State **wait**

From `wait`, only `wait` is allowed (robot is docked). Hence:

$$v_*(\text{wait}) \;=\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{wait})$$

Solving algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_*(\text{wait}) - \gamma\, v_*(\text{wait}) &= r_{\text{wait}} \\
(1-\gamma)\, v_*(\text{wait}) &= r_{\text{wait}} \\
v_*(\text{wait}) &= \dfrac{r_{\text{wait}}}{1 - \gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **closed-form solution** for the `wait` state — useful as a sanity check in numerical solvers.

---

### 3.5.4 Compact One-Line Summary (KTU-friendly)

$$\begin{aligned}
v_*(\text{high}) &= \max\!\Big\{\, r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\,[\alpha\, v_*(\text{high}) + (1-\alpha)\, v_*(\text{low})],\;\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}) \,\Big\} \\
v_*(\text{low}) &= \max\!\Big\{\, r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\,[\beta\, v_*(\text{low}) - 3(1-\beta)],\;\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{low}),\;\; \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}) \,\Big\} \\
v_*(\text{wait}) &= \dfrac{r_{\text{wait}}}{1 - \gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical insight (Sutton & Barto):** With $r_{\text{search}} = 3$, $r_{\text{wait}} = 1$, $\alpha = 0.9$, $\beta = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.9$, the optimal action at `high` is `search`; the optimal action at `low` is `recharge` once $v_*(\text{high})$ exceeds a critical threshold derived from the equations.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Equation | Linear? | Operator | Used by Algorithm |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Bellman expectation (V) | **Linear** in $v_\pi$ | $T_\pi$ | Policy Evaluation |
| Bellman expectation (Q) | **Linear** in $q_\pi$ | $T_\pi$ | MC / TD evaluation |
| Bellman optimality (V) | **Non-linear** (max) | $T_*$ | Value Iteration |
| Bellman optimality (Q) | **Non-linear** (max) | $T_*$ | Q-Learning, SARSA |
| Recycling $v_*(\text{wait})$ | Closed form | division by $1-\gamma$ | Sanity check |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU theory papers award 2–3 marks for **labelled diagrams**. The schematics below are *text-equivalent* of the canonical Sutton & Barto illustrations and are rendered using Mermaid (render-safe, no special characters inside labels).

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AGENT] -- Action A_t --> E[ENVIRONMENT]
    E -- Reward R_tplus1 --> A
    E -- Next State S_tplus1 --> A
    S_t[OBSERVE STATE S_t] --> A
    classDef agentStyle fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef envStyle fill:#B4D5FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signalStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class A agentStyle
    class E envStyle
    class S_t signalStyle
```

**Reading the loop:** At each timestep, the agent acts on the environment, which responds with the next state and the reward. The agent's policy $\pi$ maps $S_t \to A_t$; the environment's dynamics $p$ map $(S_t, A_t) \to (S_{t+1}, R_{t+1})$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Backup Diagram for the Bellman Expectation Equation $v_\pi$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[State s] -->|policy pi| A[Action a]
    A -->|kernel p| Sdash[Next State s prime]
    Sdash --> R[Reward r]
    Sdash --> Vprime[v_pi of s prime]
    R --> Sum[Sum weighted by pi and p]
    Vprime --> Sum
    Sum --> Vout[v_pi of s]
    classDef stateNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef actionNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef valueNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class S,Sdash stateNode
    class A actionNode
    class Vout,Vprime,R,Sum valueNode
```

**Reading the backup:** Open circles = *states*; solid circles = *actions*; arrows labelled by $\pi$ or $p$. The value $v_\pi(s)$ is the weighted sum (weights = $\pi(a \mid s) p(s', r \mid s, a)$) of the immediate reward $r$ plus $\gamma v_\pi(s')$.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Bellman Optimality Backup (Max over actions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[State s] -->|MAX over a| A[Action a chosen optimally]
    A -->|kernel p| Sdash[Next State s prime]
    Sdash --> R[Reward r]
    Sdash --> Vstar[v star of s prime]
    R --> Qcalc[Expected r plus gamma v star]
    Vstar --> Qcalc
    Qcalc --> Vout[v star of s]
    classDef stateStyle fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef actionStyle fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef opStyle fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class S,Sdash stateStyle
    class A actionStyle
    class R,Vstar,Qcalc,Vout opStyle
```

**Reading the backup:** Same structure as §4.2, but instead of averaging over actions weighted by $\pi$, the agent **picks the best action** (the $\max_a$). This is the structural difference between *expectation* and *optimality* backups.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Recycling Robot Finite-State MDP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[STATE HIGH] -->|action SEARCH prob alpha| H
    H -->|action SEARCH prob 1 minus alpha| L[STATE LOW]
    H -->|action WAIT| H
    L -->|action SEARCH prob beta| L
    L -->|action SEARCH prob 1 minus beta| D[TERMINAL DEAD]
    L -->|action WAIT| L
    L -->|action RECHARGE| H
    W[STATE WAIT] -->|action WAIT| W
    classDef safeState fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef riskyState fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef waitState fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deadState fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class H safeState
    class L riskyState
    class W waitState
    class D deadState
```

**Reading the MDP:** `high` (green) is the safe, energy-rich state. `low` (orange) is risky — searching may deplete the battery and trigger `dead` (red, terminal, reward $-3$). `recharge` deterministically transitions `low → high`. `wait` (purple) is the docked, lowest-energy-cost state with self-loop.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Pole-Balancing Episode Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[INITIAL STATE s_0] --> Step{STEP t = 0,1,2 ...}
    Step -->|Choose A_t via policy| Push[Apply force plus or minus F]
    Push --> Observe[Observe new s_tplus1 and reward plus 1]
    Observe --> Check{Termination condition theta exceeds 12 deg OR x exceeds 2.4 m}
    Check -->|False| Step
    Check -->|True| Term[TERMINAL STATE T episode ends]
    Term --> Reset[Reset to s_0 new episode]
    Reset --> Init
    classDef startStyle fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef stepStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef endStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    class Init,Reset startStyle
    class Step,Push,Observe,Check stepStyle
    class Term endStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each episode begins at the upright state and continues until the pole falls. Every non-terminal step yields reward $+1$. The objective $G_0 = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} \gamma^{k} \approx T$ (since the reward per step is constant $+1$) — hence maximising return is identical to maximising episode length.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark distribution rule (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme):**
> * Part A: short-answer conceptual questions, **2 marks each** (answer any from choice).
> * Part B: analytical questions, **14 marks each**, with **internal choice** between Q(A) and Q(B). Each 14-marker must contain two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model-QP]**
*State the reward hypothesis. Why is a scalar reward sufficient to express any RL goal?*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
The reward hypothesis states that **all goals can be described as the maximisation of the expected value of the cumulative scalar reward signal** received from the environment. A scalar is sufficient because (i) any multi-objective task can be combined into a single weighted sum reward (reward shaping), and (ii) scalar ordering is preserved under expectation, allowing direct comparison and maximisation. **(2 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between episodic and continuing tasks with one example each.*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
* **Episodic task:** interaction breaks into natural sub-sequences (episodes) ending in a terminal state $T$ (e.g., pole-balancing, chess game). Return $G_t = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k$.
* **Continuing task:** interaction has no terminal state; runs indefinitely (e.g., process control, autonomous navigation). Return $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ with $\gamma<1$ for convergence. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Analytical 14-Mark Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write $v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma v(s')]$ without specifying that $v(s')$ is $v_*(s')$, not $v_\pi(s')$. Marks are forfeited for this confusion. **Always annotate the value-function subscript.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Module-1 Question]**

#### (a) Define the state-value function $v_\pi(s)$ and action-value function $q_\pi(s,a)$. Derive the Bellman expectation equation for $v_\pi(s)$ in terms of the policy $\pi$ and the transition kernel $p$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
$$v_\pi(s) \;\doteq\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_t \mid S_t=s\,\bigr], \qquad q_\pi(s,a) \;\doteq\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_t \mid S_t=s,\; A_t=a\,\bigr]$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &= \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s] && \text{(definition)} \\
&= \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t=s \,\bigr] && \text{(recursive form of $G_t$, §3.1)} \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s']] && \text{(law of total expectation)} \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s')] && \text{(Markov property: future depends only on $s'$)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\; v_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_\pi(s') \,\bigr] \;}$$

---

#### (b) Using the unified-notation trick (absorbing terminal state), show that the episodic Bellman expectation equation reduces to the continuing-task form. Hence explain the rationale behind the trick. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Unified form (3 marks):** In episodic tasks, $G_t = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k$, with $R_T$ undefined. Define $R_{T+1} = R_{T+2} = \cdots = 0$ and a self-loop on the terminal state: $p(s \mid s, a) = 1$ for all $a$ once $s$ is terminal. Then:

$$\sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k \;=\; \sum_{k=t+1}^{\infty} \gamma^{k-t-1} R_k \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

The Bellman expectation equation $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma v_\pi(s')]$ becomes valid for both task classes.

**Rationale (4 marks):** The trick
* removes the need for two parallel equation systems,
* allows the same algorithm code (DP, MC, TD) to handle either task class,
* makes mathematical analysis uniform (e.g., contraction-mapping arguments require no special cases),
* is *exact*, not an approximation — the terminal state truly behaves as an absorbing zero-reward sink.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) State the Bellman optimality equation for $v_*(s)$ and $q_*(s,a)$. Justify why it is non-linear. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Equations (3 marks):**

$$v_*(s) \;=\; \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, v_*(s') \,\bigr]$$

$$q_*(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} q_*(s', a') \,\bigr]$$

**Justification of non-linearity (4 marks):** The Bellman *expectation* equation is linear in $v_\pi$ — it is a weighted average. The Bellman *optimality* equation contains $\max_a$ (or equivalently $\max_{a'}$), which is a piecewise-linear convex operator. Composing $\max$ with the linear kernel sum yields a **non-linear** operator $T_*$. As a consequence:
* Value Iteration, Q-Learning, SARSA use $T_*$ — they solve a non-linear system.
* Policy Iteration uses $T_\pi$ (linear) for evaluation, then performs an outer greedy improvement.

---

#### (b) For the Recycling Robot MDP with parameters $r_{\text{search}}, r_{\text{wait}}, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$, derive the Bellman optimality equations for the three states `high`, `low`, and `wait`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**State `high`** (2 marks):

$$v_*(\text{high}) = \max\Big\{\, r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\,[\alpha\, v_*(\text{high}) + (1-\alpha)\, v_*(\text{low})],\;\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}) \,\Big\}$$

**State `low`** (3 marks):

$$v_*(\text{low}) = \max\Big\{\, r_{\text{search}} + \gamma\,[\beta\, v_*(\text{low}) - 3(1-\beta)],\;\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{low}),\;\; \gamma\, v_*(\text{high}) \,\Big\}$$

**State `wait`** (2 marks):

$$v_*(\text{wait}) \;=\; r_{\text{wait}} + \gamma\, v_*(\text{wait}) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_*(\text{wait}) = \dfrac{r_{\text{wait}}}{1-\gamma}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Recycling Robot:** Students commonly **omit the $-3(1-\beta)$ "death penalty" term** when searching from `low`, treating the dead state as if it had zero reward. Sutton & Barto assign $-3$ explicitly; failing to include this term loses 1.5–2 marks. Also, ensure the action set at `wait` is correctly restricted to **{`wait`}** — including `search` or `recharge` is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Higher-Order Thinking Questions (University-Level)

### Q3. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Show that the Bellman optimality operator $T_*$ defined by $T_*(v)(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma v(s')]$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\ell_\infty$. Hence prove the existence of a unique optimal value function $v_*$.*

**Solution Outline (self-study):**
1. Show $\Vert T_*(v) - T_*(u) \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert v - u \Vert_\infty$ using $\vert \max_a f(a) - \max_a g(a) \vert \leq \max_a \vert f(a) - g(a) \vert$ and the fact that the inner sum is a probability-weighted average.
2. Apply Banach's fixed-point theorem.

---

### Q4. **[Internal Assessment — Sessional II, 2024]**
*For a 4-state continuing MDP with $\gamma = 0.9$, write the Bellman optimality backup equations and solve them numerically by **one iteration of Value Iteration** starting from $v_0(s) = 0$.*

(Solution: present state-by-state computation using the Bellman optimality backup with $v_1(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) r$ for the first sweep.)
