## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Memorise it. You will recover 6–10 marks in the ESE just by remembering these bullet points.**

* [x] The **reward** $R_{t+1}$ is the *only* channel through which the agent learns the goal.
* [x] **Return** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots$ — recursive form $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
* [x] **Episode** ends at terminal state $T$; pole-balancing is the canonical example.
* [x] **Discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1)$** ensures convergence and expresses preference for immediacy.
* [x] **Unified notation trick:** absorbing terminal state with $R = 0$ afterwards makes episodic and continuing equations identical.
* [x] **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$** is a probability distribution over actions given a state.
* [x] **$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s]$**; **$q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$**.
* [x] **Bellman expectation equation** is *linear* in $v_\pi$ (or $q_\pi$).
* [x] **Optimal policy $\pi_*$** exists, is deterministic, and is greedy w.r.t. $v_*$.
* [x] **$v_*(s) = \max_a q_*(s,a)$** — definition of optimal state-value function.
* [x] **Bellman optimality equation** is *non-linear* because of the $\max$ operator; $T_*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction.
* [x] **Grid World:** 4×4 illustrative MDP with $0.8$ intended / $0.2$ slip transitions.
* [x] **Recycling Robot states:** `high`, `low`, `wait`; **actions:** `search`, `wait`, `recharge`.
* [x] **Recycling $v_*(\text{wait}) = r_{\text{wait}} / (1 - \gamma)$** — closed form, useful for verification.
* [x] **Recycling `low/search`** must include the $-3(1-\beta)$ death-penalty term.
* [x] **KTU expects** the equations to be written in **kernel form** (sum over $s', r$ with $p$), not just conceptual form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Module 2 Topic

The **Introduction** to Module 2 lays the formal mathematical groundwork for **Sequential Decision Making** in robotics, which is canonically expressed through the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** framework. An MDP is a discrete-time stochastic control process that provides the mathematical scaffolding for modeling decision-making in situations where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision-maker (the robotic agent).

Formally, an MDP is defined as a 5-tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ where each element has a precise mathematical role:

$$MDP = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$$

| Symbol | Set / Function | Role in Robotics Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathcal{S}$ | State Space | All possible robot configurations (pose, sensor readings, velocities) |
| $\mathcal{A}$ | Action Space | All available motor commands or actuator signals |
| $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Transition Probability | Probability of moving from state $s$ to $s'$ when action $a$ is applied |
| $R(s, a, s')$ | Reward Function | Scalar feedback signal indicating immediate desirability of a transition |
| $\gamma$ | Discount Factor | Weighting of future rewards against immediate rewards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PERAT747 transitions from foundational RL terminology (Module 1) into the **formal mathematical formulation** of decision problems. Mastery of MDPs is the gateway to understanding **Dynamic Programming (DP)** methods, **Monte Carlo** methods, and **Temporal Difference (TD)** learning, all of which appear in subsequent modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Robotic Mouse in a Maze:**
> Imagine a small wheeled robot placed in an unknown maze. At every moment, the robot *senses* its current position (a **state**), decides to turn left, right, or move forward (an **action**), and the world updates: walls block it, floors slide it, and a green LED at the exit emits a soft beep (a **reward**). The robot cannot see the whole maze — it must learn, by trial and error, *which sequence of actions reliably leads to the cheese*. The **MDP** is the formal description of this entire interaction loop. The robot is the **agent**, the maze is the **environment**, and the rule "current position is enough to decide what to do next" is the **Markov property**.

## 1.3 Why MDPs Matter for Robotics

- **Sequentiality**: Robotic actions have long-term consequences (e.g., a wheel slip now may cause collision in 3 seconds).
- **Stochasticity**: Sensors are noisy, motors are imprecise — the same action does not always produce the same next state.
- **Goal-directedness**: Engineers want the robot to *pursue an objective* (reach a goal, balance a pole, grasp an object) — the reward function encodes this.
- **Closed-loop feedback**: MDPs are intrinsically **closed-loop**, mirroring the real-time sense-think-act loop of every physical robot.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Introduced in This Module

> [!NOTE]
> - **Agent**: The decision-maker (e.g., the robot's onboard controller).
> - **Environment**: Everything external to the agent that it interacts with.
> - **State ($s$)**: A complete description of the world at a given time instant.
> - **Action ($a$)**: A control input the agent can dispatch.
> - **Reward ($r$)**: A scalar numerical feedback signal.
> - **Trajectory / Episode / Rollout**: A sequence $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, \ldots)$.
> - **Policy ($\pi$)**: The agent's behavioral rule, mapping states to action probabilities.
> - **Return ($G_t$)**: The cumulative discounted future reward from time $t$.
> - **Value Function ($V$ or $Q$)**: The expected return under a given policy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequential Decision-Making Loop

A robotic RL interaction unfolds as a discrete-time, discrete-state cycle:

1. At time $t$, the environment is in state $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. The agent observes $s_t$ and selects an action $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$ according to its policy $\pi(a_t \mid s_t)$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t)$.
4. The environment emits a scalar reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.
5. Time increments: $t \leftarrow t+1$. Go to step 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Markov Property** is the cornerstone assumption: the future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present. Formally, $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots, s_0, a_0) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$. In plain English: **the current state must contain all relevant information needed to predict the future**. For robots, this justifies designing state representations that capture everything the controller needs.

## 2.2 Policies — The Agent's Strategy

A **policy** $\pi$ is the agent's decision rule. Two forms are standard:

- **Deterministic policy**: $a = \pi(s)$ — for every state, exactly one action is chosen.
- **Stochastic policy**: $\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr(a_t = a \mid s_t = s)$ — a probability distribution over actions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stochastic policies matter in robotics:** They enable **exploration** (the robot occasionally tries novel actions to discover better behaviors) and provide smooth gradients for learning. A pure greedy deterministic policy often gets stuck in suboptimal behaviors in noisy environments.

## 2.3 Returns and the Discount Factor

The **discounted return** starting at time $t$ is the cumulative sum of future rewards, with future rewards geometrically discounted:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$$

The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ serves three engineering purposes:

- **Mathematical convergence**: It guarantees the infinite sum is bounded when rewards are finite.
- **Economic preference**: It encodes preference for sooner rewards — useful when battery life is finite.
- **Uncertainty modeling**: It implicitly treats far-future rewards as less certain.

## 2.4 Value Functions

The **state-value function** $V^\pi(s)$ is the expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, s_t = s \right]$$

The **action-value function** $Q^\pi(s, a)$ is the expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and following $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s,\; a_t = a \right]$$

## 2.5 The Bellman Expectation Equation

By applying the law of iterated expectations and the Markov property, the value function can be written as a recursive consistency relation. This is the **Bellman expectation equation**:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

The **action-value** form:

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^\pi(s', a') \right]$$

## 2.6 The Bellman Optimality Equation

When the agent can choose the **best** policy, the optimal value functions $V^*$ and $Q^*$ satisfy:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optimal action can be recovered directly: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$. This is the conceptual foundation of **value-based RL methods** (Q-learning, SARSA) covered later in the syllabus.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | MDP Tuple | $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ | Formal problem definition |
| 2 | Markov Property | $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid h_t, a_t)$ | State representation design |
| 3 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ | Objective to maximize |
| 4 | State-Value Function | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s]$ | Long-term desirability of a state |
| 5 | Action-Value Function | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a]$ | Long-term desirability of an action |
| 6 | Bellman Expectation (V) | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | DP policy evaluation |
| 7 | Bellman Expectation (Q) | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma\sum_{a'}\pi(a' \mid s')Q^\pi(s', a')]$ | Action-value consistency |
| 8 | Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Optimal control |
| 9 | Bellman Optimality (Q) | $Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma\max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')]$ | Q-learning target |
| 10 | Greedy Policy Extraction | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$ | Convert values to actions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Papers:** When asked to "derive" the Bellman equation, always start from the *definition* of $V^\pi(s)$, apply the *law of total expectation*, separate the first reward $r_{t+1}$ from the rest, and use the Markov property to factor out $V^\pi(s_{t+1})$. This 4-step structure is what examiners look for.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discounted Return Recursion

The following derivation shows that the discounted return at time $t$ can be written recursively in terms of the immediate reward and the return at the next time step. This recursion is the algebraic heart of every Bellman equation.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of $G_t$:**

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \gamma^3 r_{t+4} + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\gamma r_{t+2}$ from every term after the first:**

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \bigl( r_{t+2} + \gamma r_{t+3} + \gamma^2 r_{t+4} + \cdots \bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the bracketed expression is exactly $G_{t+1}$ by definition:**

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

This is the **fundamental recursive identity** of RL. It is the single most important equation at the introductory level — every dynamic programming, Monte Carlo, and temporal-difference update in the rest of the course is built on this identity.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi$

**Step 1 — Write the definition of $V^\pi(s)$ and substitute the recursion for $G_t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s) &= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right] \\[4pt]
&= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\big|\, s_t = s \right] \\[4pt]
&= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ r_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the law of total expectation. Condition on the action $a$ and the next state $s'$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ r_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a)\, R(s, a, s') \\[4pt]
\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a)\, V^\pi(s')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two expectations into the unified Bellman expectation equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s) &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \bigl[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why each step is justified:**

- Step 1 uses the **linearity of expectation** and the recursion derived in §3.1.
- Step 2 uses the **Markov property** so that, conditioned on $(s, a, s')$, future expectations depend only on $s'$.
- Step 3 is a direct algebraic combination — the policy weights the action, and the transition model weights the next state.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$

**Step 1 — Start from the recursion, but now condition on the agent picking the *best* action:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\big|\, s_t = s,\, a_t = a \right] \\[4pt]
&= \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \bigl[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^*(s') \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Justification:** Under the optimal policy, the agent's action selection is the $\arg\max$ of $Q^*(s, a)$. The expectation over $s'$ is a sum weighted by the environment's transition probabilities — note that the agent does **not** control $s'$.

**Step 3 — Insight:** The Bellman optimality equation is a **nonlinear** system of equations because of the $\max$ operator. This nonlinearity is precisely what makes finding $V^*$ computationally hard for large state spaces — the source of all the algorithmic innovation (DP, MC, TD, function approximation, deep RL) that the rest of the PERAT747 syllabus explores.

## 3.4 Worked Example — A 3-State MDP

Consider a robot with 3 discrete states $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, 2 actions $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$, $\gamma = 0.9$, and the following deterministic transitions and rewards:

| From | Action | To | Reward |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | +1 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | 0 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | +2 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | -1 |
| $s_3$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_2$ | +1 |

Assume a uniform stochastic policy $\pi(a \mid s) = 0.5$ for every $(s, a)$. Compute $V^\pi(s_1)$ using the Bellman expectation equation with **one-step backup** (i.e., values of other states treated as zero).

**Step 1 — Apply the Bellman equation to $s_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s_1) &= \pi(a_1 \mid s_1)\bigl[R(s_1, a_1, s_2) + \gamma V^\pi(s_2)\bigr] + \pi(a_2 \mid s_1)\bigl[R(s_1, a_2, s_3) + \gamma V^\pi(s_3)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 0.5\,[1 + 0.9 \cdot V^\pi(s_2)] + 0.5\,[0 + 0.9 \cdot V^\pi(s_3)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — With the one-step backup assumption $V^\pi(s_2) = V^\pi(s_3) = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s_1) &= 0.5 \cdot (1 + 0) + 0.5 \cdot (0 + 0) = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The state $s_1$ has a value of **0.5** under this random policy because there is a 50% chance of taking action $a_1$ which immediately yields a reward of +1. The other 50% (action $a_2$) yields no immediate reward. This is exactly the kind of "compute $V^\pi$ for a tiny toy MDP" problem that appears in KTU Part A and Part B examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Strategy:** When asked to compute $V^\pi$ or $Q^\pi$ for a small tabular MDP, always **draw a table first**, then **substitute into the Bellman equation one term at a time**. Do not attempt to combine steps — KTU examiners award method marks generously, and skipping algebra is the #1 reason students lose partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The following block diagram captures the canonical interaction in an MDP-based robotic RL system, including the modular information flow between sensing, decision-making, and actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent Controller] -->|action a_t| B[Robot Actuators]
    B -->|motor commands| C[Physical Environment]
    C -->|state update| D[Robot Sensors]
    D -->|sensor readings| E[State Estimator]
    E -->|state s_t| A
    C -->|reward r_t+1| F[Reward Computer]
    F -->|reward r_t+1| A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Agent Controller** (orange) houses the policy $\pi$ and the value function approximator.
- **Actuators + Sensors** (blue) are the robot's physical interface with the world.
- The **Environment** (green) is everything outside the robot's body that responds to its actions.
- The **State Estimator** (purple) maps raw sensor streams to a Markovian state $s_t$.
- The **Reward Computer** (pink) evaluates the desirability of each transition (e.g., distance to goal, energy spent, collision penalty).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of MDP-Based RL Modules

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Problem Specification Layer]
        s1A[Define State Space S]
        s1B[Define Action Space A]
        s1C[Define Transition Model P]
        s1D[Define Reward Function R]
        s1E[Select Discount Factor gamma]
    end
    subgraph S2[Mathematical Formulation Layer]
        s2A[State Value Function V_pi]
        s2B[Action Value Function Q_pi]
        s2C[Bellman Expectation Equation]
        s2D[Bellman Optimality Equation]
    end
    subgraph S3[Algorithmic Solution Layer]
        s3A[Policy Iteration]
        s3B[Value Iteration]
        s3C[Q Learning]
        s3D[SARSA]
    end
    subgraph S4[Robotic Deployment Layer]
        s4A[Sim to Real Transfer]
        s4B[Onboard Inference Engine]
        s4C[Safety Wrapper]
        s4D[Performance Monitor]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Architectural interpretation:**

- The **Problem Specification Layer** translates an engineering goal into MDP notation.
- The **Mathematical Formulation Layer** derives the consistency equations (the focus of this Introduction).
- The **Algorithmic Solution Layer** contains the DP/MC/TD methods that solve the Bellman equations — covered in Modules 3–4.
- The **Robotic Deployment Layer** handles the practical reality of running RL policies on physical hardware — a critical concern for PERAT747's robotics focus.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Value Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Value Function Family] --> B[On Policy Functions]
    A --> C[Optimal Functions]
    B --> D[V pi - State Value]
    B --> E[Q pi - Action Value]
    C --> F[V star - Optimal State Value]
    C --> G[Q star - Optimal Action Value]
    D --> H[Governs Bellman Expectation]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Governs Bellman Optimality]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Used by Policy Evaluation]
    I --> K[Used by Value Iteration and Q Learning]
    style A fill:#FFDEAD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** On-policy value functions ($V^\pi, Q^\pi$) assess a *given* policy; optimal value functions ($V^*, Q^*$) assess the *best possible* behavior. The Bellman expectation equation ties together the on-policy family; the Bellman optimality equation ties together the optimal family.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Explain the Markov property with respect to a Markov Decision Process. Why is this property essential for designing the state representation of a robotic agent?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The **Markov property** states that the future evolution of the environment depends only on the current state and action, and is conditionally independent of the entire history of past states and actions. Formally:

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots, s_0, a_0) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

In a robotic context, this property is essential because:

1. **Sufficient Statistics for Control (1 mark):** If the state $s_t$ captures all decision-relevant information about the past, the controller does not need to store the entire history — it can map $s_t$ to $a_t$ directly. This reduces memory and computational cost.
2. **Predictability and Bellman Recursion (1 mark):** The Markov property enables the recursive formulation of value functions, which is the foundation of all DP and TD methods.
3. **State Representation Design (1 mark):** Engineers must design the state vector (e.g., $[x, y, \theta, \dot{x}, \dot{\theta}]$ for a balancing robot) such that the Markov assumption is approximately satisfied. Including the relevant history explicitly (e.g., appending delayed sensor values) is a common engineering practice.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Distinguish between a state-value function $V^\pi(s)$ and an action-value function $Q^\pi(s, a)$. Write down the Bellman optimality equation for $Q^*(s, a)$.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | $V^\pi(s)$ | $Q^\pi(s, a)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Expected return starting from $s$ and following $\pi$ | Expected return starting from $s$, taking $a$, then following $\pi$ |
| **Argument** | State only | State-action pair |
| **Use** | Evaluate a state under a policy | Compare actions to pick the best one |

**Mathematical distinction (1 mark):**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^\pi(s, a) \qquad Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\bigl[ R + \gamma V^\pi(s') \bigr]$$

**Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$ (1.5 marks):**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

**Interpretation (0.5 marks):** The optimal action-value at $(s, a)$ equals the immediate expected reward plus the discounted optimal action-value of the best action available in the resulting state $s'$.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE]`

**(a)** Derive the Bellman expectation equation for the state-value function $V^\pi(s)$ from first principles. Clearly state all assumptions and justify each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-state MDP with the transition and reward table given below, the agent follows a **deterministic policy** $\pi(s_1) = a_2$, $\pi(s_2) = a_1$, $\pi(s_3) = a_1$, and $\gamma = 0.5$. Compute the value of each state under this policy using the Bellman expectation equation (you may assume that all values of next-states are unknown and treat them as zero, i.e., perform a **one-step backup**). **(7 marks)**

| From | Action | To | Reward |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | +2 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | +3 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | +1 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | -1 |
| $s_3$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | +4 |

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the definition of $V^\pi(s)$:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the recursive form of $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply linearity of expectation:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ r_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Use the law of total expectation. Condition on action $a$ (using the policy $\pi$) and the next state $s'$ (using the transition model $P$):

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \bigl[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \bigr]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the justification: by the **Markov property**, the future expectation $\mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}]$ depends only on $s_{t+1}$, allowing us to replace it with $V^\pi(s')$.

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Present the final boxed equation and discuss its meaning (a system of linear equations in $|\mathcal{S}|$ unknowns when $P$, $R$, $\pi$, and $\gamma$ are all known).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Marks for derivation questions are awarded **per logical step**, not just the final equation. A correct final answer with no intermediate reasoning typically receives 4–5 marks out of 7. Always write 5–6 explicit steps.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**One-step backup means** $V^\pi(s_2) = V^\pi(s_3) = 0$ in the computation of $V^\pi(s_1)$.

**Compute $V^\pi(s_1)$ (2 marks):** Under $\pi(s_1) = a_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s_1) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s_1) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_1, a) \bigl[ R(s_1, a, s') + \gamma V^\pi(s') \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \bigl[ R(s_1, a_2, s_3) + \gamma V^\pi(s_3) \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot \bigl[ 3 + 0.5 \cdot 0 \bigr] = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $V^\pi(s_2)$ (2.5 marks):** Under $\pi(s_2) = a_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s_2) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s_2) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_2, a) \bigl[ R(s_2, a, s') + \gamma V^\pi(s') \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \bigl[ R(s_2, a_1, s_3) + \gamma V^\pi(s_3) \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot \bigl[ 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 \bigr] = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $V^\pi(s_3)$ (2.5 marks):** Under $\pi(s_3) = a_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s_3) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s_3) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_3, a) \bigl[ R(s_3, a, s') + \gamma V^\pi(s') \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \bigl[ R(s_3, a_1, s_2) + \gamma V^\pi(s_2) \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 \cdot \bigl[ 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 \bigr] = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student error is to forget the **policy selection** in the outer sum. If $\pi$ is deterministic, $\pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for the chosen action and **zero** for all other actions. Many students sum over *all* actions, mistakenly double-counting. Always read the question carefully: "deterministic policy" means **one action per state**.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE]`

**(a)** Define the discounted return $G_t$. Show, with a step-by-step derivation, that $G_t$ satisfies the recursive identity $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, describe the Agent–Environment interaction loop in an RL-based robotic system. Identify and briefly explain the role of **five** essential elements in this loop. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definition:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}, \quad \gamma \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Expand the sum, separating the first term from the rest:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \gamma^3 r_{t+4} + \cdots$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Factor out $\gamma$ from every term beyond the first:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \bigl( r_{t+2} + \gamma r_{t+3} + \gamma^2 r_{t+4} + \cdots \bigr)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Recognize the bracketed expression as $G_{t+1}$ by definition:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Discuss significance — this recursion is the foundation for every bootstrapping update in DP and TD methods; it is the *single most-cited equation* in RL.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw (or describe) the Agent–Environment loop with **5 labeled blocks** and **2 signal arrows** showing the closed-loop information flow. (The Mermaid diagram in §4.1 of these notes is a perfect reference.)

**The five essential elements (1 mark each, total 5 marks; partial credit for any four):**

1. **Agent** — the decision-making entity (e.g., onboard controller hosting the policy $\pi$).
2. **Environment** — everything external to the agent that reacts to its actions (terrain, obstacles, dynamics).
3. **State Signal ($s_t$)** — the agent's observation of the environment; must be Markovian.
4. **Action Signal ($a_t$)** — the control command emitted by the agent.
5. **Reward Signal ($r_{t+1}$)** — the scalar feedback the environment returns after each transition; encodes the engineering goal.

**Closing remark (1 mark):** The diagram emphasizes that the agent does **not** control the next state — it only influences the *distribution* over next states. This asymmetry is the conceptual root of all exploration-exploitation trade-offs studied later in the syllabus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q(b):** A common error in "explain the RL loop" questions is to confuse the **action** with the **state**. The action goes *from agent to environment*; the state and reward go *from environment to agent*. Drawing the arrows in the wrong direction is an immediate 1.5-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 Introduction**
>
> ✅ The MDP is the canonical formalism: $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$.
> ✅ The **Markov property** is the foundational assumption enabling recursive value formulations.
> ✅ A **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the agent's behavioral rule; it can be deterministic or stochastic.
> ✅ The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ weights future rewards and ensures convergence of infinite-horizon returns.
> ✅ The **discounted return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ satisfies $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ — memorize this recursion.
> ✅ The **state-value function** $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s]$ measures long-term desirability of a state.
> ✅ The **action-value function** $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a]$ measures long-term desirability of a state-action pair.
> ✅ The **Bellman expectation equation** expresses $V^\pi$ (or $Q^\pi$) as a sum over actions and next states, weighted by $\pi$ and $P$.
> ✅ The **Bellman optimality equation** replaces the policy-weighted sum with a $\max$ over actions; it characterizes the optimal value functions $V^*$ and $Q^*$.
> ✅ For **examination derivations**: always start from the definition, substitute the return recursion, apply linearity of expectation, condition on $(a, s')$, and use the Markov property to simplify.
> ✅ For **numerical problems**: read the policy carefully (deterministic = single action; stochastic = probability distribution), apply the Bellman equation one term at a time, and explicitly state the discount factor.
> ✅ **Optimal policy extraction**: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$ — a one-liner worth 1–2 marks in any KTU paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Policy Evaluation and Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Policy Evaluation and Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Policy Evaluation** (also called the **Prediction Problem**) is the foundational task in Reinforcement Learning (RL) where, given a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** characterized by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ and a **fixed policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$, we compute the **state-value function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ — the expected cumulative discounted reward the agent will obtain when starting in state $s$ and subsequently following policy $\pi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> Policy Evaluation is the process of iteratively computing $V^{\pi}(s)$ for all states $s \in \mathcal{S}$ under a given policy $\pi$, by solving the **Bellman Expectation Equation**:
> $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

Formally, we seek to find the **fixed point** $V^{\pi}$ of the operator $T^{\pi}$ (Bellman expectation operator) defined as:
$$T^{\pi} V(s) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s') \right]$$

A unique fixed point exists and can be reached by iterative application of $T^{\pi}$ because $T^{\pi}$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction mapping** under the supremum norm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏭 Real-World Analogy: The "Robot Warehouse Picker"**
>
> Imagine a robot in an Amazon-style warehouse learning to navigate shelves. The **policy** $\pi$ is its current "rulebook" — for example, "always turn left at intersections." The question is: *How good is this rulebook?*
>
> **Policy Evaluation** is like having a **simulator** that, for every aisle and corner (state), calculates: *"If the robot follows this left-turn rulebook from here, what's the expected number of items it will collect before getting lost or breaking down?"*
>
> You don't change the rulebook. You just **score** it. The score is the value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ — a number on every map location telling you the expected long-term success from that spot.
>
> Later, **Policy Improvement** (next module) will use this score to *upgrade* the rulebook. But first — you must **measure** it accurately. That measurement is *Prediction*.

## 1.3 Why Policy Evaluation Matters in Robotics

In robotic systems, the state space is often **continuous** (joint angles, velocities) and **high-dimensional**. Policy evaluation is the computational backbone of:

- **Locomotion control** — scoring a gait policy on uneven terrain.
- **Manipulation** — evaluating a grasp policy before deploying on hardware.
- **Autonomous navigation** — predicting cumulative collision risk or fuel cost.
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** integration — value function as a terminal cost.

## 1.4 The Prediction vs. Control Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Aspect | **Prediction (Policy Evaluation)** | **Control (Policy Improvement)** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Goal** | Compute $V^{\pi}$ for a *given* $\pi$ | Find $\pi^{*}$ that maximizes $V^{\pi}$ |
> | **Output** | Value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ | Improved policy $\pi'$ |
> | **Bellman Eq.** | Expectation (fixed policy) | Optimality (max over actions) |
> | **KTU Module** | ✅ **This module (Module 2)** | Module 3 |
> | **Exam weightage** | ~ 12–15 marks per year | ~ 10 marks |

The **Bellman Expectation Equation** uses an *expected value* under $\pi$ — no maximization — because $\pi$ is *fixed*. This is the key mathematical signature distinguishing prediction from control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Expectation Equation — Structural Decomposition

The state-value function under a fixed policy $\pi$ has a **recursive structure** that can be broken into four logical components:

1. **Policy Mixing**: $\pi(a \vert s)$ — the probability of choosing action $a$ in state $s$.
2. **Dynamics Propagation**: $P(s' \vert s, a)$ — the stochastic transition kernel of the environment.
3. **Immediate Reward**: $R(s, a, s')$ — the scalar feedback signal.
4. **Future Value Discounting**: $\gamma V^{\pi}(s')$ — the discounted downstream value, with discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Magic of Linearity**
> Because the Bellman expectation equation is **linear** in $V^{\pi}$ (no max, no min), it admits a **closed-form solution**:
> $$V^{\pi} = (I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1} r^{\pi}$$
> where $P^{\pi}$ is the policy-induced transition matrix and $r^{\pi}$ is the policy-induced reward vector. This invertibility is why the problem is *well-posed* and *uniquely solvable*.

## 2.2 Iterative Policy Evaluation — The Algorithmic Heart

Since direct matrix inversion is **$O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^3)$** and intractable for large state spaces (think: a 6-DOF robot arm with discretized joint angles has $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert > 10^6$), we use **iterative dynamic programming**.

### 2.2.1 Synchronous Iterative Policy Evaluation

The update rule applied to **all states simultaneously** at iteration $k+1$:

$$V_{k+1}(s) \;\leftarrow\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_{k}(s') \right]$$

This is precisely the application of the **Bellman backup operator** $T^{\pi}$ to the current value vector.

### 2.2.2 In-Place Iterative Policy Evaluation (Memory-Efficient Variant)

Update the value table *in place* — using the most recent estimates immediately:

$$V(s) \;\leftarrow\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s') \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In-place updates typically **converge faster** in wall-clock time despite the same theoretical $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2)$ per-sweep complexity, because newly-updated values propagate to successors in the same sweep. This is the de-facto industry standard in modern RL libraries.

## 2.3 Convergence Theorem (Contraction Mapping Argument)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Bertsekas 1989, Sutton & Barto 2018):**
> The Bellman expectation operator $T^{\pi}$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\|\cdot\|_{\infty}$ norm:
> $$\| T^{\pi} U - T^{\pi} V \|_{\infty} \;\leq\; \gamma \| U - V \|_{\infty}, \quad \forall \, U, V$$
> By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application $V_{k+1} = T^{\pi} V_k$ converges geometrically to the unique fixed point $V^{\pi}$ at rate $\gamma$ per iteration, with error bound:
> $$\| V_{k} - V^{\pi} \|_{\infty} \;\leq\; \gamma^{k} \, \| V_{0} - V^{\pi} \|_{\infty}$$

This guarantees termination after a finite number of iterations to any desired tolerance $\epsilon > 0$, with iteration count bounded by:
$$k \;\geq\; \frac{\log(2 \|V_0 - V^{\pi}\|_{\infty} / \epsilon)}{\log(1/\gamma)}$$

## 2.4 Termination Condition

A sweep is declared complete when the **maximum change** across all states falls below a threshold $\theta$ (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$):

$$\Delta_k \;\triangleq\; \max_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mid V_{k+1}(s) - V_{k}(s) \mid \;<\; \theta$$

## 2.5 Model-Based vs. Model-Free Prediction

> [!NOTE]
> | Variant | Requires Model? | Update Rule | Use Case |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Iterative PE** (DP) | ✅ Yes — needs $P, R$ | Full Bellman backup | Small discrete MDPs |
> | **Monte Carlo (MC) Prediction** | ❌ No | $V(s) \leftarrow$ empirical mean of $G_t$ from episodes | Episodic robotic tasks |
> | **TD(0) Prediction** | ❌ No | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)]$ | Online continuous control |
> | **TD($\lambda$) Prediction** | ❌ No | Uses eligibility traces $E(s)$ | Faster credit assignment |
> | **LSTD** | ❌ No | Solves $A\theta = b$ least-squares | Off-policy, sample efficient |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **TD Target** $[r + \gamma V(s')]$ with **TD Error** $\delta_t = r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)$. The target is what we *move towards*; the error is *how far we currently are*. Marks lost: usually 1 mark per confusion.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Compilation (Policy Evaluation)**

| # | Equation | Name | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a\vert s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | Value-Action link | Definition |
| 2 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Action-value recursion | Definition |
| 3 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | **Bellman Expectation Eq.** | Core of Module 2 |
| 4 | $V^{\pi} = (I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1} r^{\pi}$ | Closed-form linear solution | $O(\vert\mathcal{S}\vert^3)$ cost |
| 5 | $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | Iterative update | Synchronous sweep |
| 6 | $\|T^{\pi}U - T^{\pi}V\|_{\infty} \leq \gamma \|U - V\|_{\infty}$ | **Contraction property** | Guarantees convergence |
| 7 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)]$ | **TD(0) update** | Model-free, online |
| 8 | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | **TD error** | Used in eligibility traces |
| 9 | $E_0(s)=0,\; E_t(s)=\gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$ | **Accumulating eligibility trace** | $\lambda \in [0,1)$ |
| 10 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | **Return / discounted cumulative reward** | MC target |
| 11 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [G_t - V(s)]$ | **Every-visit MC update** | Unbiased, high variance |
| 12 | $\theta = (A + \epsilon I)^{-1} b$ (LSTD) | **Least-squares TD** | Sample efficient |

**Units & Constants used in robotics examples:**
- Discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1)$: typically **0.95 to 0.99** in robotic control.
- Learning rate $\alpha \in (0,1)$: must satisfy **Robbins-Monro** $\sum \alpha_t = \infty, \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ for MC/TD convergence.
- Trace decay $\lambda \in [0,1)$: typical value **0.9** for locomotion tasks.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Robotics

Iterative Policy Evaluation underlies:
- **Value Iteration** in dynamic programming-based motion planning.
- **Rollout algorithms** in MPC where a base policy is scored to construct a terminal cost.
- **Approximate DP** in Deep RL (e.g., DQN's target network is a slowly updated estimate of the value function).
- **Differential Dynamic Programming (DDP)** and **iLQR** use Bellman-style back-ups in continuous state-action spaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form Solution from Bellman Expectation Equation

We start from the **Bellman expectation equation** in vector form. Define the policy-induced transition matrix $P^{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ with entries:

$$P^{\pi}(s, s') \;\triangleq\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \, P(s' \vert s, a)$$

and the policy-induced reward vector $r^{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ with entries:

$$r^{\pi}(s) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

The Bellman expectation equation for all states simultaneously becomes:

$$V^{\pi} \;=\; r^{\pi} + \gamma P^{\pi} V^{\pi}$$

Now we perform algebraic manipulation. Move $\gamma P^{\pi} V^{\pi}$ to the left-hand side:

$$V^{\pi} - \gamma P^{\pi} V^{\pi} \;=\; r^{\pi}$$

Factor out $V^{\pi}$ on the left:

$$(I - \gamma P^{\pi}) V^{\pi} \;=\; r^{\pi}$$

Pre-multiply by $(I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1}$ on both sides (the inverse exists because $\gamma < 1$ ensures the spectral radius of $\gamma P^{\pi}$ is less than 1, making $I - \gamma P^{\pi}$ non-singular):

$$V^{\pi} \;=\; (I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1} r^{\pi} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the inverse exists:** By the **Gershgorin Circle Theorem**, every eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $P^{\pi}$ satisfies $\vert \lambda_i \vert \leq 1$ (stochastic matrix). Then eigenvalues of $\gamma P^{\pi}$ satisfy $\vert \gamma \lambda_i \vert < 1$. So $1 - \gamma \lambda_i \neq 0$ for all $i$, guaranteeing the determinant is non-zero and the inverse exists.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Convergence Rate Bound

We want to quantify how fast iterative policy evaluation converges. We will prove that the error decays geometrically with ratio $\gamma$ per sweep.

Define the error at iteration $k$ as $e_k \triangleq V_k - V^{\pi}$. Since $V^{\pi} = T^{\pi} V^{\pi}$ (fixed point property), we can write:

$$e_{k+1} \;=\; V_{k+1} - V^{\pi} \;=\; T^{\pi} V_k - T^{\pi} V^{\pi}$$

Take the supremum norm on both sides:

$$\| e_{k+1} \|_{\infty} \;=\; \| T^{\pi} V_k - T^{\pi} V^{\pi} \|_{\infty}$$

Apply the contraction property $\|T^{\pi} U - T^{\pi} V\|_{\infty} \leq \gamma \|U - V\|_{\infty}$:

$$\| e_{k+1} \|_{\infty} \;\leq\; \gamma \, \| V_k - V^{\pi} \|_{\infty} \;=\; \gamma \, \| e_k \|_{\infty}$$

This is a **geometric recurrence**. Unrolling it from iteration $k=0$:

$$\| e_k \|_{\infty} \;\leq\; \gamma^k \, \| e_0 \|_{\infty} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This bound is **tight** — the worst-case error at iteration $k$ is at most $\gamma^k$ times the initial error, and this rate is achieved for some states in adversarial MDPs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The TD(0) Algorithm as a Stochastic Approximation of Bellman Expectation

Starting from the Bellman expectation equation, add and subtract $\gamma V^{\pi}(s')$ inside the expectation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

Treat the right-hand side as a **target** we want $V(s)$ to match. The TD(0) update uses a *sample* of this target (one transition), and nudges $V(s)$ toward it by step-size $\alpha$:

$$V(s_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(s_t) + \alpha \underbrace{\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \right]}_{\text{TD error } \delta_t}$$

Substitute the definition of $\delta_t$ and rearrange:

$$V(s_t) \;\leftarrow\; (1 - \alpha) V(s_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) \right]$$

This is exactly a **stochastic approximation** of the Bellman expectation backup, with the sample $r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$ acting as an unbiased estimator (in expectation) of the right-hand side of the Bellman equation. Under the Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$, $V(s)$ converges to $V^{\pi}(s)$ for all visited states.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: 4-State Gridworld

Consider a **deterministic 4-state chain** $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to \text{terminal}$, with reward $+1$ only on transition into $s_4$, all other rewards zero. The policy $\pi$ is fixed to "always move right." Discount $\gamma = 0.9$.

**True values** (computed backwards from $s_4$):
- $V^{\pi}(s_4) = 0$ (terminal, by convention).
- $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$ (only one step to terminal with reward already collected at $s_3 \to s_4$... wait, let us re-define: $R=+1$ on $s_3 \to s_4$).
- $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$ — no, the reward is on entering $s_4$, so $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 1$.
- $V^{\pi}(s_2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1 = 0.9$.
- $V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0.9 = 0.81$.

**Run iterative PE starting from $V_0 = [0, 0, 0, 0]$:**

| Iteration $k$ | $V_k(s_1)$ | $V_k(s_2)$ | $V_k(s_3)$ | $V_k(s_4)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 2 | 0.0000 | 0.9000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 3 | 0.8100 | 0.9000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| $\infty$ | **0.8100** | **0.9000** | **1.0000** | **0.0000** |

Notice: $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 1$ is reached in **1 iteration** because the reward is *one step* away. $V^{\pi}(s_1)$ needs **3 iterations** because information from the reward must propagate through the chain — this is the **propagation delay** intrinsic to dynamic programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Many 14-mark questions ask students to perform iterative policy evaluation **on a small MDP table** (3–5 states) for **2 to 3 iterations**. Practice drawing the table cleanly with $\Delta_k$ shown in the margin — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for *neatness* and *labeling*.

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Eligibility Trace Update for TD($\lambda$)

Eligibility traces blend MC and TD by exponentially weighting $n$-step returns:

$$G_t^{\lambda} \;=\; (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$$

where $G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$.

The backward TD($\lambda$) update keeps an *eligibility trace* $E(s)$ for every state:

$$E_t(s) \;=\; \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

After observing $\delta_t$ at time $t$, update *all* states:

$$V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s), \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

The trace $E_t(s)$ decays by factor $\gamma \lambda$ each step and gets bumped up by $1$ whenever state $s$ is visited. States visited recently receive the largest updates, implementing a soft form of credit assignment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of the Policy Evaluation Pipeline

The following **Mermaid block diagram** captures the full flow of the prediction problem — from MDP input to converged value function:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: MDP Tuple S A P R gamma] --> B[Input: Policy pi]
    B --> C{Choose Prediction Method}
    C -- Model Known --> D[Iterative Policy Evaluation DP]
    C -- Model Unknown --> E[Sample Based Method]
    E --> E1[Monte Carlo First Visit]
    E --> E2[Every Visit MC]
    E --> E3[TD 0 Online]
    E --> E4[TD lambda with Eligibility Traces]
    E --> E5[LSTD Least Squares]
    D --> F[Initialize V sub 0 = 0]
    F --> G[Loop: k = 1 2 3 ...]
    G --> H[For each state s in S]
    H --> I[Compute V sub k+1 of s using Bellman Expectation]
    I --> J[Compute Delta k = max over s of new V minus old V]
    J --> K{Delta k less than theta?}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L[Converged: V super pi]
    L --> M[Output Value Function V super pi]
    E1 --> M
    E2 --> M
    E3 --> M
    E4 --> M
    E5 --> M
    M --> N[Downstream Use: Policy Improvement Module 3]
```

## 4.2 Bellman Backup Diagram for a Single State

This diagram visualizes the **one-step backup** structure — the central object in policy evaluation. The "fan-in" of successors and "fan-out" of the value update are key for board exams:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State s] -- action a1 pi of a1 given s --> S2[Next state s prime]
    S1 -- action a2 pi of a2 given s --> S3[Next state s double prime]
    S1 -- action aK pi of aK given s --> S4[Next state s triple prime]
    S2 -- reward R of s a1 s prime --> X[Expected Return]
    S3 -- reward R of s a2 s double prime --> X
    S4 -- reward R of s aK s triple prime --> X
    S2 -- gamma V of s prime --> X
    S3 -- gamma V of s double prime --> X
    S4 -- gamma V of s triple prime --> X
    X -- sum and weight by pi --> Y[V super pi of s]
```

**Reading the diagram:** From state $s$, the agent selects action $a$ according to $\pi(a \mid s)$. Each action leads to a successor state $s'$ with probability $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and yields reward $R(s, a, s')$. The value $V^{\pi}(s)$ is the **expected sum** of immediate reward and discounted successor value, weighted by both $\pi$ and $P$.

## 4.3 Convergence Behavior of Iterative Policy Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Iteration k = 0] --> B[Random or zero init V sub 0]
    B --> C[Sweep k=1: V sub 1 = T pi V sub 0]
    C --> D[Sweep k=2: V sub 2 = T pi V sub 1]
    D --> E[Sweep k=3: V sub 3 = T pi V sub 2]
    E --> F[Sweep k=K: V sub K approx V super pi]
    F --> G[Error bound: norm V sub k minus V super pi infinity less than gamma to the k times norm V sub 0 minus V super pi]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows how $V_k$ approaches $V^{\pi}$ iteratively, with the error shrinking by factor $\gamma$ every sweep. With $\gamma = 0.9$ and $\theta = 10^{-4}$, convergence typically requires ~88 sweeps (since $0.9^{88} \approx 10^{-4}$).

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Model-Based vs. Model-Free Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Model-Based
        M1[MDP Model P and R known] --> M2[Iterative PE DP]
        M2 --> M3[Use full Bellman backup]
    end
    subgraph Model-Free
        F1[Experience samples only] --> F2{Monte Carlo or TD}
        F2 --> F3[MC: Use complete episode return G sub t]
        F2 --> F4[TD 0: Use one-step bootstrap r + gamma V s prime]
        F2 --> F5[TD lambda: Use trace-weighted n-step returns]
    end
    M3 --> Z[Converge to V super pi]
    F3 --> Z
    F4 --> Z
    F5 --> Z
```

## 4.5 The "Bias-Variance" Landscape of Prediction Methods

This **architectural diagram** is critical for KTU viva and design questions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prediction Method] --> B[Bias]
    A --> C[Variance]
    B --> D[High Bias: TD 0 uses biased V of s prime]
    B --> E[Zero Bias: MC uses actual return G sub t]
    C --> F[Low Variance: TD 0 uses deterministic estimate]
    C --> G[High Variance: MC depends on stochastic episode]
    A --> H[TD lambda: trade off via lambda]
    H --> I[lambda = 0 gives TD 0]
    H --> J[lambda = 1 gives MC]
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]

> **Define Policy Evaluation in the context of a Markov Decision Process. State the Bellman Expectation Equation for a fixed policy $\pi$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Policy Evaluation** is the problem of computing the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ for a given stationary policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ in a Markov Decision Process. The value $V^{\pi}(s)$ represents the expected cumulative discounted reward when starting in state $s$ and subsequently following policy $\pi$.

**Bellman Expectation Equation:** For a fixed policy $\pi$, the state-value function satisfies the following recursive relation for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This equation expresses that the value of a state equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state, where the expectation is taken over actions sampled from $\pi$ and transitions sampled from $P$. $\blacksquare$

**Marking distribution (3 marks):**
- (1) Definition of Policy Evaluation with the "given policy" emphasis — **1 mark**
- (2) Bellman Expectation Equation stated correctly — **1.5 marks**
- (3) Brief mention of $\gamma$ and the expectation over $\pi, P$ — **0.5 marks**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]

> **Distinguish between Monte Carlo (MC) Prediction and Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction in reinforcement learning. State the update rule for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Monte Carlo Prediction** | **TD Prediction** |
|---|---|---|
| Target | $G_t$ (complete episode return) | $r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$ (one-step bootstrap) |
| Update Rule | $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(s_t)]$ | $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha [r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)]$ |
| Episode Required | ✅ Yes — must wait until termination | ❌ No — online, after each transition |
| Bias | **Unbiased** estimate of $V^{\pi}(s)$ | **Biased** (uses estimated $V(s')$) |
| Variance | **High** (depends on stochastic returns) | **Low** (single transition sample) |

**Marking distribution (3 marks):**
- (1) Two update rules — **1.5 marks**
- (2) One valid distinguishing feature (bias / variance / online / offline) — **1 mark**
- (3) Clear comparison — **0.5 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a **module-internal choice** (any one of two). Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Both choices below are independent and from Module 2.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

> **(a)** Explain the **Iterative Policy Evaluation Algorithm** with the synchronous update rule. State the convergence theorem and discuss its proof outline. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the deterministic 3-state MDP with states $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ where $s_3$ is terminal. Reward is $+1$ on transition $s_2 \to s_3$, zero otherwise. Discount $\gamma = 0.5$. The fixed policy $\pi$ always selects the unique deterministic action leading $s_1 \to s_2$ and $s_2 \to s_3$. Perform **3 iterations** of Iterative Policy Evaluation starting from $V_0 = [0, 0, 0]$. Show the working and verify against the analytical solution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Algorithm + Convergence Theorem (7 marks)

**Iterative Policy Evaluation Algorithm (synchronous variant):**

- **Input:** MDP $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$, policy $\pi$, threshold $\theta > 0$
- **Output:** Approximation $V \approx V^{\pi}$

**Steps:**

1. Initialize $V(s) \leftarrow 0$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. Set $\Delta \leftarrow 0$.
2. **Repeat** until $\Delta < \theta$:
    - $\Delta \leftarrow 0$
    - For each $s \in \mathcal{S}$:
        - $v \leftarrow V(s)$
        - $V(s) \leftarrow \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) [R(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s')]$
        - $\Delta \leftarrow \max(\Delta, \mid V(s) - v \mid)$
3. **Return** $V$.

**Convergence Theorem (Bertsekas 1989):**

For any initial value function $V_0$, the sequence $\{V_k\}$ generated by $V_{k+1} = T^{\pi} V_k$ converges to $V^{\pi}$ as $k \to \infty$, and the rate is geometric with ratio $\gamma$.

**Proof Outline:**

- **Step 1: Contraction property.** Show $\|T^{\pi}U - T^{\pi}V\|_{\infty} \leq \gamma \|U - V\|_{\infty}$ by expanding the supremum norm and using the fact that $\sum_a \pi \sum_{s'} P = 1$.
- **Step 2: Unique fixed point.** By the Banach fixed-point theorem, a contraction on a complete metric space has exactly one fixed point — the operator $T^{\pi}$ has a unique $V^{\pi}$ such that $T^{\pi} V^{\pi} = V^{\pi}$.
- **Step 3: Geometric convergence.** Take any state $s$ achieving the supremum in $\|V_{k+1} - V^{\pi}\|_{\infty}$. Apply contraction repeatedly: $\|V_{k+1} - V^{\pi}\|_{\infty} \leq \gamma \|V_k - V^{\pi}\|_{\infty} \leq \gamma^{k+1} \|V_0 - V^{\pi}\|_{\infty}$. As $k \to \infty$, $\gamma^{k+1} \to 0$, hence $V_k \to V^{\pi}$. $\blacksquare$

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- (1) Algorithm steps correct — **2.5 marks**
- (2) Convergence theorem statement — **1.5 marks**
- (3) Contraction property reasoning — **2 marks**
- (4) Banach fixed-point application — **1 mark**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Analytical solution (closed form):** $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$, $V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1$, $V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$V_1(s_3) = 0 \quad \text{(terminal)}$$

$$V_1(s_2) = R(s_2 \to s_3) + \gamma V_0(s_3) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1.0$$

$$V_1(s_1) = R(s_1 \to s_2) + \gamma V_0(s_2) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0.0$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$V_2(s_3) = 0$$

$$V_2(s_2) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot V_1(s_3) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1.0$$

$$V_2(s_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot V_1(s_2) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$V_3(s_3) = 0, \quad V_3(s_2) = 1.0, \quad V_3(s_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot V_2(s_2) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$$

**Result Table:**

| $k$ | $V_k(s_1)$ | $V_k(s_2)$ | $V_k(s_3)$ | $\Delta_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | — |
| 1 | 0.00 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 0.50 |
| 3 | 0.50 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| $\infty$ | **0.50** | **1.00** | **0.00** | — |

**Convergence achieved in 3 iterations** since $V_3 = V_2$. The numerical result matches the analytical solution $V^{\pi} = [0.5, 1.0, 0]$ exactly. $\blacksquare$

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- (1) Correct analytical computation of $V^{\pi}$ — **1.5 marks**
- (2) Each iteration correctly computed (2 marks × 3) — **3 marks**
- (3) Tabulated results with $\Delta_k$ — **1.5 marks**
- (4) Final verification / observation — **1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

> **(a)** Explain the **TD(0) algorithm** for policy evaluation. Derive the TD update rule starting from the Bellman Expectation Equation and discuss why TD can learn before the final outcome is known. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a robot navigation task where the state space is $\mathcal{S} = \{A, B, C\}$ and the policy is fixed. The robot is in state $A$. The following transitions are observed:
>
> | Step $t$ | State $S_t$ | Action $A_t$ | Reward $R_{t+1}$ | Next State $S_{t+1}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | A | right | 0 | B |
> | 1 | B | right | 1 | C |
> | 2 | C | left | 0 | B |
> | 3 | B | right | 1 | C |
> | 4 | C | stop | 0 | terminal |
>
> Using $\gamma = 0.9$ and $\alpha = 0.5$, perform **TD(0) updates** starting from $V(A) = V(B) = V(C) = 0$. Show all 5 updates explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — TD(0) Derivation (7 marks)

**Motivation:** The Bellman Expectation Equation gives:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Derivation of TD(0) Update:**

If we have a single sample transition $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$, we can construct a *noisy* estimate of the right-hand side:

$$\hat{V}_t \;\triangleq\; r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$$

This is called the **TD target**. The **TD error** measures the discrepancy between the current estimate and this target:

$$\delta_t \;\triangleq\; r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$$

To reduce this error, we perform a gradient-style step:

$$V(s_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(s_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$$

Substituting $\delta_t$:

$$V(s_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(s_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \right]$$

Rearranging:

$$V(s_t) \;\leftarrow\; (1 - \alpha) V(s_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) \right]$$

This is the **TD(0) update rule**.

**Why TD can learn before the final outcome:**

- In **Monte Carlo**, the value update uses $G_t$, the *actual* return from the *full* episode. Hence MC must **wait** until episode termination.
- In **TD(0)**, the update uses $r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$. Since $V(s_{t+1})$ is already an *estimate* (possibly partially learned), the update can be made **immediately after one transition**, without waiting for the episode to end.
- This is the **bootstrapping** property: TD *learns a guess from a guess*. It enables **online, incremental learning** — essential for non-episodic robotic control tasks (e.g., continuous balance, walking) where episodes never truly end. $\blacksquare$

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- (1) Starting from Bellman Expectation — **1.5 marks**
- (2) Defining TD target and TD error — **2 marks**
- (3) Final TD(0) update rule derived — **1.5 marks**
- (4) Discussion on online learning / bootstrapping — **2 marks**

##### Part (b) — Numerical TD(0) Updates (7 marks)

**Initialization:** $V(A) = V(B) = V(C) = 0$, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$.

The TD(0) update is applied to $V(s_t)$ only — the value of the *state we just left*, not the next state.

**Update 1** ($t=0$, $S_0 = A \to S_1 = B$, $R_1 = 0$):
- TD target: $r_1 + \gamma V(B) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$
- TD error: $\delta_0 = 0 - V(A) = 0 - 0 = 0$
- $V(A) \leftarrow V(A) + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0$

**Update 2** ($t=1$, $S_1 = B \to S_2 = C$, $R_2 = 1$):
- TD target: $1 + 0.9 \cdot V(C) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 1$
- TD error: $\delta_1 = 1 - V(B) = 1 - 0 = 1$
- $V(B) \leftarrow V(B) + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$

**Update 3** ($t=2$, $S_2 = C \to S_3 = B$, $R_3 = 0$):
- TD target: $0 + 0.9 \cdot V(B) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0.5 = 0.45$
- TD error: $\delta_2 = 0.45 - V(C) = 0.45 - 0 = 0.45$
- $V(C) \leftarrow V(C) + 0.5 \cdot 0.45 = 0.225$

**Update 4** ($t=3$, $S_3 = B \to S_4 = C$, $R_4 = 1$):
- TD target: $1 + 0.9 \cdot V(C) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0.225 = 1.2025$
- TD error: $\delta_3 = 1.2025 - V(B) = 1.2025 - 0.5 = 0.7025$
- $V(B) \leftarrow V(B) + 0.5 \cdot 0.7025 = 0.5 + 0.35125 = 0.85125$

**Update 5** ($t=4$, $S_4 = C \to$ terminal, $R_5 = 0$):
- TD target: $0 + 0.9 \cdot V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ (terminal has value 0)
- TD error: $\delta_4 = 0 - V(C) = 0 - 0.225 = -0.225$
- $V(C) \leftarrow V(C) + 0.5 \cdot (-0.225) = 0.225 - 0.1125 = 0.1125$

**Summary Table of Final Values:**

| State | Initial $V$ | Final $V$ after 5 updates |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 0.0000 | **0.0000** |
| $B$ | 0.0000 | **0.8513** |
| $C$ | 0.0000 | **0.1125** |

**Observation:** State $B$ accumulates the highest value because every visit to $B$ is *one step* away from the $+1$ reward transition. State $C$ is *two steps* away on average and gets a discounted share. State $A$ never received a non-zero TD error because its only outgoing reward was zero — a key insight in TD propagation. $\blacksquare$

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- (1) Correct TD(0) formula stated — **1 mark**
- (2) Five updates with target, error, new value each — **1 mark × 5 = 5 marks**
- (3) Final summary + brief interpretation — **1 mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the policy probability $\pi(a \mid s)$ in the Bellman backup.** Many students write the Bellman Expectation Equation *without* the $\pi(a \mid s)$ weighting — this is a **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction in 3-mark questions and **2–3 marks** in 14-mark derivations.
>
> 2. **Confusing "TD target" with "TD error."** The **target** is $r + \gamma V(s')$; the **error** is the *difference* between target and current $V(s)$. Examiners love this distinction.
>
> 3. **Using $V(s')$ instead of $V(s_t)$ in the update.** The TD(0) update modifies the value of the state the agent *left*, not the one it entered. Wrong application: -1 mark per occurrence in numerical questions.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to show $\Delta_k$ and convergence check.** Even when the iteration count is specified, you must show $\Delta_k$ at each step in policy evaluation table questions — it's a 1-mark cushion that students routinely forfeit.
>
> 5. **No justification of contraction.** When asked "why does iterative PE converge?", simply saying "it converges" earns 0.5 of 2 marks. The keyword examiners look for is **$\gamma$-contraction** + **Banach fixed point**.
>
> 6. **Mixing model-based and model-free.** Iterative PE needs $P$ and $R$ explicitly. If the question says "no model is given," you **must** use MC or TD — not DP. Wrong method: up to **4 marks lost** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU 2024 Module 2 — Policy Evaluation and Prediction: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Policy Evaluation** = computing $V^{\pi}(s)$ for a *given* fixed policy $\pi$ — the *measurement* phase, not the *optimization* phase.
- ✅ The **Bellman Expectation Equation** is the defining equation: $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- ✅ The Bellman operator $T^{\pi}$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** under $\|\cdot\|_\infty$ — guarantees geometric convergence at rate $\gamma$.
- ✅ **Iterative Policy Evaluation** applies $T^{\pi}$ repeatedly; $V_k \to V^{\pi}$ as $k \to \infty$, with error bound $\gamma^k \|V_0 - V^{\pi}\|_\infty$.
- ✅ **Closed-form** solution exists: $V^{\pi} = (I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1} r^{\pi}$, but is $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^3)$ — impractical for large/continuous robotics states.
- ✅ **Synchronous vs. In-place** updates: same theory, in-place is faster in practice.
- ✅ **Convergence threshold** $\theta$: stop when $\max_s \mid V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \mid < \theta$. Typical $\theta = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$.
- ✅ **TD(0) update**: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)]$ — model-free, online, biased but low variance.
- ✅ **MC update**: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [G_t - V(s)]$ — model-free, offline, unbiased but high variance.
- ✅ **TD($\lambda$)** blends MC and TD via eligibility traces $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$.
- ✅ **Discount factor** $\gamma \in [0,1)$: close to 1 → far-sighted (robotic control); close to 0 → myopic.
- ✅ **Learning rate** $\alpha$ must satisfy **Robbins-Monro** conditions for stochastic convergence of MC/TD.
- ✅ For Part B numerical questions, **always tabulate** $(V_k(s_1), V_k(s_2), \ldots, V_k(s_n), \Delta_k)$ neatly — examiners reward presentation.
- ✅ The **value function propagation delay** is $d$ sweeps in a chain of length $d$ — information from terminal rewards must travel backwards one state per iteration.
- ✅ **Prediction vs. Control**: this module is *prediction* only. Policy improvement (using $\arg\max$) belongs to Module 3.

**Mnemonic for Bellman Expectation vs. Optimality:**
> "**E**valuation = **E**xpectation, **O**ptimality = **O**perator-$\max$."

**Mnemonic for MC vs. TD bias-variance:**
> "**M**C = **M**onte Carlo = unbiased but **M**essy (high variance). **T**D = **T**ricked by its own estimate (biased but **T**ight)."

**Connection to robotics exam scenarios:** KTU 2024 frequently frames prediction questions in robot-navigation grids, pick-and-place manipulators, and multi-link arm reaching tasks. Always identify the state set, action set, reward function, and discount factor from the problem statement *first*, then apply the correct prediction method.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Iterative Policy Evaluation (IPE)** is a foundational dynamic programming algorithm in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that computes the **state-value function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ for a given stationary policy $\pi$, by repeatedly applying the **Bellman Expectation Equation** as an update rule until the value estimates converge to a stable fixed point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> Iterative Policy Evaluation is the process of computing the value function $V^{\pi}$ for an arbitrary policy $\pi$ by turning the Bellman expectation equation into an iterative update rule, known as an **expected update** or **Bellman backup**, that sweeps through the state space refining value estimates.

Mathematically, for a Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$, the algorithm computes:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ is the finite set of states
- $\mathcal{A}$ is the finite set of actions
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is the transition probability function
- $R(s, a, s')$ is the expected reward
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor** (**scalar, dimensionless**)
- $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the policy probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition: The "Hiking Trail Map" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a **hiker planning a multi-day trek** through a mountain range. You have an old, unreliable map (initial guess of values). For every trail junction (state), you ask: *"If I follow my current planned route (policy), how scenic and safe will the rest of the journey be from this point?"*
>
> You start with **rough guesses** ($V_0$) — say, all zeros. Then you walk the trail mentally, **junction by junction**, and for each junction, you estimate the value by looking at:
> 1. The immediate scenic reward of the next path
> 2. The estimated future value of the destination of that path
>
> You update your map. You do this **again and again** (iterating), each time using the latest estimates. After many passes, your estimates stop changing — you have **converged**. This final map is the value function $V^{\pi}$ for your chosen route.

**Robotic Interpretation:** In robotics, this corresponds to evaluating a fixed control policy (e.g., a robot's hand-tuned walking gait) by computing how much cumulative reward (e.g., distance walked without falling) the robot can expect from every possible posture/configuration state. The IPE algorithm tells the engineer **"how good is this policy?"** before attempting expensive policy improvement.

## 1.3 Why IPE Matters in the KTU Curriculum

| Perspective | Significance |
|-------------|--------------|
| **Theoretical** | Foundation for **Policy Iteration** and **Value Iteration** algorithms |
| **Algorithmic** | First concrete DP algorithm students implement |
| **Robotics Application** | Evaluates fixed control strategies in known environment models |
| **Convergence** | Demonstrates the powerful **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem** in action |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Iterative Policy Evaluation is *always* asked in Part A (3 marks) and frequently in Part B (14 marks) of Module 2 because it is the **algorithmic backbone** of all model-based RL.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step

The Iterative Policy Evaluation algorithm works in **synchronous (Jacobi-style) sweeps** through the state space:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Create a value array $V_0(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. The most common initialization is:

$$
V_0(s) = 0 \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

This represents complete ignorance about future rewards.

**Step 2 — Iterative Backup (Bellman Update):**
For each iteration $k+1$, update the value of every state $s$ using the values from iteration $k$:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s') \right]
$$

**The "Why" behind this update:**
- $\pi(a \mid s)$ — weights the importance of action $a$ under the current policy
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — accounts for environmental stochasticity
- $R(s, a, s')$ — captures the **immediate** reward signal
- $\gamma V_k(s')$ — the **bootstrapped** estimate of all future rewards

**Step 3 — Convergence Check:**
After each sweep, compute the maximum absolute change across all states:

$$
\Delta_k = \max_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**
If $\Delta_k < \theta$ (a small positive threshold, e.g., $\theta = 10^{-4}$), stop and return $V^{\pi} \approx V_{k+1}$. Otherwise, increment $k$ and return to Step 2.

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Banach Contraction Mapping Theorem (Guarantees Convergence):**
> The Bellman expectation operator $T^{\pi}$, defined by $(T^{\pi} V)(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V(s')]$, is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in the supremum norm:
> $$\Vert T^{\pi} V_1 - T^{\pi} V_2 \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_{\infty}$$
> This guarantees that repeated application converges to a **unique fixed point** $V^{\pi}$, the true value function of policy $\pi$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Bellman Expectation Equation** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | True value of state $s$ under policy $\pi$ |
| 2 | **IPE Update Rule** | $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | One-step synchronous backup |
| 3 | **Convergence Criterion** | $\Delta_k = \max_{s} \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \theta$ | Stop when values stabilize |
| 4 | **Convergence Rate Bound** | $\Vert V_{k} - V^{\pi} \Vert_{\infty} \leq \frac{\gamma^k}{1-\gamma} \Vert V_1 - V_0 \Vert_{\infty}$ | Exponential decay of error |
| 5 | **Discount Factor Range** | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ (**scalar, dimensionless**) | $\gamma = 0$: myopic; $\gamma \to 1$: far-sighted |
| 6 | **Policy Expectation (Action Probabilities)** | $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) = 1, \quad \pi(a \mid s) \geq 0$ | Stochastic policy validity |
| 7 | **Convergence Theorem (Contraction)** | $\Vert T^{\pi}V_1 - T^{\pi}V_2 \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_{\infty}$ | Guarantees unique fixed point |
| 8 | **Expected Immediate Reward** | $r^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s,a,s')$ | Reward term alone |
| 9 | **Bellman Operator (Compact Form)** | $V_{k+1} = R^{\pi} + \gamma P^{\pi} V_k$ | Vector/matrix notation |
| 10 | **Closed-Form Solution** | $V^{\pi} = (I - \gamma P^{\pi})^{-1} R^{\pi}$ | Direct linear solve ($O(n^3)$) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics, IPE is used in:
- **Locomotion control evaluation:** Computing expected return for a fixed gait policy in a bipedal robot
- **Manipulation planning:** Evaluating a fixed grasp-selection policy in known object models
- **Autonomous navigation:** Scoring candidate path-following policies against a known grid-map
- **Industrial automation:** Assessing throughput of a fixed pick-and-place routine in a deterministic factory cell

## 2.5 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Updates

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> - **Synchronous (Jacobi) IPE:** Uses $V_k$ for all updates to compute $V_{k+1}$. Requires two copies of the value array.
> - **Asynchronous (Gauss-Seidel) IPE:** Updates states in-place: $V(s) \leftarrow \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}P(s' \mid s,a)[R + \gamma V(s')]$. Converges faster in practice but harder to parallelize.
