| $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Policy — probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |
| $V^{\pi}(s)$ | State-value function under policy $\pi$ |
| $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ | Action-value function under policy $\pi$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Convention:** Unless otherwise stated, the discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$ is commonly used in exam problems; $\gamma = 0$ reduces the problem to an immediate-reward greedy search.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Broken Down

The Policy Iteration algorithm is a **two-step iterative cycle**:

#### **Step 1 — Policy Evaluation (Prediction)**

Given a policy $\pi_k$, compute its true value function $V^{\pi_k}(s)$ by solving the linear system (or iteratively) of the **Bellman Expectation Equation**:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

- **Why?** We need a numerical "score" for every state under the *current* policy to know how good the policy actually is.
- **How?** This can be done by (a) solving a system of $|\mathcal{S}|$ linear equations directly, or (b) using **iterative policy evaluation** (sweeping through states repeatedly until $V$ converges).

#### **Step 2 — Policy Improvement (Control)**

Using the newly evaluated $V^{\pi_k}(s)$, construct a new greedy policy $\pi_{k+1}$:

$$\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s') \right]$$

- **Why?** If we are in state $s$ and we know $V^{\pi_k}(s')$, we can do even better by choosing the action with the largest one-step lookahead.
- **How?** The argmax operation makes the policy **deterministic** — each state is mapped to a single best action.

#### **Step 3 — Convergence Check**

If $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$, **stop**. The current policy and value function are optimal: $\pi_k = \pi^*$, $V^{\pi_k} = V^*$. Otherwise, return to Step 1 with $\pi_{k+1}$.

### 2.2 The Bellman Optimality Equation (Target)

When the algorithm converges, the value function satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

This is a **non-linear** system of equations (due to the $\max$ operator), which is why we need the iterative PI/VI machinery rather than a direct linear solve.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bellman Expectation (state-value) | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Policy Evaluation |
| 2 | Bellman Expectation (action-value) | $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q^{\pi}(s', a')]$ | Convert between $V$ and $Q$ |
| 3 | Bellman Optimality | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Defines optimal solution |
| 4 | Greedy Policy Improvement | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | One-step lookahead improvement |
| 5 | Policy Improvement Theorem | $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s) \quad \forall s \implies V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ | Guarantees monotonic improvement |
| 6 | Contraction Property | $\Vert V_{k+1} - V^* \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_{\infty}$ | Convergence rate of inner loop |
| 7 | Discount Sum Identity | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Defines return used in $V^{\pi}$ |
| 8 | Deterministic Optimal Policy | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$ | Output of converged PI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In industrial robotics, Policy Iteration is used in **task scheduling** for autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs), in **inventory control** (classical OR problem reducible to MDP), and in **grid-world path planning** for service robots. The convergence is guaranteed and often happens in surprisingly few iterations (often $< 10$ outer loops for $|\mathcal{S}| < 100$).

### 2.4 Convergence & Termination

- **Policy Iteration** converges in a **finite number of iterations** in finite MDPs (since there are only $|\mathcal{A}|^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ deterministic policies).
- **Policy Evaluation (iterative)** converges geometrically at rate $\gamma$; the error shrinks by factor $\gamma$ each sweep.
- **Practical stopping criteria:** Stop inner PE when $\max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \theta_{inner}$ (typically $\theta = 10^{-4}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation

We start from the definition of the state-value function as the expected return starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 1 — Split the return into immediate reward and discounted future return:**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+2} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of total expectation, conditioning on the first action $A_t$ and the next state $S_{t+1}$:**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+2} \;\middle|\; S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the conditional expectation is exactly $V^{\pi}(s')$ by the Markov property:**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Policy Improvement Theorem

We wish to prove that if the greedy policy $\pi'$ satisfies $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$, then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$.

**Step 1 — Start with the right-hand side, expanding $V^{\pi}$ recursively one step using the *old* policy $\pi$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &\leq Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^{\pi}(S_{t+1}, \pi'(S_{t+1})) \mid S_t = s \right] \quad \text{(apply hypothesis to } S_{t+1} \text{)} \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} V^{\pi}(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t = s \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Repeat the substitution recursively $k$ times:**

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{k} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{k} V^{\pi}(S_{t+k}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$.** Since $V^{\pi}$ is bounded and $\gamma^{k} \to 0$ for $\gamma \in [0, 1)$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right] = V^{\pi'}(s)$$

Hence $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A 3-State MDP (KTU-Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Adapted]**  
> Consider a robot with 3 states $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$. From any state, the robot can take action $a_1$ (move) or $a_2$ (stay). The MDP is deterministic with rewards: $R(s_1, a_1) = 0$, $R(s_2, a_1) = +1$, $R(s_3, a_1) = +10$, and $R(s, a_2) = 0$ for all $s$. Transitions for $a_1$: $s_1 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_3$, $s_3 \to s_3$. Action $a_2$ is a self-loop. Let $\gamma = 0.9$.

#### **Iteration 0 — Initial Policy $\pi_0$**

Suppose $\pi_0$ chooses $a_1$ in every state (the "move" policy).

**Policy Evaluation:** Under $\pi_0$, the system is deterministic, so we have:

$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi_0}(s_1) &= 0 + 0.9 V^{\pi_0}(s_2) \\
V^{\pi_0}(s_2) &= 1 + 0.9 V^{\pi_0}(s_3) \\
V^{\pi_0}(s_3) &= 10 + 0.9 V^{\pi_0}(s_3)
\end{aligned}$$

From the third equation: $V^{\pi_0}(s_3) = \frac{10}{1 - 0.9} = 100$.  
Then $V^{\pi_0}(s_2) = 1 + 0.9 \times 100 = 91$.  
Then $V^{\pi_0}(s_1) = 0 + 0.9 \times 91 = 81.9$.

#### **Policy Improvement — Greedy Step**

Now compute $Q^{\pi_0}(s, a_1)$ and $Q^{\pi_0}(s, a_2)$ for each state:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi_0}(s_1, a_1) &= 0 + 0.9 \times 91 = 81.9 \\
Q^{\pi_0}(s_1, a_2) &= 0 + 0.9 \times V^{\pi_0}(s_1) = 0.9 \times 81.9 = 73.71
\end{aligned}$$

Greedy choice for $s_1$: still $a_1$ (since $81.9 > 73.71$).

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi_0}(s_2, a_1) &= 1 + 0.9 \times 100 = 91 \\
Q^{\pi_0}(s_2, a_2) &= 0 + 0.9 \times 91 = 81.9
\end{aligned}$$

Greedy choice for $s_2$: still $a_1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi_0}(s_3, a_1) &= 10 + 0.9 \times 100 = 100 \\
Q^{\pi_0}(s_3, a_2) &= 0 + 0.9 \times 100 = 90
\end{aligned}$$

Greedy choice for $s_3$: still $a_1$.

**Since $\pi_1 = \pi_0$, the algorithm has converged in 1 outer iteration.** The optimal policy is *"always take action $a_1$"* and the optimal value function is $V^* = (81.9, 91, 100)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice how Policy Iteration converged in just *one* outer loop in this case. This is because the initial policy was already optimal — but the inner policy evaluation did the heavy lifting by giving us the *true* value of every state, which is exactly what a robot's planner needs to make decisions.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Policy Iteration vs. Value Iteration

| Property | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inner loop | Full PE until convergence | Single Bellman backup (sweep) |
| Outer loop | Policy improvement | None (combined update) |
| Convergence | Finite (deterministic, finite MDP) | Asymptotic, geometric ($\gamma$-rate) |
| Per-iteration cost | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^{2} \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ (exact solve) | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^{2} \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ per sweep |
| Output | Both $V^*$ and $\pi^*$ | $V^*$, then $\pi^*$ extracted at end |
| Best for | Small state spaces | Larger state spaces |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Flow of the Policy Iteration Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize: Random policy pi0] --> B[Set k = 0]
    B --> C[Policy Evaluation: Compute Vk for pik]
    C --> D{Converged inner loop?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Policy Improvement: Build greedy pi k+1 from Vk]
    E --> F{pi k+1 == pik?}
    F -- No --> G[Increment k to k+1] --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Output: Optimal policy pi* and value V*]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MDP Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA[State Space S] --> Core
    InputB[Action Space A] --> Core
    InputC[Transition P] --> Core
    InputD[Reward R] --> Core
    InputE[Discount gamma] --> Core
    Core[Policy Iteration Engine]
    Core --> Mod1[Module A: Policy Evaluator]
    Core --> Mod2[Module B: Greedy Improver]
    Core --> Mod3[Module C: Convergence Monitor]
    Mod1 --> Mod3
    Mod2 --> Mod3
    Mod3 --> Decision{Stable?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Output[Optimal pi* and V*]
    Decision -- No --> Mod1
```

### 4.3 State-Value Evolution Across Iterations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State s: Initial guess V0] --> S1[Sweep 1: V1 from Bellman backup]
    S1 --> S2[Sweep 2: V2]
    S2 --> S3[Sweep k: Vk]
    S3 --> Check{Difference less than theta?}
    Check -- No --> S1
    Check -- Yes --> Stop[Vk approximates V to precision theta]
    Stop --> Improve[Greedy policy extracted from Vk]
    Improve --> PolicyCheck{Policy unchanged?}
    PolicyCheck -- No --> NewPol[New policy pik+1] --> S0
    PolicyCheck -- Yes --> Final[pi* and V* output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (for KTU exams):** In the KTU Board Exam, a well-labelled block diagram of the Policy Iteration cycle — showing the *outer* improvement loop and the *inner* evaluation loop — can fetch **2 to 3 easy marks** as a sub-part. Memorize the two-box structure: **PE (prediction) $\leftrightarrow$ PI (control)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Bellman Optimality Equation. Why is it non-linear, and how does Policy Iteration resolve this non-linearity?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$
- **Non-linearity reason (1 mark):** The $\max$ operator makes the equation a non-linear system; a direct linear-algebraic solve (as in Bellman expectation) is not possible.
- **Resolution by PI (1 mark):** Policy Iteration alternates between (a) linear policy evaluation to compute $V^{\pi_k}$, and (b) greedy action selection via $\arg\max$, which resolves the $\max$ by turning it into a discrete choice at each step.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and briefly explain the Policy Improvement Theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** If a new policy $\pi'$ is greedy with respect to the value function of the old policy $\pi$, i.e., $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$, then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$.
- **Brief Explanation (1 mark):** This theorem guarantees that the policy never gets worse after an improvement step, providing the monotonic-convergence backbone of Policy Iteration.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ starting from the definition of the expected discounted return. Clearly state the Markov property assumption used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the two main steps of the Policy Iteration algorithm. Show that Policy Iteration converges in a finite number of iterations in a finite MDP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation (7 Marks)**

We start with the definition of the state-value function:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Factor the immediate reward out of the summation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+2} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Condition on the first action $A_t$ and next state $S_{t+1}$ using the law of total expectation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+2} \;\middle|\; S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s' \right]$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Use the **Markov property** — the future depends only on $S_{t+1}$, not on the history of earlier states. The reward $R_{t+1}$ depends only on $(s, a, s')$, and the future sum is exactly $V^{\pi}(s')$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude that this is a **linear system of $|\mathcal{S}|$ equations** in $|\mathcal{S}|$ unknowns, solvable by standard methods or iteratively.

**Markov Property Assumption (1.5 marks):** The state $S_t$ is a sufficient statistic of the history; i.e., $\mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \dots) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$. This allows the future return to be written as a function of $S_{t+1}$ alone.

---

**(b) Policy Iteration Algorithm & Finite Convergence (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2.5 marks):** Draw the cycle:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize pi0] --> B[Policy Evaluation: Solve Vk]
    B --> C[Policy Improvement: Greedy pik+1]
    C --> D{Stable?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Output pi* and V*]
```

**Step 2 — Policy Evaluation (1.5 marks):** Compute $V^{\pi_k}$ by solving the linear Bellman expectation system (or iteratively until $\max_s \vert V_{new}(s) - V_{old}(s) \vert < \theta$).

**Step 3 — Policy Improvement (1.5 marks):** Construct $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s') \right]$.

**Step 4 — Finite Convergence Proof (1.5 marks):**
- In a finite MDP, the number of deterministic policies is finite: $|\mathcal{A}|^{|\mathcal{S}|} < \infty$.
- By the **Policy Improvement Theorem**, each outer iteration produces a strictly better policy unless the policy is already optimal.
- Therefore, the algorithm must terminate in **at most $|\mathcal{A}|^{|\mathcal{S}|} - 1$ outer iterations** — a finite number.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Policy Iteration** and **Value Iteration**. Use a small numerical example (2-state MDP) to demonstrate the convergence behavior of both algorithms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Policy Improvement Theorem** with a step-by-step proof. Discuss what happens when $\gamma = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table with Numerical Illustration (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inner loop | Full PE to convergence | Single Bellman backup |
| Update rule | Separate PE and PI steps | Combined $\max_a$ + Bellman backup |
| Convergence | Finite (exact) | Asymptotic ($\gamma$-rate) |
| Output | $\pi^*$ and $V^*$ together | $V^*$ first, $\pi^*$ at end |

**Numerical Example (4 marks):** Consider a 2-state MDP with $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2\}$, $\mathcal{A} = \{a, b\}$, deterministic transitions, $R(s_1, a) = 0, R(s_1, b) = 5, R(s_2, a) = 10, R(s_2, b) = 0$, $\gamma = 0.5$.

- **Value Iteration (2 marks):** Initialize $V_0 = (0, 0)$. Update $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a [R(s, a) + \gamma V_k(s')]$. After several sweeps, $V$ converges geometrically to $V^*$.
- **Policy Iteration (2 marks):** Initialize $\pi_0 = $ "always $a$." PE gives $V^{\pi_0}(s_1) = 0 + 0.5 \times 10 = 5$, $V^{\pi_0}(s_2) = 10 + 0.5 \times 10 = 15$. Greedy improvement: at $s_1$, $Q(s_1, a) = 0 + 0.5 \times 15 = 7.5$, $Q(s_1, b) = 5 + 0.5 \times 0 = 5$. So $\pi_1$ picks $a$ at $s_1$ and $a$ at $s_2$. **Convergence in 1 outer iteration.**

---

**(b) Policy Improvement Theorem — Proof and $\gamma = 0$ Discussion (7 Marks)**

**Theorem Statement (1.5 marks):** If $\pi'$ is greedy with respect to $V^{\pi}$, then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Proof (4.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** $V^{\pi}(s) \leq Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s))$ by the greedy hypothesis.

**Step 2:** Expand $Q^{\pi}$ as one-step lookahead: $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]$.

**Step 3:** Apply the hypothesis recursively: $\mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1})] \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} V^{\pi}(S_{t+2})]$.

**Step 4:** Repeat $k$ times and let $k \to \infty$; the tail $\gamma^{k} V^{\pi}(S_{t+k}) \to 0$ because $V^{\pi}$ is bounded and $\gamma < 1$.

**Step 5:** The remaining sum is precisely $V^{\pi'}(s)$. Hence $V^{\pi}(s) \leq V^{\pi'}(s)$. $\blacksquare$

**$\gamma = 0$ Discussion (1 mark):** When $\gamma = 0$, the value function collapses to $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) R(s, a)$, depending only on the immediate reward. The greedy policy simply picks the action with the highest immediate reward in each state — no lookahead is needed. The Policy Improvement Theorem still holds trivially since the discounted tail is zero.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi(a \mid s)$ term** in the Bellman expectation equation when writing it from memory. (Always include the policy weighting over actions.)
> 2. **Confusing Bellman *Expectation* with Bellman *Optimality*** — the former has $\sum_a \pi(\cdot)$, the latter has $\max_a$. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Stating "Policy Iteration converges quickly"** without justifying it with the **Policy Improvement Theorem** + finite policy space argument. This is a 3-mark item — vague answers score 0.
> 4. **Writing Value Iteration and Policy Iteration as identical** in the comparison question. Highlight the difference: VI does a *single* max-backup per sweep, PI does a *full* PE per outer loop.
> 5. **Skipping the Markov property assumption** in derivations. Always state it explicitly.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Policy Iteration = Policy Evaluation + Greedy Policy Improvement**, executed alternately until stability.
- ✅ **Bellman Expectation Equation** is *linear* in $V^{\pi}$ — solvable directly or iteratively; uses the policy weighting $\pi(a \mid s)$.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality Equation** is *non-linear* (because of $\max_a$) — it characterizes the optimal solution $V^*$.
- ✅ **Greedy improvement step:** $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')]$ — produces a *deterministic* new policy.
- ✅ **Policy Improvement Theorem** guarantees $V^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq V^{\pi_k}(s)$ for all $s$ — never worse.
- ✅ **Convergence in finite MDP:** at most $|\mathcal{A}|^{|\mathcal{S}|} - 1$ outer iterations (finite!).
- ✅ **Policy Iteration vs. Value Iteration:** PI = exact, finite convergence, expensive inner loop; VI = approximate, asymptotic convergence ($\gamma$-rate), cheap per-sweep.
- ✅ **Modified Policy Iteration (MPI):** truncate the inner PE after $k$ Bellman backups — a practical middle-ground used in large state spaces.
- ✅ **Discount factor $\gamma$:** $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ ensures convergence; $\gamma = 0$ reduces to immediate-reward greedy choice.
- ✅ **Required environment model:** Policy Iteration is **model-based** — it needs $P$ and $R$ a priori. For model-free settings, use Q-Learning or SARSA instead.
- ✅ **Robotics application:** grid-world navigation, task scheduling, inventory control, and pre-computed action maps for autonomous robots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Value Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Iteration (VI)** is a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm used to compute the **optimal value function** $V^*(s)$ and the corresponding **optimal policy** $\pi^*$ for a finite **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. It iteratively applies the **Bellman Optimality Backup Operator** to all states until the value function converges, and then extracts the greedy policy with respect to the converged value function.

In formal mathematical notation, Value Iteration is defined as the recursive application of the **Bellman optimality operator** $\mathcal{T}^*$:

$$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V_k(s') \right]$$

where:
- $V_{k}(s)$ is the value of state $s$ at iteration $k$
- $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ is the transition probability from state $s$ to $s'$ under action $a$
- $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ is the immediate reward
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor**
- $k$ is the iteration index

The algorithm terminates when $\max_{s} \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is a small convergence threshold.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hiking Map Problem**

Imagine you are standing on a mountain at dusk and need to find the safest path down to the village in the valley, but the fog is so thick that you can only see **one step ahead** at a time. You cannot plan the whole route in advance because the terrain is unknown.

**Value Iteration is like repeatedly asking every point on the mountain the question:**

> *"If I were standing here, and I magically already knew the best value (elevation) of all my neighboring points, what would be the maximum expected value I could achieve from here in one step?"*

- **Iteration 0**: You guess that every point on the mountain has a value of 0 (you are at the top, clueless).
- **Iteration 1**: Each point looks at its immediate neighbors and updates its value to "reward of moving to that neighbor + 0." You now know the value of being *one step away* from the goal.
- **Iteration 2**: Each point updates again using the *new* neighbor values. Now you know the value of being *two steps away*.
- This continues — values ripple backward through the state space like a wave — until every point "knows" its true long-term value.
- Finally, the **policy** is extracted greedily: at every point, simply walk toward the neighbor with the highest value.

This backward propagation of values is exactly what Value Iteration does in an MDP.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| State Space | $\mathcal{S}$ | Set of all possible states in the environment |
| Action Space | $\mathcal{A}$ | Set of all possible actions an agent can take |
| Transition Probability | $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ | Probability of reaching state $s'$ from state $s$ under action $a$ |
| Reward Function | $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ | Immediate scalar feedback after transition |
| Discount Factor | $\gamma$ | **Range: $[0, 1)$** — trades off immediate vs. future reward |
| Optimal Value Function | $V^*(s)$ | Maximum expected cumulative discounted reward from state $s$ |
| Bellman Optimality Operator | $\mathcal{T}^*$ | The backup operator applied in VI |

## 1.4 Prerequisites

> [!NOTE]
> Before studying Value Iteration, the student must be familiar with:
> 1. **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** formulation: the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma)$
> 2. **Bellman Expectation Equation** (for a fixed policy)
> 3. **Bellman Optimality Equation** (the fixed-point equation solved by VI)
> 4. Concepts of **policy**, **return**, and **discounted cumulative reward**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Optimality Backup Operator

The central mathematical object in Value Iteration is the **Bellman Optimality Operator** $\mathcal{T}^*$, which maps a value function $V$ to a new value function $V'$:

$$(\mathcal{T}^* V)(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V(s') \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operator $\mathcal{T}^*$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction mapping** under the supremum norm:
> $$\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$$
> This guarantees convergence to a **unique fixed point** $V^*$, the optimal value function.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Value Iteration

The Value Iteration algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialization**: Set $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (or any arbitrary bounded initialization).
2. **Iterative Update**: For each state $s$, compute the Bellman optimality backup:
   - For every action $a$, compute the action-value $Q(s, a) = \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s')\right]$
   - Set $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} Q(s, a)$
3. **Convergence Check**: Terminate when $\max_{s} \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \epsilon$.
4. **Policy Extraction**: Derive the greedy optimal policy:
   $$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')\right]$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use $\max$?** Because the optimal value function must satisfy the *best possible* action at every state, not the expected value of a *specific* policy.
- **Why does the contraction property hold?** Because the $\max$ operator is non-expansive, and the discount factor $\gamma < 1$ shrinks the distance between successive iterates.
- **Why $\gamma < 1$ is critical:** Without discounting, the operator would not be a strict contraction, and convergence would not be guaranteed for infinite-horizon MDPs.

## 2.4 Convergence Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Bound (KTU High-Yield Result)**
> After $k$ iterations of Value Iteration, the error between $V_k$ and $V^*$ is bounded by:
> $$\Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \frac{\gamma^k}{1 - \gamma} \max_{s} \vert V_1(s) - V_0(s) \vert$$

This means the number of iterations required to achieve an $\epsilon$-optimal value function is:

$$k = O\left( \log\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon (1-\gamma)}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every equation you must memorize for Value Iteration in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Purpose / Use |
|---|-------------------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Value Iteration Update | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s')\right]$ | Core update rule |
| 2 | Bellman Optimality Operator | $(\mathcal{T}^* V)(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s')\right]$ | Compact operator form |
| 3 | Contraction Property | $\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$ | Guarantees convergence |
| 4 | Fixed-Point Equation | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')\right]$ | Bellman Optimality Equation |
| 5 | Greedy Policy Extraction | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')\right]$ | Recover policy from $V^*$ |

| 11 | Asynchronous VI Update | $V(s) \leftarrow \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s')\right]$ | In-place variant, state-by-state |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Operator

> [!NOTE]
> This section shows the full mathematical derivation of why Value Iteration converges, starting from the principle of optimality.

### Step 1: Define the Optimal Return

The expected return from state $s_0$ under a policy $\pi$ is:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \, R_{t+k+1}$$

The optimal value function is the maximum expected return over all possible policies:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$

### Step 2: Apply the Markov Property

By the Markov property, the future depends only on the current state. Decompose the return into the immediate reward and the discounted future return:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

### Step 3: Introduce the Action Selection

Since the policy selects actions, the expectation can be written as a sum over actions:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \, \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

### Step 4: Use the Transition Model

The expectation can be computed by summing over next states weighted by the transition probability $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \, \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s' \right]$$

### Step 5: Recognize the Recursive Structure

The future expected return $G_{t+1}$ from the new state $s'$ is itself $V^*(s')$ under the optimal policy. Therefore:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation**, and Value Iteration solves it by iteratively applying its RHS as an update rule.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Contraction Property

We want to show that $\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$.

### Step 1: Define the difference

For each state $s$, define:

$$\delta(s) = \vert (\mathcal{T}^* V_1)(s) - (\mathcal{T}^* V_2)(s) \vert$$

### Step 2: Expand using the operator definition

$$\delta(s) = \left\vert \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_1(s')\right] - \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_2(s')\right] \right\vert$$

### Step 3: Use the non-expansive property of max

For any two functions $f$ and $g$, $\vert \max_a f(a) - \max_a g(a) \vert \leq \max_a \vert f(a) - g(a) \vert$. Therefore:

$$\delta(s) \leq \max_{a} \left\vert \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \gamma V_1(s') - \gamma V_2(s') \right] \right\vert$$

### Step 4: Factor out $\gamma$ and the probability sum

Since $\sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) = 1$, the difference becomes a probability-weighted average:

$$\delta(s) \leq \gamma \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left\vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \right\vert$$

### Step 5: Take supremum over states

$$\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty = \max_s \delta(s) \leq \gamma \max_{s'} \vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \vert = \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$$

This proves that $\mathcal{T}^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction, and by the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application converges to a unique fixed point $V^*$.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — A Simple 3-State MDP

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU-style numerical example. Work through it carefully — exam questions of this type appear frequently.

### MDP Specification

Consider an MDP with three states $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ and two actions $\{a_1, a_2\}$ per state. Let $\gamma = 0.9$.

| State | Action | Next State | Probability | Reward |
|-------|--------|------------|-------------|--------|
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 1.0 | +1 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | +2 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |

### Iteration 0

Initialize $V_0(s_1) = V_0(s_2) = V_0(s_3) = 0$.

### Iteration 1

For each state, compute the max over actions:

**State $s_1$:**

$$V_1(s_1) = \max \begin{cases} \mathcal{R}(s_1, a_1, s_2) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_2) = 1 + 0.9(0) = 1 \\ \mathcal{R}(s_1, a_2, s_1) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_1) = 0 + 0.9(0) = 0 \end{cases} = 1$$

**State $s_2$:**

$$V_1(s_2) = \max \begin{cases} \mathcal{R}(s_2, a_1, s_3) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_3) = 2 + 0.9(0) = 2 \\ \mathcal{R}(s_2, a_2, s_1) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_1) = 0 + 0.9(0) = 0 \end{cases} = 2$$

**State $s_3$:**

$$V_1(s_3) = \max \begin{cases} \mathcal{R}(s_3, a_1, s_3) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_3) = 0 + 0 = 0 \\ \mathcal{R}(s_3, a_2, s_3) + \gamma \cdot V_0(s_3) = 0 + 0 = 0 \end{cases} = 0$$

So $V_1 = (1, 2, 0)$.

### Iteration 2

**State $s_1$:**

$$V_2(s_1) = \max \begin{cases} 1 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_2) = 1 + 0.9(2) = 2.8 \\ 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_1) = 0.9 \end{cases} = 2.8$$

**State $s_2$:**

$$V_2(s_2) = \max \begin{cases} 2 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_3) = 2 + 0 = 2 \\ 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_1) = 0.9 \end{cases} = 2$$

**State $s_3$:** remains 0.

So $V_2 = (2.8, 2.0, 0)$.

### Iteration 3

**State $s_1$:**

$$V_3(s_1) = \max \begin{cases} 1 + 0.9 \cdot V_2(s_2) = 1 + 1.8 = 2.8 \\ 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_2(s_1) = 2.52 \end{cases} = 2.8$$

**State $s_2$:**

$$V_3(s_2) = \max \begin{cases} 2 + 0.9 \cdot V_2(s_3) = 2 \\ 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_2(s_1) = 2.52 \end{cases} = 2.52$$

**State $s_3$:** remains 0.

So $V_3 = (2.8, 2.52, 0)$.

### Convergence and Final Policy

The values will eventually converge. The optimal policy is:

- $\pi^*(s_1) = a_1$ (go to $s_2$)
- $\pi^*(s_2) = a_2$ (go to $s_1$, since this maximizes $V$)
- $\pi^*(s_3) = $ either (no difference — both yield 0)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice how $V(s_2)$ actually *increases* in iteration 3 because the value of $s_1$ has risen. This illustrates how values propagate backwards — improving one state can improve its predecessors.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Value Iteration vs. Policy Iteration

| Criterion | Value Iteration | Policy Iteration |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Inner loop work per outer iteration | None — direct backup | Full **Policy Evaluation** (may need many sweeps) |
| Convergence speed (outer iterations) | Slower (many iterations) | Faster (few iterations) |
| Per-iteration cost | Cheap: $O(\vert S \vert^2 \vert A \vert)$ | Expensive: full policy evaluation |
| Total wall-clock time | Often faster overall | Often slower due to policy evaluation |
| Final policy | Extracted once at the end | Naturally produced at each iteration |
| Handles large state spaces | Better (asynchronous variants) | Worse |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Value Iteration Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize V0 s = 0 for all states] --> B[Set iteration counter k = 0]
    B --> C[For each state s in S]
    C --> D[For each action a in A]
    D --> E[Compute Q s a = sum over s' of P s' given s a times R s a s' + gamma times Vk s']
    E --> F{All actions evaluated for this state}
    F --> D
    F --> G[Set Vk+1 s = max over a of Q s a]
    G --> H{All states updated?}
    H --> C
    H --> I[Compute delta = max over s of absolute value of Vk+1 s minus Vk s]
    I --> J{delta less than epsilon?}
    J --> K[Policy converged: Extract greedy policy pi star s = argmax a Q s a] --> L[Return V star and pi star]
    J --> M[Increment k = k + 1] --> C
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — VI in a Robotic Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Environment Interface]
        E1[State Observation Module]
        E2[Reward Signal Receiver]
        E3[Transition Model P s prime given s a]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Value Iteration Core]
        C1[MDP Tuple Storage]
        C2[Bellman Backup Operator T star]
        C3[Value Function Array V k]
        C4[Convergence Monitor]
        C5[Policy Extractor]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Optimal Value Function V star]
        O2[Optimal Policy pi star]
        O3[Action Selector for Robot]
    end

    E1 --> C1
    E2 --> C1
    E3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 -->|not converged| C2
    C4 -->|converged| C5
    C5 --> O1
    C5 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Convergence Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1: Early Iterations k=1 to 5]
        P1A[Large value updates in every state]
        P1B[Backup operator has high contraction effect]
        P1C[Values rapidly approach near optimal]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2: Mid Iterations k=5 to 20]
        P2A[Smaller incremental updates]
        P2B[Values stabilize on ordering]
        P2C[Optimal action choices identified]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3: Late Iterations k greater than 20]
        P3A[Updates shrink to less than epsilon]
        P3B[Value function at fixed point V star]
        P3C[Greedy policy extraction yields pi star]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

## 4.4 State-Value Update Diagram (Backup Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State s at iteration k] --> A1{Action a sub 1}
    S0 --> A2{Action a sub 2}
    S0 --> A3{Action a sub 3}
    A1 --> Q1[Q s a1 = sum P R plus gamma V k s prime]
    A2 --> Q2[Q s a2 = sum P R plus gamma V k s prime]
    A3 --> Q3[Q s a3 = sum P R plus gamma V k s prime]
    Q1 --> MAX[max operator]
    Q2 --> MAX
    Q3 --> MAX
    MAX --> V1[V k plus 1 s = max value]
    V1 --> NEXT[Used in next iteration backup]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *Define Value Iteration. State the Bellman optimality equation used in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Value Iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that computes the optimal value function $V^*(s)$ for an MDP by iteratively applying the Bellman optimality backup until convergence. The update rule is:

$$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V_k(s') \right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the discount factor. The algorithm converges to $V^*$ as $k \to \infty$, after which the optimal policy is obtained greedily.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for mentioning convergence/greedy extraction.)*

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *Why is Value Iteration guaranteed to converge? Mention the key mathematical property.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Value Iteration is guaranteed to converge because the Bellman optimality operator $\mathcal{T}^*$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** under the supremum norm:

$$\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$$

By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application of a contraction mapping in a complete metric space converges to a unique fixed point — in this case, the optimal value function $V^*$. The contraction property holds because (a) the max operator is non-expansive and (b) the discount factor $\gamma < 1$ shrinks the gap between successive iterates.

*(Award: 1 mark for contraction property, 1 mark for Banach theorem, 1 mark for explaining why contraction holds.)*

---

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse Value Iteration with Policy Iteration — examiners specifically check whether you used the *max* operator (VI) vs. the *expectation under $\pi$* (PI).
> - **Do NOT** omit the discount factor $\gamma$ from your update equation — this is a 1-mark penalty trap.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation from first principles. Explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Consider the MDP given below. Apply Value Iteration for **two complete iterations** starting from $V_0(s) = 0$ for all states, with $\gamma = 0.9$. Identify the optimal action at each state after the two iterations. **(7 marks)**

**MDP Table:**

| State | Action | Next State | Probability | Reward |
|-------|--------|------------|-------------|--------|
| $A$ | $\text{left}$ | $B$ | 0.7 | +1 |
| $A$ | $\text{left}$ | $A$ | 0.3 | 0 |
| $A$ | $\text{right}$ | $A$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $B$ | $\text{up}$ | $A$ | 0.5 | +2 |
| $B$ | $\text{up}$ | $B$ | 0.5 | 0 |
| $B$ | $\text{down}$ | $B$ | 1.0 | 0 |

#### Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of Bellman Optimality Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the optimal value function as the maximum expected return over all policies:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Split the return into immediate reward and discounted future return using the Markov property:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Introduce the action choice by taking the maximum over actions first (since the optimal policy is greedy w.r.t. $V^*$):

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Sum over next states weighted by transition probabilities:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation**.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Role of $\gamma$:
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ ensures the **infinite sum converges** to a finite value (geometric series).
- It encodes **preference for immediate rewards** — lower $\gamma$ means the agent is *myopic*, higher $\gamma$ means *far-sighted*.
- It is essential for the **contraction property** — without $\gamma < 1$, the Bellman operator would not be a strict contraction and convergence would not be guaranteed.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Two Iterations of Value Iteration (7 marks)

**Iteration 0:** $V_0(A) = 0$, $V_0(B) = 0$.

**Iteration 1 (3 marks):**

**State $A$:**

- Action "left": $Q_1(A, \text{left}) = 0.7(1 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.3(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.7$
- Action "right": $Q_1(A, \text{right}) = 1.0(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0$
- $V_1(A) = \max(0.7, 0) = 0.7$

**State $B$:**

- Action "up": $Q_1(B, \text{up}) = 0.5(2 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.5(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 1.0$
- Action "down": $Q_1(B, \text{down}) = 1.0(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0$
- $V_1(B) = \max(1.0, 0) = 1.0$

So $V_1 = (0.7, 1.0)$.

**Iteration 2 (3 marks):**

**State $A$:**

- Action "left": $Q_2(A, \text{left}) = 0.7(1 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0) + 0.3(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0.7) = 0.7(1.9) + 0.3(0.63) = 1.33 + 0.189 = 1.519$
- Action "right": $Q_2(A, \text{right}) = 1.0(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0.7) = 0.63$
- $V_2(A) = \max(1.519, 0.63) = 1.519$

**State $B$:**

- Action "up": $Q_2(B, \text{up}) = 0.5(2 + 0.9 \cdot 0.7) + 0.5(0 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0) = 0.5(2.63) + 0.5(0.9) = 1.315 + 0.45 = 1.765$
- Action "down": $Q_2(B, \text{down}) = 1.0(0 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0) = 0.9$
- $V_2(B) = \max(1.765, 0.9) = 1.765$

So $V_2 = (1.519, 1.765)$.

**Optimal Actions After 2 Iterations (1 mark):**

- $\pi^*(A) = \text{left}$
- $\pi^*(B) = \text{up}$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** State and prove the **contraction property** of the Bellman optimality operator $\mathcal{T}^*$. Hence justify why Value Iteration converges to a unique optimal value function. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare Value Iteration and Policy Iteration. State the **per-iteration computational complexity** of each and discuss the trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Contraction Property Proof (7 marks)

**Statement (1 mark):** The Bellman optimality operator $\mathcal{T}^*$ defined by:

$$(\mathcal{T}^* V)(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s') \right]$$

satisfies:

$$\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$$

**Proof (5 marks):**

For any state $s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert (\mathcal{T}^* V_1)(s) - (\mathcal{T}^* V_2)(s) \vert &= \left\vert \max_{a} f_1(s, a) - \max_{a} f_2(s, a) \right\vert
\end{aligned}$$

where $f_i(s, a) = \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \left[ \mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_i(s') \right]$.

Using the non-expansive property of max:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert (\mathcal{T}^* V_1)(s) - (\mathcal{T}^* V_2)(s) \vert &\leq \max_{a} \vert f_1(s, a) - f_2(s, a) \vert \\
&= \max_{a} \left\vert \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \cdot \gamma (V_1(s') - V_2(s')) \right\vert \\
&\leq \gamma \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \vert \\
&\leq \gamma \max_{s'} \vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \vert
\end{aligned}$$

The last inequality holds because $\sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) = 1$, so the weighted average cannot exceed the maximum.

Taking the supremum over $s$ on both sides:

$$\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Consequence (1 mark):** By the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem, a contraction on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point. Hence Value Iteration converges to a unique $V^*$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — VI vs. PI Comparison (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Value Iteration | Policy Iteration |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Inner loop | None — direct max backup | Full Policy Evaluation |
| Outer iterations | Many (until $\epsilon$ small) | Few (until policy stable) |
| Per-iteration cost | $O(\vert S \vert^2 \vert A \vert)$ | $O(\vert S \vert^2 + \vert S \vert \vert A \vert)$ per PE sweep, many sweeps |
| When to stop | Value function stable | Policy stable |
| Final output | Extract greedy once | Already has policy |

**Trade-offs (4 marks):**

1. **Value Iteration** is preferred when the state space is large because each iteration is cheap, and asynchronous variants (Gauss-Seidel, prioritized sweeping) can prune the work further.
2. **Policy Iteration** often converges in **fewer outer iterations** because each step makes a *policy* improvement, not just a value update — but the inner policy evaluation can be very expensive.
3. **Modified Policy Iteration** is a hybrid: do a fixed number of PE sweeps (e.g., $k$ sweeps) before each improvement, balancing cost and convergence.
4. In practice, both algorithms scale **polynomially** with $\vert S \vert$ and $\vert A \vert$, but both suffer from the **curse of dimensionality** for high-dimensional robotic state spaces — motivating function approximation and approximate dynamic programming.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Value Iteration**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Value Iteration computes the **optimal value function $V^*(s)$** for a finite MDP by iteratively applying the Bellman optimality backup.
- [x] The update uses the **max operator** over actions — this is the key difference from Policy Evaluation.
- [x] The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is **mandatory** for convergence.
- [x] Convergence is guaranteed by the **contraction mapping property** of $\mathcal{T}^*$.

### Must-Memorize Equations
- [x] Update rule: $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\left[\mathcal{R}(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s')\right]$
- [x] Contraction: $\Vert \mathcal{T}^* V_1 - \mathcal{T}^* V_2 \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty$
- [x] Error bound: $\Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \frac{\gamma^k}{1-\gamma} \max_{s} \vert V_1(s) - V_0(s) \vert$
- [x] Iteration count: $k = O\left(\frac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{1-\gamma}\right)$
- [x] Per-iteration cost: $O(\vert S \vert^2 \vert A \vert)$

### Key Distinctions (Frequently Tested)
- [x] **VI vs. PI**: VI does *not* maintain an explicit policy between iterations; PI alternates between full policy evaluation and policy improvement.
- [x] **Max vs. Expectation**: VI uses $\max_a$ (greedy); PE uses $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ (expectation under fixed policy).
- [x] **Bellman Optimality vs. Bellman Expectation**: $V^*$ satisfies the optimality equation; $V^\pi$ satisfies the expectation equation for a fixed $\pi$.

### Common Student Mistakes (Avoid These!)
- [x] Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$ in the update.
- [x] Confusing the $\max$ operator with summation.
- [x] Stating that "Value Iteration always converges in few iterations" — this is wrong; convergence depends on $\gamma$ and the reward structure.
- [x] Claiming the algorithm works for continuous state spaces without approximation.
- [x] Omitting the policy extraction step at the end of the algorithm.

### Practical Implementation Tips
- [x] Use **in-place updates** (asynchronous VI) to speed up convergence.
- [x] Use the stopping criterion $\max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \epsilon$ with $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ or $10^{-6}$ for engineering accuracy.
- [x] For very large state spaces, use **prioritized sweeping** or **real-time dynamic programming (RTDP)**.
- [x] Value Iteration assumes a **known transition model** $\mathcal{P}$ and reward function $\mathcal{R}$ — for unknown models, use model-free RL methods (Q-learning, SARSA).

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Value Iteration**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Methods: Monte Carlo Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Methods: Monte Carlo Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Monte Carlo Methods: Monte Carlo Prediction

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Reinforcement Learning (RL), **Monte Carlo (MC) Methods** are a class of learning algorithms that estimate the value of states (or state-action pairs) and discover optimal policies purely from **experience** — sequences of states, actions, and rewards obtained by interacting with an environment. Unlike Dynamic Programming (DP), MC methods do **not** require a complete model of the environment's transition dynamics or reward function.

**Monte Carlo Prediction** specifically refers to the task of computing the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ for a given policy $\pi$, by averaging the empirical returns observed from multiple episodes of interaction following that policy.

$$V_\pi(s) \doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s\right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\middle|\, S_t = s\right]$$

The core operational principle is the **Law of Large Numbers**: as the number of episodes (sample trajectories) $N(s) \to \infty$, the sample average of observed returns converges to the true expected return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Monte Carlo methods are characterized by three defining properties:
> 1. **Model-Free**: No prior knowledge of $P(s' \vert s, a)$ or $R(s, a, s')$ is required.
> 2. **Episodic Tasks Only**: Requires complete episodes to compute the return $G_t$.
> 3. **Sample-Based Learning**: Learns from sampled returns, not from a known model.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a robot waiter in a busy restaurant, and your manager wants to know: *"How much tip money does a customer typically leave if you serve Table 7?"* 

You don't have a formula. You can't do math. All you can do is **observe** what actually happens. So for the next 100 days, you record the tip from every customer at Table 7. On Day 1, you get ₹150. Day 2, ₹80. Day 3, ₹220. By Day 100, you average all 100 tips and conclude: *"On average, Table 7 leaves about ₹120."*

**That average IS your Monte Carlo estimate.** The 100 days of observation are your 100 episodes. The running average is the MC Prediction algorithm.

- Each completed day (episode) provides one **return sample**.
- Averaging many samples approximates the true expectation.
- The more days you observe, the closer you get to the truth (convergence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Idea**: Monte Carlo Prediction turns *unknown expectations* into *known averages* by leveraging repeated real-world (or simulated) experience. It is **learning from samples**, not from equations.

---

## 1.3 Why Monte Carlo Prediction Matters in Robotics

For a KTU robotics student, MC prediction is critical because:

- **Robot state spaces are often huge or continuous** — exact DP becomes infeasible.
- **Robot dynamics are rarely perfectly known** — model-free methods are essential.
- **Real robot rollouts** provide natural episode data (e.g., one full pick-and-place trial).
- MC prediction serves as the **value-estimation backbone** for downstream policy improvement (covered in subsequent topics: MC Control, SARSA, Q-Learning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Monte Carlo Prediction

MC prediction executes through the following structured logic:

### Step 1: Episode Generation
- Starting from a state $s$, the agent follows policy $\pi$ until a terminal state is reached.
- An episode is the trajectory:
$$\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T, S_T)$$
where $S_T$ is terminal.

### Step 2: Return Computation
For each visited state $S_t$ at time $t$, the return $G_t$ is computed backwards from the terminal reward:
$$G_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T$$

### Step 3: Sample Averaging
The value estimate $V_\pi(s)$ is updated as the average of all returns observed after visiting $s$:
$$V_\pi(s) \approx \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G^{(i)}(s)$$

### Step 4: Convergence Check (Implicit)
By the **Law of Large Numbers**:
$$\lim_{N(s) \to \infty} \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G^{(i)}(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] = V_\pi(s)$$

---

## 2.2 Two Variants: First-Visit vs Every-Visit MC

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes these two MC prediction variants. Understanding the distinction is a frequently tested high-yield concept.

### **First-Visit Monte Carlo (FVMC)**
- For each episode, identify the **first time step** $t$ at which state $s$ is visited.
- Average the returns $G_t$ only from these first visits.
- **Unbiased estimator** with variance $O(1/N)$.

### **Every-Visit Monte Carlo (EVMC)**
- For each episode, use **every occurrence** of state $s$ to contribute a return sample.
- **Biased** (initially) but converges to the same value.
- Slightly lower mean-squared error in practice for many problems.

### **The Bias-Variance Tradeoff**

| Aspect | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|---|---|---|
| **Bias** | Zero (unbiased) | Non-zero initially |
| **Variance** | Higher | Lower (more samples per episode) |
| **Convergence** | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ |
| **Sample Efficiency** | Uses fewer samples | Uses more samples per episode |
| **KTU Preference** | Standard textbook choice | Alternative valid method |

---

## 2.3 Incremental Implementation (Constant Memory)

For robotics deployment, storing every episode's return is memory-expensive. The **incremental MC update** solves this using a running mean:

$$N(S_t) \leftarrow N(S_t) + 1$$
$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \frac{1}{N(S_t)}\bigl(G_t - V(S_t)\bigr)$$

This can be generalized using a **constant step-size $\alpha$** for non-stationary problems:
$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \bigl(G_t - V(S_t)\bigr)$$

where $0 < \alpha \leq 1$. When $\alpha = 1/N(S_t)$, we recover the standard sample-average update.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for KTU exam questions on Monte Carlo Prediction. Memorize these.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State-Value Definition | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \vert S_t = s]$ | Definition of value to estimate |
| 2 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative future reward |
| 3 | Finite-Horizon Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Episodic return (used in MC) |
| 4 | FVMC Estimate | $V(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G^{(i)}(s)$ | First-visit value estimate |
| 5 | EVMC Estimate | $V(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G^{(i)}_j(s)$ | Every-visit value estimate |
| 6 | Incremental Update | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t - V(S_t)]$ | Online constant-memory update |
| 7 | Sample-Avg Update | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \frac{1}{N(S_t)}[G_t - V(S_t)]$ | When $\alpha = 1/N$ |
| 8 | Convergence Guarantee | $\lim_{N \to \infty} V(s) \to V_\pi(s)$ w.p. 1 | Law of Large Numbers |
| 9 | Episode Definition | $\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, \dots, S_T)$ | MC requires terminal state |
| 10 | Convergence Rate | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ | MSE rate for both variants |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In KTU-aligned robotics research and industry:

- **Autonomous Navigation**: MC prediction estimates how promising a grid cell is for a mobile robot before committing to a path.
- **Manipulation Tasks**: Evaluates grasp states in a robotic arm by averaging outcomes over many simulated pick attempts.
- **Drone Trajectory Planning**: Predicts the value of altitude/heading states from real flight episodes.
- **Game AI**: Classic application — Blackjack (the canonical Sutton & Barto example) and Backgammon.
- **Recommendation Systems**: MC evaluation of user states through logged interaction traces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Expectation to Sample Average

The MC prediction algorithm is built on one of the most elegant derivations in statistical learning. Below is the complete, exhaustive mathematical justification.

### Starting Point: The True Value Function

By definition, the value of a state $s$ under policy $\pi$ is the expected return:

$$
V_\pi(s) \doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s\right]
$$

This expectation is taken over the distribution of returns $G_t$ induced by stochastic policy $\pi$ and stochastic environment transitions.

### Step 1: Expand the Expectation

Using the formal definition of an expectation over a discrete random variable:

$$
\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s\right] = \sum_{g} g \cdot \Pr(G_t = g \mid S_t = s)
$$

Since $\Pr(G_t = g \mid S_t = s)$ is **unknown** (we don't have a model), we cannot compute this sum directly.

### Step 2: Apply the Law of Large Numbers

Let $G^{(1)}, G^{(2)}, \dots, G^{(n)}$ be $n$ **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** samples drawn from $\Pr(G_t = g \mid S_t = s)$. By the **Weak Law of Large Numbers**:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \Pr\!\left(\left\vert \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)} - \mathbb{E}[G] \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) = 0 \quad \text{for any } \epsilon > 0
$$

Thus, the sample average converges **in probability** to the true expectation:

$$
V_\pi(s) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)}
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Estimator

For finite $n$, the MC prediction estimator is:

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)}
$$

This is an **unbiased estimator**: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{V}_\pi(s)] = V_\pi(s)$.

### Step 4: Quantify Convergence Rate

The mean-squared error (MSE) of the estimator decreases as $O(1/n)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(\hat{V}_\pi(s) - V_\pi(s)\bigr)^2\right] = \frac{\mathrm{Var}(G_t \mid S_t = s)}{n}
$$

Hence the standard error is $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ — to halve the error, we need **4×** as many episodes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Incremental Update from Sample Average

This derivation shows how the running-mean update emerges naturally.

### Starting Point: The Sample Average

$$
V_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)}
$$

### Step 1: Separate the Last Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_n &= \frac{1}{n}\!\left(G^{(n)} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} G^{(i)}\right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{n} G^{(n)} + \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} G^{(i)} \\
    &= \frac{1}{n} G^{(n)} + \frac{n-1}{n} V_{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Rearrange Using $V_{n-1} = V_{n-1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_n &= V_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n} G^{(n)} - \frac{1}{n} V_{n-1} \\
    &= V_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n}\bigl(G^{(n)} - V_{n-1}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the elegant incremental form:

$$
\boxed{V_n = V_{n-1} + \alpha_n \bigl(G^{(n)} - V_{n-1}\bigr)}
$$

where $\alpha_n = 1/n$ is the step-size.

### Step 3: Extend to Constant Step-Size

Substituting $\alpha_n = \alpha$ (constant) yields the **exponentially weighted average**:

$$
V_n = (1 - \alpha)^n V_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i} G^{(i)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams**: The constant step-size version gives **more weight to recent returns**, which is crucial for non-stationary robotic environments (e.g., changing terrain, battery degradation).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: MC Prediction on a 3-State Chain

Consider a 3-state Markov Reward Process (no actions) with states $\{A, B, C\}$ where $C$ is terminal. Episodes under a fixed stochastic policy yield the following 5 sample episodes (with $\gamma = 1$):

| Episode | Trajectory (states with rewards) | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, +0, B, +0, C, +1 | $G_A = 1, \; G_B = 1$ |
| 2 | B, +1, C, +0 | $G_B = 0$ |
| 3 | A, +0, B, +0, C, +2 | $G_A = 2, \; G_B = 2$ |
| 4 | A, +1, B, +0, C, +0 | $G_A = 0, \; G_B = 0$ |
| 5 | B, +1, C, +1 | $G_B = 1$ |

### First-Visit MC Estimates

**State A** (visited first in episodes 1, 3, 4):
$$
\hat{V}(A) = \frac{G^{(1)} + G^{(3)} + G^{(4)}}{3} = \frac{1 + 2 + 0}{3} = 1.0
$$

**State B** (visited first in episodes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5):
$$
\hat{V}(B) = \frac{1 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 1}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
$$

### Every-Visit MC Estimates

**State B** is visited once per episode here, so first-visit = every-visit in this case. But for **State A**, if we hypothetically revisited it within an episode, those additional returns would be included.

---

## 3.4 Convergence Proof Outline (Sketch for Advanced Marks)

For **14-mark KTU questions**, the convergence of First-Visit MC can be summarized:

1. **Assumption**: Returns $G_t$ from state $s$ are i.i.d. with finite variance $\sigma^2 < \infty$.
2. **Apply WLLN**: $\bar{G}_n \xrightarrow{P} \mathbb{E}[G_t] = V_\pi(s)$.
3. **Strong Law**: $\bar{G}_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} V_\pi(s)$ with probability 1.
4. **MSE bound**: $\mathbb{E}[(\bar{G}_n - V_\pi(s))^2] = \sigma^2 / n \to 0$.
5. **Conclusion**: $\hat{V}_n(s) \to V_\pi(s)$ almost surely as $n \to \infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU valuation, writing the WLLN statement with the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ style argument earns full theoretical credit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Monte Carlo Prediction Loop

The diagram below illustrates the complete MC Prediction algorithm flow, from episode generation through value update. Every node uses a safe alphanumeric identifier prefixed with letters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START MC Prediction Algorithm] --> B0[Initialize V of s arbitrarily for all s]
    B0 --> C0[Initialize Returns list s empty for all s]
    C0 --> D0{Generate episode using policy pi}
    D0 --> E0[Episode tau equals S0 A0 R1 ... ST]
    E0 --> F0[For each step t from T-1 down to 0]
    F0 --> G0[Compute return Gt = R of t+1 + gamma R of t+2 + ...]
    G0 --> H0{First-Visit MC Check}
    H0 --> I0[Is s the FIRST visit in this episode]
    I0 -->|Yes| J0[Append Gt to Returns list s]
    I0 -->|No| K0[Skip for First Visit MC]
    J0 --> L0[Update V of s equals average of Returns list s]
    K0 --> F0
    L0 --> F0
    F0 --> M0{All steps processed}
    M0 -->|Yes| N0[Increment episode counter]
    N0 --> O0{Has convergence been reached}
    O0 -->|No| D0
    O0 -->|Yes| P0[OUTPUT Estimated V of pi]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: MC Prediction in a Robotic System

The following diagram maps how MC Prediction integrates into a real robotic RL pipeline, treating each functional module as a labeled block with safe identifiers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[ROBOT ENVIRONMENT] --> E2[State Observation Sensor]
    E2 --> E3[Policy Module pi]
    E3 --> E4[Action Selection A of t]
    E4 --> E5[Motor Controller Hardware Layer]
    E5 --> E6[Execute Action on Physical Robot]
    E6 --> E7[Reward Signal R of t+1]
    E7 --> E8[Episode Logger and Buffer]
    E8 --> E9[Episode Complete Check]
    E9 -->|Episode In Progress| E1
    E9 -->|Episode Complete| E10[Return Calculator Module]
    E10 --> E11[First-Visit Detection Module]
    E11 --> E12[Incremental Value Updater V of s]
    E12 --> E13[Value Function Estimate V of pi]
    E13 --> E14{Convergence Criterion Satisfied}
    E14 -->|No| E1
    E14 -->|Yes| E15[OUTPUT Final V of pi for Downstream Control]
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: First-Visit vs Every-Visit MC Sampling

This diagram clarifies the sampling difference for a single episode containing repeated state visits.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Episode Trajectory S1 S2 S3 S1 S4 S5] --> Y1{First-Visit MC Logic}
    X1 --> Z1{Every-Visit MC Logic}
    Y1 --> Y2[Identify only the first occurrence of S1]
    Y2 --> Y3[Sample ONE return from S1 in this episode]
    Z1 --> Z2[Identify all occurrences of S1 in this episode]
    Z2 --> Z3[Sample TWO returns from S1 in this episode]
    Y3 --> Y4[Contribute 1 sample to V of S1]
    Z3 --> Z4[Contribute 2 samples to V of S1]
    Y4 --> OUT1[Smaller sample count per episode]
    Z4 --> OUT2[Larger sample count per episode]
    OUT1 --> RES1[Higher variance but unbiased]
    OUT2 --> RES2[Lower variance but biased initially]
```

---

## 4.4 Convergence Behavior Schematic

This block diagram maps the iterative refinement of $V(s)$ as the number of episodes increases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Episode 1: Initial V of s equals 0] --> P2[Episode 2: V of s updated using G1]
    P2 --> P3[Episode 3: V of s updated using G2]
    P3 --> P4[Episode N: V of s approximately equals true V of pi]
    P4 --> P5{Error Analysis}
    P5 --> P6[MSE decreases as 1 over N]
    P5 --> P7[Variance decreases as 1 over N]
    P5 --> P8[Bias zero for FVMC after first visit]
    P6 --> P9[Convergence Guarantee by Law of Large Numbers]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[OUTPUT V of s converges to V of pi]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Monte Carlo Prediction. Why is it called a model-free method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Monte Carlo Prediction is a reinforcement learning technique used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ of a given policy $\pi$ by averaging the empirical returns observed from a collection of complete episodes generated by following that policy.

It is called **model-free** because it does not require prior knowledge of the environment's transition probability function $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or the reward function $R(s, a, s')$. Instead, it learns purely from sampled experience (state-action-reward trajectories). **(2 marks)** The value estimate converges to the true expected return via the Law of Large Numbers. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between First-Visit and Every-Visit Monte Carlo methods in one line each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **First-Visit MC (FVMC)**: Averages the returns $G_t$ corresponding to the **first time** state $s$ is visited within each episode. *(1 mark)*
- **Every-Visit MC (EVMC)**: Averages the returns $G_t$ corresponding to **every occurrence** of state $s$ within each episode. *(1 mark)*
- **Key Difference**: FVMC produces an **unbiased estimator** with higher variance, whereas EVMC is **biased initially** but uses more samples per episode, often giving lower mean-squared error. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Consider a robot operating in a 4-state grid world with states $\{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$ where $S_4$ is terminal. The discount factor is $\gamma = 0.9$. The following 4 episodes were observed under policy $\pi$:**

| Episode | Trajectory (State, Reward) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $S_1, +1, S_2, +0, S_3, +0, S_4, +5$ |
| 2 | $S_2, +1, S_3, +0, S_4, +2$ |
| 3 | $S_1, +0, S_3, +1, S_4, +3$ |
| 4 | $S_1, +2, S_2, +0, S_4, +1$ |

#### **(a)** Compute the First-Visit MC estimate of $V_\pi(S_1)$, $V_\pi(S_2)$, and $V_\pi(S_3)$. (7 marks)

#### **(b)** Using an incremental MC update with learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$, show the sequence of updates to $V(S_1)$ episode-by-episode, starting from $V(S_1) = 0$. Compare with the sample-average result. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute returns for each first-visit occurrence.**

For Episode 1, trajectory is $(S_1, +1, S_2, +0, S_3, +0, S_4, +5)$.
- First visit to $S_1$ at $t=0$: 
$$
G_0^{(1)} = 1 + (0.9)(0) + (0.9)^2 (0) + (0.9)^3 (5) = 1 + 3.645 = 4.645
$$
- First visit to $S_2$ at $t=1$: 
$$
G_1^{(1)} = 0 + (0.9)(0) + (0.9)^2 (5) = 4.05
$$
- First visit to $S_3$ at $t=2$: 
$$
G_2^{(1)} = 0 + (0.9)(5) = 4.5
$$

For Episode 2, trajectory is $(S_2, +1, S_3, +0, S_4, +2)$.
- First visit to $S_2$ at $t=0$: 
$$
G_0^{(2)} = 1 + (0.9)(0) + (0.9)^2 (2) = 1 + 1.62 = 2.62
$$
- First visit to $S_3$ at $t=1$: 
$$
G_1^{(2)} = 0 + (0.9)(2) = 1.8
$$

For Episode 3, trajectory is $(S_1, +0, S_3, +1, S_4, +3)$.
- First visit to $S_1$ at $t=0$: 
$$
G_0^{(3)} = 0 + (0.9)(1) + (0.9)^2 (3) = 0 + 0.9 + 2.43 = 3.33
$$
- First visit to $S_3$ at $t=1$: 
$$
G_1^{(3)} = 1 + (0.9)(3) = 3.7
$$

For Episode 4, trajectory is $(S_1, +2, S_2, +0, S_4, +1)$.
- First visit to $S_1$ at $t=0$: 
$$
G_0^{(4)} = 2 + (0.9)(0) + (0.9)^2 (1) = 2 + 0.81 = 2.81
$$
- First visit to $S_2$ at $t=1$: 
$$
G_1^{(4)} = 0 + (0.9)(1) = 0.9
$$

**Step 2: Compute sample averages.**

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(S_1) = \frac{4.645 + 3.33 + 2.81}{3} = \frac{10.785}{3} \approx 3.595
$$

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(S_2) = \frac{4.05 + 2.62 + 0.9}{3} = \frac{7.57}{3} \approx 2.523
$$

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(S_3) = \frac{4.5 + 1.8 + 3.7}{3} = \frac{10.0}{3} \approx 3.333
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The incremental update formula is:
$$
V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\bigl(G_t - V(S_t)\bigr)
$$

With $V(S_1)$ starts at $0$ and $\alpha = 0.5$:

**After Episode 1** (using $G = 4.645$):
$$
V(S_1) = 0 + 0.5(4.645 - 0) = 2.3225
$$

**After Episode 3** (next first-visit, $G = 3.33$):
$$
V(S_1) = 2.3225 + 0.5(3.33 - 2.3225) = 2.3225 + 0.50375 = 2.82625
$$

**After Episode 4** (next first-visit, $G = 2.81$):
$$
V(S_1) = 2.82625 + 0.5(2.81 - 2.82625) = 2.82625 - 0.008125 = 2.818125
$$

**Comparison with sample average**:
- Incremental result: $V(S_1) \approx 2.818$
- Sample-average result: $V(S_1) \approx 3.595$
- The discrepancy arises because $\alpha = 0.5$ is **much larger** than $1/N$ for $N=3$. Larger $\alpha$ gives more weight to recent samples and **less to history**. If we had used $\alpha = 1/N$, both methods would converge to the same answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** Explain the Law of Large Numbers and show how it guarantees convergence of Monte Carlo Prediction. Derive the MSE bound. (7 marks)

#### **(b)** A robotic arm performs a pick-and-place task. The state $s$ represents the gripper's pre-grasp pose. Across 6 trials, the returns obtained are: $\{4.2, 5.1, 3.8, 5.5, 4.7, 5.0\}$. Compute the MC prediction estimate $V(s)$. After 2 more trials returning $4.9$ and $5.3$, what is the updated estimate? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**The Law of Large Numbers (LLN):**
Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d. random variables with finite expectation $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. Then:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \Pr\!\left(\left\vert \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i - \mu \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) = 0 \quad \text{(Weak LLN)}
$$

**Application to MC Prediction:**
The returns $G^{(i)}$ observed after visiting state $s$ are i.i.d. samples from the conditional distribution $\Pr(G_t = g \mid S_t = s)$ with mean $V_\pi(s)$ and bounded variance. Therefore:

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)} \xrightarrow{P} V_\pi(s) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty
$$

**MSE Bound Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{MSE}\bigl(\hat{V}_\pi(s)\bigr) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(\hat{V}_\pi(s) - V_\pi(s)\bigr)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)} - V_\pi(s)\right)^2\right] \\
&= \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{Var}(G^{(i)}) \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the MSE decreases as $O(1/n)$, and the standard error as $O(1/\sqrt{n})$. **(Full derivation, all steps shown.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Initial estimate after 6 trials:**

$$
\hat{V}_6(s) = \frac{4.2 + 5.1 + 3.8 + 5.5 + 4.7 + 5.0}{6} = \frac{28.3}{6} \approx 4.717
$$

**Updated estimate after 8 trials (adding 4.9 and 5.3):**

$$
\hat{V}_8(s) = \frac{28.3 + 4.9 + 5.3}{8} = \frac{38.5}{8} = 4.8125
$$

**Alternative using incremental update:**

After trial 7 (return = 4.9):
$$
V_7(s) = \frac{28.3 + 4.9}{7} = \frac{33.2}{7} \approx 4.743
$$

After trial 8 (return = 5.3):
$$
V_8(s) = \frac{33.2 + 5.3}{8} = \frac{38.5}{8} = 4.8125
$$

Both methods yield the same answer, as expected for sample-average updates.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Discount factor misuse**: Many students forget to apply $\gamma^k$ when computing returns across time steps. **Always** write the explicit discounted sum before substituting numbers. Losing 1–2 marks here.
> 
> 2. **First-visit vs every-visit confusion**: When a state appears multiple times in one episode, only the **first occurrence** counts for FVMC. Mixing the two methods in one calculation leads to wrong estimates and lost marks.
> 
> 3. **Incremental update step-size**: Using $\alpha = 1/N$ gives the sample average; using constant $\alpha$ does **not**. Examiners check this distinction. State clearly which form you are using.
> 
> 4. **Terminal state handling**: The terminal state's value is **zero** ($V(S_T) = 0$), and rewards from terminal transitions still count in the return. Omitting the terminal reward loses marks.
> 
> 5. **i.i.d. assumption**: MC convergence requires returns from the same state to be approximately i.i.d. If episodes are correlated (e.g., persistent noise), the LLN does not strictly apply. Examiners appreciate this nuance for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Monte Carlo Prediction** estimates $V_\pi(s)$ by **averaging empirical returns** from complete episodes. No model required.
- **Episodic only**: MC methods cannot bootstrap; every estimate requires waiting for a terminal state.
- **First-Visit MC**: Unbiased, averages only the first occurrence of $s$ per episode. This is the standard textbook choice.
- **Every-Visit MC**: Uses every occurrence of $s$; biased initially but uses more samples per episode.
- **Convergence**: Guaranteed by the **Law of Large Numbers** as $N(s) \to \infty$; MSE decays as $O(1/N)$.
- **Incremental update**: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t - V(S_t)]$ enables constant-memory online learning.
- **Constant step-size $\alpha$**: Gives recent returns more weight; essential for **non-stationary** robotic environments.
- **Discount factor $\gamma$**: Applied as $\gamma^k$ to the $k$-th future reward when computing the return $G_t$.
- **Robot applications**: Navigation, manipulation, drone planning, game AI — anywhere episodes are natural and models are unknown.
- **Bias-variance tradeoff**: FVMC has zero bias but higher variance; EVMC trades bias for lower variance.
- **No bootstrapping**: Unlike DP, MC does not use one state's estimate to update another — it uses the actual sampled return.
- **i.i.d. assumption**: Returns for the same state must be approximately independent across episodes for LLN to apply.
- **Sample efficiency**: MC can be sample-inefficient because each return requires a full episode to be generated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Action Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Estimation of Action Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monte Carlo (MC) Estimation of Action Values** is a model-free, learning-from-experience methodology in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that directly estimates the *expected return* (the action-value function, $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$) for every state-action pair $(s, a)$ under a given policy $\pi$ by averaging the actual returns observed in sampled episodes.

Mathematically, the *true* action-value function under a target policy $\pi$ is defined as the expected cumulative discounted (or undiscounted) reward obtained when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$:
