$$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \,\bigm|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$

The Monte Carlo estimate $\hat{Q}_{\pi}(s, a)$ is computed as the **empirical sample mean** of the returns $G_t^{(1)}, G_t^{(2)}, \dots, G_t^{(N)}$ collected from $N$ episodes after the first (or every) visit to the state-action pair $(s, a)$:

$$\hat{Q}_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PERAT747):**
> MC methods require only **experience** (sample trajectories/episodes) — no prior knowledge of the environment's transition probabilities $p(s' \vert s, a)$ or reward function $r(s, a, s')$. They learn *from sampled returns alone*. This property is called **model-free estimation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **food delivery robot** in a college canteen. For every possible counter (state) and dish selection (action), the robot wants to know the *average customer satisfaction score* it will collect in the long run. The robot cannot compute a complex probability model of the canteen — instead, it just **makes deliveries, observes the feedback rating, and writes down the average rating** for each (counter, dish) combination it has tried.

That running average is the **Monte Carlo estimate of the action value** $Q(s, a)$. Over many trials, the average naturally converges to the true long-run expected satisfaction, by the **Law of Large Numbers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two flavours of MC estimation exist:**
> 1. **First-Visit MC**: Average returns only from the *first* time $(s, a)$ is visited in an episode.
> 2. **Every-Visit MC**: Average returns from *every* occurrence of $(s, a)$ in an episode.
>
> Both converge to $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$, but First-Visit MC has a simpler theoretical treatment (unbiased and variance can be analysed term-by-term).

---

## 1.3 The Core Challenge: Exploration

To estimate $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ reliably, the agent must visit **every** state-action pair multiple times. If some actions are never tried, their values remain unknown — the agent will *exploit* familiar actions and *ignore* potentially superior unexplored ones. This is the classical **exploration–exploitation dilemma**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two canonical solutions to the exploration problem (KTU 2024 syllabus):**
> - **Exploring Starts (ES):** Every episode begins with a randomly chosen state-action pair $(s_0, a_0)$, guaranteeing all pairs can be sampled. Assumes non-zero probability of starting anywhere.
> - **$\varepsilon$-soft / Soft Policies:** With probability $1-\varepsilon$ the agent acts greedily with respect to current $Q$-estimates; with probability $\varepsilon$ it picks a random action. In the limit, every action has $\geq \varepsilon/{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ probability of being selected.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ | True action value under policy $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\hat{Q}(s, a)$ | MC sample-mean estimate | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $G_t$ | Return from time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $N(s, a)$ | Visit count for pair $(s, a)$ | $\mathbb{N}$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Exploration probability | $(0, 1)$ |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Robotics

In real robotic systems, the transition dynamics $p(s' \mid s, a)$ are typically **unknown or intractable** (slippery floors, complex contact dynamics, deformable objects). MC estimation of action values lets the robot learn optimal manipulation/navigation policies **purely from trial-and-error interaction** with the physical environment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Return $G_t$ — Foundation of MC Estimation

The quantity being averaged is the *return* $G_t$ — the cumulative (possibly discounted) reward received from time $t$ onwards until the end of an episode (MC methods operate on **episodic tasks** with a terminal state).

For the undiscounted episodic case:

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \dots + R_T$$

For the discounted infinite-horizon case:

$$G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \le \gamma < 1$$

where $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** that geometrically penalises rewards received far in the future, ensuring convergence of the infinite sum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why discounting?** For robotic tasks with potentially infinite durations, discounting both **mathematically guarantees convergence** of the return and reflects the engineering reality that *immediate rewards are usually more reliable* (e.g., sensors drift, batteries drain).

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Initialise:** For every state-action pair $(s, a)$ in the environment, set counters $N(s, a) = 0$ and value estimates $\hat{Q}(s, a) = 0$. Initialise the policy $\pi$ (e.g., a random or $\varepsilon$-soft policy).
2. **Generate Episode:** Starting from a state-action pair (either an *exploring start* or via the current soft policy), execute the policy in the environment until a terminal state is reached. Record the sequence $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_T$.
3. **Compute Returns:** For each timestep $t$, compute the return $G_t$ by summing rewards from $t$ to $T$ (with optional discount $\gamma$).
4. **Update Estimates (First-Visit):** For every state-action pair $(s, a)$ that appears as the **first occurrence** in the episode, increment $N(s, a) \leftarrow N(s, a) + 1$ and update the running mean:
   
   $$\hat{Q}(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; \hat{Q}(s, a) + \frac{1}{N(s, a)}\bigl(G_t - \hat{Q}(s, a)\bigr)$$
5. **Policy Improvement:** Make $\pi$ greedy (or $\varepsilon$-soft greedy) with respect to the updated $\hat{Q}$ — i.e., for every state $s$, set:
   
   $$\pi(s) \;\in\; \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \hat{Q}(s, a)$$
6. **Convergence Criterion:** Repeat steps 2–5 until the change in $\hat{Q}$ or $\pi$ between successive episodes falls below a chosen tolerance $\delta$, or until a fixed number of episodes $N_{\text{episodes}}$ is reached.

---

## 2.3 Incremental Update Form (Equivalence with Sample Mean)

The incremental update rule is mathematically **identical** to computing the sample mean — it is just a numerically stable, memory-efficient reformulation. Expanding the recursion verifies this:

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}\bigl(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}\bigr) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equivalence is the **board-exam favourite**. Examiners often ask students to prove that the incremental formula is mathematically equivalent to the sample mean. Memorise the derivation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| True action value | $Q_{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | By definition under policy $\pi$ |
| Return (undiscounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} R_k$ | Episodic task with horizon $T$ |
| Return (discounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Requires $0 \le \gamma < 1$ |
| MC sample mean estimate | $\hat{Q}(s, a) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)}$ | Across $N$ independent returns |
| Incremental MC update | $\hat{Q}_{N} = \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1})$ | Online / single-pass version |
| Greedy policy | $\pi(s) = \arg\max_{a} \hat{Q}(s, a)$ | Breaks ties arbitrarily |
| $\varepsilon$-soft policy | $\pi(a \mid s) = 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ if $a = a^{*}$, else $\frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ | Ensures all actions have $\geq \varepsilon/\vert\mathcal{A}\vert$ probability |
| Convergence rate (LLN) | $\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N \to \infty} \hat{Q}(s, a) = Q_{\pi}(s, a)\right) = 1$ | Almost-sure convergence |
| Variance bound (Hoeffding) | $\Pr\!\left(\vert \hat{Q} - Q_{\pi} \vert > \epsilon\right) \le 2 \exp\!\left(-2N\epsilon^{2}/(b - a)^{2}\right)$ | Returns bounded in $[a, b]$ |
| Exploration prob. floor | $\pi(a \mid s) \geq \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ for all $a$ | Glie (greedy in the limit with infinite exploration) property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility of this framework:**
> - **Robotic manipulation** — learning grasp success probabilities without a contact-dynamics model.
> - **Drone navigation** — estimating long-horizon energy cost of an aerial manoeuvre from real flights.
> - **Autonomous driving** — learning lane-change utility from observed human-driver episodes.
> - **Process control** — approximating optimal setpoint policies in unknown chemical reactors.

---

## 2.5 Why MC Estimation of Action Values Works — The "Why"

By the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)**, as the number of independent samples $N \to \infty$, the empirical mean $\hat{Q}(s, a)$ converges *almost surely* to the true expectation $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$. The key requirement is that the samples be **i.i.d.**, which they are not strictly — they are correlated within an episode. However, by averaging over **many independent episodes** (each starting from an exploring start or a soft-policy trajectory), the correlation effect vanishes asymptotically, and the convergence is preserved.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Incremental Update ≡ Sample Mean (Full Proof)

**Goal:** Show that the iterative update rule

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}\bigl(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}\bigr)$$

produces the same numerical value as the closed-form sample mean

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i.$$

**Step 1 — Base case (N = 1):** The first sample alone is the mean.

$$\hat{Q}_{1} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{0} + \frac{1}{1}\bigl(G_1 - \hat{Q}_{0}\bigr) \;=\; G_1$$

**Step 2 — Inductive hypothesis:** Assume that after processing $N-1$ samples the running estimate equals the sample mean of those samples:

$$\hat{Q}_{N-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply the update rule for the $N$-th sample:**

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}\bigl(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the inductive hypothesis:**

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{1}{N}\!\left(G_N - \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i\right)$$

**Step 5 — Distribute the $1/N$ factor and group terms:**

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i - \frac{1}{N(N-1)}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{G_N}{N}$$

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{N-1} - \frac{1}{N(N-1)}\right)\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{G_N}{N}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the coefficient:**

$$\frac{1}{N-1} - \frac{1}{N(N-1)} \;=\; \frac{N}{N(N-1)} - \frac{1}{N(N-1)} \;=\; \frac{N-1}{N(N-1)} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{G_N}{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The proof is by strong induction and is the *single most important derivation* a KTU examiner can ask on this topic. Practice writing it from memory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bias–Variance Decomposition of the MC Estimator

Let $\mu \triangleq Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ denote the true mean return and $\sigma^{2}$ its variance. The MC estimator's **mean-squared error (MSE)** can be decomposed as:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{Q} - \mu)^{2}\right] \;=\; \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{Q}] - \mu\bigr)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{Q} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{Q}])^{2}\right]}_{\text{Variance}}$$

For First-Visit MC, each visit to $(s, a)$ is an independent sample, so:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{Q}] \;=\; \mu \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Bias} = 0$$

$$\mathrm{Var}(\hat{Q}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{MSE} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$

Thus, the **standard error** of the MC estimate decreases as $1/\sqrt{N}$:

$$\text{SE}(\hat{Q}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $1/\sqrt{N}$ decay rate is fundamental: to halve the standard error, one must collect **four times** as many episodes. This is the operational cost of MC estimation and a common board-exam question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Variants

| Variant | Visit Rule | Sample Independence | Bias | Variance | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Visit MC** | First occurrence per episode | ✅ Independent | Unbiased | Lower | Theoretical analysis, board proofs |
| **Every-Visit MC** | Every occurrence per episode | ❌ Correlated within episode | Unbiased* | Slightly higher | Empirical use, often converges faster in practice |
| **Incremental MC** | First- or every-visit | Depends on rule | Unbiased | Identical to non-incremental | Online / streaming robotic data |
| **Weighted MC (off-policy)** | Importance-sampled | Weighted | Unbiased (if IS ratios defined) | High (variance blow-up) | Off-policy learning from demonstrations |

\* Every-Visit MC is unbiased only under certain technical conditions (e.g., bounded returns).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A robotic arm performs a pick-and-place task. The state-action pair $(s_{1}, a_{2})$ is visited 4 times across 4 episodes with the following observed returns:

$$G^{(1)} = 10, \quad G^{(2)} = 14, \quad G^{(3)} = 6, \quad G^{(4)} = 18$$

**Compute the MC action-value estimate $\hat{Q}(s_1, a_2)$ and its standard error.**

**Step 1 — Sample mean:**

$$\hat{Q} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\bigl(10 + 14 + 6 + 18\bigr) \;=\; \frac{48}{4} \;=\; 12$$

**Step 2 — Sample variance:**

$$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(G^{(i)} - \hat{Q}\bigr)^{2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\bigl[(10-12)^{2} + (14-12)^{2} + (6-12)^{2} + (18-12)^{2}\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\bigl[4 + 4 + 36 + 36\bigr] \;=\; \frac{80}{3} \;\approx\; 26.67$$

**Step 3 — Standard error:**

$$\text{SE}(\hat{Q}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{26.67}}{\sqrt{4}} \;\approx\; \frac{5.164}{2} \;\approx\; 2.58$$

**Conclusion:** $\hat{Q}(s_1, a_2) = 12 \pm 2.58$ (one standard error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** If the task designer needs the estimate to be accurate within $\pm 1$ unit with 95% confidence, the required sample size is $N = (1.96 \cdot \sigma / 1)^{2} \approx 102$ episodes — a direct consequence of the $1/\sqrt{N}$ decay.

---

## 3.5 Convergent Sequence Property of the Incremental Update

Observe that the update is a fixed-point iteration. Rearranging:

$$\hat{Q}_{N} - \hat{Q}_{N-1} \;=\; \frac{G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}}{N} \;\to\; 0 \quad \text{as } N \to \infty$$

This shows that the change in the estimate vanishes asymptotically — a **necessary condition** for convergence. Combined with the LLN, the sequence $\{\hat{Q}_{N}\}_{N=1}^{\infty}$ converges almost surely to $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Monte Carlo Action-Value Estimation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Episode Generation]) --> initQ[Initialize Q hat to zero for all state-action pairs]
    initQ --> initN[Initialize visit count N s a to zero]
    initN --> initPi[Initialize policy pi: random or epsilon soft]
    initPi --> choice{Exploring Start or Soft Policy?}
    choice -->|Exploring Start| es[Randomly sample initial state s0 and action a0]
    choice -->|Soft Policy| sp[Sample s0 from env, choose a0 from epsilon soft pi]
    es --> step
    sp --> step
    step[/Step through environment: take action, observe reward R, next state/]
    step --> termCheck{Terminal state reached?}
    termCheck -->|No| step
    termCheck -->|Yes| record[Record full episode trajectory]
    record --> compG[For each timestep t, compute return G t using rewards t+1 to T]
    compG --> fvCheck{First visit to state-action pair s a?}
    fvCheck -->|Yes| updN[Increment N s a by 1]
    updN --> updQ[Update Q hat s a using incremental formula]
    updQ --> nextSA
    fvCheck -->|No| nextSA[Move to next state-action pair in episode]
    nextSA --> morePairs{More pairs in this episode?}
    morePairs -->|Yes| fvCheck
    morePairs -->|No| impPi[Policy improvement: set pi s as greedy wrt Q hat]
    impPi --> convCheck{Convergence criterion met?}
    convCheck -->|No| startA
    convCheck -->|Yes| outA([Output: optimal Q hat and greedy policy pi star])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the MC Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph envBlock[Environment Module]
        envState[Current State s t]
        envTrans[Transition Dynamics p s prime, r, given s t, a t]
        envOut[Next State s t+1 and Reward R t+1]
    end

    subgraph agentBlock[Agent Module]
        policyUnit[Policy Module: epsilon soft or greedy]
        actionUnit[Action Selector]
        epBuffer[Episode Buffer: stores trajectory]
        returnCalc[Return Calculator: sums rewards]
        mcUpdate[MC Update Engine: incremental mean]
        qTable[(Q hat Table: s x A matrix)]
        visitTab[(Visit Counter N s a)]
    end

    envState --> actionUnit
    actionUnit --> envTrans
    envTrans --> envOut
    envOut --> epBuffer
    envOut --> policyUnit
    policyUnit --> actionUnit
    epBuffer --> returnCalc
    returnCalc --> mcUpdate
    qTable --> mcUpdate
    visitTab --> mcUpdate
    mcUpdate --> qTable
    mcUpdate --> visitTab
    mcUpdate --> policyUnit
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Exploration vs Exploitation Regime

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Explore : With probability epsilon
    Initialize --> Exploit : With probability 1 minus epsilon
    Explore --> ObserveReward : Random action sampled
    Exploit --> ObserveReward : a star equals arg max a Q hat s a
    ObserveReward --> UpdateEstimate : Incremental MC update of Q hat
    UpdateEstimate --> CheckConvergence
    CheckConvergence --> Explore : Not converged
    CheckConvergence --> Exploit : Converged
    CheckConvergence --> [*] : Policy stable
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first flowchart traces the *episodic* MC algorithm from episode generation through return computation, value update, and policy improvement. The block diagram shows the *modular* architecture of the estimator (suitable for ROS-based robotic implementations). The state diagram illustrates the perpetual dance between **exploration** and **exploitation** that MC methods must manage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic implementation note:** In a real ROS 2 stack, the *Episode Buffer* maps to a `std::vector<Transition>` stored in a node's memory, the *Return Calculator* runs backward through the episode to compute $G_t$ in $O(T)$, and the *MC Update Engine* writes to a shared `Q-hat` lookup table (often a 2D NumPy array or an Eigen matrix).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Define the action-value function $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ in a Markov Decision Process and explain how a Monte Carlo method estimates it from sampled episodes.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**
The action-value function $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ under a policy $\pi$ is defined as the expected return $G_t$ when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and following $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \,\bigm|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$

Monte Carlo (MC) estimation approximates this expectation by *empirical averaging*: the agent generates episodes using $\pi$, records the return $G_t$ observed after every visit to $(s, a)$, and computes the sample mean of those returns. As the number of visits $N(s, a) \to \infty$, by the Law of Large Numbers, the sample mean converges to the true expectation. MC estimation is **model-free** — it requires no knowledge of transition probabilities or reward functions. Two variants exist: **First-Visit MC** (average over the *first* occurrence per episode, giving unbiased estimates) and **Every-Visit MC** (average over *all* occurrences, slightly lower variance in practice but correlated samples).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Law of Large Numbers and explain its role in justifying the convergence of the Monte Carlo action-value estimator.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**
The (Strong) Law of Large Numbers (LLN) states that for a sequence of i.i.d. random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots$ with finite mean $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_i]$:

$$\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i = \mu\right) \;=\; 1$$

In the MC action-value estimator, each observed return $G_t^{(i)}$ after visiting $(s, a)$ in episode $i$ is treated as a sample of the random variable $G \mid S = s, A = a$. Averaging across $N$ episodes produces $\hat{Q}(s, a) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)}$. By the LLN, this empirical mean converges *almost surely* to the true expectation $Q_{\pi}(s, a) = \mu$ as $N \to \infty$. The LLN thus provides the theoretical foundation that guarantees MC estimates become exact in the infinite-sample limit, justifying the entire MC estimation framework.

---

## Part B — Full-Question Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Define the action-value function $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ and derive the incremental Monte Carlo update rule. Show mathematically that the incremental update is equivalent to the sample-mean estimator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot arm operates in a grid-world pick-and-place task. The state-action pair $(s_{3}, a_{1})$ is visited across 6 episodes, yielding returns: $G = \{8, 12, 7, 15, 9, 11\}$. Compute (i) the MC estimate $\hat{Q}(s_3, a_1)$, (ii) the sample variance, and (iii) the standard error. State the engineering implication. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The action-value function is the expected return when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \,\bigm|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$

**Step 2 — Incremental update derivation (2 marks):**
Define $\hat{Q}_{N-1}$ as the running estimate after $N-1$ samples. When the $N$-th return $G_N$ is observed, the new sample mean is:

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \;=\; \frac{N-1}{N}\hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{G_N}{N}$$

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}\bigl(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Proof of equivalence (3 marks):**
By strong induction on $N$:

- **Base case** ($N=1$): $\hat{Q}_{1} = \hat{Q}_{0} + (G_1 - \hat{Q}_{0}) = G_1$ — trivially the mean of one sample.
- **Inductive step:** Assume $\hat{Q}_{N-1} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i$. Then:

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}\!\left(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1}\right)$$

Substituting the hypothesis:

$$\hat{Q}_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{1}{N}\!\left(G_N - \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} G_i + \frac{G_N}{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Sample mean (2 marks):**

$$\hat{Q} \;=\; \frac{1}{6}\bigl(8 + 12 + 7 + 15 + 9 + 11\bigr) \;=\; \frac{62}{6} \;\approx\; 10.33$$

**Step 2 — Sample variance (3 marks):**

$$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(G^{(i)} - \hat{Q}\bigr)^{2}$$

Deviations: $(8-10.33)^{2} = 5.43$, $(12-10.33)^{2} = 2.79$, $(7-10.33)^{2} = 11.09$, $(15-10.33)^{2} = 21.81$, $(9-10.33)^{2} = 1.77$, $(11-10.33)^{2} = 0.45$.

$$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{5}(5.43 + 2.79 + 11.09 + 21.81 + 1.77 + 0.45) \;=\; \frac{43.34}{5} \;\approx\; 8.67$$

**Step 3 — Standard error (1 mark):**

$$\text{SE} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{8.67}}{\sqrt{6}} \;\approx\; \frac{2.94}{2.45} \;\approx\; 1.20$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implication (1 mark):**
The estimate is $\hat{Q}(s_3, a_1) = 10.33 \pm 1.20$. To reduce the standard error to **below 0.5**, the robot must run $N \geq (1.96 \cdot 2.94 / 0.5)^{2} \approx 133$ episodes, illustrating the **$\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$ sample complexity** of MC methods. In a deployment, the engineer must budget for 13× more episodes to triple the precision.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Explain the **exploration–exploitation dilemma** in the context of Monte Carlo estimation of action values. Compare the **Exploring Starts** and **$\varepsilon$-soft** strategies as solutions, with their respective assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the $\varepsilon$-soft policy formulation, derive the policy-improvement step for a 3-action environment with $\varepsilon = 0.1$ when the current value estimates are $\hat{Q}(s, a_1) = 5$, $\hat{Q}(s, a_2) = 7$, $\hat{Q}(s, a_3) = 3$. State the resulting action probabilities and identify which action is selected most often in the long run. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Step 1 — The dilemma (2 marks):**
The exploration–exploitation dilemma arises because, to estimate $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ accurately for *every* action, the agent must visit all actions repeatedly. However, the goal of policy improvement is to **exploit** — i.e., always pick the action with the highest estimated $Q$. If the agent always exploits, untried actions are never sampled, their $Q$-values remain at their initial guess (often 0), and the policy becomes stuck on a *locally optimal but globally suboptimal* action. Conversely, excessive exploration wastes effort on demonstrably inferior actions.

**Step 2 — Exploring Starts (2 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Each episode begins with a *random* state-action pair $(s_0, a_0)$ drawn uniformly from the state-action space.
- **Assumption:** Non-zero probability of starting at *any* $(s, a)$. In real robots, this is often *unrealistic* — you cannot teleport a robot's arm into a state it cannot physically reach.
- **Limitation:** Fails for *continuing* (non-episodic) tasks and for tasks with physical reachability constraints.

**Step 3 — $\varepsilon$-soft policies (2 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** At every state, the agent picks the greedy action with probability $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$, and any non-greedy action with probability $\varepsilon/{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$. Thus every action is sampled with probability $\geq \varepsilon/{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$.
- **Assumption:** The environment is *episodic* or contains a way to *reset* the agent to a state distribution (soft policies alone don't guarantee return-to-start).
- **Advantage:** Compatible with **continuing tasks**; doesn't require teleportation.
- **Limitation:** Suboptimal actions are sampled forever — the agent never becomes *fully* greedy. The **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) schedule decays $\varepsilon \to 0$ over time, recovering greedy behaviour asymptotically.

**Step 4 — Convergence note (1 mark):**
Under either scheme, MC policy iteration converges to the *optimal* action-value function $Q^{*}$ and the optimal policy $\pi^{*}$ as the number of episodes $\to \infty$, provided all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often (the **infinite exploration** condition).

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Identify greedy action (1 mark):**
With $\hat{Q}(s, a_1) = 5$, $\hat{Q}(s, a_2) = 7$, $\hat{Q}(s, a_3) = 3$, the greedy action is $a_{2}$ (highest $Q$-value).

**Step 2 — $\varepsilon$-soft probability formula (2 marks):**
For a 3-action environment with $\varepsilon = 0.1$:

$$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert} \;=\; 0.9 + \frac{0.1}{3} \;=\; 0.9 + 0.0\overline{3} \;\approx\; 0.9333$$

for the greedy action, and

$$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert} \;=\; \frac{0.1}{3} \;\approx\; 0.0333$$

for every non-greedy action.

**Step 3 — Apply to each action (2 marks):**

$$\pi(a_1 \mid s) \;\approx\; 0.0333$$

$$\pi(a_2 \mid s) \;\approx\; 0.9333 \quad \text{(greedy)}$$

$$\pi(a_3 \mid s) \;\approx\; 0.0333$$

**Verification:** $\sum \pi(a_i \mid s) = 0.0333 + 0.9333 + 0.0333 = 1.0$ ✅

**Step 4 — Long-run conclusion (2 marks):**
The action $a_2$ is selected approximately **93.33% of the time**, while $a_1$ and $a_3$ are each selected only **3.33% of the time**. In the long run (many episodes), the agent **predominantly exploits** the best-known action but still **explores** the alternatives just enough to update their $Q$-estimates and detect any change in their relative quality. As the value estimates stabilise and the policy becomes more confident, a GLIE schedule would gradually reduce $\varepsilon$ to enhance exploitation further.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the conditioning in the expectation.** Writing $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t]$ instead of $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ loses 1 mark — the conditioning makes it an *action-value* function, not a state-value function.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{\pi}(s)$ and $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$.** $V$ is a function of *state only*; $Q$ is a function of *state-action pair*. Examiners will deduct marks if you interchange them.
> 3. **Missing the discount factor in $G_t$** for infinite-horizon derivations. Always state $0 \le \gamma < 1$ explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the base case** in the induction proof. Even if the inductive step is flawless, omitting the $N=1$ base case is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{N-1}$ vs $\frac{1}{N}$ distinction.** The MC action-value estimate is a *sample mean*, so use $\frac{1}{N-1}$ for the unbiased *sample variance* (Bessel's correction) but $\frac{1}{N}$ for the *sample mean*. Mixing them up is a common error.
> 6. **Not stating the assumptions** of the chosen exploration scheme (e.g., physical reachability for Exploring Starts).
> 7. **Forgetting the GLIE condition** for convergence proofs — MC methods only converge to the optimal policy if every state-action pair is visited *infinitely often*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MC Estimation** = sample-mean estimation of $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ from observed returns in episodes. **Model-free**, requires only experience.
- **Return $G_t$** = cumulative (possibly discounted $\gamma$) reward from time $t$ to terminal. $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Two MC variants:** First-Visit (unbiased, simpler theory) vs Every-Visit (correlated samples, sometimes lower empirical error).
- **Incremental update** $\hat{Q}_{N} = \hat{Q}_{N-1} + \frac{1}{N}(G_N - \hat{Q}_{N-1})$ is *mathematically identical* to the sample mean — proved by induction. **This is a board-exam favourite.**
- **Convergence:** By the **Law of Large Numbers**, $\hat{Q}(s, a) \to Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ almost surely as $N \to \infty$. Standard error decays as $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$.
- **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma:** Must visit all $(s, a)$ pairs. Solutions:
  - **Exploring Starts** — random initial $(s_0, a_0)$; requires non-zero start probability everywhere.
  - **$\varepsilon$-soft policies** — $\pi(a \mid s) \geq \varepsilon/{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ for all $a$; works for continuing tasks.
  - **GLIE** — $\varepsilon$ decays to 0; guarantees convergence to optimal $\pi^{*}$.
- **Policy Improvement:** Set $\pi(s) = \arg\max_{a} \hat{Q}(s, a)$ (greedy w.r.t. current estimate).
- **Convergence criteria:** All state-action pairs visited *infinitely often* + GLIE policy schedule.
- **Robotic relevance:** Enables model-free learning of manipulation, navigation, and control policies directly from physical interaction — no analytic model of friction, contact, or aerodynamics required.
- **Computational cost:** To halve the standard error, quadruple the number of episodes — the operational $1/\sqrt{N}$ bottleneck of MC methods.
- **Off-policy caveat:** Standard MC estimation is *on-policy* (estimates $Q_{\pi}$ for the policy used to generate data). Off-policy estimation requires **importance sampling** corrections, which blow up variance — an important follow-up topic in Module 3.
- **Key symbols to memorise:** $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$, $\hat{Q}(s, a)$, $G_t$, $N(s, a)$, $\gamma$, $\varepsilon$, $\pi(a \mid s)$, GLIE.
- **Board-proof checklist:** Always state (i) the definition with conditioning, (ii) the LLN justification, (iii) the incremental-vs-mean equivalence proof, (iv) the exploration assumption, and (v) the convergence condition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monte Carlo (MC) Control** is a model-free reinforcement learning paradigm that learns the optimal policy by directly sampling complete episodes of agent–environment interaction. Unlike Dynamic Programming, it does **not** require a known transition model $P(s' \vert s, a)$ or reward function $R(s, a)$. MC Control estimates the **action-value function** $Q_\pi(s, a)$ by averaging the empirical returns observed after visiting state-action pair $(s, a)$ under a behaviour policy, and then improves the policy greedily with respect to this estimate.

The generic MC Control problem statement is:

$$
\pi^* \;\leftarrow\; \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \right]
$$

where the optimal action-value function is defined as:

$$
Q^*(s,a) \;=\; \max_{\pi} \; Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

and the optimal policy is recovered by acting greedily on $Q^*$:

$$
\pi^*(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \; Q^*(s,a)
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Roulette Player**
> Imagine you walk into a casino with **no idea** how roulette works. You have no rulebook (no model), so you cannot compute probabilities. What do you do? You **play thousands of spins**, keep a mental tally of which betting choices gave you the highest *average winnings*, and gradually shift your bets toward those options. Monte Carlo Control is exactly this:
> - **No model** is assumed.
> - You learn the value of actions by **sampling real returns**.
> - You **average** the outcomes to form an estimate, and improve your policy based on what worked best.

The agent becomes its own "experimental physicist" — collecting data, averaging, and refining.

## 1.3 Why Monte Carlo Control? (KTU Motivation)

| Drawback of DP | Monte Carlo Advantage |
|---|---|
| Requires full transition model $P$ | Model-free — learns from experience |
| Must sweep *all* states | Only visits states reachable in episodes |
| Suffers from curse of dimensionality | Empirically scales to large/continuous tasks |
| Suffers from model-bias | Estimates are unbiased (as $N \to \infty$) |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standards (KTU-marked)

- **Episode** — a finite trajectory $\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \ldots, S_T)$.
- **Return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$.
- **First-visit MC** — averages returns following the *first* occurrence of $(s,a)$ in an episode.
- **Every-visit MC** — averages returns following *every* occurrence of $(s,a)$.
- **Behaviour policy** $b$ — policy used to generate episodes.
- **Target policy** $\pi$ — policy being improved/evaluated.
- **Stationary vs Non-stationary** — depends on whether action probabilities are time-homogeneous.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalised Policy Iteration (GPI) Backbone of MC Control

Monte Carlo Control is fundamentally a **Generalised Policy Iteration (GPI)** algorithm alternating two processes:

1. **Policy Evaluation** — estimate $Q \approx Q_\pi$ from sampled episodes.
2. **Policy Improvement** — make the policy greedy w.r.t. $Q$.

$$
\pi_{k+1}(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \; Q_{\pi_k}(s, a)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Result (Sutton & Barto):** Under *every-visit* MC with exploration (e.g., exploring starts or $\varepsilon$-soft), $Q_{\pi_k}$ converges to $Q^*$, and $\pi_k$ converges to $\pi^*$ for any sequence of episodes.

## 2.2 Two Canonical MC Control Variants (Exam Favourite)

### Variant A — On-Policy MC Control (Exploring Starts)

**Update rule for action-value:**

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha \bigl[ G_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \bigr]
$$

where $\alpha = \tfrac{1}{N(S_t, A_t)}$ for averaging, and $G_t$ is the empirical return.

**Exploring-Starts Assumption:** every state-action pair has a non-zero probability of being chosen as the initial pair $(S_0, A_0)$ of an episode.

### Variant B — On-Policy MC Control ($\varepsilon$-Soft / $\varepsilon$-Greedy)

To avoid the unrealistic exploring-starts assumption, we restrict the policy to be **$\varepsilon$-soft**:

$$
\pi(a \mid s) \;\geq\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, \quad \forall a \in \mathcal{A}(s)
$$

The improvement step is then:

$$
\pi'(a \mid s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a = A^* = \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a) \\[2mm]
\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a \neq A^*
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An **$\varepsilon$-soft** policy is *not* strictly greedy — it keeps exploring. The family of $\varepsilon$-soft policies is **closed** under improvement, guaranteeing convergence.

### Variant C — Off-Policy MC Control (Importance Sampling)

When the policy used to **generate** data (behaviour $b$) differs from the policy being **improved** (target $\pi$):

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} \;=\; \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

The **ordinary importance-sampling estimator** of $v_\pi(s)$ is:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \, G_t}{\vert \mathcal{T}(s) \vert}
$$

The **weighted importance-sampling estimator** (lower variance, biased in finite samples) is:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \, G_t}{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1}}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Defines the learning target |
| MC Target Update | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_t - Q(S_t,A_t)\bigr]$ | Incremental averaging rule |
| Recursive Return | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$ | Used in backward-view MC |
| GLIE Condition 1 | $\lim_{k \to \infty} N_k(s,a) = \infty$ | Infinite exploration |
| GLIE Condition 2 | $\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \mid s) \to \arg\max_{a} Q^{\pi_k}(s,a)$ | Greedy in the limit |
| $\varepsilon$-soft Closure | $\pi(a \mid s) \geq \varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert$ | Ensures policy-improvement closure |
| Importance Ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \,/\, b(A_k \mid S_k)$ | Off-policy correction factor |
| Ordinary IS | $V(s) = \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_i \rho_i G_t^{(i)}$ | Unbiased, high variance |
| Weighted IS | $V(s) = \tfrac{\sum_i \rho_i G_t^{(i)}}{\sum_i \rho_i}$ | Biased, low variance |
| $\varepsilon$-greedy Action | $A^* = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Greedy improvement |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics manipulation learning** — robot learns grasp policies by physical rollouts.
- **Autonomous driving simulators** — CARLA-style episodic training of lane-keeping agents.
- **Game AI** — AlphaGo's early training stages used MC-style self-play rollouts.
- **Industrial process control** — episodic batch processes (e.g., semiconductor manufacturing) where transitions are unknown.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Incremental Monte Carlo Update

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that the running average update of MC converges to the expected return $q_\pi(s,a)$.

We have a sequence of returns $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_n$ observed after visiting $(s,a)$. The empirical mean is:

$$
Q_n(s,a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i
$$

To make the update *online* (using only the most recent return), we write:

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G_i
$$

Splitting the sum:

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1} \left( G_{n+1} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i \right)
$$

Recognising that $\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i = n \cdot Q_n$:

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1} \left( G_{n+1} + n \, Q_n(s,a) \right)
$$

Now separate and rearrange:

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; \frac{n}{n+1} Q_n(s,a) \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1} G_{n+1}
$$

Express $Q_n$ twice to isolate the new return term:

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_n(s,a) \;-\; \frac{1}{n+1} Q_n(s,a) \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1} G_{n+1}
$$

$$
Q_{n+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_n(s,a) \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1} \bigl( G_{n+1} - Q_n(s,a) \bigr)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalisation:** Replace $\tfrac{1}{n+1}$ by a step-size $\alpha$ to obtain the textbook form used in the formula sheet:
> $$Q(S_t,A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_t - Q(S_t,A_t)\bigr]$$
> This converges *for any* $\alpha_t$ sequence satisfying $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Importance Sampling Ratio under Off-Policy MC

**Setup:** Episode $\tau$ generated by behaviour $b$; we want to estimate $v_\pi(s)$.

The probability of a *single* episode under policy $b$ is:

$$
\Pr(\tau \mid b) \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} b(A_k \mid S_k) \, P(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)
$$

Under target policy $\pi$:

$$
\Pr(\tau \mid \pi) \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, P(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)
$$

The **importance-sampling ratio** cancels the (unknown) transition dynamics:

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} \;=\; \frac{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) P(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} b(A_k \mid S_k) P(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}
$$

All $P$ terms cancel pairwise:

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

For returns starting at time $t$ within an episode, we trim the product:

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} \;=\; \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

For a state $s$ appearing at multiple time indices, the **ordinary IS estimator** is:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_{t_i:T(t_i)-1}^{(i)} \, G_{t_i}^{(i)}
$$

The **weighted IS estimator** (used in MC Control) divides by the sum of weights to stabilise variance:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_{t_i:T(t_i)-1}^{(i)} \, G_{t_i}^{(i)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_{t_i:T(t_i)-1}^{(i)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GLIE Convergence of $\varepsilon$-Soft MC Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Sutton & Barto, KTU Module 2).** If a sequence of $\varepsilon_k$-greedy policies satisfies:
> (i) every state-action pair is visited infinitely often: $\;N_k(s,a) \to \infty$,
> (ii) $\;\varepsilon_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$,
> then $Q^{\pi_k} \to Q^*$ and $\pi_k \to \pi^*$.

**Sketch of proof (examinable):**

1. **Infinite exploration ⇒** $Q^{\pi_k}$ converges to the true $Q$ for each fixed $\pi_k$ (law of large numbers).
2. **Greedy in the limit ⇒** each improvement step yields a strictly better policy in the $\varepsilon$-soft family (policy-improvement theorem).
3. **Monotonic policy-value sequence** $\{v_{\pi_k}\}$ is bounded above by $v^*$, hence converges.
4. **Limit consistency:** if $v_{\pi_k} = v^*$, the policy must be optimal, otherwise some $\varepsilon$-exploration would still allow improvement — contradiction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing On-Policy vs Off-Policy MC Control

| Property | On-Policy MC | Off-Policy MC |
|---|---|---|
| Uses behaviour policy $b$ | $b = \pi$ | $b \neq \pi$ |
| Coverage requirement | $b$ must be the policy of interest | $b$ must visit all $\pi$-reachable $(s,a)$ |
| Variance | Low | High (amplified by $\rho$) |
| Bias (weighted IS) | Unbiased | Slightly biased in finite samples |
| Typical use | Standard GPI control | Re-using old data, learning $\pi^*$ from exploratory $b$ |
| KTU-mandated algorithm name | "On-policy first-visit MC control" | "Off-policy MC control with weighted IS" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of On-Policy MC Control (Exploring Starts)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start of Episode]) --> initPair[Initialise S0, A0 from exploring-starts distribution]
    initPair --> stepRollout[Roll out step: take action, observe Rt+1, St+1]
    stepRollout --> terminalCheck{Is St+1 terminal?}
    terminalCheck -- No --> stepRollout
    terminalCheck -- Yes --> backwardPass[Backward pass: compute Gt for each t]
    backwardPass --> updateQ[Update Q St,At using MC average rule]
    updateQ --> improvePolicy[Improve policy: pi aIs = argmaxa Q s,a]
    improvePolicy --> nextEp{Next episode available?}
    nextEp -- Yes --> initPair
    nextEp -- No --> stopNode([Output optimal pi and Q])

    style startNode fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style stopNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style updateQ fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style improvePolicy fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Off-Policy MC Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TARGET[Target Policy pi]
        piPolicy[pi aIs - policy being improved]
    end

    subgraph BEHAVIOUR[Behaviour Policy b]
        bPolicy[b aIs - typically epsilon-soft or uniform]
    end

    episodes[Generate episodes under behaviour b] --> dataset[Trajectory buffer: S0,A0,R1,S1,A1,...]
    dataset --> ratioCalc[Compute importance-sampling ratio rho]
    ratioCalc --> weightedAvg[Weighted IS average of returns]
    weightedAvg --> qEst[Update Q estimate]
    qEst --> greedyImprove[pi becomes greedy w.r.t. Q]
    greedyImprove --> policyCheck{pi changed?}
    policyCheck -- Yes --> bPolicy
    policyCheck -- No --> converge([Converged - output pi])

    style episodes fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style ratioCalc fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style weightedAvg fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style converge fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — GPI Loop Schematic for MC Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EVAL[Policy Evaluation - MC]
        e1[Sample many episodes under pi]
        e2[Average empirical returns Gt]
        e3[Form estimate Q approx q pi]
        e1 --> e2 --> e3
    end

    subgraph IMPROVE[Policy Improvement - Greedy]
        i1[For every state s:]
        i2[pi new s = argmaxa Q s,a]
        i3[Ensure epsilon-soft closure]
        i1 --> i2 --> i3
    end

    EVAL --> IMPROVE
    IMPROVE --> EVAL
    i3 -.-> terminal((pi approx pi star))

    style EVAL fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style IMPROVE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style terminal fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Monte Carlo Control Subsystems

| Subsystem | Input | Output | Key Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Episode Generator | Current policy $\pi$ / $b$ | Trajectory $\tau$ | $\pi \to$ environment step |
| Return Computer | Trajectory $\tau$, $\gamma$ | Return list $G_0, G_1, \ldots$ | Backward recursion $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ |
| Q-Estimator | Returns, $\alpha$ or count $N$ | Updated $Q(s,a)$ | Running average update |
| Policy Improver | $Q(s, \cdot)$ | Updated $\pi$ | Greedy / $\varepsilon$-greedy / $\varepsilon$-soft |
| Importance Sampler (off-policy only) | Trajectory, $b$, $\pi$ | Weights $\rho_{t:T-1}$ | Product of $\pi/b$ ratios |
| Convergence Monitor | Episode index $k$ | Stopping flag | GLIE / $\varepsilon$ schedule check |

---
