<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Q: Define off-policy learning. Why is the importance sampling ratio required in off-policy prediction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Off-policy learning refers to evaluating or improving a *target policy* $\pi$ using experience generated by a *different behavior policy* $b$. The importance sampling ratio is required because it corrects the distributional mismatch between $b$ and $\pi$ — re-weighting returns observed under $b$ so that they form an unbiased estimate of $V_\pi(s)$. Without this ratio, naive averaging of returns would estimate $V_b(s)$, not $V_\pi(s)$. The ratio is defined as:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

**(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for the ratio formula.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 1]`
**Q: Compare Ordinary Importance Sampling (OIS) and Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS) with respect to bias and variance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OIS | WIS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Bias | Unbiased | Biased (finite $N$) |
| Variance | Unbounded | Bounded |
| Convergence | Asymptotically unbiased | Asymptotically unbiased |
| Practical Use | Short episodes | Long episodes |

WIS is generally preferred in practice because its bounded variance makes convergence more reliable. The bias vanishes as $N \to \infty$.

**(Award 1 mark for OIS, 1 mark for WIS, 1 mark for the practical preference.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - When computing importance weights, students often forget the **product over time**. A single-step ratio is only valid for a one-step MDP; multi-step trajectories require the *full product*.
> - The **coverage assumption** is frequently omitted — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if it's not stated.
> - In WIS derivations, students confuse the denominator (sum of weights) with $N$. Always clarify: the denominator normalizes by total weight, not by the number of episodes.
> - Do not write the importance sampling ratio for a single action and call it "the" ratio. The product form must be clearly written.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q7(b)]`

**(a) Derive the importance sampling ratio for off-policy Monte-Carlo prediction. Clearly state the coverage assumption. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Statement of Setup:**
We want to estimate $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ for target policy $\pi$, but trajectories are sampled under behavior policy $b$.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Derivation of Ratio:**
The probability of a trajectory $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, \dots, s_T)$ under policy $\mu$ is:

$$\Pr(\tau \mid \mu) = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \mu(a_k \mid s_k) \cdot p(s_{k+1}, r_{k+1} \mid s_k, a_k)$$

The ratio of trajectory probabilities under $\pi$ and $b$ is:

$$\frac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} = \frac{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(a_k \mid s_k) \cdot p(s_{k+1}, r_{k+1} \mid s_k, a_k)}{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} b(a_k \mid s_k) \cdot p(s_{k+1}, r_{k+1} \mid s_k, a_k)}$$

The transition terms $p(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ cancel (the environment is the same), giving:

$$\rho_{0:T-1} = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(a_k \mid s_k)}{b(a_k \mid s_k)}$$

For an episode starting at time $t$:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(a_k \mid s_k)}{b(a_k \mid s_k)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Coverage Assumption:**
We require that for every state $s$ and action $a$:

$$\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \Rightarrow b(a \mid s) > 0$$

That is, the behavior policy must assign non-zero probability to every action the target policy might take. Without this, the ratio $\pi(a \mid s)/b(a \mid s)$ is undefined (division by zero) and the importance sampling estimator fails.

---

**(b) Explain the bias-variance tradeoff between Ordinary Importance Sampling (OIS) and Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS). Use an example to illustrate. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — OIS Analysis:**
The Ordinary Importance Sampling estimator is:

$$V_{OIS}(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho^{(i)} G^{(i)}$$

Taking the expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}_b[V_{OIS}(s)] = \mathbb{E}_b[\rho G] = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G] = V_\pi(s)$$

Hence OIS is **unbiased**. However, its variance is:

$$\text{Var}_b[V_{OIS}] = \frac{1}{N} \text{Var}_b[\rho G]$$

Since $\rho$ is a product of ratios, it can be **exponentially large** for long episodes, leading to unbounded variance.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — WIS Analysis:**
The Weighted Importance Sampling estimator is:

$$V_{WIS}(s) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho^{(i)} G^{(i)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho^{(i)}}$$

The denominator is itself a random variable. The bias is:

$$\mathbb{E}_b[V_{WIS}(s)] \neq V_\pi(s) \quad \text{(for finite } N\text{)}$$

But since $\mathbb{E}_b[\rho] = 1$, the denominator converges to $N$ in expectation, and the bias vanishes as $N \to \infty$. The variance is bounded because the denominator normalizes the influence of any single large weight.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Illustrative Example:**
Consider the bandit example from §3.3 with 3 episodes. OIS gave 12.08 and WIS gave 9.06. The difference (≈3) is exactly the *bias* contribution of WIS for that sample. With more episodes, the two estimates would converge, but the OIS estimate would fluctuate much more (higher variance).

**Conclusion:** WIS is preferred in practice for long episodes; OIS is preferred only when unbiasedness is critical and episodes are short.

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q8(a)]`

**(a) Explain the per-decision importance sampling estimator. How does it differ from ordinary and weighted importance sampling? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Motivation:**
In standard OIS/WIS, a single product $\rho_{t:T-1}$ scales the *entire* return $G_t$. If an early action has a tiny $b$-probability, the entire return — including rewards earned *after* — gets demoted. This is statistically inefficient.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Per-Decision Formula:**
The per-decision importance sampling estimator is:

$$V_{PDIS}(s) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{T}(s) \vert} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \sum_{h=t}^{T(t)-1} \rho_{t:h-1} \, \gamma^{h-t-1} R_{h+1}$$

Note that:
- The weight $\rho_{t:h-1}$ involves only actions from time $t$ up to time $h-1$.
- The reward at time $h+1$ is multiplied by its *appropriate* discount factor $\gamma^{h-t-1}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Comparison:**

| Aspect | OIS | WIS | Per-Decision IS |
|--------|-----|-----|-----------------|
| Weight scope | Full trajectory | Full trajectory | Up to reward's causal parents |
| Variance | High | Moderate | Lower than OIS |
| Bias | Zero | Small | Zero |

Per-decision IS exploits the causal structure — a reward at time $h$ cannot depend on actions after $h$, so weighting it by $\rho_{t:h-1}$ is sufficient and gives lower variance than $\rho_{t:T-1}$.

---

**(b) An agent in a 2-state MDP uses $\pi(a_1 \mid s) = 0.8$, $\pi(a_2 \mid s) = 0.2$ as the target policy and $b(a_1 \mid s) = 0.5$, $b(a_2 \mid s) = 0.5$ as the behavior policy. Three episodes are observed from $s_0$: (1) $a_1$, $G = 8$; (2) $a_2$, $G = 12$; (3) $a_1$, $G = 4$. Compute OIS and WIS estimates. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Importance Weights:**
- For $a_1$: $\pi/b = 0.8/0.5 = 1.6$
- For $a_2$: $\pi/b = 0.2/0.5 = 0.4$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Tabulate:**

| Episode | Action | $G$ | $W$ | $W \times G$ |
|---------|--------|-----|-----|--------------|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 8 | 1.6 | 12.8 |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 12 | 0.4 | 4.8 |
| 3 | $a_1$ | 4 | 1.6 | 6.4 |

**Step 3 (2 marks) — OIS Calculation:**

$$V_{OIS} = \frac{12.8 + 4.8 + 6.4}{3} = \frac{24.0}{3} = 8.0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — WIS Calculation:**

$$V_{WIS} = \frac{24.0}{1.6 + 0.4 + 1.6} = \frac{24.0}{3.6} \approx 6.667$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**
- OIS treats the reweighted returns as equally representative.
- WIS down-weights the $a_2$ episode (low $W$) and gives an estimate closer to the $a_1$-dominated distribution under $\pi$.
- The two estimates bracket the true $V_\pi(s)$ (which lies between, given the small sample size).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Off-policy learning** = learning about $\pi$ from data generated by $b \neq \pi$.
- **Coverage assumption** $b(a \mid s) > 0$ whenever $\pi(a \mid s) > 0$ — *mandatory*; without it, the importance ratio is undefined.
- **Importance sampling ratio** for a trajectory is the **product** over time of single-step ratios:
$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$
- **OIS** is **unbiased but has unbounded variance** — use for short episodes.
- **WIS** is **biased for finite $N$ but has bounded variance** — preferred for long episodes.
- **Per-decision IS** is a causal variance-reduction variant that weights each reward only by the importance ratios of its causal predecessors.
- **Effective Sample Size (ESS):** $n_{eff} = \frac{(\sum_i \rho_i)^2}{\sum_i \rho_i^2}$ is the diagnostic for whether IS is working.
- **In robotics:** off-policy prediction enables safe data reuse from human demonstrations or exploratory controllers.
- **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Always write the *product* form, not a single ratio. Always state the coverage assumption. Always distinguish OIS from WIS clearly.
- **Big Picture:** Off-policy prediction is the *statistical backbone* of Q-Learning, Expected SARSA, and offline/batch RL — all built on top of the importance sampling framework you studied here.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incremental Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental Implementation** in Reinforcement Learning refers to a class of computational strategies in which the value estimates of states (or state-action pairs) are updated **recursively and on-the-fly**, after every observed transition or short trajectory window, without the need to store the full history of returns. It transforms a batch statistical operation into an **O(1) memory, O(1) per-step update** procedure.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PERAT747 – Reinforcement Learning for Robotics, Module 2), the term "Incremental Implementation" is studied at two distinct but connected levels:

1. **Incremental Mean Estimation** – computing running averages of returns as they are observed.
2. **Incremental n-step Bootstrapping** – applying running-average style updates to n-step TD (Temporal Difference) targets, n-step Sarsa, n-step Expected Sarsa, off-policy n-step methods, and the **Tree-Backup (TB(λ))** algorithm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Running Exam Average"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a B.Tech student who has written 5 internal assessments. Instead of re-adding all 5 scores every time a new test result comes in, the student tracks only the *current average* and the *count*. When a new score $x_{n+1}$ arrives, the average is updated with a single arithmetic step.

**Mathematical Mapping of the Analogy:**

| Concept in the Analogy | RL Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Current exam average $A_n$ | Current value estimate $V_n(S)$ |
| New score $x_{n+1}$ | New observed return $G_t$ |
| Count of exams $n$ | Visit counter $N(S)$ |
| Step size $1/n$ | Step size $\alpha$ (or $1/N(S)$) |

The same logic scales to a robotic control loop: a quadruped robot learning to walk cannot "remember" the reward of every single footfall across millions of timesteps — it must update its value function **incrementally** while running, with a single number in memory per state.

### 1.3 Why Incremental Implementation Matters in Robotics

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory & Latency Constraint:** A mobile robot operating at 100 Hz generates ~8.6 million state transitions per day. Batch Monte-Carlo storage of returns is **physically infeasible** on embedded processors. Incremental updates keep the memory footprint at O(‖S‖) — one running mean per state.

The three engineering motivations are:

- **O(1) memory** per state — no return history retained.
- **Online learning** — the policy can improve *while* the robot is acting.
- **Constant-time per-step computation** — bounded real-time latency for safety-critical control loops (e.g., autonomous braking, surgical robotics).

### 1.4 A Subtle but Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant step-size vs. averaging step-size:**
> - When the step size is $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ (visit-counter form), the update *exactly* produces the sample mean.
> - When $\alpha$ is a **fixed constant** ($0 < \alpha < 1$), the update produces an **exponentially weighted moving average** — older samples are gradually forgotten, which is desirable in non-stationary robotic environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Equation Reframing Trick

The classical sample mean
$$
\bar{x}_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$
is rewritten (Sutton & Barto, Chapter 2) into a **recursive form** by adding and subtracting $\bar{x}_{n-1}$:
$$
\bar{x}_n = \bar{x}_{n-1} + \frac{1}{n}\bigl(x_n - \bar{x}_{n-1}\bigr)
$$
This single manipulation is the genesis of *every* incremental RL update rule, because it replaces a sum over history with a single **error-correction step**.

### 2.2 The Generic Incremental Update Schema

For any quantity $X_n$ and new observation $Y$, the RL update template is:
$$
X_{n+1} \;\longleftarrow\; X_n \;+\; \alpha \bigl[ Y - X_n \bigr]
$$

- $\alpha$ → **step size** (learning rate).
- $Y - X_n$ → **error term** (target − current estimate).
- $X_n$ → the running estimate (mean, value, Q-value, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Every incremental update in RL is of the form `[New] = [Old] + [StepSize] × [Target − Old]`. If you remember only this template, you can derive **95% of the algorithms** in Module 2.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verified. Pipes (`|`) inside tables are replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Algorithm | Incremental Update Equation | Target Term $Y$ | Step Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Incremental Sample Mean** | $V_{n+1} = V_n + \dfrac{1}{n}\bigl(G - V_n\bigr)$ | $G$ (sample return) | $\dfrac{1}{n}$ |
| 2 | **Constant-α Mean (EWMA)** | $V_{n+1} = V_n + \alpha\bigl(G - V_n\bigr)$ | $G$ | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| 3 | **n-step TD Prediction** | $V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]$ | $G_{t:t+n}$ | $\alpha$ |
| 4 | **n-step Sarsa** | $Q_{t+n}(S_t,A_t) = Q_{t+n-1}(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t,A_t)\bigr]$ | $G_{t:t+n}$ | $\alpha$ |
| 5 | **n-step Expected Sarsa** | Same as n-step Sarsa but target uses expectation over $A_{t+n+1}$ | $G_{t:t+n}$ (expected) | $\alpha$ |
| 6 | **n-step Off-Policy TD** | $V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha\,\rho_{t:t+n-1}\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]$ | $G_{t:t+n}$ | $\alpha\rho_{t:t+n-1}$ |
| 7 | **n-step Tree Backup (TB)** | $Q_{t+n}(S_t,A_t) = Q_{t+n-1} + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n}^{TB} - Q_{t+n-1}\bigr]$ | $G_{t:t+n}^{TB}$ | $\alpha$ |
| 8 | **Q(σ) Hybrid** | $Q_{t+n} = Q_{t+n-1} + \alpha\,\rho_{t+1:t+n-1}\bigl[G_{t:t+n}^{\sigma} - Q_{t+n-1}\bigr]$ | $G_{t:t+n}^{\sigma}$ | $\alpha\rho$ |

### 2.4 Definition of the n-step Return $G_{t:t+n}$

The n-step return is the **cornerstone quantity** that every incremental n-step algorithm updates toward:

$$
G_{t:t+n} \;\equiv\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n}\,V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})
$$

Boundary condition:
$$
G_{t:t+n} \;=\; G_t \quad \text{if } t+n \geq T \text{ (terminal time)}
$$

### 2.5 The Off-Policy Importance Sampling Ratio

When the **behaviour policy** $\mu$ differs from the **target policy** $\pi$, every transition in the n-step window is weighted by:

$$
\rho_{t:h} \;\equiv\; \prod_{k=t}^{\min(h,\,T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The product runs from $k = t$ (the action being updated) up to $k = h-1$ for off-policy n-step TD, and $k = t+1$ to $k = h-1$ for action-value methods. Getting these index bounds wrong is the most common valuation error.

### 2.6 Tree-Backup (TB) n-step Return — No Importance Sampling Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} \;=\;& R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1})\,Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1},a) \\
&+ \gamma^{2} \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+2})\,Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+2},a') + \cdots \\
&+ \gamma^{n} \sum_{a_{n}} \pi(a_{n} \mid S_{t+n})\,Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}, a_{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Robotic locomotion controllers** (ANYmal, Spot, Atlas): incremental TD updates on value functions for foothold scoring, in real time at 200 Hz.
- **Autonomous driving (Apollo, Waymo)**: incremental Q-learning over a discretized lane-keep state space.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva): incremental Expected Sarsa for path planning under non-stationary pedestrian traffic.
- **Surgical robotics (da Vinci Research Kit)**: incremental n-step off-policy learning from surgeon demonstrations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Incremental Mean (From Sum to Recursion)

**Goal:** Derive $V_{n+1} = V_n + \frac{1}{n}(G - V_n)$ from the classical sample-mean definition.

**Step 1 — Start with the batch (non-incremental) sample mean** over the first $n$ observed returns:
$$
V_n \;\equiv\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i
$$

**Step 2 — Split the sum into the first $n-1$ terms and the new $n$-th term:**
$$
V_n \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\Biggl[\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} G_i \;+\; G_n\Biggr]
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the inner sum by $\frac{n-1}{n-1}$ to factor out $V_{n-1}$:**
$$
V_n \;=\; \frac{n-1}{n}\cdot\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} G_i \;+\; \frac{1}{n}G_n
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} G_i \;\equiv\; V_{n-1}$ and substitute:**
$$
V_n \;=\; \frac{n-1}{n}V_{n-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{n}G_n
$$

**Step 5 — Split $\frac{n-1}{n}V_{n-1}$ as $\bigl(1 - \frac{1}{n}\bigr)V_{n-1}$ and regroup:**
$$
V_n \;=\; V_{n-1} \;-\; \frac{1}{n}V_{n-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{n}G_n
$$

**Step 6 — Collect the $\frac{1}{n}$ terms together (add $V_{n-1} - V_{n-1} = 0$ for visual symmetry):**
$$
V_n \;=\; V_{n-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{n}\bigl(G_n \;-\; V_{n-1}\bigr)
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\,V_n \;=\; V_{n-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{n}\bigl(G_n - V_{n-1}\bigr)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The new estimate equals the *old* estimate plus a *correction term* proportional to the prediction error $(G_n - V_{n-1})$. The "weight" given to the new sample decays as $1/n$, which is the *only* weight sequence that makes the running recursion *exactly equal* to the batch mean.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Incremental n-step TD Prediction

**Starting point:** The batch (off-line) n-step TD target is:
$$
G_{t:t+n} \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})
$$

**Step 1 — Define the n-step TD error (advantage) at time $t$ as:**
$$
\delta_t \;\equiv\; G_{t:t+n} \;-\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Use the generic running-average template `[New] = [Old] + α·[Target − Old]`:**
$$
V_{t+n}(S_t) \;\longleftarrow\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \;+\; \alpha\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Re-index cleanly. Since we only update the state $S_t$ that was visited at time $t$, all other state values remain unchanged in the same time step:**
$$
V_{t+n}(s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
V_{t+n-1}(s) + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(s)\bigr], & s = S_t \\[6pt]
V_{t+n-1}(s), & s \neq S_t
\end{cases}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\,V_{t+n}(S_t) \;=\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \;+\; \alpha\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence condition (KTU):** The incremental n-step TD converges with probability 1 to the true value function $v_\pi$ if the step size sequence satisfies the **Robbins-Monro conditions**:
> - $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k(a) \;=\; \infty$
> - $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k^{2}(a) \;<\; \infty$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Off-Policy n-step TD with Importance Sampling

**Setup:** We wish to estimate $v_\pi$ using trajectories generated by a different behaviour policy $\mu$. Each action $A_k$ has a relative probability $\frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$.

**Step 1 — Multiply the n-step return by the cumulative importance ratio over the $n$-step window (from $k = t$ to $k = t+n-1$):**
$$
\rho_{t:t+n-1} \;\equiv\; \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the incremental update template, *scaling the step size* by $\rho$ to correct the off-policy bias:**
$$
V_{t+n}(S_t) \;\longleftarrow\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \;+\; \alpha\,\rho_{t:t+n-1}\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — State the final form with the explicit product expanded:**
$$
V_{t+n}(S_t) \;=\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \;+\; \alpha \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)} \bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variance Warning:** Long importance-sampling products lead to high variance. In practice, $n$ is kept small (typically $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$) for off-policy robotics tasks. Off-policy Tree-Backup avoids the product entirely.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tree-Backup n-step Return (Recursive, No IS Needed)

**Step 1 — Define the 1-step backup (base case, $n = 1$):**
$$
G_{t:t+1} \;\equiv\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid S_{t+1})\,Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1},a)
$$

**Step 2 — Hypothesize a recursive structure:** assume the $(n-1)$-step return is defined. Then:
$$
G_{t:t+n} \;\equiv\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \mid S_{t+1})\,Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1},a) + \gamma\,\pi(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1})\,G_{t+1:t+n}
$$

**Step 3 — The unrolled (closed-form) n-step version is a sum of expected values over all *unsampled* actions at each step, with the *sampled* action's contribution being itself a recursive $(n-1)$-step return.**

**Step 4 — Write the unrolled form (used in incremental updates):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} \;=\;& R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1},a) \\
&+ \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}) \Bigl( R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+2}) Q(S_{t+2},a') + \cdots \Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — The incremental Q-update is then simply:**
$$
Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) \;\longleftarrow\; Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n}^{TB} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t)\bigr]
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation in Robotics | Recommended Incremental Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Stationary, on-policy locomotion | n-step Sarsa (fixed $\alpha$) | Low variance, simple |
| Non-stationary human crowds (changing $\pi$) | n-step Expected Sarsa | No inner maximization bias |
| Learning from demonstrations (off-policy) | Tree-Backup TB($\lambda$) | No importance-sampling variance |
| Long-horizon credit assignment | n-step TD with large $n$ | Reduces bias of 1-step TD |
| Real-time strict latency (1 ms loop) | 1-step TD / Sarsa | Smallest per-step compute |
| Exploration-only noisy demonstrations | Off-policy n-step TD with $\rho$ | Corrects distribution mismatch |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Incremental Update Engine

The following diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic incremental n-step RL update loop, suitable for an embedded robotic controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    envState[Environment State S_t] --> policyAct[Policy pi or mu selects Action A_t]
    policyAct --> execute[Robot executes A_t]
    execute --> observeR[Observe Reward R_tplus1]
    observeR --> nextState[Observe Next State S_tplus1]
    nextState --> ringBuffer[Ring Buffer of n-step History]
    observeR --> ringBuffer
    ringBuffer --> nstepReturn[Compute n-step Return G_ttn]
    nstepReturn --> tableLook[Fetch current value V_t or Q_t]
    tableLook --> errorCalc[Compute Error G minus V]
    errorCalc --> stepSizeMod[Multiply by step size alpha times importance ratio rho]
    stepSizeMod --> incUpdate[Incremental Update V_tplusn equals V_t plus correction]
    incUpdate --> tableWrite[Write back to value table]
    tableWrite --> policyImprove[Policy Improvement Step]
    policyImprove --> envState
    tableWrite --> logOut[Telemetry Log to Host]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Incremental Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New RL Task in Robotics] --> q1{On-policy or Off-policy?}
    q1 --> onPol[On-Policy]
    q1 --> offPol[Off-Policy]
    onPol --> q2{Need action values?}
    q2 --> noQ[No - Use State Values]
    q2 --> yesQ[Yes - Use State Action Values]
    noQ --> tdOn[n-step TD Prediction]
    yesQ --> sarsaOn[n-step Sarsa]
    sarsaOn --> q3{Suffer from maximisation bias?}
    q3 --> yesBias[Yes - Use Expected Sarsa]
    q3 --> noBias[No - Plain Sarsa is fine]
    offPol --> q4{Can you afford importance sampling variance?}
    q4 --> noIS[No - Use Tree Backup TB lambda]
    q4 --> yesIS[Yes - Use n-step Off-Policy TD with rho]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Time Axis of the n-step Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t: State S_t sampled"] --> t1["t+1: A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1"]
    t1 --> t2["t+2: A_t+1, R_t+2, S_t+2"]
    t2 --> t3["t+3: ... up to t+n-1"]
    t3 --> tn["t+n: Bootstrap with V_t+n-1 of S_t+n"]
    tn --> update[Update V_t+n of S_t]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic — Conceptual Ring Buffer for an n-step Window

```
+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
| t |t+1|t+2|t+3|t+4|t+5|t+6|...|  <- time index
+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
| S | A | R | S | A | R | S |...
| t | t |t+1| t+1| t+1| t+2| t+2
+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
         ↑
         |__( n-step return G_{t:t+n} )__
         |
     Value update fires when n steps accumulated
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]  
> **State and derive the incremental rule for computing the running mean of a sequence of returns. Why is the step size $\frac{1}{n}$ special?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The running mean $V_n$ of returns $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_n$ is computed recursively as:
$$
V_{n+1} \;=\; V_n \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1}\bigl(G_{n+1} - V_n\bigr)
$$

**Derivation sketch:** Start from $V_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G_i$, split the sum, factor out $V_n$, and rearrange. The step size $\frac{1}{n}$ is special because it is the *only* weight sequence that makes the recursive update produce an output *mathematically identical* to the batch mean — no approximation error accumulates.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]  
> **Differentiate between constant-α incremental updates and 1/n step-size updates. In which robotic scenario would you prefer constant-α?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | $\alpha = \frac{1}{n}$ | $\alpha = $ constant |
|---|---|---|
| Output equals batch mean? | Exactly | Approximately (exponentially weighted) |
| Forgets old data? | No (all weighted equally) | Yes (geometric decay) |
| Suitable for non-stationary tasks? | No | Yes |
| Suitable for stationary tasks? | Yes (only at convergence) | Yes |

**Robotic scenario preferring constant-α:** A quadruped robot learning to walk on terrain whose friction *changes over time* (mud → wet leaves → dry concrete). The robot must gradually forget old walking strategies.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice: A or B)

> **Question A** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]  
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the incremental update rule for n-step TD prediction, starting from the definition of the n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$. State the Robbins-Monro convergence conditions.  
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A mobile robot is learning the value function for a grid-world navigation task. At time $t$, it visits state $S_t$ and observes a 3-step return of $G_{t:t+3} = 12.4$. The current value estimate is $V_{t+2}(S_t) = 9.8$, and the step size is $\alpha = 0.3$. Compute the updated value $V_{t+3}(S_t)$ using the incremental n-step TD update.

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

- **1 mark** — Correct statement of n-step return:
$$
G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})
$$

- **1 mark** — Definition of n-step TD error:
$$
\delta_t \;\equiv\; G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)
$$

- **2 marks** — Application of the generic running-mean template to derive the incremental update:
$$
V_{t+n}(S_t) \;\longleftarrow\; V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]
$$

- **2 marks** — Robbins-Monro convergence conditions:
  - $\sum_k \alpha_k(a) = \infty$
  - $\sum_k \alpha_k^2(a) < \infty$

- **1 mark** — Boundary handling for terminal states: $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ if $t+n \geq T$.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $G_{t:t+3} = 12.4$
- $V_{t+2}(S_t) = 9.8$
- $\alpha = 0.3$

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Compute the prediction error (TD error):**
$$
\delta_t = G_{t:t+3} - V_{t+2}(S_t) = 12.4 - 9.8 = 2.6
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Apply the incremental update formula:**
$$
V_{t+3}(S_t) = V_{t+2}(S_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
V_{t+3}(S_t) = 9.8 + (0.3)(2.6) = 9.8 + 0.78
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Final answer:**
$$
\boxed{\,V_{t+3}(S_t) = 10.58\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The state $S_t$ is "more valuable than previously thought" because the 3-step return exceeded the old estimate. The robot's value table moves upward by 0.78 units, which is 30% of the discrepancy — a controlled, stable correction.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]  
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the off-policy n-step TD algorithm with importance sampling. Derive the incremental update equation and discuss its variance problem.  
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A robot is learning a target policy $\pi$ from a behaviour policy $\mu$. In a 2-step window, the action probabilities are: $\pi(A_t \mid S_t) = 0.8$, $\mu(A_t \mid S_t) = 0.4$, $\pi(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}) = 0.6$, $\mu(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}) = 0.3$. Compute the importance sampling ratio $\rho_{t:t+1}$.

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Off-Policy n-step TD [7 marks]:**

- **1 mark** — Define behaviour and target policies: $\mu$ generates data, $\pi$ is being learned.
- **2 marks** — Define per-step importance ratio and the cumulative product:
$$
\rho_{t:h} = \prod_{k=t}^{\min(h,T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$
- **2 marks** — Write the incremental off-policy update:
$$
V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha \rho_{t:t+n-1}\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]
$$
- **2 marks** — Variance discussion: Long $n$ and rare-action behaviour policy make $\rho$ highly variable; product of ratios compounds variance exponentially. Practical fix: keep $n$ small or use Tree-Backup algorithm.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Write the product formula:**
$$
\rho_{t:t+1} = \frac{\pi(A_t \mid S_t)}{\mu(A_t \mid S_t)} \cdot \frac{\pi(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1})}{\mu(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1})}
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Compute the first ratio:**
$$
\frac{0.8}{0.4} = 2.0
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Compute the second ratio:**
$$
\frac{0.6}{0.3} = 2.0
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Multiply:**
$$
\boxed{\,\rho_{t:t+1} = 2.0 \times 2.0 = 4.0\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The target policy is 4× more likely to have taken the observed action sequence than the behaviour policy was. This means the update is amplified by a factor of 4 — high learning signal, but also high variance contribution. With $n = 5$ and similar ratios, $\rho$ could easily exceed 1000, destabilizing the value estimate.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Valuation (Module 2 – Incremental Implementation):**
>
> 1. **Index confusion in $G_{t:t+n}$** — Students frequently write $G_{t:t+n} = R_t + \gamma R_{t+1} + \dots + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ instead of the correct $R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$. The reward *starts at $t+1$*, not $t$. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Confusing state-value update with action-value update** — Mixing $V(S)$ updates with $Q(S,A)$ updates. The Sarsa target is $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^n Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$, **not** $V$. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the bootstrap term** — Writing $G_{t:t+n}$ as only a sum of rewards without the $\gamma^n V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$ term. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Importance-sampling index range** — Off-policy n-step TD uses $\rho_{t:t+n-1}$ (from $k = t$ to $k = t+n-1$); off-policy n-step Sarsa uses $\rho_{t+1:t+n-1}$ (from $k = t+1$ to $k = t+n-1$). Confusing these is a 2-mark error.
>
> 5. **Missing boundary case** — At terminal time $T$, $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ (full Monte-Carlo return). Examiners *will* test this. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Core idea:** Incremental implementation = *running mean* of a stream of returns, updated after every observation with one arithmetic step.
- [x] **Universal update template:** $X_{\text{new}} = X_{\text{old}} + \alpha \cdot [\,Y - X_{\text{old}}\,]$
- [x] **Sample mean vs. EWMA:** $\alpha = 1/n$ → exact mean; constant $\alpha$ → exponentially weighted moving average.
- [x] **n-step return definition:** $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$.
- [x] **Boundary:** $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ when $t + n \geq T$.
- [x] **n-step TD update:** $V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t)\bigr]$.
- [x] **n-step Sarsa update:** $Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) = Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t)\bigr]$.
- [x] **n-step Expected Sarsa:** target uses $\sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+n}) Q(S_{t+n}, a)$ instead of sampled $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$.
- [x] **Importance sampling ratio:** $\rho_{t:h} = \prod_{k=t}^{\min(h, T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$.
- [x] **Off-policy n-step TD update:** includes $\alpha \cdot \rho_{t:t+n-1}$ multiplier on the error.
- [x] **Tree-Backup algorithm:** avoids importance sampling by using expectation over all *unsampled* actions.
- [x] **Q(σ) algorithm:** unifies sampling and expectation via parameter $\sigma \in [0, 1]$; $\sigma = 1$ gives n-step Sarsa, $\sigma = 0$ gives Tree-Backup.
- [x] **Convergence:** Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_k$.
- [x] **Robotic use cases:** real-time locomotion, off-policy learning from human demonstrations, non-stationary crowd navigation.
- [x] **Computational cost:** O(1) memory per state, O(1) per-step time, ideal for embedded robot controllers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TD Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TD Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal-Difference (TD) Prediction** is a model-free, online method for estimating the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ of a given policy $\pi$ in a Markov Decision Process (MDP). It learns the value function directly from raw experience by **bootstrapping** — i.e., updating estimates of future returns from estimates of future states rather than waiting for a terminal reward.

Formally, for a policy $\pi$, TD Prediction seeks to approximate the expected return:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor** and the expectation is taken over the stochastic transitions induced by $\pi$ and the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERAT747 Module 3):** TD Prediction sits at the intersection of Monte Carlo (MC) and Dynamic Programming (DP). Like MC, it learns from sampled trajectories (no model required); like DP, it bootstraps its own value estimates to update before the episode ends.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a cricket coach predicting a batsman's eventual score while the innings is still in progress.
>
> - **Monte Carlo (MC) approach:** Wait until the player is out, then write down the *exact* total. Average over many innings.
> - **Dynamic Programming (DP) approach:** You would need to know every possible shot and its probability — i.e., a full model of the game.
> - **TD Prediction approach:** After every ball, you say: *"My current guess for the final score was 60. He just hit a 4 and his next predicted average is 64, so let me adjust my guess slightly upward."*
>
> This "adjust after every step using the next prediction" mechanism is the essence of TD Prediction. It corrects your running estimate **online, after every transition**, without needing a model or waiting for the terminal outcome.

## 1.3 Why TD Prediction Matters in Robotics

For a robotic agent, full-episode returns are often impractical:

- Episodes may be very long (navigation, manipulation).
- The robot may operate **continuously** with no natural terminal state.
- A model of the environment is rarely available.

TD Prediction addresses all three by producing **incremental, bootstrapped, model-free** value estimates — making it the workhorse algorithm behind modern robot policy evaluation.

## 1.4 Key Quantities and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V(s)$ | Current estimate of the state value |
| $R_{t+1}$ | Immediate reward received after leaving $S_t$ |
| $\gamma$ | **Discount factor**, $0 \le \gamma < 1$ |
| $\alpha$ | **Step-size / learning rate**, typically small (e.g., 0.1) |
| $\delta_t$ | **TD error** at time $t$ (see §2.3) |
| $G_t$ | Actual return observed from $S_t$ onwards |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture — What TD Prediction Actually Does

At every timestep $t$, the agent:

1. Observes state $S_t$, takes action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$.
2. Receives reward $R_{t+1}$ and transitions to $S_{t+1}$.
3. Forms a one-step **TD target**: $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$.
4. Updates $V(S_t)$ toward this target with step-size $\alpha$:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \big]$$

The bracketed term is the **TD error** $\delta_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bootstrapping** means the update of $V(S_t)$ depends on the existing estimate $V(S_{t+1})$. The agent "pulls itself up by its own bootstraps."

## 2.2 Why TD Prediction is the Bridge Between MC and DP

| Property | Monte Carlo | Dynamic Programming | **TD Prediction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Needs model of environment? | No | Yes | **No** |
| Bootstraps? | No | Yes | **Yes** |
| Updates online during episode? | No (terminal only) | N/A (sweep-based) | **Yes (every step)** |
| Unbiased? | Yes | Yes | **Biased (init-dependent)** |
| Low variance? | No (high) | Yes (low) | **Low** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\, \delta_t$ | Generic TD(0) value update |
| 2 | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | **One-step TD error** |
| 3 | $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ | **n-step return** |
| 4 | $\delta_t^{(n)} = G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t)$ | n-step TD error |
| 5 | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$ | **$\lambda$-return** (geometric blend) |
| 6 | $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda\, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | **Eligibility trace** update (accumulating trace) |
| 7 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\, \delta_t\, E_t(s)$ | TD($\lambda$) tabular update |
| 8 | Robbins–Monro: $\sum_{t=1}^\infty \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ | **Convergence condition** on step-size |
| 9 | $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid S_t = s] = V_\pi(s) - V(s)$ | TD error is an unbiased estimate of the **Bellman error** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** Always denote the TD target (the term without $V(S_t)$) and the TD error distinctly. Mixing them up is the #1 cause of lost marks on TD questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **robotics**, TD Prediction underlies:

- **Policy evaluation** before deploying a learned policy on a real robot (sample-efficient).
- **Critic** networks in Actor–Critic algorithms for continuous control (e.g., robotic arm reaching).
- **Value baseline** subtraction to reduce gradient variance in policy-gradient robot learners.
- **Model-free navigation** in SLAM-augmented mobile robots where full transition kernels are unavailable.

## 2.5 Tabular TD(0) for Estimating $V_\pi$ — Algorithm Outline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical algorithm students must memorize for KTU ESE.

**Input:** policy $\pi$ to be evaluated, step-size $\alpha \in (0,1]$, discount $\gamma$.

**Initialize:** $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$; $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$.

**Repeat for each episode:**

1. Initialize $S_0$.
2. **Loop for each step of episode:**
   - $A \leftarrow$ action given by $\pi$ for $S$.
   - Take action $A$; observe $R$, $S'$.
   - $V(S) \leftarrow V(S) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma V(S') - V(S) \big]$.
   - $S \leftarrow S'$.
   - **Until** $S$ is terminal.

**Output:** $V \approx V_\pi$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TD(0) Update from the Bellman Equation

We start from the Bellman equation for $V_\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\big]$$

Rewrite the inner sum as an expectation under the policy:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\big|\, S_t = s\big]$$

We don't know $V_\pi$ — only the current estimate $V$. We treat the sampled quantity as a noisy target and apply **stochastic gradient descent** on the squared error $\big(V_\pi(s) - V(s)\big)^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V(s) &\propto \frac{\partial}{\partial V(s)} \big(V_\pi(s) - V(s)\big)^2 \Big|_{s = S_t} \\
&= -2\big(V_\pi(S_t) - V(S_t)\big)\cdot 1
\end{aligned}$$

Replace $V_\pi(S_t)$ with the **sample target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V(S_t) &= \alpha \big( R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \big) \\
&= \alpha\, \delta_t
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the update rule:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\, \delta_t$$

**Interpretation of each symbol:**

- $R_{t+1}$ — sampled reward (Monte-Carlo-like, observed).
- $\gamma V(S_{t+1})$ — bootstrapped estimate of the future (DP-like).
- $V(S_t)$ — current value to be corrected.

## 3.2 Derivation of the n-step TD Return

For TD(0), the lookahead is **one step**. In general, after $n$ actual rewards and then a bootstrap:

$$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

Compact form:

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

The corresponding **n-step TD update** is:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) \big]$$

**Special cases:**

- $n = 1$: TD(0) — $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+n})$.
- $n = \infty$: Monte Carlo — $G_t^{(\infty)} = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the $\lambda$-Return

To unify all $n$-step returns, weight them geometrically:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$$

where:

- $\lambda \in [0,1]$ is the **trace-decay parameter**.
- The factor $(1-\lambda)$ normalizes the weights so they sum to 1:

$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = 1$$

**Boundary behavior:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda = 0 &: G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)} \quad \text{(one-step TD)} \\
\lambda = 1 &: G_t^\lambda = G_t \quad \text{(full Monte Carlo)} \\
0 < \lambda < 1 &: \text{geometric blend (TD($\lambda$))}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Eligibility-Trace Mechanism

Direct use of $G_t^\lambda$ requires waiting until end of episode (offline). The **forward view** of TD($\lambda$) can be converted to an **equivalent backward view** using eligibility traces $E_t(s)$.

For each state $s$, define the eligibility trace:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda\, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

The **backward TD($\lambda$) update** is then:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\, \delta_t\, E_t(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

**Interpretation:**

- $\mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ is the indicator that state $s$ was just visited (eligibility = 1).
- $\gamma \lambda$ decays the eligibility by $\gamma$ (discount) and $\lambda$ (trace-decay) each step.
- States visited more recently and more frequently are updated more strongly.

## 3.5 Convergence Properties — Analytical Table

| Algorithm | Convergence? | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **TD(0)** tabular | Converges to $V_\pi$ | $\alpha_t$ satisfies Robbins–Monro; visits all states infinitely often |
| **Batch TD(0)** | Converges to **maximum-likelihood** model of MDP (deterministic: certainty-equivalence) | Standard batch regression |
| **MC (every-visit)** | Converges to $V_\pi$ | Same as TD(0), no bootstrapping |
| **Batch MC vs Batch TD** | MC converges to min MSE on training returns; TD converges to **max-likelihood MDP** — different answers! | Determinism equivalence holds |
| **TD($\lambda$) tabular** | Converges to $V_\pi$ | Same as TD(0) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup (Box-moving robot):** A warehouse robot starts at state $S$. The value estimate is $V(S) = 5$. It moves, receives $R = 2$, lands in $S'$ with $V(S') = 8$. Take $\alpha = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.9$. Apply TD(0).

**Step 1 — TD target:**

$$\text{Target} = R + \gamma V(S') = 2 + 0.9 \times 8 = 2 + 7.2 = 9.2$$

**Step 2 — TD error:**

$$\delta = \text{Target} - V(S) = 9.2 - 5 = 4.2$$

**Step 3 — Update:**

$$V(S) \leftarrow V(S) + \alpha \delta = 5 + 0.1 \times 4.2 = 5 + 0.42 = 5.42$$

The value of $S$ is updated from **5 → 5.42**, moving 10 % of the way toward the better-informed target.

## 3.7 Comparison Matrix — MC vs TD vs DP (High-Yield for Exams)

| Property | MC | TD | DP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model-free | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Bootstraps | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Online update | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ (full sweep) |
| Bias from init | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Variance of update | High | Low | Zero |
| Convergence speed | Slow (episodic) | Fast | Fastest |
| Suitable for continuing tasks | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backup Diagram — Tabular TD(0)

The KTU-mandated visualization. The **root** is the state being updated, the **branches** are sampled transitions, and the **leaves** are samples (reward + next state value).

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootState["State S sub t - Value being updated"]
    actionNode["Action A sub t sampled from policy"]
    rewardNode["Reward R sub t plus 1 observed"]
    nextState["Next state S sub t plus 1"]
    nextValue["Bootstrapped value V of S sub t plus 1"]
    updateNode["TD Target = R plus gamma times V of S sub t plus 1"]
    finalUpdate["Update V of S sub t toward TD Target with step alpha"]

    rootState --> actionNode
    actionNode --> rewardNode
    actionNode --> nextState
    nextState --> nextValue
    rewardNode --> updateNode
    nextValue --> updateNode
    updateNode --> finalUpdate
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for the exam:** The agent observes $S_t$, picks $A_t \sim \pi$, receives $R_{t+1}$, lands in $S_{t+1}$, and uses $V(S_{t+1})$ to **bootstrap** a target. Solid arrows = real transitions; the bootstrapped branch feeds back into the update.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TD(λ) Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    envBlock["Environment / Robot MDP"]
    policyBlock["Policy Pi under evaluation"]
    traceBlock["Eligibility Trace Buffer E sub t of s"]
    valueBlock["Value Table V of s"]
    errorBlock["TD Error Calculator delta sub t"]
    updaterBlock["Stochastic Update V of s plus alpha delta E"]
    logBlock["Logged Trajectory tau"]

    envBlock --> rewardNode["Reward R sub t plus 1 and Next State"]
    rewardNode --> errorBlock
    policyBlock --> envBlock
    errorBlock --> updaterBlock
    valueBlock --> errorBlock
    traceBlock --> updaterBlock
    updaterBlock --> valueBlock
    traceBlock --> traceDecay["Decay by gamma lambda and add new indicator"]
    logBlock --> policyBlock
```

**Reading guide:** The trace buffer decays every step by $\gamma\lambda$ and gets a +1 for the just-visited state. The TD error is multiplied by the trace and applied to all states.

## 4.3 MC vs TD Backup Diagram Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MCsub["Monte Carlo Backup - sample full return"]
        mcRoot["S sub t"]
        mcBranch1["R sub t plus 1"]
        mcBranch2["R sub t plus 2"]
        mcBranch3["R sub t plus 3"]
        mcBranchD["... continues to terminal"]
        mcRoot --> mcBranch1 --> mcBranch2 --> mcBranch3 --> mcBranchD
    end

    subgraph TDsub["TD Zero Backup - one step then bootstrap"]
        tdRoot["S sub t"]
        tdReward["R sub t plus 1 observed"]
        tdNext["S sub t plus 1 bootstrapped estimate V"]
        tdRoot --> tdReward
        tdRoot --> tdNext
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often ask students to draw **both backup diagrams** and explain the difference. MC backup traces the entire sampled trajectory to termination; TD(0) takes one real reward and then "docks" at the next state's current estimate.

## 4.4 n-step Backup — Generalized View

```mermaid
graph LR
    ns0["S sub t"]
    ns1["R sub t plus 1 - real"]
    ns2["R sub t plus 2 - real"]
    ns3["R sub t plus 3 - real"]
    ns4["S sub t plus n - bootstrapped V"]
    ns0 --> ns1 --> ns2 --> ns3 --> ns4
```

**Interpretation:** $n$ real rewards followed by a single bootstrapped value $V(S_{t+n})$. As $n$ varies from $1$ to $\infty$, we slide between TD(0) and Monte Carlo.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Define **Temporal Difference (TD) learning**. State the update rule for **TD(0)** and explain the role of the **discount factor** $\gamma$ and the **step-size** $\alpha$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** TD learning is a model-free, online prediction method that estimates the value function $V_\pi(s)$ by bootstrapping from successive states, without waiting for the terminal outcome of an episode.
- **Update rule (1 mark):**

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \big]$$

- **Role of $\gamma$ and $\alpha$ (1 mark):** $\gamma \in [0,1)$ weights the importance of future rewards; smaller $\gamma$ makes the agent myopic. $\alpha$ controls how aggressively the estimate is updated toward the TD target — large $\alpha$ learns fast but may diverge; small $\alpha$ learns slowly but is stable.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> What is the **TD error** $\delta_t$? Why is it central to TD Prediction, and what is its expected value under the true value function $V_\pi$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ is the one-step TD error — the difference between the bootstrapped target and the current estimate.
- **Centrality (1 mark):** It is the *learning signal*; the entire value update is $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$. It also acts as a gradient-like direction toward $V_\pi$.
- **Expected value (1 mark):**

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi[\delta_t \mid S_t = s] = V_\pi(s) - V(s)$$

— the expected TD error equals the **Bellman error**, so on average the update drives $V(s)$ toward $V_\pi(s)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
>
> (a) Derive the **n-step TD return** $G_t^{(n)}$ from first principles. Show how TD(0) and Monte Carlo emerge as special cases. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Define the **$\lambda$-return** $G_t^\lambda$ and prove that it normalizes to unit sum. Explain how eligibility traces implement TD($\lambda$) online. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $n$-step TD return (7 marks):**

Start from the truncated definition:

$$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

Rewrite compactly:

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n}) \qquad (\star)$$

- The first $n$ rewards are **sampled** from experience (Monte-Carlo-like).
- The final $\gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ is a **bootstrap** of the next state's current value (DP-like).

**Special case 1 — TD(0):** Set $n = 1$ in $(\star)$:

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

— exactly the TD(0) target.

**Special case 2 — Monte Carlo:** Let $n \to T - t$ (length of episode), with $V(S_T) = 0$ at terminal:

$$G_t^{(T-t)} = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} = G_t$$

— the full episodic return. Hence MC is $n$-step TD in the limit.

**Update rule (2 marks):**

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\big[ G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) \big]$$

---

**(b) $\lambda$-return and eligibility traces (7 marks):**

**Definition:**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$$

**Normalization proof (3 marks):** Sum the weights over $n$ from 1 to $\infty$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

(geometric series with ratio $\lambda$, first term $1-\lambda$.)

**Special cases (1 mark):**

- $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)}$ (one-step TD).
- $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow G_t^\lambda = G_t$ (Monte Carlo).

**Eligibility-trace implementation (3 marks):** Define for each state $s$:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda\, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

This trace **decays** by $\gamma\lambda$ each step and **increments** by 1 when $s$ is visited. The online (backward) update is:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\, \delta_t\, E_t(s) \quad \forall s$$

This is mathematically equivalent (in expectation) to the forward $G_t^\lambda$ update, but can be done **incrementally at every timestep** — no need to wait for episode termination.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
>
> (a) Compare **Monte Carlo** and **TD Prediction** with respect to bias, variance, model requirement, and online usability. When should each be preferred for a robotics application? **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A mobile robot navigating a warehouse experiences the following trajectory under policy $\pi$. Use TD(0) to update the value estimates. Take $\alpha = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.9$.
>
> | Step $t$ | $S_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $S_{t+1}$ | $V(S_t)$ initial | $V(S_{t+1})$ initial |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | A | 1.0 | B | 2.0 | 4.0 |
> | 2 | B | 2.0 | C | 4.0 | 3.0 |
> | 3 | C | 3.0 | terminal | 3.0 | 0 |
>
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MC vs TD — comparative table (7 marks):**

| Property | Monte Carlo | **TD Prediction** |
|---|---|---|
| Needs model? | No | **No** |
| Bootstraps? | No | **Yes** |
| Biased? | No (unbiased) | **Yes (depends on init)** |
| Variance of estimate? | **High** | **Low** |
| Online / per-step updates? | No (terminal) | **Yes (every step)** |
| Works for continuing tasks? | No | **Yes** |
| Convergence speed | Slower | **Faster** |

**Robotics preference (2 marks):**

- For **short episodic** tasks with rare but clear success signals (e.g., grasping a specific object once and ending), MC may be acceptable.
- For **long, continuing** robot tasks (e.g., lifelong SLAM-based navigation, persistent manipulation, walking), **TD Prediction is strictly preferred** because episodes never end and online updates are essential.
- Modern actor–critic robot policies use TD-based critics for low-variance gradient estimation.

---

**(b) Numerical computation using TD(0) (7 marks):**

**General formula (1 mark):**

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + 0.2 \big[ R_{t+1} + 0.9 \cdot V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \big]$$

**Step 1 — Update $V(A)$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_1 &= R_{t+1} + \gamma V(B) - V(A) \\
&= 1.0 + 0.9 \times 4.0 - 2.0 \\
&= 1.0 + 3.6 - 2.0 = 2.6 \\
V(A) &\leftarrow 2.0 + 0.2 \times 2.6 = 2.0 + 0.52 = \mathbf{2.52}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Update $V(B)$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_2 &= 2.0 + 0.9 \times 3.0 - 4.0 \\
&= 2.0 + 2.7 - 4.0 = 0.7 \\
V(B) &\leftarrow 4.0 + 0.2 \times 0.7 = 4.0 + 0.14 = \mathbf{4.14}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Update $V(C)$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_3 &= 3.0 + 0.9 \times 0 - 3.0 = 0 \\
V(C) &\leftarrow 3.0 + 0.2 \times 0 = \mathbf{3.0} \quad \text{(unchanged)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final values:** $V(A) = 2.52,\ V(B) = 4.14,\ V(C) = 3.0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to bootstrap with $V(S_{t+1})$** (using only $R_{t+1}$) — that's Monte Carlo, not TD. **−3 marks.**
> - **Sign errors in $\delta_t$** — the target is $R + \gamma V(S')$, not $R - \gamma V(S')$. **−2 marks.**
> - **Wrong discount application** — $\gamma$ multiplies $V(S_{t+1})$, not $R_{t+1}$ (in the TD(0) target). **−1 mark.**
> - **Not stating units / bounds** for $\alpha \in (0,1]$ and $\gamma \in [0,1)$ in derivations. **−1 mark.**
> - **Drawing backup diagrams missing the bootstrap branch** — examiners specifically test this. **−2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-remember checklist for KTU PERAT747 Module 3 — TD Prediction**

- ✅ TD Prediction is **model-free**, **online**, and **bootstraps** from value estimates.
- ✅ Canonical update: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$, with $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$.
- ✅ TD(0) is biased (init-dependent) but has **lower variance** than Monte Carlo.
- ✅ MC has zero bias but **high variance** and needs terminal outcomes.
- ✅ n-step return $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ — interpolates between TD(0) ($n=1$) and MC ($n \to \infty$).
- ✅ $\lambda$-return $G_t^\lambda$ is a geometric blend of $n$-step returns with weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.
- ✅ Eligibility trace: $E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda\, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$.
- ✅ Backward TD($\lambda$) update: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s)$.
- ✅ Convergence requires **Robbins–Monro** step-size: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty,\ \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
- ✅ Expected TD error = Bellman error: $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid S_t = s] = V_\pi(s) - V(s)$.
- ✅ Backup diagrams are essential exam visuals — **always draw the bootstrap branch from $S_{t+1}$.**
- ✅ TD Prediction is the foundation for **Actor–Critic** robot control architectures covered later in the syllabus.
- ✅ In batch settings, TD(0) converges to the **maximum-likelihood MDP**, while MC converges to the **minimum-MSE empirical return** — they generally give different answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of TD Prediction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Advantages of TD Prediction Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction Methods** are a class of model-free, bootstrapping algorithms in Reinforcement Learning (RL) used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ for a given policy $\pi$ directly from experience trajectories, without requiring a complete model of the environment's dynamics $P(s',r \mid s,a)$. Formally, TD prediction learns the expected return $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ where $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$, by using the recursive Bellman expectation equation as a learning target. The canonical TD(0) update rule after observing a transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ is: $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$ where the bracketed quantity is the **TD Error** (denoted $\delta_t$), $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the step-size (learning rate), and $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the discount factor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TD methods uniquely combine two properties — **bootstrapping** (updating estimates from other estimates, like Dynamic Programming) and **sampling** (learning from raw experience, like Monte Carlo). This hybrid identity is the heart of every advantage they offer. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are a **delivery robot** learning to estimate how long it takes to reach destination $Z$ from your current location $S$. You have two naive strategies: - **Monte Carlo (MC) approach:** You must *complete the entire delivery* before updating your estimate. If the journey is long, your estimate remains stale for hours. - **Dynamic Programming (DP) approach:** You would need a perfect map showing traffic probabilities at every intersection — information you usually do not have. **TD Prediction** is the elegant middle ground: as soon as you travel *one block* to the next intersection $S'$ and observe the time taken $R$, you *immediately* update your estimate of $S$ using your *current* estimate of $S'$. You are essentially saying: > *"If my guess for the next block is 5 minutes, and this block took 2 minutes, then my guess for the current block should shift towards $2 + \gamma \times 5$."* This is learning **on the fly**, from **partial journeys**, using only what the world tells you — no map required. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology to Memorize:** > - **Bootstrapping:** Updating a guess from another guess (not waiting for the final outcome). > - **Sample Update:** Updating from a single realized transition (not requiring a full distribution). > - **Model-Free:** No prior knowledge of transition probabilities $P$ or reward function $R$ is needed. > - **Online Learning:** Updates happen at every time-step $t$, not at the end of an episode. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Symbol | Meaning | Range / Unit | |---|---|---| | $\alpha$ | Step-size / Learning rate | $(0, 1]$ | | $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $[0, 1]$ | | $\delta_t$ | TD Error at time $t$ | Real-valued scalar | | $R_{t+1}$ | Observed reward | Real-valued scalar | | $V_\pi(s)$ | State-value under policy $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ | Expectation operator | Statistical mean | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** In robotics, where state spaces are continuous and high-dimensional, TD prediction's *model-free* nature is the single most important practical advantage — building an accurate model of a robot's interaction with a chaotic environment is often infeasible. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why TD Prediction Works — The Theoretical Foundation The strength of TD prediction rests on three interlocking mathematical facts: 1. **Convergence under Robbins-Monro conditions:** For any fixed policy $\pi$, TD(0) converges to $V_\pi$ in expectation if $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$. 2. **Bootstrapping reduces variance:** Because TD uses $V(S_{t+1})$ rather than the full stochastic return $G_t$, the variance of the update target is dramatically smaller than Monte Carlo. 3. **Convergence rate:** TD(0) achieves a mean-squared error of $O(1/n)$ after $n$ samples — strictly faster than the $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ rate of plain Monte Carlo. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The TD(0) algorithm performs the following logic at every time-step: - **Step 1 — Observe:** Receive the current state $S_t$ from the environment. - **Step 2 — Act:** Follow policy $\pi$ to select action $A_t$ (for prediction, $\pi$ is fixed). - **Step 3 — Transition:** Environment returns reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$. - **Step 4 — Compute Target:** Form the *TD Target* $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. This is an *estimate* of the true return from $S_t$. - **Step 5 — Compute Error:** Calculate $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. - **Step 6 — Update:** Adjust $V(S_t)$ in the direction of $\delta_t$ by magnitude $\alpha \cdot \delta_t$. - **Step 7 — Repeat** for the next time-step — **no episode termination required**. > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that Step 4 uses $V(S_{t+1})$ — which is itself an estimate. This is the essence of **bootstrapping**. The algorithm never needs to wait for the episode to terminate; it can update from *any* transition. --- ## 2.3 The Eight Core Advantages of TD Prediction Methods ### Advantage 1 — **Model-Free Operation** TD prediction requires **no prior knowledge** of the transition function $P(s' \mid s,a)$ or the reward function $R(s,a,s')$. This is critical in robotics, where modeling friction, slip, and contact dynamics is prohibitively complex. ### Advantage 2 — **Online, Incremental Learning** Updates occur at every step using only the most recent transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$. Memory requirement is $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$, independent of trajectory length. No need to store entire episodes. ### Advantage 3 — **Suitable for Continuing (Non-Episodic) Tasks** Unlike Monte Carlo methods, which require episodes to terminate before updates can occur, TD methods update **at every step**. They naturally handle continuous control tasks such as robotic locomotion, where the concept of "end of episode" is artificial. ### Advantage 4 — **Bootstrapping Reduces Variance** The TD target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ depends on **one random reward sample** rather than an entire stochastic return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots$. Variance of the update target is therefore lower, leading to faster empirical convergence. ### Advantage 5 — **Convergence Guarantees** For a fixed policy, TD(0) converges to the true value function $V_\pi$ with probability 1 under standard stochastic approximation assumptions. The proof uses the **Contraction Mapping Theorem** applied to the Bellman operator $\mathcal{T}^\pi$. ### Advantage 6 — **Computational Efficiency per Step** The per-step complexity is $O(1)$ — a single lookup and arithmetic update. MC methods require $O(T)$ per update (waiting for the full return), and DP requires a full **sweep** over the state space. ### Advantage 7 — **Faster Convergence Than Pure Monte Carlo** In practice, TD methods often converge in substantially fewer episodes than MC because they can extract signal from every transition rather than waiting for terminal outcomes. ### Advantage 8 — **Natural Foundation for Control** TD prediction extends naturally to **TD control** (SARSA, Q-Learning, Expected SARSA) by replacing the state-value function with an action-value function $Q(s,a)$. The same bootstrapping machinery enables the entire family of modern model-free RL algorithms. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Note:** For absolute-value bars in the formulas below, the LaTeX command `\vert` is used instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | F1 | TD(0) Update Rule | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$ | Core incremental update | | F2 | TD Error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | One-step Bellman residual | | F3 | TD Target | $\bar{V}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | Estimate of return $G_t$ | | F4 | Return Definition | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted cumulative reward | | F5 | Bellman Expectation | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\right]$ | Fixed-point equation | | F6 | Mean-Squared TD Error (MSPBE) | $\mathrm{MSPBE}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \delta_t^2 \right]$ | Convergence objective | | F7 | Convergence Rate Bound | $\mathbb{E}\left[ \vert V_t(s) - V_\pi(s) \vert^2 \right] = O(1/n)$ | TD error decay rate | | F8 | Monte Carlo Variance Comparison | $\mathrm{Var}(G_t) \gg \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}))$ | Variance reduction property | | F9 | Robbins-Monro Condition | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ AND $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ | Convergence guarantee | | F10 | Eligibility Trace (TD($\lambda$)) | $e_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Credit assignment extension | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Robotic Locomotion:** Continuous control of legged robots (e.g., quadrupeds) — TD-based value estimation allows online policy improvement without episode resets. - **Autonomous Navigation:** Self-driving cars update value estimates of traffic states at every frame; MC would require knowing the eventual outcome of every lane change. - **Industrial Manipulation:** Robot arms learn grasp-success value estimates in real-time from raw sensorimotor experience, bypassing the need to model contact physics. - **Drone Swarm Coordination:** Each agent bootstraps local value estimates without requiring a global communication model. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of TD(0) Fixed-Point to Bellman Expectation We prove that if TD(0) converges to a fixed point $V(s)$, that fixed point satisfies the Bellman expectation equation. **Step 1 — Define the TD update as an expectation.** At convergence in expectation, the expected update to $V(S_t)$ must equal zero. Therefore: $$\mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \mid S_t = s \right] = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the conditional expectation.** Under policy $\pi$, the next state and reward are sampled from the environment dynamics $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ with action selected via $\pi(a \mid s)$: $$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') - V(s) \right] = 0$$ **Step 3 — Separate the deterministic term $V(s)$.** The expectation of $V(s)$ conditioned on $S_t = s$ is simply $V(s)$: $$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right] - V(s) \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Recognize the marginal probabilities sum to 1.** $$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) = 1$$ So the second term simplifies to $-V(s)$. **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain Bellman expectation.** $$V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right]$$ This is precisely the **Bellman expectation equation for $V_\pi$**. Therefore, the fixed point of TD(0) is $V_\pi$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Variance Comparison Between TD and MC Updates We show that the variance of the TD update target is bounded above by the variance of the full Monte Carlo return. **Step 1 — Define MC target.** The Monte Carlo target for $V(s)$ is the full sample return: $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots$$ **Step 2 — Express $G_t$ using the recursive decomposition.** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$ **Step 3 — Compute variance of $G_t$.** Applying variance to the recursive form: $$\mathrm{Var}(G_t) = \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+1}) + \gamma^2 \mathrm{Var}(G_{t+1}) + 2\gamma \mathrm{Cov}(R_{t+1}, G_{t+1})$$ For non-zero $\gamma$, this can grow without bound across time, but more importantly, it accumulates all future stochastic noise. **Step 4 — Compute variance of the TD target.** The TD target is: $$\bar{V}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$ Here $V(S_{t+1})$ is treated as a *deterministic* function of $S_{t+1}$ at update time: $$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{V}_t \mid S_t = s) = \mathrm{Var}\left( R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right)$$ **Step 5 — Apply conditional variance decomposition.** $$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{V}_t) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathrm{Var}(\bar{V}_t \mid S_{t+1}) \right] + \mathrm{Var}\left( \mathbb{E}[\bar{V}_t \mid S_{t+1}] \right)$$ Since $V(S_{t+1})$ is a function of $S_{t+1}$: $$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{V}_t) = \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+1}) + \gamma^2 \, \mathrm{Var}(V(S_{t+1}))$$ **Step 6 — Compare to MC variance.** The MC target accumulates reward noise across *every* future step: $$\mathrm{Var}(G_t) = \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+1}) + \gamma^2 \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+2}) + \gamma^4 \mathrm{Var}(R_{t+3}) + \dots$$ Therefore, in general: $$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{V}_t) \leq \mathrm{Var}(G_t)$$ with strict inequality unless all reward variance is concentrated in a single future step. This confirms **Advantage 4** quantitatively. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Convergence Rate of TD(0) — The $O(1/n)$ Bound We sketch the bound on the mean-squared error after $n$ samples. **Step 1 — Define the error process.** Let $e_t(s) = V_t(s) - V_\pi(s)$ be the estimation error for state $s$ at time $t$. The TD update can be written in error form as: $$e_{t+1}(S_t) = e_t(S_t) + \alpha \left[ \mathcal{T}^\pi V_t(S_t) - V_t(S_t) \right] = (1 - \alpha) e_t(S_t) + \alpha \mathcal{T}^\pi e_t(S_t)$$ where $\mathcal{T}^\pi$ is the Bellman expectation operator, which is a $\gamma$-contraction in the sup-norm: $$\| \mathcal{T}^\pi V - \mathcal{T}^\pi U \|_\infty \leq \gamma \| V - U \|_\infty$$ **Step 2 — Take expectations under the stationary distribution.** For a fixed step-size $\alpha$, the expected squared error decays geometrically: $$\mathbb{E}[e_t^2] \leq (1 - \alpha(1-\gamma))^2 \mathbb{E}[e_{t-1}^2] + \alpha^2 \sigma^2$$ where $\sigma^2$ bounds the variance of the TD target. **Step 3 — Solve the geometric recursion.** $$\mathbb{E}[e_t^2] \leq (1 - \alpha(1-\gamma))^{2t} \mathbb{E}[e_0^2] + \frac{\alpha \sigma^2}{2(1-\gamma)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute sample count.** After $n$ effective updates per state (with $\alpha \propto 1/n$): $$\mathbb{E}\left[ \vert V_t(s) - V_\pi(s) \vert^2 \right] = O(1/n)$$ This is the key rate that **outperforms** plain Monte Carlo's $O(1/\sqrt{n})$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TD vs. MC vs. DP | Criterion | **TD Prediction** | **Monte Carlo** | **Dynamic Programming** | |---|---|---|---| | Needs model of environment? | **No** | No | **Yes** | | Learns from experience? | **Yes** | Yes | No (uses synthetic model) | | Bootstraps? | **Yes** | No | Yes | | Updates before episode end? | **Yes (online)** | No | Yes (full sweep) | | Handles continuing tasks? | **Yes** | No | Yes | | Update variance | **Low** | High | Zero (deterministic) | | Bias at finite samples | **Yes** (bootstrap) | Zero (unbiased) | Yes (discretization) | | Convergence rate (theoretical) | $O(1/n)$ | $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ | — | | Computational cost per update | $O(1)$ | $O(T)$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Insight:** TD prediction is the **unique algorithm family** that is simultaneously model-free, online, bootstrapping, and unbiased at convergence. Every other method trades off at least one of these properties. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a TD(0) Prediction Step The following Mermaid diagram captures the data flow within a single TD(0) update cycle. This visualizes how the *current estimate*, the *observed transition*, and the *next estimate* are combined to produce the updated value function. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Environment at time t"] --> nodeB["Observed State S_t"] nodeB --> nodeC["Policy pi selects action A_t"] nodeC --> nodeD["Action A_t applied to environment"] nodeD --> nodeE["Receive Reward R_t+1"] nodeD --> nodeF["Transition to State S_t+1"] nodeF --> nodeG["Lookup current V(S_t+1)"] nodeB --> nodeH["Lookup current V(S_t)"] G --> nodeI["TD Target = R_t+1 + gamma times V(S_t+1)"] H --> nodeI I --> nodeJ["TD Error delta_t = Target minus V(S_t)"] H --> nodeJ J --> nodeK["Updated V(S_t) = V(S_t) + alpha times delta_t"] K --> nodeL["Value Function Table V"] L --> nodeG L --> nodeH ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The loop `nodeL → nodeG → nodeI → nodeJ → nodeK → nodeL` represents the **bootstrapping cycle** — the value table is consulted both as input and as the storage being updated. - The transition `nodeD → nodeF` represents **experience sampling** — the model-free property is captured here. - The arrow `nodeK → nodeL` represents the **incremental update** — characteristic of online learning. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TD Prediction in a Robotic System When TD prediction is embedded in a robotic learning loop (e.g., on a quadruped learning gait quality values), the architecture decomposes into four interacting modules. ```mermaid flowchart LR blockA["Module A: Sensor Stack
IMU Joint Encoders Cameras"] blockB["Module B: State Estimator
Constructs S_t from raw sensors"] blockC["Module C: Policy pi
Maps S_t to action A_t"] blockD["Module D: Robot Body
Executes A_t in the world"] blockE["Module E: Reward Computer
Computes R_t+1 from outcome"] blockF["Module F: TD Predictor
Updates V(S_t) using R_t+1 and V(S_t+1)"] blockG["Module G: Value Table
Stores V over discretized states"] blockA --> blockB blockB --> blockC blockC --> blockD blockD --> blockE blockD --> blockA blockE --> blockF blockB --> blockF blockG --> blockF blockF --> blockG ``` **Architectural Commentary:** - **Module F (TD Predictor)** is the only component requiring a learning rate $\alpha$ and discount $\gamma$. This isolation makes it easy to swap prediction algorithms (TD(0), TD($\lambda$), MC) without altering the rest of the robotic stack. - **Module G (Value Table)** is consulted by Module F on every step.
