For high-dimensional continuous state spaces in robotics, this module is typically replaced by a function approximator (neural network), giving rise to **Deep TD methods** such as DQN. - The **bidirectional arrow** between Modules F and G captures the essence of *bootstrapping*: the predictor reads from the table and writes back to it in the same step. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List any four advantages of TD prediction methods over Monte Carlo methods.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Model-free operation** — TD methods do not require knowledge of the transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s,a)$, whereas DP requires them (MC is also model-free but this is shared, so it is not counted as an advantage *over MC*; instead we list the differentiating advantages). 2. **Online learning** — TD updates occur after every transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ without waiting for episode termination; MC must wait for $G_t$ to be realized. 3. **Lower update variance** — TD target uses only one sampled reward $R_{t+1}$ plus a deterministic estimate $V(S_{t+1})$; MC target accumulates stochastic noise from all future rewards. 4. **Suitable for continuing tasks** — TD works naturally for non-episodic problems; MC requires episodic structure. 5. *(Bonus)* Faster empirical convergence due to bootstrapping. *(4 marks awarded for 4 correctly explained points. Each point must include the principle, not just a label.)* --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define the TD error $\delta_t$ and explain what it represents intuitively.** **Model Answer:** The TD error at time $t$ is defined as: $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ **Intuitive meaning:** $\delta_t$ is the *one-step temporal discrepancy* between the predicted value $V(S_t)$ and the better-informed estimate $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ obtained after observing one real transition. If $\delta_t > 0$, the previous estimate was an *under-estimate*; if $\delta_t < 0$, it was an *over-estimate*. TD(0) adjusts $V(S_t)$ in the direction of this error by magnitude $\alpha \cdot \delta_t$, so over many iterations the values approach $V_\pi$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an internal choice between two alternatives. Each sub-question is typically worth 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the TD(0) prediction algorithm with its update rule. Discuss the property of bootstrapping and why it is useful. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare TD prediction with Monte Carlo prediction along the dimensions of: (i) bias, (ii) variance, (iii) convergence assumptions, and (iv) suitability for continuing tasks. Use mathematical expressions to support your answer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) **Step 1 — State the algorithm.** TD(0) prediction estimates $V_\pi(s)$ for a fixed policy $\pi$ using the iterative update: $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$ **Step 2 — Describe the operational loop.** For each step $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$: - Observe $S_t$. - Take action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$. - Observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$. - Compute $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. - Update $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$. **Step 3 — Define bootstrapping.** Bootstrapping refers to updating an estimate of a quantity using *other estimates of the same quantity*, rather than waiting for a final observed value. In TD(0), $V(S_t)$ is updated using $V(S_{t+1})$ — an existing estimate of the value of the next state. **Step 4 — Justify usefulness.** - **Computational:** No need to wait for terminal outcomes. - **Statistical:** Lower-variance update targets due to fewer stochastic terms. - **Structural:** Allows learning in continuing (non-episodic) tasks. - **Theoretical:** Convergence to $V_\pi$ is guaranteed under Robbins-Monro conditions. #### Model Solution for Part (b) **(i) Bias:** MC targets $G_t$ are *unbiased* estimators of $V_\pi(S_t)$: $$\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s] = V_\pi(s)$$ TD targets $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ are *biased* because $V(S_{t+1})$ is an estimate, not the true $V_\pi(S_{t+1})$: $$\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s] \neq V_\pi(s) \quad \text{(at finite } t\text{)}$$ However, the bias vanishes at convergence. **(ii) Variance:** From our earlier derivation: $$\mathrm{Var}(R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})) \leq \mathrm{Var}(G_t)$$ TD has strictly lower variance because only $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$ are random in the target. **(iii) Convergence assumptions:** - MC: requires only that $\alpha_t$ satisfies Robbins-Monro and episodes terminate. - TD(0): additionally requires that the Markov chain induced by $\pi$ visits every state infinitely often in the limit, and that $\gamma < 1$ for the contraction property. **(iv) Continuing tasks:** - MC: **inapplicable** to non-episodic continuing tasks since $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ may be infinite or have unbounded variance. - TD: **naturally applicable** because the one-step update never requires episode termination. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** State and prove that the fixed point of TD(0) satisfies the Bellman expectation equation for the on-policy value function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robot learns to navigate a 3-state Markov chain with state space $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, discount $\gamma = 0.9$, and learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. Starting with $V(s_1) = 0$, $V(s_2) = 0$, $V(s_3) = 0$, the robot experiences the following sequence of transitions under a fixed policy: $$(s_1, r = +1, s_2), \quad (s_2, r = 0, s_3), \quad (s_3, r = +2, s_1)$$ Compute $V(s_1)$, $V(s_2)$, and $V(s_3)$ after processing this single trajectory using TD(0). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) **Statement:** If TD(0) converges to a value function $V$, then $V$ must satisfy: $$V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right] \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ **Proof:** At convergence, the expected change to $V(S_t)$ per update is zero: $$\mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \mid S_t = s \right] = 0$$ Expanding the conditional expectation: $$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') - V(s) \right] = 0$$ Since $V(s)$ is constant with respect to the inner sums: $$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right] = V(s) \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)$$ The right-hand side simplifies because the inner probabilities sum to 1: $$V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right]$$ This is exactly the Bellman expectation equation. Hence the fixed point of TD(0) is $V_\pi$. $\blacksquare$ #### Model Solution for Part (b) **Setup:** Given $\alpha = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.9$, initial values $V(s_1) = V(s_2) = V(s_3) = 0$. **Transition 1:** $(s_1, r = +1, s_2)$ - Current: $V(s_1) = 0$ - TD target: $R + \gamma V(s_2) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0 = 1$ - TD error: $\delta = 1 - 0 = 1$ - Updated $V(s_1) = 0 + 0.1 \times 1 = 0.1$ **Transition 2:** $(s_2, r = 0, s_3)$ - Current: $V(s_2) = 0$ - TD target: $R + \gamma V(s_3) = 0 + 0.9 \times 0 = 0$ - TD error: $\delta = 0 - 0 = 0$ - Updated $V(s_2) = 0 + 0.1 \times 0 = 0$ **Transition 3:** $(s_3, r = +2, s_1)$ - Current: $V(s_3) = 0$ - TD target: $R + \gamma V(s_1) = 2 + 0.9 \times 0.1 = 2 + 0.09 = 2.09$ - TD error: $\delta = 2.09 - 0 = 2.09$ - Updated $V(s_3) = 0 + 0.1 \times 2.09 = 0.209$ **Final values after one trajectory:** $$\boxed{V(s_1) = 0.100, \quad V(s_2) = 0.000, \quad V(s_3) = 0.209}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Order of operations is critical.** Students often compute all TD targets using *initial* values instead of the *updated* values. The TD target for Transition 3 must use $V(s_1) = 0.1$ (already updated), not $V(s_1) = 0$. Failing this loses 2 marks. > 2. **Don't confuse reward positions.** Reward $R_{t+1}$ is observed *after* taking action at state $S_t$. Always pair $S_t \rightarrow R_{t+1} \rightarrow S_{t+1}$. > 3. **Always quote the formula before substitution.** Examiners award marks for the *method* as well as the numeric answer. Writing the update rule once before substitution secures 1 mark even if arithmetic slips occur. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Advantages of TD Prediction Methods** - ✅ **TD prediction is the unique hybrid of DP (bootstrapping) and MC (sampling).** - ✅ The update rule is $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ with $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. - ✅ **Model-free:** no $P(s',r \mid s,a)$ required. - ✅ **Online:** updates at every step, no episode termination needed. - ✅ **Low variance:** one-step target vs. full stochastic return. - ✅ **Convergence guarantee:** to $V_\pi$ under Robbins-Monro conditions. - ✅ **Convergence rate:** $O(1/n)$ mean-squared error — better than MC's $O(1/\sqrt{n})$. - ✅ **Handles continuing tasks:** unlike MC, no episodic assumption. - ✅ **Computational efficiency:** $O(1)$ per update. - ✅ **Foundation for control:** extends directly to SARSA, Q-Learning, Expected SARSA. - ✅ The **fixed point** of TD(0) satisfies the **Bellman expectation equation** (this is the proof you must memorize for the 7-mark derivation question). - ✅ **Bias-variance trade-off:** TD is biased at finite time but has lower variance; MC is unbiased but higher variance. - ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Confusing $R_{t+1}$ (reward *after* $A_t$) with $R_t$ (reward *before* $A_t$). - ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Using stale value-table entries instead of *just-updated* ones during within-trajectory computation. - ✅ The TD error $\delta_t$ is the **fundamental learning signal** of all temporal-difference methods — every advantage ultimately stems from how it reduces variance while remaining unbiased at convergence.

topics.title: Optimality of TD(0)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimality of TD(0)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**TD(0) (Temporal Difference learning with parameter n = 0)** is a *one-step bootstrap* model-free value-estimation algorithm used in Reinforcement Learning (RL) to learn the state-value function $V^\pi(s)$ of a Markov Decision Process (MDP) under a fixed policy $\pi$.

The update rule applied at each transition $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ is:

$$V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) + \alpha_t \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$

The bracketed quantity is called the **TD-error**, denoted $\delta_t$:

$$\delta_t \;\equiv\; R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality of TD(0)** refers to the formal proof that the iterates $V_t$ generated by the TD(0) update converge to the *true* value function $V^\pi$ (the unique fixed point of the Bellman operator), provided the standard Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation conditions hold.

### 1.2 Intuition — The Weather Forecast Analogy

Imagine you are a daily weather forecaster in Kerala trying to predict the *evening temperature* given the *morning temperature*. You do not have a physical climate model, only yesterday's observations.

- **Monte-Carlo (MC) method:** Wait until evening, observe the actual temperature, then adjust your morning prediction by the *full error* between predicted and observed. Slow, but unbiased (requires the day's full rollout).
- **TD(0) method:** Around noon, you look at the temperature then and slightly nudge your morning prediction toward this *intermediate, partially-correct* estimate. You bootstrap from your own current belief rather than waiting for ground truth.

This **mid-day nudging** is exactly what TD(0) does: each transition, it pulls the current value estimate $V(S_t)$ partway toward a **bootstrapped target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. The fundamental question of *optimality* is: does this repeated "nudging" eventually settle at the true value function $V^\pi$? The answer, proven rigorously, is **yes** — under mild regularity conditions, TD(0) finds the *globally optimal* (minimum mean-squared-error) estimate of $V^\pi$ *asymptotically*, despite being a fully online, incremental, model-free method.

> [!NOTE]
> In robotics, this is the workhorse algorithm for **value-function approximation** in tasks such as legged-locomotion reward shaping, manipulator reaching, and drone hover-control, where full Monte-Carlo rollouts are infeasible or unsafe.

### 1.3 Physical / Numerical Constants Highlighted

- **Discount factor:** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ (typical: $\gamma = 0.99$).
- **Step-size sequence:** $\alpha_t$ with $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ (e.g., $\alpha_t = 1/t$).
- **Contraction factor:** $\gamma$ in the sup-norm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bellman Operator $T^\pi$

For a fixed policy $\pi$, the **Bellman expectation operator** maps any value function $V$ to a new function $T^\pi V$:

$$(T^\pi V)(s) \;\equiv\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \,\big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

$$= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r + \gamma V(s') \,\bigr]$$

The **fixed point** of $T^\pi$ is the true value function $V^\pi$, defined by $V^\pi = T^\pi V^\pi$.

### 2.2 The TD(0) Update as a Stochastic Operator

Define the *empirical* one-step Bellman operator $\hat{T}^\pi_t$ acting on the current sample transition:

$$(\hat{T}^\pi_t V)(s) \;\equiv\; \mathbb{1}\{S_t = s\}\bigl[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})\,\bigr] + \mathbb{1}\{S_t \neq s\}\,V(s)$$

Then the TD(0) update is equivalent to:

$$V_{t+1}(s) \;=\; V_t(s) + \alpha_t(s)\bigl[\,(\hat{T}^\pi_t V_t)(s) - V_t(s)\,\bigr]$$

This is a **stochastic approximation** (Robbins-Monro) of the true update $V_{t+1} = T^\pi V_t$, which itself is a *deterministic* contraction.

### 2.3 Contraction Mapping Property

For any two value functions $U, V$:

$$\|T^\pi U - T^\pi V\|_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma\,\|U - V\|_\infty$$

Since $\gamma \in [0,1)$, $T^\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** on the complete metric space $(\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|}, \|\cdot\|_\infty)$.

### 2.4 Optimality Theorem (Informal Statement)

> **Theorem (Dayan 1992; Tsitsiklis & Van Roy 1997).** Let $\{(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1})\}_{t \geq 0}$ be a sequence of i.i.d. (or more generally, Markovian with uniform ergodicity) samples from policy $\pi$. If the step-sizes satisfy $\sum_t \alpha_t(s) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2(s) < \infty$ for all $s$, and every state is visited infinitely often with probability 1, then $V_t \to V^\pi$ almost surely.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha_t [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$ | TD(0) update rule | $\alpha_t \in (0,1)$, $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 2 | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | TD-error at step $t$ | Scalar real number |
| 3 | $(T^\pi V)(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s]$ | Bellman expectation operator | Function on $\mathcal{S}$ |
| 4 | $\\|T^\pi U - T^\pi V\\|_\infty \leq \gamma\\|U - V\\|_\infty$ | $\gamma$-contraction in sup-norm | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 5 | $V^\pi = T^\pi V^\pi$ | Bellman fixed-point equation | Unique solution |
| 6 | $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty,\ \sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro step-size condition | Ensures convergence a.s. |
| 7 | $\alpha_t = 1/t$ (canonical choice) | Harmonic step-size | Satisfies #6 |
| 8 | $\mathbb{E}[V_t] \to V^\pi$ | Convergence in expectation | With diminishing $\alpha_t$ |
| 9 | $\mathbb{P}\!\bigl(\lim_{t\to\infty} V_t(s) = V^\pi(s)\bigr) = 1$ | Almost-sure convergence | For each $s \in \mathcal{S}$ |
| 10 | $\|V_t - V^\pi\\|_\infty \leq \gamma^t \\|V_0 - V^\pi\\|_\infty$ | Geometric contraction rate | Deterministic $V$-iteration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** TD(0) optimality underpins *every* deep RL algorithm that bootstraps — DQN, Actor-Critic, PPO — by guaranteeing that the value-head loss converges when its target is a TD-target. In robotics, this is what makes value-function-based low-level controllers (e.g., quadruped gait critics) theoretically sound.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — TD(0) Target Equals the Bellman Operator's Sample Realization

We begin from the definition of $V^\pi$:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\right],\qquad G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

Decompose $G_t$ as the immediate reward plus the discounted future return:

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\,G_{t+1}$$

Take the conditional expectation with respect to $S_t = s$:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\,R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\bigr] \;+\; \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\,G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\bigr]$$

By the *Markov property* and the law of total expectation, $\mathbb{E}[G_{t+1}\mid S_t=s] = \sum_{s'} \Pr(S_{t+1}=s'\mid S_t=s)\,V^\pi(s')$. Therefore:

$$V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\,\bigl[\,r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\,\bigr] \;=\; (T^\pi V^\pi)(s)$$

Hence $V^\pi$ is a fixed point of $T^\pi$. The **TD(0) target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ is precisely an *unbiased sample* of $(T^\pi V)(S_t)$ evaluated at the observed transition.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-Contraction in $\|\cdot\|_\infty$

Let $U, V$ be arbitrary value functions. For any state $s$:

$$\bigl|(T^\pi U)(s) - (T^\pi V)(s)\bigr| \;=\; \left|\sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\,\gamma\bigl[U(s') - V(s')\bigr]\right|$$

Factor out $\gamma$:

$$=\;\gamma\;\left|\sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\,\bigl[U(s') - V(s')\bigr]\right|$$

Since $\sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a) = 1$ (probability measure), apply the triangle inequality and bound the inner expression by $\max_{s'}\vert U(s') - V(s')\vert = \|U - V\|_\infty$:

$$\bigl|(T^\pi U)(s) - (T^\pi V)(s)\bigr| \;\leq\; \gamma\,\|U - V\|_\infty$$

The bound holds uniformly over all $s$, so:

$$\|T^\pi U - T^\pi V\|_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma\,\|U - V\|_\infty$$

This proves that $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction with Lipschitz constant $\gamma$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Convergence Rate of Deterministic Value Iteration

Define $V^{(k+1)} = T^\pi V^{(k)}$. Then:

$$\|V^{(k+1)} - V^\pi\|_\infty \;=\; \|T^\pi V^{(k)} - T^\pi V^\pi\|_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma\,\|V^{(k)} - V^\pi\|_\infty$$

Applying this $k$ times by induction:

$$\|V^{(k)} - V^\pi\|_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma^k\,\|V^{(0)} - V^\pi\|_\infty$$

Since $\gamma < 1$, $\gamma^k \to 0$ exponentially fast. The convergence is *linear* (geometric) with rate $\gamma$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bias-Variance Decomposition of TD(0) Update

Write $V_{t+1} - V^\pi = V_{t+1} - T^\pi V_t + T^\pi V_t - V^\pi$:

$$\|V_{t+1} - V^\pi\|_\infty \;\leq\; \|V_{t+1} - T^\pi V_t\|_\infty \;+\; \|T^\pi V_t - V^\pi\|_\infty$$

The first term is the **sampling noise** (variance) bounded by $O(\alpha_t)$ in expectation under bounded-reward assumption. The second term is the **contraction error** bounded by $\gamma \|V_t - V^\pi\|_\infty$. Combining:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\|V_{t+1} - V^\pi\|_\infty\right] \;\leq\; \gamma\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\|V_t - V^\pi\|_\infty\right] \;+\; C\,\alpha_t$$

For a constant step-size $\alpha_t = \alpha$, the steady-state error bound is $C\alpha/(1-\gamma)$, vanishing as $\alpha \to 0$. This demonstrates TD(0)'s **asymptotic optimality**: the only irreducible error is the stochastic noise, which vanishes under the Robbins-Monro conditions.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Full Convergence Proof Skeleton via ODE Method (Kushner-Clark)

Define the *mean-field* ODE associated with the TD(0) update:

$$\dot{v}(t) \;=\; T^\pi v(t) - v(t)$$

Equilibria of this ODE satisfy $T^\pi v^* = v^*$, i.e., $v^* = V^\pi$. Because $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction, the ODE has a **unique globally asymptotically stable** equilibrium at $V^\pi$. By the Kushner-Clark theorem on stochastic approximation, if

$$\sum_t \alpha_t(s) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_t \alpha_t(s)^2 < \infty,$$

then the discrete iterates $V_t$ track the ODE trajectory and converge to $V^\pi$ almost surely. This is the formal justification for the *optimality* claim of TD(0).

### 3.6 Convergence-Error Bound (Convex Combinations)

Combining contraction and step-size effects with weights $w_t = \prod_{k<t}(1-\alpha_k)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\|V_t - V^\pi\|_\infty \;\leq\;&\; \underbrace{\gamma^t\|V_0 - V^\pi\|_\infty}_{\text{contraction bias}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{t-1}\gamma^{t-1-k}\alpha_k\,\varepsilon_k}_{\text{cumulative noise}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\varepsilon_k$ is the one-step sampling error of the empirical operator $\hat{T}^\pi_k$. Both terms tend to zero under the Robbins-Monro schedule.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TD(0) Single-Step Update Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agent at State St] --> B[Take Action At using Policy pi]
    B --> C[Environment returns Reward R_t+1 and next State S_t+1]
    C --> D[Lookup current value V of St from table]
    D --> E[Lookup current value V of S_t+1 from table]
    E --> F[Compute TD target R_t+1 plus gamma times V of S_t+1]
    F --> G[Compute TD error delta_t = target minus V of St]
    G --> H[Update V of St with step alpha times delta_t]
    H --> I[Move to S_t+1 and repeat until convergence]
    I --> J[Convergence check: norm of delta less than threshold]
    J --> K[Return optimal value function V superscript pi]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Convergence Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEL["Bellman Operator Layer (Deterministic)"]
        T1["T pi operator: V maps to T pi V"]
        T2["Contraction property: sup norm contraction with factor gamma"]
        T3["Unique fixed point: V superscript pi equals T pi V"]
    end

    subgraph TD["TD Zero Layer (Stochastic Approximation)"]
        S1["Sample transition St, R, S_t+1"]
        S2["Empirical operator hat T"]
        S3["Robbins-Monro update with step alpha"]
    end

    subgraph K["Convergence Conditions Layer"]
        K1["Step size: sum alpha equals infinity"]
        K2["Step size: sum alpha squared less than infinity"]
        K3["All states visited infinitely often"]
    end

    subgraph R["Optimality Result Layer"]
        R1["Kushner-Clark ODE method"]
        R2["Almost sure convergence to V superscript pi"]
        R3["Geometric rate gamma to the power t in expectation"]
    end

    BEL --> TD
    TD --> K
    K --> R
    T1 --> T2 --> T3
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    K1 --> K2 --> K3
    R1 --> R2 --> R3
```

### 4.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BF["Bias contribution (vanishes with t)"]
        B1["Iteration k starts at V sub zero"]
        B2["Apply T pi repeatedly k times"]
        B3["Bias less than gamma to the power k times initial error"]
    end

    subgraph VA["Variance contribution (vanishes with alpha)"]
        V1["Each sample contributes noise O of alpha times error"]
        V2["Diminishing alpha kills cumulative variance"]
        V3["Total variance O of alpha divided by one minus gamma"]
    end

    BF --> SUM[Total error bound: bias plus variance less than epsilon]
    VA --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Optimality: V sub t converges to V superscript pi as t and one over alpha go to infinity]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*State the optimality theorem of the TD(0) algorithm. Mention the two key conditions on the step-size sequence $\alpha_t$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TD(0) converges to the true value function $V^\pi$ of policy $\pi$ almost surely, provided the step-size sequence $\{\alpha_t\}$ satisfies:
1. $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s) = \infty$ (sufficient exploration/correction),
2. $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s)^2 < \infty$ (diminishing noise).

Additionally, all states must be visited infinitely often (Gloria conditions). The proof relies on viewing TD(0) as a stochastic approximation of the Bellman expectation operator $T^\pi$, which is a $\gamma$-contraction in sup-norm.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the TD-error and explain its role in the TD(0) update. Why does TD(0) bootstrap?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The TD-error is $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. It measures the discrepancy between the current estimate and the one-step bootstrapped target. TD(0) bootstraps because its update uses the *current* estimate $V(S_{t+1})$ instead of the *true* sampled return $G_t$, making it a one-step look-ahead rather than a full Monte-Carlo rollout. This reduces variance but introduces bias that disappears asymptotically.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]*

**(a)** *Derive the Bellman expectation operator $T^\pi$ for a fixed policy $\pi$ starting from the definition of the state-value function $V^\pi(s)$. Show that $V^\pi$ is its unique fixed point. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Prove that $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in the sup-norm and use this to derive the geometric convergence rate of value iteration. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — Definition:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\bigg|\, S_t = s\,\right]$$

Step 2 — Split the sum:

$$\sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\,\sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+2}$$

Step 3 — Conditional expectation with Markov property:

$$\begin{aligned}
V^\pi(s) &= \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1}\mid S_t=s] \;+\; \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+2} \,\bigg|\, S_t=s\,\right] \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\,r \;+\; \gamma\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} p(s'\mid s,a)\,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1}\mid S_{t+1}=s'\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Combine:

$$V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\bigr] \;=\; (T^\pi V^\pi)(s)$$

Step 5 — Uniqueness: any fixed point $U$ satisfying $T^\pi U = U$ must equal $V^\pi$ because $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction (proved in part b) and Banach's fixed-point theorem guarantees a unique fixed point.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Step 1 — Sup-norm definition: $\|U - V\|_\infty = \max_s \vert U(s) - V(s)\vert$.

Step 2 — Compute the operator difference:

$$(T^\pi U)(s) - (T^\pi V)(s) = \gamma\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} p(s'\mid s,a)\,\bigl[U(s') - V(s')\bigr]$$

Step 3 — Bound absolute value and use the probability measure property:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bigl|(T^\pi U)(s) - (T^\pi V)(s)\bigr| &\leq \gamma\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} p(s'\mid s,a)\,\bigl|U(s') - V(s')\bigr| \\
&\leq \gamma\,\max_{s'}\bigl|U(s') - V(s')\bigr|\cdot\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} p(s'\mid s,a) \\
&= \gamma\,\|U - V\|_\infty
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Uniformity over $s$ gives $\|T^\pi U - T^\pi V\|_\infty \leq \gamma\,\|U - V\|_\infty$.

Step 5 — Convergence rate by induction:

$$\|V^{(k)} - V^\pi\|_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma^k\,\|V^{(0)} - V^\pi\|_\infty$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]*

**(a)** *State and prove the optimality (convergence) theorem for TD(0). List all required assumptions clearly. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the bias-variance trade-off between TD(0) and Monte-Carlo methods. Show why TD(0) is preferable in robotics applications with long horizons. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — **Statement:** Let $V_t(s)$ denote the estimate of $V^\pi(s)$ after $t$ updates of TD(0). Under assumptions (A1)–(A4) below, $V_t(s) \to V^\pi(s)$ almost surely for every state $s$.

Step 2 — **Assumptions:**
- (A1) Robbins-Monro: $\sum_t \alpha_t(s) = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t(s)^2 < \infty$.
- (A2) Bounded rewards: $|R_{t+1}| \leq R_{\max} < \infty$.
- (A3) Discount $\gamma \in [0,1)$.
- (A4) Every state visited infinitely often (Gloria/Chung conditions).

Step 3 — **Proof sketch via stochastic approximation:**
View the update as $V_{t+1}(s) = V_t(s) + \alpha_t(s)\bigl[F_t(s) - V_t(s)\bigr]$ where $F_t(s)$ is the sampled Bellman target. The expected value of $F_t(s)$ given the history $\mathcal{F}_t$ is $(T^\pi V_t)(s)$, so the update has mean drift $T^\pi V_t - V_t$. The associated ODE is $\dot{v}(t) = T^\pi v(t) - v(t)$, whose only equilibrium is $V^\pi$. Because $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction, this equilibrium is globally asymptotically stable. By the Kushner-Clark theorem, the discrete iterates $V_t$ converge to $V^\pi$ almost surely.

Step 4 — Conclude optimality: $V_t \to V^\pi$ in probability and almost surely.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Step 1 — **Monte-Carlo** target uses the *full sampled return* $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$. This target is *unbiased*: $\mathbb{E}[G_t\mid S_t=s] = V^\pi(s)$. However, its variance is $O\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\gamma^2}\right)$ and grows with horizon length.

Step 2 — **TD(0)** target uses $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. This target is *biased* because $V(S_{t+1}) \neq V^\pi(S_{t+1})$ in finite iterations. However, its variance is bounded independent of horizon.

Step 3 — **Trade-off summary:**
- MC: unbiased but high variance, $O(T)$ variance for horizon $T$.
- TD(0): biased but low variance, $O(1)$ variance.

Step 4 — **Robotics motivation:** In a robotic manipulation task with horizon $T = 1000$, MC's variance explodes, making learning infeasible. TD(0)'s unit-step lookahead keeps variance bounded while the bias shrinks geometrically at rate $\gamma^t$. Hence TD(0) dominates in practice for long-horizon control problems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the Robbins-Monro condition** $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ — it is *not optional*; without it, the iterates chatter and never settle.
> 2. **Confusing the sup-norm with the Euclidean norm** — the contraction proof *requires* $\|\cdot\|_\infty$, not $\|\cdot\|_2$.
> 3. **Omitting the Markov property** when decomposing $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ — this is the step where most students slip up.
> 4. **Not stating "almost surely"** — the convergence is a.s., not just in expectation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **TD(0) target** = $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$; uses *bootstrapping* (one-step look-ahead).
- **TD-error** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ — drives the entire update.
- **Bellman operator** $T^\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in sup-norm → unique fixed point = $V^\pi$.
- **Optimality theorem** requires Robbins-Monro step-size + every-state-visited condition → a.s. convergence to $V^\pi$.
- **Convergence rate** of deterministic $V$-iteration is geometric: $\gamma^t$.
- **Bias-variance trade-off:** TD(0) is biased but low-variance; MC is unbiased but high-variance.
- **ODE method (Kushner-Clark):** TD(0) iterates track $\dot{v} = T^\pi v - v$ which has globally stable equilibrium $V^\pi$.
- **Robotics relevance:** TD(0) optimality underpins all bootstrapped deep-RL critics (DQN, PPO, SAC) used in legged robots, manipulators, and drones.
- **KTU marker favourites:** always mention the *two* Robbins-Monro conditions, the *contraction factor* $\gamma$, and the *fixed-point equation* $V^\pi = T^\pi V^\pi$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sarsa: On-policy TD Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sarsa: On-policy TD Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SARSA (State–Action–Reward–State–Action)** is an **on-policy Temporal Difference (TD) control algorithm** in Reinforcement Learning used to learn the action-value function $Q(s, a)$ by bootstrapping from the value of the action actually taken in the next state, under the same policy that is being improved. The name SARSA is an acronym for the five-tuple transition $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ required to perform a single update.

The canonical update rule for SARSA is:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

where:
- $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** ($0 < \alpha \leq 1$)
- $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** ($0 \leq \gamma < 1$)
- $R_{t+1}$ is the **immediate reward** observed after taking action $A_t$ in state $S_t$
- $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ uses the action *selected by the behavior policy* — not the greedy one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Policy vs. Off-Policy Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **SARSA (On-policy):** Learns the value of the policy *currently being followed*, including its exploratory moves.
> - **Q-Learning (Off-policy):** Learns the optimal action-value function regardless of the policy's exploration choices.
> This single difference has **major practical consequences** in stochastic or safety-critical robotic environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Driving School Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a learner driver taking lessons. Every time the instructor (the *environment*) yells *"BRAKE!"* the student learns. There are **two different driving schools** the student might consult:
> 1. **SARSA's driving school:** The student asks, *"What would I do if I kept driving my current way?"* — i.e., he queries his **own current driving habits**, including his tendency to occasionally drift into the other lane (exploration).
> 2. **Q-Learning's driving school:** The student asks, *"What is the very best thing I could possibly do?"* — assuming an idealized, perfect, never-risky driver.
>
> **SARSA therefore learns to drive *safely given that you are still a slightly reckless driver.*** In a foggy mountain road, SARSA's policy will deliberately stay *away* from the cliff edge because it accounts for the chance of randomly swerving. Q-Learning, on the other hand, will drive *closer* to the cliff because in expectation the optimal path hugs the edge.
>
> **This is why SARSA is preferred for robotics safety — it learns the value of a *realistic* policy, not an idealized one.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate (step-size) | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ (commonly $0.9$ to $0.99$) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Exploration probability ($\varepsilon$-greedy) | Decays to $0$ as $t \to \infty$ |
| $Q(s,a)$ | Estimated action-value | Initialized to $0$ or optimistic |
| $G_t$ | Return from time $t$ | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Guarantee (Sutton & Barto, 2018):** SARSA converges with probability **1** to the optimal action-value $q_*$ provided all state–action pairs are visited infinitely often and the policy satisfies the **GLIE (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration)** conditions. The learning rate $\alpha$ must satisfy the standard Robbins–Monro stochastic approximation conditions: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Why Bootstrapping Works in SARSA

SARSA lives at the intersection of two powerful ideas in Reinforcement Learning:

1. **Temporal Difference (TD) Learning** — uses bootstrapping (sample-and-update using the current value estimate rather than waiting for the final return).
2. **Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)** — interleaves *policy evaluation* (estimate $Q^\pi$) and *policy improvement* (act greedily w.r.t. $Q$).

The remarkable insight of SARSA is that we can perform **both evaluation and improvement in a single update**, because the next action $A_{t+1}$ is drawn from the *same* policy we are evaluating.

### 2.1.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialize** $Q(s, a)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$.
2. **Observe** initial state $S_t$ from the environment.
3. **Choose** $A_t$ from $S_t$ using a soft policy derived from $Q$ (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy).
4. **Take action** $A_t$; observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$.
5. **Choose** $A_{t+1}$ from $S_{t+1}$ using the same soft policy.
6. **Update** the Q-value using the SARSA five-tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.
7. **Set** $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$, $A_t \leftarrow A_{t+1}$ and repeat from step 4 until $S_t$ is terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Notice that the update in step 6 uses $A_{t+1}$ — the action we *will actually take* — not the greedy action. This is what makes SARSA **on-policy**. If we replaced $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ with $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$, the algorithm would magically transform into **Q-Learning**.

## 2.2 The TD Error — The Heart of SARSA

The bracketed term in the update is called the **TD error** $\delta_t$:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

This $\delta_t$ is the **sample** of the one-step Bellman error. The update pushes $Q(S_t, A_t)$ in the direction that reduces $\delta_t$ by a factor of $\alpha$. Over many visits, the action-value function converges to satisfy the Bellman expectation equation:

$$q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, q_\pi(s', a') \right]$$

## 2.3 GLIE — The Convergence Condition for SARSA

For SARSA to converge to the optimal action-value function $q_*$, the behavior policy must be **GLIE**:

| GLIE Property | Formal Statement | Practical Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Infinite Exploration** | Every $(s,a)$ pair is visited infinitely often | $\varepsilon$-decay schedule must keep $\varepsilon > 0$ long enough |
| **Greedy in the Limit** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \pi_t(a \mid s) = \mathbf{1}[a = \arg\max_a Q_t(s,a)]$ | $\varepsilon$ must decay to $0$ eventually |

> [!TIP]
> **Recommended schedule:** $\varepsilon_t = \frac{1}{t}$ or $\varepsilon_t = 0.1 / (1 + t/1000)$ — both satisfy GLIE.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | LaTeX Expression | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SARSA Update** | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha \left[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\right]$ | Every non-terminal step |
| **TD Error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)$ | Diagnosing learning progress |
| **Expected SARSA** | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha \left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1},a) - Q(S_t,A_t)\right]$ | Variance reduction in on-policy setting |
| **n-step SARSA Return** | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ | Generalizing to $n$-step returns |
| **Bellman Optimality (target)** | $q_*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} q_*(S_{t+1},a') \mid S_t=s, A_t=a\right]$ | Q-Learning target (for comparison) |
| **Policy Improvement** | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Greedy step in GPI |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

SARSA is **the workhorse algorithm for safety-sensitive robotic control** because:

- It accounts for **exploration noise** during learning, yielding policies that are robust to actuator stochasticity and sensor noise.
- It naturally extends to **Expected SARSA** (variance reduction) and **n-step SARSA** (better credit assignment for long-horizon tasks like locomotion).
- In **robotic manipulation**, SARSA with function approximation (e.g., tile coding or neural networks) has been used to learn grasp stability, peg-in-hole insertion, and bipedal balance.
- **Autonomous driving** research papers frequently cite SARSA over Q-learning to learn lane-change policies that avoid the "cliff-walking" pathology of Q-learning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SARSA Update Rule from the Bellman Equation

We begin with the **Bellman expectation equation for action-values** under policy $\pi$:

$$q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

> **Textual Logic:** The action-value of taking $a$ in $s$ under $\pi$ equals the expected one-step reward plus the discounted action-value of the *next* state–action pair (which is sampled from the *same* policy $\pi$).

Expanding the expectation:

$$q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, q_\pi(s', a') \right]$$

### Step 1 — Take a stochastic sample

We draw a single sample transition $(R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ and a single action $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$. This gives us the **sample-based estimate** of the right-hand side:

$$\hat{q} = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, q_\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$$

### Step 2 — Form the stochastic error

The **TD error** is the difference between this sample and the current estimate:

$$\delta_t \triangleq \hat{q} - Q(S_t, A_t) = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

### Step 3 — Apply stochastic gradient ascent

Treating $Q(S_t, A_t)$ as a parameter and $\delta_t$ as the (signed) gradient of a mean-squared-error loss, we update along $\delta_t$ with step-size $\alpha$:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\, \delta_t$$

Substituting $\delta_t$:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

> **Conclusion:** This is the SARSA update. It is a *stochastic approximation* to solving the Bellman expectation equation. The expectation in Bellman's equation is replaced by a *single sample*, and the parameter $Q$ is nudged towards that sample.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Expected SARSA Has Lower Variance

In Expected SARSA, we replace the sample $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ with its expectation under the next-state policy:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi[Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})] - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

**Variance analysis:**

$$\text{Var}\left[ Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \right] = \mathbb{E}\left[ Q^2 \right] - \left( \mathbb{E}[Q] \right)^2 \geq 0$$

Because $A_{t+1}$ is sampled *randomly* from $\pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$, the sample $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ adds irreducible variance to the SARSA target. Expected SARSA **averages this out** while remaining on-policy (the expectation is over the same $\pi$). Hence:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[\text{Expected SARSA target}\right] \leq \text{Var}\!\left[\text{Standard SARSA target}\right]$$

with the only additional cost being the computation of the weighted sum over $a' \in \mathcal{A}(S_{t+1})$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A robot arm has 3 states ($s_1, s_2, s_3$) and 2 actions (left, right). All $Q$ values are initially $0$. The robot is in $s_1$, takes action **right**, receives reward $R = +1$, transitions to $s_2$, and under $\varepsilon$-greedy with $\varepsilon = 0.5$ picks **right** again. Given $\alpha = 0.1$ and $\gamma = 0.9$, compute the SARSA update for $Q(s_1, \text{right})$.

**Step 1 — Identify the five-tuple:**

$$(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) = (s_1, \text{right}, +1, s_2, \text{right})$$

**Step 2 — Read the current Q-values:**

$$Q(s_1, \text{right}) = 0, \quad Q(s_2, \text{right}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the TD error:**

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

$$\delta_t = (+1) + (0.9)(0) - (0) = +1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the update:**

$$Q(s_1, \text{right}) \leftarrow Q(s_1, \text{right}) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$$

$$Q(s_1, \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + (0.1)(+1) = +0.1$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot arm's Q-value for moving right in $s_1$ is now $+0.1$. If the next iteration from $s_2$ (after taking right) leads to a terminal state with reward $+10$, the back-propagation through $Q(s_2, \text{right})$ will eventually pull $Q(s_1, \text{right})$ much higher.

## 3.4 Convergence Proof Sketch (Robbins–Monro Argument)

Define the operator $T^\pi$ (Bellman expectation operator):

$$(T^\pi Q)(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q(s', a') \right]$$

The fixed point of $T^\pi$ is $q_\pi$. The SARSA update is a **stochastic approximation** to fixed-point iteration on $T^\pi$. By the **Robbins–Monro theorem**, the sequence $Q_t$ converges to $q_\pi$ with probability **1** if:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s, a) < \infty \quad \forall (s,a)$$

> These are the **Robbins–Monro conditions** for stochastic approximation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | SARSA (On-policy) | Q-Learning (Off-policy) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target uses** | $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ — actual next action | $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ — greedy |
| **Converges to** | $q_\pi$ of behavior policy | $q_*$ (optimal) |
| **Exploration impact** | Affects learned value | Does *not* affect learned value |
| **Safety / risk sensitivity** | High (accounts for slips) | Low (assumes optimal execution) |
| **Variance** | Higher | Lower |
| **Best for** | Robotics, real-world control | Simulation, planning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SARSA Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

The diagram below shows the iterative computational flow of the SARSA algorithm, highlighting how the five-tuple is assembled and fed into the update step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA[Initialize Q table] --> initB[Set epsilon exploration rate]
    initB --> initC[Observe initial state S]
    initC --> stepA[Select action A using epsilon greedy]
    stepA --> stepB[Take action A in environment]
    stepB --> stepC[Observe reward R and next state Snext]
    stepC --> stepD[Select next action Anext using epsilon greedy]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute TD error delta]
    stepE --> stepF[Update Q of S A]
    stepF --> stepG{Is Snext terminal}
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH[Decay epsilon and restart episode]
    stepG -->|No| stepI[Shift S equals Snext and A equals Anext]
    stepH --> initC
    stepI --> stepA
```

## 4.2 On-Policy vs Off-Policy: Block Comparison

The diagram contrasts the **bootstrapping source** in SARSA versus Q-Learning. Note the single difference at the target node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SARSA_ON[ON POLICY SARSA]
        s1[State St] --> a1[Action At from behavior policy]
        a1 --> env1[Environment]
        env1 --> r1[Reward R]
        env1 --> sn1[Next State Stplus1]
        sn1 --> an1[Action Atplus1 from behavior policy]
        an1 --> q1[Q Stplus1 Atplus1]
        r1 --> sum1[Sum R plus gamma]
        q1 --> sum1
        sum1 --> delta1[TD Error]
        qsa1[Q St At] --> delta1
        delta1 --> up1[Update Q St At]
    end
    subgraph QL_OFF[OFF POLICY Q LEARNING]
        s2[State St] --> a2[Action At from behavior policy]
        a2 --> env2[Environment]
        env2 --> r2[Reward R]
        env2 --> sn2[Next State Stplus1]
        sn2 --> mx[Max over all actions a]
        mx --> q2[max Q Stplus1 a]
        r2 --> sum2[Sum R plus gamma]
        q2 --> sum2
        sum2 --> delta2[TD Error]
        qsa2[Q St At] --> delta2
        delta2 --> up2[Update Q St At]
    end
```

## 4.3 GLIE Convergence Behavior Schematic

The schematic below depicts the two simultaneous requirements of GLIE — exploration must eventually die out, but every state–action pair must still be visited infinitely often.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLIE[GLIE CONVERGENCE CONDITIONS]
        e1[Epsilon greedy policy] --> c1{Condition 1}
        c1 -->|Infinite exploration| t1[Visit every s a infinitely often]
        c1 -->|Greedy in limit| t2[Epsilon decays to 0]
        t1 --> conv[Converges to q star with prob 1]
        t2 --> conv
    end
```

## 4.4 Cliffs Walking Example (Famous KTU Illustration)

The classic **Cliff Walking** example from Sutton & Barto perfectly illustrates the difference between SARSA and Q-Learning. SARSA learns the *safer* longer path; Q-Learning learns the *riskier* optimal path because it does not penalize exploratory falls.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIFF[CLIFF WALKING GRID]
        S[Start S] -->|SARSA safe path| P1[Path along top edge]
        S -->|Q Learning optimal path| P2[Path along cliff edge]
        P1 --> G[Goal G]
        P2 --> G
        P1 -.danger.-> C[Cliff penalty]
        P2 -.danger.-> C
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this for the exam:** Whenever you see a question asking *"Why does SARSA differ from Q-Learning in stochastic environments?"*, this cliff example is your one-line answer. The path SARSA picks is *sub-optimal in expectation* but *safer under $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Set]**

### Question A1
**Differentiate between on-policy and off-policy TD control methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | On-Policy (SARSA) | Off-Policy (Q-Learning) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Policy used for update | Same as behavior policy | Different (greedy target) |
| Action used in bootstrap | $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$ | $\arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ |
| Converges to | $q_\pi$ of behavior policy | $q_*$ (optimal) |
| Example | SARSA, Expected SARSA | Q-Learning, Double Q-Learning |

On-policy methods learn the value of the policy currently being executed, including its exploration. Off-policy methods learn the value of an *optimal* policy while following a *different* behavior policy.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for table, 1 for example.)*

### Question A2
**State the Robbins–Monro conditions for stochastic approximation. Why are they required for SARSA convergence?**

**Model Answer:**

The Robbins–Monro conditions for the learning rate schedule $\alpha_t(s,a)$ are:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s, a) < \infty \quad \forall (s, a)$$

The first condition ensures the algorithm has **enough "energy"** to reach the optimum from any starting point (no premature freezing). The second condition ensures the **step sizes shrink fast enough** to dampen stochastic noise. Together they guarantee that the SARSA update — which is a *stochastic* sample of the Bellman operator — converges to its fixed point with probability 1.

*(3 marks — 1 for each condition, 1 for explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3 Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the SARSA update rule starting from the Bellman expectation equation. Clearly state the role of the five-tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ in the derivation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the Bellman expectation equation for action-values:**

$$q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2 — Note that the right-hand side is an expectation over both the environment's stochasticity and the policy's stochasticity.** Drawing a single sample from each gives:

$$\hat{q} = R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$$

**Step 3 — Form the temporal-difference error $\delta_t$** between this sample and the current estimate $Q(S_t, A_t)$:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

**Step 4 — Perform a stochastic gradient step** with learning rate $\alpha$:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$$

**Step 5 — Substitute to obtain the canonical SARSA update:**

$$\boxed{Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]}$$

**Role of the five-tuple:**
- $(S_t, A_t)$ — the *current* state–action whose Q-value we update.
- $R_{t+1}$ — the *immediate consequence* of taking $A_t$ in $S_t$, providing the one-step supervision signal.
- $(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ — the *next* state–action pair sampled from the *same* policy $\pi$, providing the bootstrapping target. The fact that $A_{t+1}$ is sampled from the behavior policy is what makes the algorithm **on-policy**.

*(7 marks — 1 for Bellman equation, 2 for sample substitution, 1 for TD error, 1 for update, 1 for box, 1 for the five-tuple explanation.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the GLIE property. Show how a schedule of the form $\varepsilon_t = c/t$ satisfies GLIE. What happens to SARSA's convergence if GLIE is violated?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of GLIE:** A policy sequence $\{\pi_t\}$ is **Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration** if:
1. Every state–action pair is visited infinitely often: $\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\#(s,a) \text{ visits in } N \text{ steps}}{N} > 0 \quad \forall (s,a)$.
2. The policy becomes greedy in the limit: $\lim_{t \to \infty} \pi_t(a \mid s) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[ a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_t(s, a') \right]$.

**Proof that $\varepsilon_t = c/t$ satisfies GLIE:**

*Infinite exploration:* $\varepsilon_t = c/t > 0$ for all finite $t$, so the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy has non-zero probability of every action in every state. With enough samples, the count of visits to each $(s,a)$ grows without bound.

*Greedy in the limit:*

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \varepsilon_t = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{c}{t} = 0$$

Hence the policy converges to greedy in the limit.

**Consequences of violating GLIE:**
- *If exploration dies out too fast* (e.g., $\varepsilon_t = 0$ after some finite $T$): the agent may have insufficient samples of bad actions and could converge to a *sub-optimal* local maximum.
- *If exploration never dies out* (e.g., $\varepsilon_t = 0.1$ constant): the agent keeps exploring forever, so the policy never becomes greedy. SARSA converges to $q_\pi$ for the *stationary* $\varepsilon$-soft policy, not the true optimum $q_*$. Hence some asymptotic regret remains.

*(7 marks — 2 for GLIE definition, 3 for the $\varepsilon_t = c/t$ proof, 2 for violation consequences.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare SARSA and Q-Learning in detail. Use the "Cliff Walking" example to justify why SARSA is preferred in safety-critical robotic applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular comparison:**

| Aspect | SARSA | Q-Learning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Update target | $R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ |
| Uses actual next action? | Yes | No |
| Converges to | $q_\pi$ of behavior policy | $q_*$ (optimal) |
| On-policy / off-policy | On-policy | Off-policy |
| Variance of update | Higher (depends on $A_{t+1}$ sample) | Lower (deterministic max) |
| Robustness to exploration | High (learns safe value) | Low (assumes optimal play) |

**Cliff Walking Example:**
Consider a $4 \times 12$ grid where the agent must travel from Start to Goal. The bottom row (between Start and Goal) is the *cliff* — stepping into it yields a reward of $-100$ and resets the agent. Under $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration with $\varepsilon = 0.1$:

- **Q-Learning** learns the *shortest* path — along the cliff edge — because its target is the *optimal* max, ignoring the chance of slipping.
- **SARSA** learns the *safer* path — along the top edge — because it accounts for the $0.1$ probability of falling off the cliff on any exploratory step, which accumulates significant negative reward.

**Justification for robotics:**
In real-world robotics (e.g., autonomous driving, legged locomotion, surgical robots), exploration-induced failures are *catastrophic and irreversible* (e.g., a robot falling off a staircase). SARSA's learnt policy is **robust to the very exploration noise it uses**, making it the *de facto* choice for safety-critical domains. Q-Learning's "optimal" path is an *idealization* — it presumes a perfect controller, which does not exist on a noisy physical robot.

*(7 marks — 2 for table, 2 for cliff example, 3 for robotics justification.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Expected SARSA update rule and prove that its target has variance at most that of standard SARSA.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall standard SARSA:**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

Here $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$ is *sampled*, so $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ is a **random variable** even conditioned on $S_{t+1}$.

**Step 2 — Take the expectation over $A_{t+1}$:**

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}\!\left[Q(S_{t+1}, a)\right] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 3 — Substitute this expectation into the bootstrap target** to obtain Expected SARSA:

$$\boxed{Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Variance comparison.** Conditional on $S_{t+1}$, the standard SARSA target is $T_1 = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$. Its conditional variance is:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[T_1 \mid S_{t+1}\right] = \gamma^2\, \text{Var}_{a \sim \pi}\!\left[Q(S_{t+1}, a)\right]$$

For Expected SARSA, the target is $T_2 = R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[Q(S_{t+1}, a)]$, which is a **deterministic** function of $S_{t+1}$ (the randomness in $A_{t+1}$ has been averaged out). Therefore:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[T_2 \mid S_{t+1}\right] = 0 \leq \gamma^2 \text{Var}\!\left[Q(S_{t+1}, a)\right] = \text{Var}\!\left[T_1 \mid S_{t+1}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Cost–benefit trade-off.** Expected SARSA eliminates the $A_{t+1}$ sampling variance at the cost of summing over $\vert \mathcal{A}(S_{t+1}) \vert$ Q-values. This is cheap for discrete actions but expensive (requires integration) for continuous action spaces.

*(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 3 for variance proof, 2 for trade-off discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the SARSA update with the Q-Learning update in your answer scripts. Examiners **deduct 2 marks immediately** if the bootstrap uses $\max_a$ instead of $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.
> 2. **Always state the GLIE conditions explicitly** when asked about convergence — partial credit is given even if you forget the $\varepsilon_t = c/t$ example.
> 3. **In numericals**, do *not* skip the step of selecting $A_{t+1}$ using the $\varepsilon$-greedy rule. The question is *testing* whether you understand that $A_{t+1}$ is *sampled* — not greedy.
> 4. **Common sign error:** the TD error is $R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(\cdot) - Q(S_t,A_t)$, not the other way around. Reversing the sign leads to a divergent algorithm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Sarsa)**

- [ ] **SARSA = State–Action–Reward–State–Action.** Update needs *all five* elements per step.
- [ ] **Update rule:** $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)]$
- [ ] **TD error:** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$.
- [ ] **On-policy** — learns value of the policy *currently being followed* (including exploration).
- [ ] **Off-policy comparison** — Q-Learning uses $\max_a Q(S_{t+1},a)$; SARSA uses $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.
- [ ] **Convergence condition:** GLIE — infinite exploration + greedy in the limit.
- [ ] **Learning-rate schedule must satisfy Robbins–Monro:** $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
- [ ] **Expected SARSA** averages over $A_{t+1}$, reducing variance to zero conditionally on $S_{t+1}$.
- [ ] **n-step SARSA** generalizes to $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$.
- [ ] **Cliff Walking** is the canonical KTU illustration: SARSA = safe long path; Q-Learning = risky short path.
- [ ] **Preferred for robotics safety** because it accounts for exploration-induced slips.
- [ ] **Initial Q-values** can be set optimistically to encourage initial exploration (still satisfies GLIE).
- [ ] **Termination** of an episode resets $S_t$ to the start state but **does not** reset $Q$ values.
- [ ] **Single update per environment step** in tabular SARSA; multiple updates per step are a function-approximation technique, not part of vanilla SARSA.
- [ ] **Not the same as actor–critic** — SARSA is a *tabular* or *linear* value-based method; actor–critic maintains a separate parameterized policy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Q-learning: Off-policy TD Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Q-learning: Off-policy TD Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q-Learning (Off-Policy Temporal Difference Control)** is a model-free, value-based reinforcement learning algorithm introduced by Christopher Watkins in 1989, which directly learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ by iteratively updating estimates using the Bellman optimality equation, regardless of the policy being followed during action selection.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Q-learning** is an **off-policy Temporal Difference (TD) control** algorithm. The term "off-policy" signifies that the algorithm learns the value of the **optimal policy** (target policy) while behaving according to a **different exploratory policy** (behavior policy) used to generate experience trajectories. 

The agent maintains a Q-table (or Q-function approximator) $Q: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, which estimates the expected cumulative discounted reward of taking action $a$ in state $s$ and subsequently following the optimal policy. The update rule is governed by the **Bellman Optimality Equation** rather than the Bellman Expectation Equation used in on-policy methods like SARSA.

The core update rule for Q-learning is given as:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** with $0 < \alpha \leq 1$
- $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** with $0 \leq \gamma < 1$
- $R_{t+1}$ is the immediate reward
- $\max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ is the maximum Q-value over all possible actions in the next state

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Clueless Tourist with a Guidebook"**
>
> Imagine you are a tourist in a new city trying to find the best restaurant. You hire a local guide (the **behavior policy** — e.g., an $\epsilon$-greedy policy that sometimes takes you to random places). However, you personally keep a notebook (the **Q-table**) where you write down, for every intersection (state) and every possible turn (action), the *best* possible rating you could get if you were to take the *optimal* route from that point onward.
>
> The local guide might drag you down a strange alley, but in your notebook, you don't record the guide's choice — you record the *hypothetical best turn* at each intersection. Over time, your notebook converges to the true optimal restaurant map, even though you never actually followed that optimal path consistently.
>
> - The **guide's path** = behavior policy (exploratory)
> - The **notebook's entries** = target policy (greedy with respect to Q)
> - The **notebook itself** = Q-function

### 1.3 Why "Off-Policy"? A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | On-Policy TD (SARSA) | Off-Policy TD (Q-Learning) |
|---|---|---|
| Policy being evaluated | The same policy used to act | The optimal greedy policy |
| Update target uses | $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ where $A_{t+1}$ is the *next action actually taken* | $\max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ over *all* actions |
| Risk awareness | Yes (incorporates exploratory moves into value) | No (assumes best action is always taken next) |
| Convergence | More conservative, safer in risky environments | More aggressive, learns optimal regardless of agent's mistakes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** In Q-learning, the agent *learns about the optimal policy* while it *behaves according to a different (typically exploratory) policy*. This is the essence of off-policy learning and is a frequently asked concept in Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Walk-Through of the Q-Learning Algorithm

The Q-learning algorithm proceeds through the following structured sequence at every time step:

1. **State Observation**: The agent observes the current environmental state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$.

2. **Action Selection (Behavior Policy)**: Using an $\epsilon$-greedy policy derived from current Q-values:
   - With probability $1 - \epsilon$: select $A_t = \arg\max_{a} Q(S_t, a)$ (exploitation)
   - With probability $\epsilon$: select $A_t$ uniformly at random from $\mathcal{A}$ (exploration)

3. **Environment Interaction**: Execute $A_t$, receive reward $R_{t+1}$ and observe next state $S_{t+1}$.

4. **TD Target Computation**: The off-policy target assumes the agent will behave optimally *next*, regardless of the next action actually taken:
   
   $$Y_t^{\text{Q-learning}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$$

5. **TD Error Calculation**: The discrepancy between the bootstrap target and the current estimate:
   
   $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

6. **Q-Value Update**: Apply the stochastic gradient-descent-like step:
   
   $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$$

7. **State Transition**: Set $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$ and repeat until terminal state is reached.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why $\max$ instead of expectation?** Because Q-learning assumes the target policy is deterministic greedy: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$. For a greedy deterministic policy, the expectation collapses to the maximum.
- **Why an exploratory behavior policy?** Without exploration, the agent would never discover higher-reward actions it hasn't tried. The $\epsilon$-greedy strategy provides the **coverage** property — every state-action pair must be visited infinitely often for convergence.
- **Why does the max operator enable off-policy learning?** Because it "pretends" the agent will always make the best choice, separating the policy being learned from the policy being executed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Q-Learning Update Rule | $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)]$ | Core incremental update step |
| 2 | TD Target (Q-Learning) | $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ | Bootstrap estimate of optimal value |
| 3 | TD Error | $\delta_t = Y_t - Q(S_t, A_t)$ | Magnitude of update signal |
| 4 | Optimal Action-Value (Bellman) | $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Fixed-point definition |
| 5 | $\epsilon$-Greedy Action Selection | $A_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a} Q(S_t, a) & \text{w.p. } 1-\epsilon \\ \text{random } a \in \mathcal{A} & \text{w.p. } \epsilon \end{cases}$ | Behavior policy for exploration |
| 6 | Greedy Target Policy | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$ | Deterministic optimal policy extracted from Q |
| 7 | Convergence Condition | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s, a) < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro conditions for all $(s, a)$ |
| 8 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative objective being maximized |
| 9 | Action-Value Definition | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Expected return from $s, a$ under policy $\pi$ |
| 10 | Max-Operator Trick | $\max_{a} Q(s, a) = \sum_{a} \pi^*(a \mid s) \cdot Q(s, a)$ for $\pi^*$ greedy | Justifies max as a valid expectation under greedy policy |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Robotics

Q-learning is foundational in robotics for tasks where an explicit model of the environment is unavailable or impractical:

- **Mobile Robot Navigation**: Learning obstacle-avoidance policies in unknown indoor environments.
- **Manipulation Tasks**: Teaching robotic arms grasping policies without dynamic models of object friction.
- **Drone Control**: Hovering and trajectory tracking in turbulent wind conditions.
- **Industrial Automation**: Optimizing pick-and-place cycles where hand-engineering the policy is infeasible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Caveat:** In high-dimensional robotic state spaces (e.g., raw camera images), tabular Q-learning is infeasible. This motivates **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)**, which approximate $Q(s, a; \theta)$ using neural networks — but the underlying update principle remains identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Q-Learning Update Rule from First Principles

We begin with the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for action-values, which is the defining fixed-point equation for the optimal Q-function $Q^*$:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

**Step 1 — Convert to a Stochastic Approximation Problem**

Since the true dynamics $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ are unknown, we treat the right-hand side as a noisy sample. At time $t$, the agent samples a transition $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ and forms a stochastic estimate:

$$\hat{Y}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Robbins-Monro Stochastic Approximation Scheme**

The general stochastic approximation update is:

$$Q_{t+1}(s, a) = Q_t(s, a) + \alpha_t(s, a) \left[ \hat{Y}_t - Q_t(s, a) \right]$$

Substituting $\hat{Y}_t$:

$$Q_{t+1}(S_t, A_t) = Q_t(S_t, A_t) + \alpha_t \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(S_{t+1}, a') - Q_t(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the TD Error**

Defining the temporal-difference error $\delta_t$:

$$\delta_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(S_{t+1}, a') - Q_t(S_t, A_t)$$

The update simplifies to:

$$Q_{t+1}(S_t, A_t) = Q_t(S_t, A_t) + \alpha_t \cdot \delta_t$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Guarantee**

By the **Robbins-Monro theorem**, $Q_t(s, a) \rightarrow Q^*(s, a)$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$ for all $(s, a)$ if:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s, a) < \infty$$

These conditions ensure the updates are large enough to overcome initial error but small enough to settle.

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 4-state gridworld with states $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ where $S_4$ is terminal and yields reward $+10$. All other transitions yield $R = 0$. Let $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$. The agent experiences the following episode:
$$S_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} S_2 \xrightarrow{a_2} S_3 \xrightarrow{a_3} S_4 (\text{terminal}, R = +10)$$

Initial Q-table (all zeros except some existing values shown):

| State | $a_1$ | $a_2$ | $a_3$ | $a_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $S_3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step-by-Step Q-Learning Updates (Reverse Order, Backward View):**

**Update 3 — At transition $(S_3, a_3) \rightarrow S_4$ with $R = +10$:**

$$\max_{a} Q(S_4, a) = 0 \quad \text{(terminal state, all Q-values are 0)}$$

$$Q(S_3, a_3) \leftarrow Q(S_3, a_3) + 0.5 \left[ 10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0 \right] = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 10 = 5.0$$

**Update 2 — At transition $(S_2, a_2) \rightarrow S_3$ with $R = 0$:**

$$\max_{a} Q(S_3, a) = 5.0 \quad \text{(just updated)}$$

$$Q(S_2, a_2) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ 0 + 0.9 \cdot 5.0 - 0 \right] = 0.5 \cdot 4.5 = 2.25$$

**Update 1 — At transition $(S_1, a_1) \rightarrow S_2$ with $R = 0$:**

$$\max_{a} Q(S_2, a) = 2.25$$

$$Q(S_1, a_1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ 0 + 0.9 \cdot 2.25 - 0 \right] = 0.5 \cdot 2.025 = 1.0125$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Q-Learning vs. SARSA

This is a frequently tested comparison in KTU exams. Note the critical differences in the update target:

| Feature | SARSA (On-Policy) | Q-Learning (Off-Policy) |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Target** | $R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ |
| **Policy Type** | Both behavior and target = $\epsilon$-greedy | Behavior = $\epsilon$-greedy, Target = greedy |
| **Next Action** | Requires knowing $A_{t+1}$ actually taken | Does not require next action |
| **Convergence To** | Near-optimal **safe** policy | Optimal policy |
| **Cliff-Walking Behavior** | Learns long safe path | Learns short risky path |
| **Update Source** | Five-tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | Four-tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ |
| **Bias-Variance** | Lower variance, higher bias | Higher variance, lower bias |
| **Complexity** | Same per-step cost | Slightly higher (max over actions) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cliff-Walking Example (Classic Sutton & Barto Illustration):** In the cliff-walking gridworld, SARSA learns the longer but safer path along the top of the cliff because it accounts for the $\epsilon$-chance of stepping off. Q-Learning, oblivious to this risk in its target, learns the shorter optimal path *right along the cliff edge*, occasionally falling due to exploration — a phenomenon that beautifully illustrates the off-policy vs on-policy distinction.

### 3.4 Mathematical Intuition for Off-Policy Correction

The off-policy nature of Q-learning can be understood through **importance sampling**, though Q-learning achieves off-policy learning *without* explicit importance sampling ratios. The reasoning:

Under the optimal greedy policy $\pi^*$, the probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ is $1$ if $a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a')$, else $0$. The expectation of Q-values under $\pi^*$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi^*}[Q(s, a)] = \sum_{a} \pi^*(a \mid s) Q(s, a) = \max_{a} Q(s, a)$$

This shows that $\max$ is exactly the expectation under a greedy policy. Hence, Q-learning implicitly assumes a greedy target policy while using any exploratory behavior policy to gather data — no importance sampling ratios required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Q-Learning Algorithm Operational Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start Episode]) --> B[Initialize Q-table to zeros]
    B --> C[Observe current state S_t]
    C --> D{epsilon-greedy action selection}
    D -->|exploit with prob 1-eps| E[Select A_t = argmax Q S_t a]
    D -->|explore with prob eps| F[Select A_t randomly from A]
    E --> G[Execute A_t in environment]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Receive reward R_t+1 and next state S_t+1]
    H --> I[Compute TD target Y_t = R_t+1 + gamma times max Q S_t+1 a]
    I --> J[Compute TD error delta_t = Y_t minus Q S_t A_t]
    J --> K[Update Q S_t A_t = Q S_t A_t + alpha times delta_t]
    K --> L{Is S_t+1 terminal?}
    L -->|No| M[Set S_t = S_t+1]
    M --> D
    L -->|Yes| N{More episodes?}
    N -->|Yes| A
    N -->|No| O([Extract optimal policy pi from Q])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Off-Policy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Agent[Agent Module]
        direction TB
        A1[Behavior Policy mu: epsilon-greedy] --> A2[Action Selection]
        A2 --> A3[Q-Table Storage]
    end
    
    subgraph Environment[Environment Module]
        direction TB
        B1[Current State S_t] --> B2[Transition Dynamics]
        B2 --> B3[Reward R_t+1]
        B2 --> B4[Next State S_t+1]
    end
    
    subgraph Learner[Learning Module]
        direction TB
        C1[Sampled Transition Tuple] --> C2[TD Target: R + gamma max Q]
        C2 --> C3[TD Error Computation]
        C3 --> C4[Q-Value Update]
        C4 --> C5[Target Policy pi: greedy w.r.t. Q]
    end
    
    A3 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    A2 -->|action A_t| B1
    C5 --> A1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram: On-Policy vs. Off-Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OnPolicy[SARSA On-Policy Architecture]
        direction LR
        O1[Behavior Policy equals Target Policy] --> O2[Update uses Q S_t+1, A_t+1]
        O2 --> O3[Converges to near-optimal safe policy]
    end
    
    subgraph OffPolicy[Q-Learning Off-Policy Architecture]
        direction LR
        P1[Behavior Policy: epsilon-greedy] --> P2[Update uses max over actions]
        P2 --> P3[Converges to optimal policy]
    end
    
    OnPolicy -.contrasted with.-> OffPolicy
```

### 4.4 Schematic Explanation of the Update Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts above depict the data flow within a single Q-learning iteration. Notice the critical two-pathway structure in the off-policy block diagram: actions flow from the behavior policy to the environment, but the **target for value updates** is computed as if the agent followed a greedy policy. This separation is the architectural hallmark of off-policy learning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Q-learning and explain why it is classified as an off-policy algorithm.*

**Model Answer:**
Q-learning is a model-free, value-based reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ directly. It is classified as off-policy because the **target policy** being learned (the greedy policy $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$) is **different** from the **behavior policy** used to generate experience (typically an $\epsilon$-greedy policy). The update uses $\max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$, which assumes optimal action selection, regardless of the action actually taken. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the Q-learning update equation and identify each term.*

**Model Answer:**
The Q-learning update rule is:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$
