# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Reinforcement Learning Timeline for n-step Sarsa

This diagram shows the **backup diagram** (the conceptual tree of future steps) of n-step Sarsa over a window of length **n = 4**. The agent samples an action at each node (white circle = state, black dot = action), accumulates **n** rewards, and bootstraps from **Q** at the (n+1)-th state–action pair.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS0["S0"] -- "A0" --> nodeA0(("•"))
    nodeA0 -- "R1" --> nodeS1["S1"]
    nodeS1 -- "A1" --> nodeA1(("•"))
    nodeA1 -- "R2" --> nodeS2["S2"]
    nodeS2 -- "A2" --> nodeA2(("•"))
    nodeA2 -- "R3" --> nodeS3["S3"]
    nodeS3 -- "A3" --> nodeA3(("•"))
    nodeA3 -- "R4" --> nodeS4["S4"]
    nodeS4 -- "A4" --> nodeA4(("•"))
    nodeA4 -. "Q-bootstrap" .-> nodeQ4["Q S4 A4"]
    nodeA0 -. "delta propagated" .-> nodeUpdate["Update Q S0 A0"]
    nodeA1 -. "delta propagated" .-> nodeUpdate
    nodeA2 -. "delta propagated" .-> nodeUpdate
    nodeA3 -. "delta propagated" .-> nodeUpdate
```

> **Reading the diagram**: A *solid arrow* indicates an actual environment transition; a *dotted arrow* indicates either the bootstrap lookup or the back-propagated update to older Q-values.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram represents the **computational architecture** of n-step Sarsa within a robotic RL agent (e.g., a drone controller). It maps the *interaction* between the agent, the environment, the n-step buffer, and the Q-update engine.

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef env fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef agent fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef buffer fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef update fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    envRobot["ROBOT / ENVIRONMENT"]:::env
    actSelect["ACTION SELECTOR eps greedy"]:::agent
    sensorRead["SENSOR READ State Reward"]:::env
    ringBuf["N STEP RING BUFFER length n"]:::buffer
    targetForm["N STEP TARGET FORMER G t t+n"]:::update
    qUpdate["Q VALUE UPDATE SGD step"]:::update
    policyOut["POLICY OUTPUT action vector"]:::agent

    envRobot -- "current state S_t" --> actSelect
    actSelect -- "A_t" --> envRobot
    envRobot -- "R_{t+1} S_{t+1}" --> sensorRead
    sensorRead -- "transition tuple" --> ringBuf
    ringBuf -- "n stored tuples" --> targetForm
    qUpdate -- "Q function" --> actSelect
    targetForm -- "TD target G" --> qUpdate
    qUpdate -- "updated Q" --> policyOut
    policyOut --> actSelect
```

> **Engineering reading**: The **RING BUFFER** is the structural element that distinguishes n-step Sarsa from 1-step Sarsa. The **TARGET FORMER** is the only place where the discount factor **γ** is explicitly applied.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Algorithm Pseudocode Flowchart (Tabular n-step Sarsa)

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef start fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    classDef stop fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24

    startNode([START n step Sarsa]):::start
    initQ["Initialize Q s a for all s a"]:::process
    initN["Choose n alpha gamma epsilon"]:::process
    initS["Initialize S_0"]:::process
    pickA["Choose A_0 from pi eps greedy"]:::process
    loopCheck{episode\nnot done?}:::decision
    takeAct["Take action A_t observe R and S"]:::process
    pickNextA["Choose A_{t+1} using pi"]:::process
    storeBuff["Store S A R A in buffer"]:::process
    nCheck{t - t0 greater\nthan equal n - 1?}:::decision
    computeG["Compute G_{tau tau+n}"]:::process
    applyUpd["Update Q S_tau A_tau"]:::process
    tauShift["tau leftarrow tau + 1"]:::process
    setOld["S leftarrow S t plus 1 A leftarrow A t plus 1"]:::process
    stopNode([END episode]):::stop

    startNode --> initQ --> initN --> initS --> pickA --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- "yes" --> takeAct --> pickNextA --> storeBuff --> nCheck
    nCheck -- "yes" --> computeG --> applyUpd --> tauShift --> setOld --> loopCheck
    nCheck -- "no" --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- "no" --> stopNode
```

> **KTU Tip**: The `tau` (Greek "τ") notation here is the textbook (Sutton & Barto) convention — it denotes the *time of the state whose estimate is being updated*, which lags behind the *current time t* by approximately **n** steps.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define n-step Sarsa. Write the expression for its n-step return and state the role of the bootstrap term.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**
n-step Sarsa is an *on-policy TD control algorithm* that updates action-value estimates by averaging rewards over **n** actual transitions and then bootstrapping using the Q-value of the (n+1)-th state–action pair.

$$G_{t:t+n} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1} \;+\; \gamma^{n}\, Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$$

**Role of bootstrap term**: The term $\gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ allows the algorithm to *terminate the look-ahead* at depth **n** without needing the full episode, which makes it usable in **continuing (non-episodic)** tasks and reduces variance compared to Monte Carlo.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**How does n-step Sarsa differ from one-step Sarsa and Monte Carlo control? Mention the bias–variance implications.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**
- **1-step Sarsa**: bootstraps after **1** step → high bias, low variance.
- **n-step Sarsa**: bootstraps after **n** steps → moderate bias and variance (tunable via **n**).
- **Monte Carlo**: bootstraps at episode end (no bootstrapping) → zero bias, high variance; requires episodic tasks.

As **n** increases, **bias decreases** (target is closer to true return) but **variance increases** (more random rewards accumulated).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> 1. Students often *forget the bootstrap term* $\gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ and write the return as a pure sum of rewards — this is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU valuation. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. Students confuse **n-step Sarsa** with **n-step Q-learning** by using `max_{a'} Q(...)` instead of the *on-policy* `Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})` — this changes the algorithm class. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. Students mis-state the update *time* — the update for `(Sₜ, Aₜ)` occurs at `t + n`, **not** at `t + 1`. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. Neglecting to mention the **on-policy / ε-greedy** nature in the answer loses **CO (Course Outcome) mapping marks**. *(−1 mark)*

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the n-step Sarsa return starting from the Bellman optimality equation for action-values. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a deterministic MDP with **γ = 0.8**, **α = 0.4**, **n = 3**. A trajectory produces the following table (terminal state has Q = 0):

| **t** | **Sₜ** | **Aₜ** | **Rₜ₊₁** | **Sₜ₊₁** | **Q(Sₜ₊₁, Aₜ₊₁)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | s₁ | a₁ | 1 | s₂ | 5 |
| 1 | s₂ | a₂ | 2 | s₃ | 4 |
| 2 | s₃ | a₃ | 3 | s₄ | 6 |
| 3 | s₄ | a₄ | 4 | s₅ (terminal) | 0 |

Initial Q-values: Q(s₁, a₁) = 2, Q(s₂, a₂) = 3, Q(s₃, a₃) = 4. Perform all n-step updates triggered up to **t = 3**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Bellman Equation (1 mark):**

$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q^{*}(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \,\bigm|\, S_t=s, A_t=a\,\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — First Recursion (1 mark):**

$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;\approx\; \mathbb{E}[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} Q^{*}(S_{t+2}, A_{t+2})\,]$$

**Step 3 — Recurse to Depth n (2 marks):**

$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;\approx\; \mathbb{E}\Bigl[\,\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} Q^{*}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})\,\Bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Replace Q* with current Q (1 mark):**

$$G_{t:t+n} \;\triangleq\; \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$$

**Step 5 — State the update and assumptions (2 marks):**
- *Assumption 1*: On-policy (same policy π generates all actions).
- *Assumption 2*: Markov property holds.
- *Assumption 3*: γ < 1 ensures convergence of the geometric series.

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t, A_t)\,\bigr]$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Update for (s₁, a₁) at τ = 0 (2 marks):**

$$G_{0:3} \;=\; 1 + (0.8)(2) + (0.8)^{2}(3) + (0.8)^{3}(4)$$

$$G_{0:3} \;=\; 1 + 1.6 + 1.92 + 2.048 \;=\; 6.568$$

$$Q(s_1, a_1) \;\leftarrow\; 2 + 0.4\,[\,6.568 - 2\,] \;=\; 2 + 0.4\,(4.568) \;=\; 2 + 1.8272 \;=\; 3.8272$$

**Step 2 — Update for (s₂, a₂) at τ = 1 (2 marks):**

$$G_{1:3} \;=\; 2 + (0.8)(3) + (0.8)^{2}(4)$$

$$G_{1:3} \;=\; 2 + 2.4 + 2.56 \;=\; 6.96$$

$$Q(s_2, a_2) \;\leftarrow\; 3 + 0.4\,[\,6.96 - 3\,] \;=\; 3 + 0.4\,(3.96) \;=\; 3 + 1.584 \;=\; 4.584$$

**Step 3 — Update for (s₃, a₃) at τ = 2 (2 marks):**

$$G_{2:3} \;=\; 3 + (0.8)(4) + (0.8)^{2}(0) \;=\; 3 + 3.2 + 0 \;=\; 6.2$$

$$Q(s_3, a_3) \;\leftarrow\; 4 + 0.4\,[\,6.2 - 4\,] \;=\; 4 + 0.4\,(2.2) \;=\; 4 + 0.88 \;=\; 4.88$$

**Step 4 — Final answer summary (1 mark):**

$$Q(s_1, a_1) \approx 3.83, \quad Q(s_2, a_2) \approx 4.58, \quad Q(s_3, a_3) \approx 4.88$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the bias–variance tradeoff in n-step Sarsa as a function of the parameter **n**. Support your answer with a sketch of the typical error curves.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe how n-step Sarsa can be extended to function approximation for use in robotics with continuous state spaces. Write the corresponding weight update equation and explain each term.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Bias Component (3 marks):**
The bias of n-step Sarsa arises because the bootstrap uses the *current* (imperfect) estimate **Q** rather than the true **Q\***. As **n** increases, the bootstrap term is delayed and **bias decreases** (for **n → ∞**, bias → 0 in episodic tasks).

**Variance Component (3 marks):**
Variance arises from the stochasticity of the **n** accumulated rewards $R_{t+1}, \ldots, R_{t+n}$. By the central limit theorem, summing more i.i.d.-like random variables **increases variance approximately linearly with n**. Thus, variance grows with **n**.

**Combined Tradeoff Sketch (1 mark):**

```
   Error
     ^                       MC
     |                     /
     |                   /
     |       * n_optimal
     |       /\
     |      /  \
     |     /    \      <-- n-step Sarsa
     |    /      \
     | 1-step    \
     | Sarsa      \
     |____________\______> n
                  1   n_optimal   infinity
```

The **optimal n*** lies where the sum of squared bias and variance is minimized — a classic U-shaped error curve.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Replace tabular Q with parameterized function (2 marks):**
Use $\hat{q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}) \approx Q(s, a)$ where **w** is the weight vector of a neural network or linear basis.

**Step 2 — Weight update equation (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{w}_{t+n} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{w}_{t+n} \;+\; \alpha\,\bigl[\,G_{t:t+n} - \hat{q}(S_t, A_t, \mathbf{w})\,\bigr]\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{q}(S_t, A_t, \mathbf{w})$$

**Step 3 — Term-by-term explanation (3 marks):**
- $G_{t:t+n}$: n-step bootstrap target (same as tabular form).
- $\hat{q}(S_t, A_t, \mathbf{w})$: current action-value prediction.
- $\delta_t = G_{t:t+n} - \hat{q}$: n-step TD error.
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{q}$: gradient w.r.t. weights (for linear FA: simply the feature vector; for DNN: backpropagated gradient).
- $\alpha$: learning rate.

This formulation is the foundation of **deep n-step Sarsa** used in continuous-control robotic tasks (e.g., locomotion, manipulation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: n-step Sarsa is an **on-policy TD control** algorithm that bootstraps after exactly **n** real environment transitions.
>
> ✅ **Core Return**: $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$.
>
> ✅ **Update Rule**: $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t, A_t)]$.
>
> ✅ **Update is Deferred**: Update for $(S_t, A_t)$ happens at time $t + n$, not $t + 1$.
>
> ✅ **On-Policy Action**: Bootstrap uses $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ where $A_{t+n} \sim \pi$ (same policy) — NOT $\max_{a'} Q$.
>
> ✅ **Boundary Cases**:
>   - $n = 1$ → classical Sarsa(0).
>   - $n \to \infty$ → Monte Carlo control (episodic only).
>
> ✅ **Bias–Variance**:
>   - Larger **n** → lower bias, higher variance.
>   - Smaller **n** → higher bias, lower variance.
>
> ✅ **Terminal State Handling**: If episode ends before $n$ steps, replace bootstrap term with **0** (absorbing terminal state).
>
> ✅ **Function Approximation Form**: $\mathbf{w}_{t+n} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_{t+n} + \alpha\,[G_{t:t+n} - \hat{q}(S_t, A_t, \mathbf{w})]\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{q}$.
>
> ✅ **Robotic Use Cases**: Manipulator reaching, drone obstacle avoidance, bipedal gait learning — where $n \in [3, 10]$ is common.
>
> ✅ **Convergence**: Under standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins–Monro step-size, GLIE policy), n-step Sarsa converges in the tabular case to the optimal action-value function $Q^{*}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: n-step Off-policy Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** n-step Off-policy Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**n-step Off-policy Learning** is a class of temporal-difference (TD) reinforcement learning algorithms that combine two powerful ideas: (i) **n-step bootstrapping**, in which the return estimate is formed by looking `n` steps into the future before truncating with a bootstrapped value function, and (ii) **off-policy learning**, in which the value function of a *target policy* `π` is learned while the agent actually behaves according to a different *behavior policy* `b`.

The central technical device that enables this combination is the **importance sampling ratio** `ρ`, which corrects for the mismatch between the probabilities of the observed trajectory under the two policies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> *n-step off-policy methods use a target policy π to learn the value function, while the agent follows a behavior policy b. Importance sampling ratios re-weight the n-step returns so that the expected update under b equals the update that would occur under π.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical researcher** trying to estimate how effective a new drug is (target policy `π`). You do not have the budget to run a controlled trial, so you read **observational data** collected by other doctors who prescribed drugs to whoever they thought needed them (behavior policy `b`).

The data is **biased**: doctors gave the drug to the sickest patients. To recover an unbiased estimate, you must **reweight** each patient outcome by the ratio of:
- The probability that **your** protocol would have prescribed the drug to that patient, to
- The probability that **the actual doctor** prescribed it.

Patients whose treatment matches your protocol receive weight `1`. Patients whose treatment was unlikely under your protocol (but happened anyway) receive a large weight, amplifying their effect.

**In RL terms:**
- The **doctor's policy** = behavior policy `b(A|S)`
- **Your protocol** = target policy `π(A|S)`
- The **weight** = importance sampling ratio `ρ = π(A|S) / b(A|S)`
- The **patient outcome** = the observed n-step return

This reweighting is the mathematical miracle that lets an agent learn *as if* it followed `π`, even when it really followed `b`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mental Hook:** Importance sampling answers the question — *"How probable was this trajectory under my target policy, relative to the policy that actually generated it?"*

---

## 1.3 The Three Policy Layers

| Symbol | Name | Role |
|:------:|:-----|:-----|
| `π` | Target Policy | The policy whose value function we want to **learn** |
| `b` | Behavior Policy | The policy actually used to **generate** experience |
| `μ` (often equal to `b` or `π`) | Control Policy | Sometimes used during action selection / control |

---

## 1.4 Why n-step Off-policy Matters in Robotics

In robotic systems, off-policy learning is critical because:
1. **Safety**: A robot cannot explore dangerous actions, but it must still learn the value of those actions for planning.
2. **Data Reuse**: Demonstrations from a human (expert `b`) must be used to learn a new policy `π`.
3. **Sample Efficiency**: Off-policy data from previous tasks can be reused for new tasks (transfer).
4. **Coverage**: A single exploratory policy can produce data for many target policies simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Combining n-step returns with off-policy importance sampling yields algorithms (n-step Sarsa, n-step Expected Sarsa, Tree-backup) that retain the variance-reduction benefit of multi-step returns while correcting for distributional mismatch — a key requirement for real-world robot learning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks — Why Each Piece Exists

### Step 1: The On-policy n-step Return
For an **on-policy** learner (where `b = π`), the n-step return is:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$$

**Why?** A one-step TD method uses only one actual reward plus a bootstrap; this introduces bias from an imperfect `V`. A Monte Carlo method waits until the episode ends; this introduces high variance. n-step is the **interpolation knob** — by adjusting `n`, the designer trades off bias vs. variance.

### Step 2: The Importance Sampling Ratio
When `b ≠ π`, each action `A_k` in the trajectory has probability `π(A_k|S_k)` under the target and `b(A_k|S_k)` under behavior. To reweight the trajectory probability, we take the product:

$$\rho_{t:h} = \prod_{k=t}^{\min(h, T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

**Why a product?** A trajectory's probability factorizes over time-steps. Each ratio corrects one step; multiplying them corrects the whole trajectory.

### Step 3: The Off-policy n-step Return
The naive off-policy n-step return simply multiplies the on-policy n-step return by the importance sampling ratio:

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\text{off}} = \rho_{t:t+n-1} \, G_{t:t+n}$$

where:

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The product is taken from `t` to `t+n-1` (the n actions whose rewards we observed), **not** including the bootstrap step `t+n`, because `V(S_{t+n})` is a state-value estimate and does not involve an action under `b`.

### Step 4: The Update Rule
The value update is then a stochastic approximation step using this corrected return:

$$V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha \, \rho_{t:t+n-1} \big[ G_{t:t+n}^{\text{off}} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \big]$$

The factor `α` is the **constant step-size parameter** (typically small, e.g., **0.1**), and the bracketed term is the n-step TD error.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Notes / Boundary |
|:-:|:--------|:-------|:-----------------|
| 1 | $\rho_t = \dfrac{\pi(A_t \mid S_t)}{b(A_t \mid S_t)}$ | Single-step importance ratio | Equals 1 when `π = b` |
| 2 | $\rho_{t:h} = \displaystyle\prod_{k=t}^{h} \rho_k$ | Trajectory importance ratio | `h = T-1` if episode ends early |
| 3 | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ | On-policy n-step return | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ is the **discount factor** |
| 4 | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \rho_{t:t+n-1}\big[G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t)\big]$ | Off-policy n-step TD update for prediction | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the **step-size** |
| 5 | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha \rho_{t+1:t+n-1}\big[G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t,A_t)\big]$ | Off-policy n-step **Sarsa** update | Product starts at `t+1` because first action was chosen under `b` |
| 6 | $G_{t:t+n}^{\text{ExpSarsa}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1},a) + \dots + \gamma^n Q(S_{t+n},A_{t+n})$ | n-step Expected Sarsa target | Removes inner sampling variance for `S_{t+1}` |
| 7 | Tree-backup error: $\delta_t^{\text{TB}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a\mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1},a) - Q(S_t,A_t)$ | One-step tree-backup TD error | Used as building block for n-step tree-backup |
| 8 | Off-policy n-step Tree-backup: $G_{t:t+n}^{\text{TB}} = Q(S_t,A_t) + \displaystyle\sum_{k=t}^{\min(t+n,T-1)} \Big(\prod_{i=t+1}^{k} \gamma \pi(A_i\mid S_i)\Big) \delta_k^{\text{TB}}$ | Off-policy return **without** importance sampling | Lower variance, requires `π` to be known at all states |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of n-step Off-policy |
|:-------|:---------------------------------|
| **Robotic Manipulation** | Learn grasping policies from human demonstrations (off-policy) using multi-step credit assignment (n-step) |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Replay buffer data collected under an exploratory policy to safely learn an improved policy |
| **Game Playing (AlphaZero-style)** | Reuse old self-play data to refine a target policy without re-exploring the environment |
| **Healthcare RL** | Retrospective off-policy evaluation of treatment policies using logged observational data |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Estimate long-term user-engagement value from logged interactions of a deployed policy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Importance sampling is **unbiased** but **high-variance** when `π` and `b` differ greatly. Tree-backup trades a small bias for a dramatic variance reduction by avoiding product-of-ratios entirely.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the n-step Off-policy TD Error

We will derive the off-policy n-step Sarsa update from the principle that the expected value update under the behavior policy must equal the update that would occur under the target policy.

### Step A — Define the n-step on-policy return
Begin with the standard on-policy n-step return, terminating with a value bootstrap at step `t+n`:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$$

**Logic:** Each step contributes its reward discounted by $\gamma^k$. After `n` steps, we stop using real rewards and bootstrap from the estimated value of the state we reached.

---

### Step B — Compute the on-policy expectation
The expected value update under policy `π` is:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\big[ G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t \big] = \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_t) \mathbb{E}\big[ G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t, A_t = a \big]$$

This is the expectation we **want** to perform, but we **cannot** sample from `π` directly.

---

### Step C — Re-express using the behavior distribution
Using the identity `π(a|S) = ρ · b(a|S)` where `ρ = π(a|S)/b(a|S)`:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\big[ G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t \big] = \sum_a b(a \mid S_t) \, \rho_{t} \, \mathbb{E}\big[ G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t, A_t = a \big]$$

**Logic:** Pull the importance ratio inside the expectation. This means a sample from `b`, multiplied by `ρ_t`, is an unbiased estimator of the `π`-expectation at step `t`.

---

### Step D — Apply the same correction recursively
For the second action `A_{t+1}` we must also reweight. Because the trajectory probability factorizes:

$$\Pr_\pi(\tau) = \Pr_b(\tau) \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \rho_k$$

we obtain the full off-policy expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\big[ G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t \big] = \mathbb{E}_b\Big[ \rho_{t:t+n-1} \, G_{t:t+n} \mid S_t \Big]$$

where the **trajectory importance ratio** is:

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

**Logic:** This is the rigorous derivation of the product-of-ratios formula seen in Section 2. Each time-step adds one multiplicative correction factor.

---

### Step E — Form the stochastic update rule
A stochastic-gradient step minimizing the squared off-policy error gives the n-step Sarsa update:

$$Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) \;=\; Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha \, \rho_{t+1:t+n-1} \Big[ G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \Big]$$

**Logic of the index shift:** The product starts at `t+1` because `A_t` was *already selected* and we are now updating the value of the pair `(S_t, A_t)`. The first action was drawn from `b`, not `π`, so we reweight all *subsequent* actions only.

> [!NOTE]
> For **prediction** (learning `V`), the product starts at `t` (the action that led to `S_t`) because `S_t` is the quantity being updated.

---

### Step F — Sanity check: when `π = b`
Substituting `π = b` gives `ρ_k = 1` for all `k`, so:

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = 1$$

and the update collapses back to the standard on-policy n-step Sarsa:

$$Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) = Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\big[ G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \big]$$

**Logic:** This is the consistency check that proves the off-policy formula is a strict generalization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tree-backup Return (no importance sampling)

The Tree-backup algorithm eliminates importance sampling by **expecting out** the action probabilities at every step along the off-policy trajectory.

### Step 1 — Define the one-step tree-backup error
The one-step TD error, expected over the next action under `π`:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

**Logic:** Instead of sampling an action and risking a mismatch, we sum the Q-values weighted by `π`.

### Step 2 — Extend to two steps
The two-step target uses the one-step error at `t+1`, then bootstraps:

$$G_{t:t+2}^{\text{TB}} = Q(S_t, A_t) + \delta_t + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}) \, \delta_{t+1}$$

**Logic:** The factor `π(A_{t+1}|S_{t+1})` weights the next error by the probability of the action actually taken — but no importance sampling is required because `π` is queried, not corrected against `b`.

### Step 3 — Generalize to n steps (the tree diagram)
The full n-step Tree-backup return is:

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\text{TB}} = Q(S_t, A_t) + \sum_{k=t}^{\min(t+n, T-1)} \Big( \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} \gamma \pi(A_i \mid S_i) \Big) \, \delta_k$$

**Logic:** Each leaf of the n-step lookahead tree contributes an error `δ_k`, weighted by the product of `π` probabilities along the path that was actually traversed. Off-policy actions that were not taken are accounted for by the **expectation** over `π` baked into each `δ_k`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | n-step Sarsa (IS) | n-step Expected Sarsa | n-step Tree-backup |
|:---------|:------------------|:----------------------|:-------------------|
| Importance sampling | Yes (product of ratios) | Yes, but inner ratio replaced by expectation | **None** |
| Variance | High when π ≠ b | Moderate | **Low** |
| Bias | Unbiased | Unbiased (for one step) → approx. after | Slight bias from expectation assumption |
| Requires `b` to be known | Yes | Yes | No (only `π`) |
| Computes `π` probabilities at visited states | No | Yes (at `S_{t+1}` only) | Yes (at every step) |
| Best for | Low-to-moderate `π/b` mismatch | Moderate mismatch | Strong mismatch, large `n` |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose an agent observes the 3-step trajectory (n = 3):

- `S_0 → A_0 → R_1 = 2.0 → S_1 → A_1 → R_2 = 1.0 → S_2 → A_2 → R_3 = 3.0 → S_3`
- Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$
- Step-size $\alpha = 0.2$
- `V(S_3) = 5.0`

**Compute the on-policy n-step return:**

$$G_{0:3} = 2.0 + (0.9)(1.0) + (0.9)^2(3.0) + (0.9)^3(5.0)$$

$$G_{0:3} = 2.0 + 0.9 + 2.43 + 3.645 = 8.975$$

**Suppose** the importance ratios are $\rho_0 = 0.8, \rho_1 = 1.2, \rho_2 = 0.5$:

$$\rho_{0:2} = (0.8)(1.2)(0.5) = 0.48$$

**Off-policy corrected return:**

$$G_{0:3}^{\text{off}} = (0.48)(8.975) = 4.308$$

**Update `V(S_0)` from its old value `V(S_0) = 7.0`:**

$$V(S_0) \leftarrow 7.0 + (0.2)(0.48)\big[8.975 - 7.0\big]$$

$$V(S_0) \leftarrow 7.0 + (0.096)(1.975) = 7.0 + 0.1896 = 7.1896$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **effective step-size** in off-policy n-step updates is `α · ρ_{t:t+n-1}`, which is why step-sizes must be smaller when ratios are large.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers below are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) to comply with the strict Mermaid parser rules. Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric — no markdown formatting, no HTML tables, no special characters inside double quotes.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Off-policy n-step Learning Pipeline

This diagram captures how raw behavior-policy trajectories are transformed into a corrected update for the target policy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    envA["ENVIRONMENT S T"] --> policyA["BEHAVIOR POLICY B"]
    policyA --> actA["ACTION A T FROM B"]
    actA --> envB["ENVIRONMENT S T+1 AND REWARD R T+1"]
    envB --> store["REPLAY BUFFER TRAJECTORY STORE"]
    store --> extr["EXTRACT N STEP TRAJECTORY T TO T+N"]
    extr --> compR["COMPUTE IMPORTANCE RATIO PI OVER B PRODUCT T TO T+N-1"]
    compR --> compG["COMPUTE N STEP RETURN G T T+N"]
    compG --> mul["MULTIPLY RETURN BY IMPORTANCE RATIO PRODUCT"]
    mul --> td["COMPUTE TD ERROR RETURN MINUS CURRENT VALUE"]
    td --> upd["UPDATE V OR Q WITH STEP SIZE ALPHA TIMES RATIO"]
    upd --> targetA["TARGET POLICY PI UPDATED"]
    extr -. optional tree path .-> tb["TREE BACKUP COMPUTATION NO IMPORTANCE SAMPLING"]
    tb --> upd
    targetA --> policyA
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Importance Sampling as a Reweighting Lens

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEHAVIOR
        bstate["S T"]
        bnext["S T+1 FROM B ACTION"]
    end
    subgraph CORRECTION
        ratio["IMPORTANCE RATIO RHO T EQUALS PI OVER B"]
        product["PRODUCT RATIO T TO T+N MINUS 1"]
    end
    subgraph TARGET
        tnext["HYPOTHETICAL S T+1 UNDER PI"]
        tval["V PI OR Q PI TARGET UPDATE"]
    end
    bstate --> bnext
    bnext --> ratio
    ratio --> product
    product --> tval
    tnext -. expectation reference .-> tval
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Which Off-policy n-step Variant to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["NEED TO LEARN OFF POLICY"] --> q1{"CAN YOU COMPUTE PI AT EVERY STATE"}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"IS VARIANCE OR BIAS WORSE IN YOUR PROBLEM"}
    q2 -- VARIANCE --> tree["USE N STEP TREE BACKUP"]
    q2 -- BIAS --> exp["USE N STEP EXPECTED SARSA"]
    q1 -- NO --> is["USE N STEP SARSA WITH IMPORTANCE SAMPLING"]
    is --> q3{"IS PI TO B RATIO NEAR ONE"}
    q3 -- YES --> safe["SAFE USE N STEP SARSA DIRECTLY"]
    q3 -- NO --> smallA["USE SMALL STEP SIZE ALPHA AND SHORT N"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision tree is a heuristic. In practice, students should justify algorithm choice based on the **ratio of policy probabilities**, the **discount horizon**, and the **available compute** for per-state `π` evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`
**Define the importance sampling ratio used in n-step off-policy reinforcement learning and explain why it is required.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The importance sampling ratio at time `t` is defined as:

$$\rho_t = \frac{\pi(A_t \mid S_t)}{b(A_t \mid S_t)}$$

It is the ratio of the probability of the action taken under the **target policy** `π` to the probability of the same action under the **behavior policy** `b`. (1 mark)

When learning off-policy, the trajectory `(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, …, S_{t+n})` is generated by `b`, but the value function we wish to learn is that of `π`. To correct the distributional mismatch, we multiply the n-step return by the product of these ratios for all observed actions. (1 mark)

This reweighting makes the expected value of the corrected return under `b` equal to the expectation under `π`, yielding an **unbiased** estimate of the n-step return for the target policy. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`
**What is the difference between off-policy n-step Sarsa and the n-step Tree-backup algorithm in terms of variance and bias?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Off-policy n-step Sarsa uses **importance sampling ratios** (a product of `π/b` probabilities) to correct the return. This produces an **unbiased** estimate of the target policy's value but suffers from **high variance** when `π` and `b` differ substantially. (1.5 marks)

The n-step Tree-backup algorithm avoids importance sampling entirely. Instead, it takes the expectation over `π` at each step along the trajectory. This trades a small amount of bias (because the expectation may not be exact for stochastic transitions) for a **significant reduction in variance**. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the update rule for the **off-policy n-step TD prediction** algorithm. Clearly state the importance sampling ratio, the off-policy n-step return, and the value update equation. Explain how the formula reduces to the standard on-policy n-step TD update when `π = b`.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an observed 4-step trajectory with rewards `[R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, R_{t+3}, R_{t+4}] = [1, 2, 1, 3]`, discount factor `γ = 0.9`, current value `V(S_t) = 4.5`, and importance ratios at the four visited steps `ρ = [0.9, 1.1, 0.8, 1.2]`, compute: (i) the product of ratios, (ii) the n-step return if `V(S_{t+4}) = 6`, and (iii) the updated `V(S_t)` with step-size `α = 0.3`. Comment on the magnitude of the update.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

Step 1: Define single-step importance ratio (1 mark):

$$\rho_k = \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

Step 2: Define the trajectory importance ratio as the product over `n` actions (1 mark):

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \rho_k$$

Step 3: Define the on-policy n-step return (1 mark):

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

Step 4: Form the off-policy return by multiplying (1 mark):

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\text{off}} = \rho_{t:t+n-1} \cdot G_{t:t+n}$$

Step 5: Write the value update (1 mark):

$$V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha \, \rho_{t:t+n-1}\big[ G_{t:t+n}^{\text{off}} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \big]$$

Step 6: Reduction to on-policy case (1 mark):
When `π = b`, every ratio `ρ_k = 1`, hence `ρ_{t:t+n-1} = 1`. The equation collapses to the standard on-policy n-step TD update with no reweighting.

Step 7: Justification of unbiasedness (1 mark):
Because `E_b[ ρ · X ] = E_π[ X ]`, the corrected return is an unbiased estimator of the target policy's expected n-step return.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**(i) Product of ratios [2 marks]:**

$$\rho_{t:t+3} = (0.9)(1.1)(0.8)(1.2)$$

$$= 0.9 \times 1.1 = 0.99$$

$$= 0.99 \times 0.8 = 0.792$$

$$= 0.792 \times 1.2 = 0.9504$$

**(ii) n-step return [2 marks]:**

$$G_{t:t+4} = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 + 0.9^2 \cdot 1 + 0.9^3 \cdot 3 + 0.9^4 \cdot 6$$

$$= 1 + 1.8 + 0.81 + 2.187 + 3.7694 = 9.5664$$

Off-policy corrected return:

$$G_{t:t+4}^{\text{off}} = (0.9504)(9.5664) = 9.0917$$

**(iii) Updated value [2 marks]:**

$$\Delta = \alpha \, \rho_{t:t+3} \big[ G_{t:t+4}^{\text{off}} - V(S_t) \big]$$

$$= (0.3)(0.9504)(9.0917 - 4.5)$$

$$= (0.3)(0.9504)(4.5917)$$

$$= (0.28512)(4.5917) = 1.3092$$

$$V_{\text{new}}(S_t) = 4.5 + 1.3092 = 5.8092$$

**Comment [1 mark]:** The update magnitude is **moderate (~29% of the old value)**, which is acceptable here because the product of ratios is close to 1, meaning the behavior and target policies are well-aligned. If any single ratio were very small or very large, the update could be unstable.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Tree-backup algorithm** for n-step off-policy learning. Derive the one-step tree-backup error and the n-step tree-backup return formula. State one advantage and one disadvantage compared to importance-sampled n-step Sarsa.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a robot navigation task where the target policy `π` chooses action `right` with probability 0.7 and `up` with probability 0.3 in every state. The behavior policy `b` uses ε-greedy with `ε = 0.2` over three actions `{right, up, left}`, choosing `right` 60% of the time in the initial state and 40% for `up` and `left` combined. (i) Compute the importance sampling ratio `ρ` if the robot actually takes the action `right`. (ii) If this ratio is multiplied by a second ratio of 1.5 from the next step, find the trajectory importance ratio. (iii) State what happens to variance as the product of ratios grows.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Tree-backup algorithm [7 marks]**

Definition (1 mark): The Tree-backup algorithm is an off-policy n-step method that avoids importance sampling by computing an expectation over `π` at each unvisited branch of the n-step lookahead tree.

One-step tree-backup TD error (2 marks):

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

n-step tree-backup return (2 marks):

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\text{TB}} = Q(S_t, A_t) + \sum_{k=t}^{\min(t+n,T-1)} \left( \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} \gamma \pi(A_i \mid S_i) \right) \delta_k$$

**Advantage (1 mark):** Lower variance than importance-sampled n-step Sarsa because there is no multiplicative product of ratios; weights are bounded by `γ < 1` and `π ≤ 1`.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):** Requires the full distribution `π(·|S_k)` at every visited state, and is biased when the environment transition dynamics make the expectation over actions an imperfect substitute for sampling.

---

**Part (b) — Robot Navigation [7 marks]**

**(i) Importance sampling ratio at the first step [3 marks]:**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\pi(\text{right} \mid S_1)}{b(\text{right} \mid S_1)} = \frac{0.7}{0.6} = 1.1667$$

**(ii) Trajectory ratio after two steps [2 marks]:**

$$\rho_{1:2} = (1.1667)(1.5) = 1.75$$

**(iii) Variance behavior [2 marks]:**
As the product of ratios grows, the variance of the importance-weighted return grows **super-linearly** (proportional to the square of the product of ratios in the worst case). When `π` and `b` are poorly aligned — for example, when the target assigns near-zero probability to an action the behavior took — the ratio can become extremely large, dominating the update and causing instability. Practitioners mitigate this by **truncating** ratios (e.g., clipping to a maximum value of `c ≈ 5–10`) or by switching to Tree-backup when mismatch is severe.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on n-step off-policy questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the product bounds.** The product in `ρ_{t:t+n-1}` runs from `t` to `t+n-1` (the *actions taken*), not to `t+n` (the bootstrap state). Marking the wrong index loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Sarsa vs. TD(0).** For n-step **Sarsa**, the update is on the action-value `Q(S_t, A_t)` and the product starts at `t+1`. For n-step **V** prediction, the product starts at `t`. Examiners routinely test this.
> 3. **Skipping the sanity check.** Many full-mark answers explicitly show that `π = b` collapses the formula to the on-policy case. Omitting this loses at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing variance and bias claims.** Importance sampling gives unbiased but high-variance updates; Tree-backup gives biased but low-variance updates. Swapping these is a frequent error.
> 5. **Numerical sign errors in the TD error term.** Remember the bracket is `[G − V]` not `[V − G]`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — n-step Off-policy Learning**

- **Two policies:** `π` (target, to be learned) and `b` (behavior, generates data). Off-policy = `π ≠ b`.
- **Importance sampling ratio:** `ρ_t = π(A_t|S_t) / b(A_t|S_t)` — corrects one step.
- **Trajectory ratio:** product over `n` actions — corrects the full n-step return.
- **Off-policy n-step return:** `G^{off} = ρ_{t:t+n-1} · G_{t:t+n}`.
- **Update:** `V(S_t) ← V(S_t) + α · ρ · [G − V(S_t)]`.
- **Bias vs Variance:** Importance sampling = unbiased, high variance. Tree-backup = slightly biased, low variance.
- **Index convention:** Sarsa update product starts at `t+1`; V-prediction product starts at `t`; Tree-backup uses `γπ` weights along the path.
- **Reduction check:** When `π = b`, all `ρ = 1` and the formula reduces to the standard on-policy n-step update.
- **Effective step-size:** `α · ρ` controls update magnitude — small `α` or clipped `ρ` is needed for stability.
- **Tree-backup vs Sarsa choice:** Use Tree-backup when `π/b` mismatch is large and `π` is known; use Sarsa-with-IS when the mismatch is mild and unbiasedness is critical.
- **Robotics relevance:** Off-policy n-step methods underpin safe robot learning from demonstrations, replay-buffer Q-learning variants, and offline RL pipelines where exploration is impossible.
- **Syllabus tag:** KTU PERAT747, Module 3, Topic — *n-step Off-policy Learning*. Mastery required for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions involving derivations, formulas, and numerical examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Off-policy Learning Without Importance Sampling: The n-step Tree Backup Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Off-policy Learning Without Importance Sampling: The n-step Tree Backup Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **n-step Tree Backup Algorithm** is a temporal-difference (TD) learning method that performs **off-policy control without using importance sampling**. It achieves this by constructing a **backup diagram shaped like a tree**, where the target policy's action probabilities are explicitly used to weight the expected values over the *non-taken* actions at each level, while the *taken* action branches downward into a deeper backup.

The algorithm generalizes the **one-step Tree Backup** (which reduces to Q-learning when the target policy is greedy) to an arbitrary horizon **n**, producing an n-step return that is a weighted sum over an exponentially expanding set of trajectory possibilities — hence the name "tree."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This algorithm is the canonical answer to the question — *"How can an agent learn about the optimal policy while following a different (behavior) policy, WITHOUT suffering the high variance of importance sampling ratios?"*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tourist Planning a Road Trip**

Imagine a tourist named **Agent** driving through a city following a *behavior policy* (a GPS that takes some weird detours). However, the tourist is planning a *target policy* trip (the optimal scenic route). The tourist cannot rewind time and re-experience the bad detours taken by the GPS.

- **Importance Sampling** = "Estimate how much I'd have enjoyed the scenic route by multiplying each weird detour by a huge weight." Result: the numbers are mathematically correct, but **wildly unstable** (high variance).
- **n-step Tree Backup** = "Instead of weighting the detours taken, I'll consider ALL possible scenic-route alternatives at every fork, and use the GPS's actual detours only for the *one specific* action actually executed. Then I sum the weighted alternatives."

In other words, **the agent acknowledges that the behavior policy chose one path, and computes the target policy's expected return as a probability-weighted sum of all other options plus the chosen one's continuation**. This avoids the importance sampling ratio entirely.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Target Policy ($\pi$)** | The policy being learned about (typically the optimal / greedy policy). |
| **Behavior Policy ($b$)** | The policy used to generate experience (often $\varepsilon$-greedy). |
| **Tree Backup Return ($G_{t:t+n}$)** | The n-step return built by weighting non-taken actions by $\pi$ and recursing on the taken action. |
| **Backup Diagram** | A graphical tree structure showing which state-action pairs contribute to the return. |
| **Off-Policy** | Learning about a policy different from the one generating data. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Idea — Why "Tree"?

In the standard **n-step off-policy TD** (with importance sampling), we have:

$$G_{t:t+n} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$$

To make this off-policy, each transition gets multiplied by an importance sampling ratio $\rho_{t:t+k} = \prod_{i=t+1}^{t+k} \pi(A_i \vert S_i) / b(A_i \vert S_i)$. This is unbiased but **high-variance**.

The n-step Tree Backup **eliminates** $\rho$ entirely by **summing the expectation over all actions** at each step, weighted by $\pi$, but only on the branch that was *actually* taken does it continue the recursion deeper. This forms a tree.

### 2.2 The n-step Tree Backup Return — General Formula

The n-step return for the tree backup, starting at time $t$ with horizon $n$, is defined recursively:

$$G_{t:t+n} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) \, Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) \;+\; \gamma \, \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) \, G_{t+1:t+n}$$

with the leaf target $G_{t:t+1} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t}(S_{t+1}, a)$.

### 2.3 Derivation Intuition Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 (Leaf, n=1):** A one-step backup treats the next state as terminal. The return is the immediate reward plus the discounted expected Q-value under $\pi$.
- **Step 2 (Tree structure at n=2):** At state $S_{t+1}$, the action $A_{t+1}$ was actually taken by $b$. The expectation under $\pi$ includes ALL actions, not just $A_{t+1}$. We split the sum:
  - **Non-taken actions** ($a \neq A_{t+1}$): replaced by their $Q$-value (a "leaf").
  - **Taken action** ($a = A_{t+1}$): recurse one level deeper using the next reward and the next state.
- **Step 3 (n-step generalization):** The recursion continues for $n$ levels, building an exponential tree of action possibilities.
- **Step 4 (No importance sampling):** The ratio $\pi/b$ never appears because we have already integrated out the action selection — we are computing the expectation under $\pi$ directly, not reweighting samples from $b$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t}(S_{t+1}, a)$ | 1-step Tree Backup return (leaf) |
| 2 | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1}) G_{t+1:t+n}$ | n-step Tree Backup return (recursive) |
| 3 | $Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) = Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t)]$ | Online update for the learned state-action value |
| 4 | $\pi = $ greedy w.r.t. $Q$ | When $\pi$ is greedy, only the argmax action has nonzero probability, simplifying the formula |
| 5 | If $\pi$ is greedy: $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} 0 + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1} G_{t+1:t+n}$ | For greedy $\pi$, formula reduces to Q-learning-like behavior |
| 6 | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Discount factor (note: 1-step return is **2-step** in this context due to leaf definition) |
| 7 | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ | Learning rate; constant for stationary problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the "1-step" terminology:** The "1-step" tree backup return $G_{t:t+1}$ actually contains **one reward** $R_{t+1}$ and a bootstrap from $Q$, equivalent to a standard one-step TD target.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics & Autonomous Systems:** When physical interaction with the environment is expensive/dangerous, the robot follows a *safe exploration policy* but learns the *optimal control policy* without retraining. Tree backup is lower-variance than importance-sampled off-policy methods, leading to **more stable learning** with fewer samples.
- **Recommendation Systems:** The deployed recommender may be a stochastic A/B test policy; the target is the optimal recommendation policy.
- **Industrial Control:** The behavior policy may include operator overrides; the learned policy should still converge to optimal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the n-step Tree Backup Return

We will derive the n-step return by unrolling the recursive definition for **n = 1, 2, 3** to make the pattern concrete.

#### 3.1.1 Base Case — n = 1 (One-Step Tree Backup)

Starting from the leaf definition:

$$G_{t:t+1} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) \, Q_t(S_{t+1}, a)$$

- **Logic:** $R_{t+1}$ is the immediate reward. The future is approximated by the expected Q-value under $\pi$ at the next state. We sum over **all** actions $a$ weighted by $\pi(a \vert S_{t+1})$.
- **No recursion needed** — this is the leaf.
- This is precisely the **one-step Q-learning target when $\pi$ is greedy**, because the greedy $\pi$ puts mass 1 on the argmax.

#### 3.1.2 Inductive Step — Unrolling for n = 2

Substitute the general recursive formula with $n=2$:

$$G_{t:t+2} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) \, Q_{t+1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \, \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) \, G_{t+1:t+2}$$

Now expand $G_{t+1:t+2}$ using the leaf definition at the next step:

$$G_{t+1:t+2} = R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert S_{t+2}) \, Q_{t+1}(S_{t+2}, a')$$

Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+2} =\; & R_{t+1} \\
& + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) \, Q_{t+1}(S_{t+1}, a) \\
& + \gamma \, \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) \Big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert S_{t+2}) \, Q_{t+1}(S_{t+2}, a') \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first two lines are rewards and Q-evaluations at depth 1. The third line carries forward the *chosen* action $A_{t+1}$ and recurses one more level. Notice the tree has **1 branch at depth 1** (the chosen action) and **all actions considered at depth 2** (the leaves). Total leaf-action combinations = $|\mathcal{A}|^1$ at depth 1 + $|\mathcal{A}|^1$ for the recursive step that itself has $|\mathcal{A}|$ leaves = 2 effective horizons, but the tree has grown to $1 + |\mathcal{A}|$ leaves in total at the deepest level.

#### 3.1.3 Inductive Step — n = 3 (Showing the Tree Pattern)

Now extend by one more level. The pattern of $G_{t+1:t+3}$ is the same as $G_{t:t+2}$ shifted in time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t+1:t+3} =\; & R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+2}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+2}) Q_{t+2}(S_{t+2}, a) \\
& + \gamma \pi(A_{t+2} \vert S_{t+2}) G_{t+2:t+3}
\end{aligned}
$$

And $G_{t+2:t+3} = R_{t+3} + \gamma \sum_{a''} \pi(a'' \vert S_{t+3}) Q_{t+2}(S_{t+3}, a'')$.

Substituting fully into $G_{t:t+3}$ produces **three reward terms** $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, R_{t+3}$ and a tree of $Q$-evaluations at leaves with discount factors $\gamma^k$.

**Tree Shape (for n=3):**
- Root state $S_t$ — only the specific $S_t, A_t$ update.
- Branch 1: action $A_{t+1}$ taken → state $S_{t+1}$.
  - Sub-branch 1: action $A_{t+2}$ taken → state $S_{t+2}$.
    - Leaves: all $a \in \mathcal{A}$ with weight $\pi(a \vert S_{t+3})$.
  - Sub-branch 2: any $a \neq A_{t+2}$ at $S_{t+2}$ → leaf Q-value.
- Branch 2: any $a \neq A_{t+1}$ at $S_{t+1}$ → leaf Q-value.

Total number of leaf Q-evaluations = $|\mathcal{A}|^{n-1}$? No — actually the tree has $1 + (n-1) \cdot |\mathcal{A}|$ leaves in total, with weights that **sum to 1** by construction.

### 3.2 Specialization to Greedy Target Policy (Connection to Q-Learning)

If $\pi$ is greedy, then $\pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) = 1$ only when $A_{t+1} = \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$, and $0$ otherwise.

The recursive formula collapses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} & = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} 0 \cdot Q + \gamma \cdot \pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1}) \cdot G_{t+1:t+n} \\
& = R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{A_{t+1} = \arg\max Q\}} \cdot G_{t+1:t+n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** When $\pi$ is greedy and the behavior $b$ chose the greedy action, the algorithm **continues recursing**; when $b$ chose a non-greedy action, the recursion **terminates with 0**. This is the Q-learning-like behavior, but with multi-step targets.

### 3.3 The Update Equation and Pseudocode Outline

The action-value update (note: $Q$ is updated only for the $(S_t, A_t)$ pair — the root of the tree):

$$Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) \doteq Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \big]$$

**Algorithm Steps (off-line, episodic):**
1. **Input:** policy $\pi$ to be learned, behavior policy $b$, step size $\alpha \in (0,1]$, $n \geq 1$.
2. **Initialize** $Q(s,a)$ arbitrarily for all $s, a$; $Q(\text{terminal}, \cdot) = 0$.
3. **For each episode:**
   a. Generate trajectory $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \ldots$ using $b$.
   b. For $t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ until $T$ (terminal):
      - Compute the tree backup return $G_{t:t+n}$ using stored rewards and current $Q$.
      - If $t+n < T$: use the $n$-step backup; else, truncate to the terminal horizon.
      - Update $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t, A_t)]$.
4. **Output:** $Q \approx q_\pi$.

### 3.4 Comparison with Importance-Sampled Off-Policy

| Property | Importance-Sampled n-step | n-step Tree Backup |
|---|---|---|
| Uses $\rho_{t:t+k-1}$? | Yes | **No** |
| Variance | High | **Lower** |
| Bias | Unbiased (with proper $\rho$) | Slight (uses $Q$ at leaves) |
| Computation | Stores products of $\rho$'s | Stores probabilities under $\pi$ |
| Convergence | Yes, under standard conditions | Yes, tabular convergence proven |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Tree backup trades a tiny bit of bias (from using $Q$ estimates at non-sampled leaves) for **substantial variance reduction**, making it preferable in high-stakes robotics scenarios where sample efficiency matters.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Tree Backup Diagram (3-Step) — Mermaid Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture flow** of how the n-step Tree Backup aggregates information from a 3-level tree. Each node represents a logical aggregation step, and the edges represent the data flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["STATE S_t ACTION A_t"] --> n1R["REWARD R_t+1"]
    rootA --> n1NT["NON-TAKEN ACTIONS at S_t+1 weighted by pi"]
    rootA --> n1T["TAKEN ACTION A_t+1 weighted by pi branch"]

    n1R --> n1Sum["DISCOUNTED AGGREGATION gamma factor"]
    n1NT --> n1Sum
    n1T --> n2Branch

    n2Branch --> n2R["REWARD R_t+2"]
    n2Branch --> n2NT["NON-TAKEN ACTIONS at S_t+2 weighted by pi"]
    n2Branch --> n2T["TAKEN ACTION A_t+2 weighted by pi branch"]

    n2R --> n2Sum["DISCOUNTED AGGREGATION gamma squared factor"]
    n2NT --> n2Sum
    n2T --> n3Leaf

    n3Leaf --> n3Q["Q VALUE ESTIMATES at S_t+3 for all actions"]
    n3Leaf --> n3Pi["PROBABILITIES pi at S_t+3"]
    n3Q --> n3Sum["EXPECTED VALUE SUM pi times Q"]
    n3Pi --> n3Sum

    n2Sum --> finalG["COMPOSITE RETURN G_t:t+3"]
    n3Sum --> finalG
    n1Sum --> finalG

    finalG --> update["Q UPDATE Q S_t A_t increases by alpha times G minus Q"]
```

### 4.2 Algorithmic Block Diagram — Information Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TRAJECTORY["EXPERIENCE GENERATION UNDER BEHAVIOR b"]
        T1["STATE S_t ACTION A_t"] --> T2["ENVIRONMENT STEP"]
        T2 --> T3["REWARD R_t+1 NEXT STATE S_t+1"]
        T3 --> T4["ACTION A_t+1 via b"]
    end

    subgraph TREEBACKUP["TREE BACKUP COMPUTATION"]
        T4 --> C1["RECURSIVE G COMPUTATION at all levels"]
        C1 --> C2["WEIGHT BY pi for non taken actions"]
        C2 --> C3["WEIGHT BY pi times recurse for taken action"]
        C3 --> C4["FORM n STEP RETURN G_t:t+n"]
    end

    subgraph VALUEUPDATE["VALUE FUNCTION UPDATE"]
        C4 --> U1["COMPUTE TD ERROR delta = G - Q"]
        U1 --> U2["Q S_t A_t increases by alpha times delta"]
        U2 --> U3["STORE UPDATED Q TABLE"]
    end

    U3 --> POLICY["TARGET POLICY pi BUILT FROM Q"]
    POLICY -.->|guides| TRAJECTORY
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Tree Structure (Plain-Text Annotation)

For the **n=3 tree backup** starting at $(S_t, A_t)$:

```
                        (S_t, A_t)  ← root, only THIS gets Q-updated
                             │
            ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
            │                                 │
       R_{t+1} term              Taken action A_{t+1} under π weight
            │                                 │
   γ·Σ_{a≠A_{t+1}} π(a|S_{t+1})Q(S_{t+1},a)     │
            │                                 │
            │                  ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
            │                  │                             │
            │              R_{t+2} term         Taken action A_{t+2} under π weight
            │                  │                             │
            │     γ²·Σ_{a≠A_{t+2}} π(a|S_{t+2})Q(S_{t+2},a)  │
            │                  │                             │
            │                  │         γ³·Σ_a π(a|S_{t+3})Q(S_{t+3},a)
            │                  │         ← deepest leaf (full expectation)
            │                  │
            └─────────┬────────┘
                      ▼
            G_{t:t+3}  (n-step tree backup return)
                      ▼
        Q(S_t, A_t) ← Q(S_t, A_t) + α[G_{t:t+3} - Q(S_t, A_t)]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Insight:** The tree **widens** at each level on the "non-taken action" branches (each becomes a leaf immediately with weight $\pi(a)$) and **deepens** by one level on the "taken action" branch. This is why it is called a *tree* — the action space *branches* out at every step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Why does the n-step Tree Backup algorithm not require importance sampling ratios, unlike standard off-policy n-step methods?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The n-step Tree Backup algorithm avoids importance sampling by **explicitly computing the expectation under the target policy $\pi$** at every non-sampled action branch of the backup tree. Instead of reweighting a sample drawn from behavior policy $b$ by the ratio $\pi(a)/b(a)$, the algorithm **sums over all actions** with weights $\pi(a \vert S_{t+1})$ directly, and only the **single action** $A_{t+1}$ actually taken by $b$ continues the recursion deeper. This means the *behavior policy's choice is only used to determine which branch to extend*, while the target policy's distribution is used to weight all alternatives — entirely sidestepping the need for importance sampling ratios and thus avoiding their high variance. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the recursive definition of the n-step Tree Backup return $G_{t:t+n}$ and identify the base case.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The recursive definition is:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) G_{t+1:t+n}$$

**Base case (n=1):**

$$G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_t(S_{t+1}, a)$$

The base case treats the next state as a terminal of the recursion, with the future value given by the expected Q-value under $\pi$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the motivation behind the n-step Tree Backup algorithm. How does it differ conceptually from importance-sampled off-policy n-step TD methods? Discuss the **variance–bias trade-off**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **2-step Tree Backup return** $G_{t:t+2}$ explicitly. Show all intermediate substitution steps. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

The n-step Tree Backup algorithm is motivated by a key limitation of importance-sampled off-policy methods: although the importance sampling ratio $\rho_{t:t+k-1} = \prod \pi(A_i)/b(A_i)$ produces an **unbiased** estimator, it has **exponentially growing variance** with the horizon $n$. In real-world robotics — where data is noisy and samples are expensive — this variance can render learning infeasible.

The Tree Backup algorithm **eliminates** the importance sampling ratio by changing the *form* of the return. Instead of reweighting sampled trajectories, it **directly computes the expected return under $\pi$** by summing over all possible actions at each level, using the action actually taken by $b$ only to identify the branch that should be **extended** one more level deeper.

**Variance–Bias Trade-off:**

| Aspect | Importance-Sampled n-step | Tree Backup |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | Unbiased | Slight bias (uses estimated $Q$ at leaves) |
| Variance | High (grows with $n$) | **Low** (no ratio products) |
| Sample efficiency | Low (needs many samples) | **High** (good for robotics) |
| Stability | Sensitive to rare events | **Robust** |

The tree backup trades a **small bias** (from bootstrapping on the $Q$ estimates at the leaves of the tree) for a **substantial variance reduction**. In tabular settings with sufficient state-action coverage, this bias vanishes as $Q \to q_\pi$, giving us the **best of both worlds**. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1:** Start with the recursive definition for general $n$:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) G_{t+1:t+n}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $n = 2$:

$$G_{t:t+2} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) G_{t+1:t+2}$$

**Step 3:** Expand $G_{t+1:t+2}$ using the base case (n=1) shifted by one time step:

$$G_{t+1:t+2} = R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert S_{t+2}) Q_{t+1}(S_{t+2}, a')$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the expression from Step 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+2} =\; & R_{t+1} \\
& + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+1}(S_{t+1}, a) \\
& + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1}) \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert S_{t+2}) Q_{t+1}(S_{t+2}, a') \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (optional cleanup):** Verify that the weights sum to 1. The coefficient of $R_{t+1}$ is 1, the coefficients on $Q_{t+1}(S_{t+1}, a)$ for $a \neq A_{t+1}$ are $\gamma \pi(a \vert S_{t+1})$ and the coefficient on the term involving $S_{t+2}$ is $\gamma \pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1})$, which itself is multiplied by $\pi$-weights summing to 1. Hence the overall probability mass is conserved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Draw the **backup diagram** for the 3-step Tree Backup algorithm. Label all branches, leaves, weights, and the single Q-value that gets updated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot is learning a control policy using an exploratory behavior policy $b$ that is $\varepsilon$-greedy with $\varepsilon = 0.1$, while the target policy $\pi$ is **fully greedy** (Q-learning style). Show how the n-step Tree Backup return **simplifies** under these conditions and explain its connection to standard Q-learning. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Tree Backup Diagram for n = 3 (text-form annotation):**

The backup originates at the root node $(S_t, A_t)$, which is the **only** state-action pair whose Q-value will be updated. Three branches extend from it:

- **Branch 1 (the taken-action spine):**
  - $S_t \to S_{t+1}$ via $A_{t+1}$, weighted by $\pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1}) = 1$ (greedy).
  - $S_{t+1} \to S_{t+2}$ via $A_{t+2}$, weighted by $\pi(A_{t+2}\vert S_{t+2}) = 1$ (greedy).
  - At $S_{t+3}$: a **full set of leaves**, one for every action $a \in \mathcal{A}$, each weighted by $\pi(a \vert S_{t+3})$.
- **Branch 2 (non-taken at $S_{t+1}$):** Each $a \neq A_{t+1}$ becomes a **leaf** with weight $\pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) = 0$ (greedy) → contributes nothing.
- **Branch 3 (non-taken at $S_{t+2}$):** Each $a \neq A_{t+2}$ becomes a **leaf** with weight $\pi(a \vert S_{t+2}) = 0$ (greedy) → contributes nothing.

The composite return is:

$$G_{t:t+3} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 \sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+3}) Q(S_{t+3}, a)$$

This is the same target as multi-step Q-learning when the behavior policy happened to take greedy actions at $S_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+2}$. If the behavior policy chose a non-greedy action at any step, that branch is **terminated with 0**, and the return is built from a shorter horizon. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1:** When $\pi$ is fully greedy, $\pi(a \vert S) = 1$ if $a = \arg\max_a Q(S,a)$ and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the recursive definition:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} 0 \cdot Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}\{A_{t+1} = \arg\max Q\} \cdot G_{t+1:t+n}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
G_{t:t+n} = 
\begin{cases}
R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot G_{t+1:t+n}, & \text{if } A_{t+1} = \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) \\
R_{t+1}, & \text{otherwise (chain terminates)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4:** Connection to Q-learning. Standard one-step Q-learning is the case $n=1$ with greedy $\pi$:

$$G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$$

This is the n=1 tree backup with greedy $\pi$ and **without** importance sampling (since we take the max). For $n > 1$ with greedy $\pi$, the algorithm produces a **multi-step Q-learning target** that is conceptually an $n$-step extension of Q-learning, but without the need for importance sampling — instead, the algorithm naturally handles the off-policy correction by **truncating the chain at any step where the behavior policy took a non-greedy action**. The exploration noise $\varepsilon$ in $b$ is automatically absorbed by the early termination of the recursion, with no $\rho$-ratio product accumulating. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **MOST COMMON MARK LOSS:** Students often write the formula for n-step Tree Backup with the **importance sampling ratio** $\rho$ included. This is a fundamental conceptual error — the whole point of tree backup is to *avoid* $\rho$.
> - Students forget that the **base case is $G_{t:t+1}$** (one reward + bootstrap) and not $G_{t:t}$.
> - When asked to draw the diagram, students must **label the weights $\pi$ on every branch** and indicate that **only the root $(S_t, A_t)$ is updated**, not the leaves.
> - In the greedy specialization, students forget to mention the **indicator function** $\mathbb{1}\{A_{t+1} = \arg\max Q\}$ that governs when the recursion continues.
> - The state-action Q update uses $G_{t:t+n}$ with the **$Q$ from time $t+n-1$** (the most recent estimate at the time of the update), not from time $t$. This off-policy/off-line nature is often missed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Off-policy without importance sampling:** Tree backup achieves off-policy learning by **integrating** over the target policy's action distribution, not reweighting samples.
- [x] **Why "tree"?** The backup structure branches at every step: non-taken actions become leaves; the taken action extends the tree.
- [x] **Recursive formula:** $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a \neq A_{t+1}} \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma \pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1}) G_{t+1:t+n}$.
- [x] **Base case (n=1):** $G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q_t(S_{t+1}, a)$.
- [x] **Update rule:** $Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t)]$.
- [x] **Only root updated:** Only the $(S_t, A_t)$ pair receives the Q-value update; leaf Q-values are not updated directly.
- [x] **Greedy target policy → Q-learning-like behavior:** When $\pi$ is greedy, the recursion continues only if $b$ chose a greedy action; non-greedy actions terminate the chain at 0.
- [x] **Variance vs. Bias:** Tree backup has **lower variance** but **slight bias** (vanishes as $Q$ converges) compared to importance-sampled methods.
- [x] **Behavior policy role:** $b$ only determines *which action's branch* to extend; the weighting of all alternatives comes from $\pi$.
- [x] **Convergence:** Tabular convergence is proven under standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro, all state-action pairs visited infinitely often, $\pi$ greedy in the limit).
- [x] **Sample efficiency:** Lower variance → fewer samples needed → ideal for **robotics** with expensive real-world interactions.
- [x] **Connection to expected SARSA:** One-step tree backup with stochastic $\pi$ is identical to **Expected Sarsa** — another off-policy algorithm without importance sampling.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
