topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Value Function Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — VALUE FUNCTION APPROXIMATION (VFA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Function Approximation (VFA)** is the family of methods in Reinforcement Learning (RL) in which the exact value function $V_\pi(s)$ or $Q_\pi(s, a)$ — which maps every state (or state-action pair) to its expected return — is replaced by a **parameterized function** $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (or $\hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$), where $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a vector of tunable weights. The agent **learns $\boldsymbol{\theta}$** from sampled experience so that $\hat{V}$ closely imitates the true value function over the entire state space.

In tabular RL (small, discrete environments like a $4 \times 4$ grid), every state can hold its own number in a lookup table. As soon as the state space becomes large (e.g., a robot arm with 6 joint angles discretized at $1°$ resolution → $360^6 \approx 2 \times 10^{15}$ states) or **continuous** (camera pixel inputs, joint torques, lidar scans), a table becomes physically impossible. VFA solves this by **generalizing** experience from visited states to **unvisited, similar states**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Map" Intuition

Imagine you are a meteorologist. You have never measured the temperature in *Kochi* on *August 14* directly, but you have:
1. Temperatures from surrounding cities on that day,
2. Temperatures in Kochi on neighbouring days,
3. A general climate model of Kerala.

You **interpolate** the unknown value using the model and the known samples. VFA does precisely the same thing for the value function:
- The **known samples** = observed returns $G_t$ from states the agent has visited.
- The **model** = a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (linear, decision tree, neural network).
- The **interpolation** = tuning $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to minimize the error between predictions and targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical for robotics?** A robot's state space (joint positions, velocities, end-effector pose, contact forces) is overwhelmingly **continuous**. A humanoid has ~30+ degrees of freedom, each continuous. Without VFA, classical RL is useless on such platforms. VFA is the bridge that makes RL deployable on real robots (e.g., quadruped locomotion, dexterous manipulation, autonomous driving).

### 1.3 The Curse of Dimensionality — Why Tabular Methods Fail

| Aspect | Tabular RL | Value Function Approximation |
|---|---|---|
| State representation | One entry per state | Compact parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| Memory | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ | $O(d)$ where $d \ll \vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ |
| Generalization | None — visit each state to learn it | Yes — similar states share parameters |
| Continuous states | Impossible | Native support |
| Robotics applicability | Toy worlds (CartPole only) | Real platforms (humanoids, drones) |

### 1.4 Categories of Function Approximators

> [!NOTE]
> **Three major families used in KTU syllabus and modern robotics:**

1. **Linear Function Approximation** — $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(s)$, where $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a hand-crafted feature vector (e.g., tile coding, radial basis functions, Fourier basis). *Mathematically tractable, provably convergent (under TD(0) with linear FA).*
2. **Neural Network Approximation** — Deep Q-Network (DQN), Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Universal approximators, scale to high-dimensional inputs (images, point clouds). *Less stable, requires tricks (target networks, experience replay, gradient clipping).*
3. **Decision Tree / Locally Weighted Regression** — Non-parametric; used in specialised settings like Fitted Q-Iteration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Objective — Mean Squared Value Error (VE)

The approximation is judged by how well it imitates the **true value function** $V_\pi(s)$ under the **on-policy distribution** $\mu(s)$ — the fraction of time the agent spends in state $s$ when following $\pi$.

$$
\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \,\bigl[\, V_\pi(s) \;-\; \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal goal of VFA:** Find $\boldsymbol{\theta}^\ast = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
> In practice, $V_\pi(s)$ is unknown; we substitute an **estimate** (Monte-Carlo return $G_t$ or TD/RBellman target).

### 2.2 On-Policy Distribution $\mu(s)$

For an ergodic Markov chain under policy $\pi$:
$$
\mu(s) \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbb{P}(S_t = s \mid S_0, \pi)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\mu(s)$ weights the error by **how often** the agent visits $s$ — frequent states matter more than rare ones. This prevents the model from "wasting capacity" on unreachable states.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Symbol meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Approximate value | $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Parameterized; $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 2 | Value error | $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_s \mu(s) \bigl[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr]^2$ | On-policy weighted MSE |
| 3 | Linear form | $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(s)$ | $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ feature vector |
| 4 | Gradient (linear) | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Why linear FA is computationally simple |
| 5 | Stochastic gradient descent (generic) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \bigl[\, \text{target} - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]^2$ | $\alpha$ = step size (learning rate) |
| 6 | **Gradient Monte Carlo** | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) \,\bigr]\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Unbiased; uses episodic return $G_t$ |
| 7 | **Semi-gradient TD(0)** | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) \,\bigr]\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Bootstrapped; **semi**-gradient because target depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| 8 | TD(0) error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Scalar instantaneous error |
| 9 | Robbins-Monro conditions | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty, \quad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^2 < \infty$ | Required for stochastic approximation convergence |
| 10 | n-step return | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^n \hat{V}(S_{t+n}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Used in n-step semi-gradient TD |
| 11 | $\lambda$-return | $G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_{t:t+n}$ | Geometric blend of n-step returns |
| 12 | **LSTD matrix form** | $\hat{\mathbf{A}} = \sum_t \mathbf{x}(S_t) \bigl[\mathbf{x}(S_t) - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\bigr]^T$ | $\hat{\mathbf{A}} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{b}$ |
| 13 | LSTD RHS vector | $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \sum_t \mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}$ | Empirical second-moment term |
| 14 | LSTD closed-form | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \hat{\mathbf{A}}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ | Direct solution, no step-size tuning |
| 15 | Q-approximation | $\hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | State-action value; foundation of DQN |
| 16 | **DQN loss** | $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}} \bigl[\bigl(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr)^2\bigr]$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ = target network (frozen) |
| 17 | Deadly triad components | Off-policy + Bootstrapping + Function Approximation | Causes **divergence** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $S_t$ = state at time $t$; $R_{t+1}$ = reward after transition; $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ = discount factor; $G_t$ = Monte-Carlo return; $\alpha$ = step size; $\lambda$ = eligibility-trace decay; $\mathcal{D}$ = replay buffer; $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ = target network parameters.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robot locomotion** (Boston Dynamics, MIT Mini-Cheetah): VFA + policy gradient on joint angle state spaces with $\sim 10^{18}$ effective states.
- **Autonomous driving** (Wayve, Tesla FSD): CNN-based VFA over raw camera pixels — state $s$ = image, $\hat{V}$ = deep network.
- **Industrial manipulators**: Linear FA with engineered features (e.g., distance to target) for fast, sample-efficient learning.
- **Drone racing**: Continuous-state VFA achieves superhuman performance (e.g., Swift, 2021 Nature paper).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient Monte-Carlo Update Rule

**Goal:** Find $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ minimizing $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_s \mu(s) \bigl[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr]^2$. Since $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$, we replace $V_\pi(s)$ with a sample $G_t$ at the visited state.

**Step 1 — Sample-based loss at time $t$:**
$$
\mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]^2
$$

> Text: The squared error between the observed episodic return $G_t$ and the network's prediction.

**Step 2 — Gradient of the loss with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:**
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; -2 \,\bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

> Text: Apply chain rule: $d/d\boldsymbol{\theta}(u^2) = 2u \cdot du/d\boldsymbol{\theta}$, with $u = G_t - \hat{V}$.

**Step 3 — Stochastic gradient descent step:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> Text: The $1/2$ cancels the factor of $2$ in the gradient.

**Step 4 — Substitute (simplified):**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) \,\bigr]\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> Text: This is the **Gradient Monte Carlo (GMC)** update. It is **unbiased** because the target $G_t$ does not depend on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

**Step 5 — Specialisation to linear FA:** Substitute $\hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(S_t)$ and $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V} = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$:
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, G_t - \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^T \mathbf{x}(S_t) \,\bigr]\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

> Text: This is the **Least-Mean-Squares (LMS) / Widrow-Hoff rule** — a famous result in adaptive filtering, rediscovered in RL.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Semi-Gradient TD(0) Update Rule

**Goal:** Replace the expensive MC return with a one-step bootstrap target — useful in continuing (non-episodic) tasks like robot balance.

**Step 1 — One-step return (TD target):**
$$
\tilde{V}_t \;\equiv\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> Text: Estimate of $V_\pi(S_t)$ using the immediate reward and the next state's value.

**Step 2 — TD error:**
$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) \;-\; \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> Text: Difference between TD target and current prediction; can be positive or negative.

**Step 3 — Loss at time $t$ (squared TD error):**
$$
\mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \delta_t^2
$$

> Text: The $1/2$ is conventional to simplify the gradient.

**Step 4 — Partial / semi-gradient (treat target as constant w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$):**
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}_t \;\approx\; -\,\delta_t \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why "semi"-gradient?** We differentiate *only* through $\hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ and **ignore** the dependence of the target $\hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. If we differentiated the target too, the fixed point would become a true Bellman solution, but the algorithm is no longer a true gradient and is no longer guaranteed to converge with non-linear FA.

**Step 5 — Semi-gradient TD(0) update:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \, \delta_t \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

> Text: Expand $\delta_t$: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\bigr] \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$.

**Step 6 — Linear case:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^T \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^T \mathbf{x}(S_t) \,\bigr]\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

> Text: Converges (with appropriate $\alpha$ decay) to the **TD fixed point**: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$, which is **not** the minimum of $\overline{VE}$ in general — it has a "bias" called the **TD bias** $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{TD}) \le \overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{VE})$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Least-Squares TD (LSTD)

**Goal:** Solve the TD fixed point $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ directly from offline data, avoiding step-size tuning.

**Step 1 — Linear FA TD fixed point (in expectation):**
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl(\mathbf{x}(S_t)^T \boldsymbol{\theta} - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})^T \boldsymbol{\theta} - R_{t+1}\bigr)\,\bigr] = 0
$$

> Text: Stationarity of the semi-gradient TD update under on-policy distribution $\mu$.

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl(\mathbf{x}(S_t)^T - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})^T\bigr)\,\bigr] \boldsymbol{\theta} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}\,\bigr]
$$

> Text: Pull $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ out of the expectation (it is a constant in expectation), and rearrange matrix multiplication.

**Step 3 — Define matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{b}$:**
$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl(\mathbf{x}(S_t)^T - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})^T\bigr)\,\bigr], \quad \mathbf{b} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}\,\bigr]
$$

> Text: $\mathbf{A}$ is a $d \times d$ matrix; $\mathbf{b}$ is a $d \times 1$ vector.

**Step 4 — Empirical estimates from samples:**
$$
\hat{\mathbf{A}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl[\mathbf{x}(S_t) - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\bigr]^T, \quad \hat{\mathbf{b}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}
$$

> Text: Use the sample mean as an unbiased estimator of the expectation.

**Step 5 — Closed-form LSTD solution:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{LSTD} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{A}}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{b}}
$$

> Text: Solved by matrix inversion (or pseudo-inverse if $\hat{\mathbf{A}}$ is singular). Data-efficient because it uses all transitions in the batch optimally. Cost: $O(d^2 n + d^3)$ — prohibitive for large $d$, hence the variant **Recursive Least-Squares TD (RLSTD)** for streaming data.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability & Convergence Properties

| Algorithm | Target | Gradient Type | Bias | Convergence (linear FA) | Convergence (non-linear FA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gradient MC** | $G_t$ | True gradient | None (unbiased) | Yes, to $\overline{VE}$ min | Yes (to local min) |
| **Semi-gradient TD(0)** | $R + \gamma \hat{V}(S')$ | Semi-gradient | TD bias | Yes, to TD fixed point | **Not guaranteed** |
| **Semi-gradient TD($\lambda$)** | $G_t^{\lambda}$ | Semi-gradient | Smaller bias | Yes | **Not guaranteed** |
| **LSTD** | Same as TD(0) | Closed-form | Same as TD(0) | Yes (data-efficient) | N/A (linear only) |
| **LSTD($\lambda$)** | $\lambda$-return | Closed-form | Smaller | Yes | N/A (linear only) |
| **DQN** | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ | Semi-gradient | Off-policy bias | — | Empirically stable (with target net + replay) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Deadly Triad** — Sutton & Barto (2018): Off-policy learning + Bootstrapping + Function Approximation can interact to **destabilize** Q-learning. Empirically, the value estimates diverge to infinity. Stability fixes: target network (DQN), Polyak averaging, double estimators (Double DQN), distributional RL, retrace.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VFA Pipeline in an RL Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Environment] --> B[State s_t Sensor Readings]
    B --> C[Feature Extractor x_s_t]
    C --> D[Parameterized Function Approximator]
    E[Parameter Vector theta] --> D
    D --> F[Predicted Value V_hat_s_t_theta]
    G[Reward r_t from Environment] --> H[Loss Computation]
    F --> H
    I[TD Target or MC Return] --> H
    H --> J[Gradient of Loss w.r.t. theta]
    J --> K[Optimizer SGD RMSprop Adam]
    K --> L[Updated Parameters theta_new]
    L --> E
    M[Policy pi from V or Q] --> A
    F --> M
```

> **Architecture walk-through:** Raw sensor state $\rightarrow$ feature vector $\mathbf{x}(s_t)$ (hand-crafted or CNN) $\rightarrow$ approximator outputs $\hat{V}(s_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ $\rightarrow$ loss computed against MC/TD target $\rightarrow$ gradient backprop $\rightarrow$ optimizer updates $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ $\rightarrow$ new $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ used for next prediction. The loop is closed by the policy $\pi$ derived from the value function.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Deadly Triad & Stability Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DeadlyTriad[DEADLY TRIAD]
        P1[Off Policy Learning]
        P2[Bootstrapping]
        P3[Function Approximation]
    end

    DeadlyTriad --> X[Instability and Divergence of Values]
    X --> Y[Q estimates go to infinity]

    subgraph Fixes[Stability Mechanisms]
        F1[Target Network theta_minus frozen for C steps]
        F2[Experience Replay buffer D]
        F3[Gradient Clipping]
        F4[Reward Clipping]
        F5[Double DQN decoupled selection and evaluation]
        F6[Polyak Averaging soft updates]
    end

    Fixes --> Z[Stable Q learning DQN Rainbow etc]
```

> **Read carefully:** Each arrow from the deadly triad contributes to instability. The fixes listed are the empirical and theoretical countermeasures used in modern deep RL for robotics (DQN, SAC, TD3, PPO).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Update Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sampled Transition s_t r_t s_tplus1] --> B{Update Method}
    B --> C[Gradient Monte Carlo]
    B --> D[Semi Gradient TD 0]
    B --> E[LSTD Closed Form]

    C --> F1[Target = G_t full return]
    D --> F2[Target = r_t + gamma V_hat s_tplus1]
    E --> F3[Target = solution of A theta = b]

    F1 --> G1[Unbiased but high variance]
    F2 --> G2[Biased but low variance online]
    F3 --> G3[Optimal use of data offline]
```

> **Engineering selection rule:** Use **Gradient MC** when episodes are short and you can afford high variance. Use **Semi-gradient TD(0)** for online continuing tasks (robot balancing). Use **LSTD** when you have a fixed offline dataset and you want sample efficiency and no step-size tuning.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Value Function Approximation. Why is it necessary in large or continuous state spaces? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Definition:** Value Function Approximation (VFA) is a class of RL algorithms in which the value function $V_\pi(s)$ or $Q_\pi(s, a)$ is represented by a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ with parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, rather than a lookup table.
- **(1 mark)** The parameters are learned from sampled experience so that $\hat{V}$ closely matches the true value function over the entire state space.
- **(1 mark)** **Necessity:** In large (e.g., backgammon: $10^{20}$ states) or continuous (robot joint angles) state spaces, tabular storage and per-state updates are physically impossible. VFA generalizes knowledge from visited states to similar unvisited states using shared parameters — a compact representation.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Gradient Monte Carlo and Semi-gradient TD(0) updates in the context of VFA. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Target:** Gradient MC uses the **full Monte-Carlo return** $G_t$ as target (unbiased, high variance, requires episode termination). Semi-gradient TD(0) uses the **bootstrapped target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ (biased, low variance, online).
- **(1 mark)** **Gradient:** Gradient MC computes a **true gradient** of the squared error because the target $G_t$ is independent of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. Semi-gradient TD(0) computes only a **partial (semi) gradient**, treating the target as constant in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- **(1 mark)** **Convergence:** Both converge with linear FA; Gradient MC converges to a local minimum of $\overline{VE}$, semi-gradient TD(0) converges to the TD fixed point (with bias). With non-linear FA, semi-gradient TD(0) is **not guaranteed to converge** — the deadly triad risk.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question offers an internal choice. Both alternatives are given below; attempt either.**

### Question A (14 Marks): Linear VFA — Derivation, Algorithm, and Robotic Application `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the **Gradient Monte Carlo** update for linear value function approximation $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(s)$. Show the final update rule clearly. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loss definition (1 mark):**
$$
\mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]^2
$$

**Step 2 — True gradient (2 marks):**
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}_t \;=\; -2 \bigl[\, G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

**Step 3 — Linear specialization (2 marks):**
For $\hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(S_t)$:
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Update rule (2 marks):**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \,\bigl[\, G_t - \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^T \mathbf{x}(S_t) \,\bigr]\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

> This is the **LMS (Widrow-Hoff) rule**. The feature vector $\mathbf{x}(S_t)$ plays the role of the input, and the TD error-like term $[G_t - \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^T \mathbf{x}(S_t)]$ is the innovation.

---

#### (b) Explain **Semi-gradient TD(0)** for VFA. Derive the update rule, discuss the deadly triad, and mention two engineering fixes. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — One-step bootstrap target (1 mark):**
$$
\tilde{V}_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

**Step 2 — TD error (1 mark):**
$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Semi-gradient (2 marks):**
Treat $\tilde{V}_t$ as constant in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \, \delta_t \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)
$$

For linear FA: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \, \delta_t \, \mathbf{x}(S_t)$.

**Step 4 — The Deadly Triad (1 mark):**
Off-policy learning + bootstrapping + function approximation together can cause the value estimates to **diverge** (Sutton & Barto 2018). Theoretically demonstrated using the famous "Baird's counterexample".

**Step 5 — Two engineering fixes (2 marks):**
1. **Target Network (DQN fix):** Periodically freeze $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ and use it for the target; copy from $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ every $C$ steps. Breaks the harmful feedback loop.
2. **Experience Replay:** Store transitions in a buffer $\mathcal{D}$ and sample mini-batches uniformly to update; decorrelates samples, reduces oscillations.
*(Other valid fixes: gradient clipping, Polyak averaging, Double DQN, reward/observation clipping.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Batch Methods & LSTD `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) State and explain the **mean squared value error** objective. What is the role of the on-policy distribution $\mu(s)$? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Objective function (2 marks):**
$$
\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \bigl[\, V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\bigr]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Term-by-term meaning (2 marks):**
- $V_\pi(s)$: the true value of state $s$ under policy $\pi$ (a scalar target).
- $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$: the approximation's prediction.
- Their difference squared: the squared error at state $s$.

**Step 3 — Role of $\mu(s)$ (2 marks):**
$\mu(s)$ is the **on-policy state distribution** — the stationary probability of being in $s$ when following $\pi$. It ensures the objective weights errors by **how often the agent is in each state**. Frequent states (e.g., the centre of a robot's workspace) dominate the loss, while rarely visited states (e.g., near workspace boundaries) are weighted down. This focuses learning capacity on practically relevant regions.

**Step 4 — Why a distribution? (1 mark):**
In tabular RL, we can simply minimise the error at every state with equal weight — but that is equivalent to $\mu(s) = 1/|\mathcal{S}|$. With VFA, we cannot fit all states exactly, so we choose what to fit best via $\mu$.

---

#### (b) Derive the **Least-Squares TD (LSTD)** solution $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \hat{\mathbf{A}}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{b}}$. What are its advantages over semi-gradient TD(0)? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — TD fixed point (1 mark):**
For linear FA, the semi-gradient TD(0) update has a fixed point satisfying:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl(\mathbf{x}(S_t)^T \boldsymbol{\theta} - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})^T \boldsymbol{\theta} - R_{t+1}\bigr)\,\bigr] = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrangement (2 marks):**
$$
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl(\mathbf{x}(S_t)^T - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})^T\bigr)\,\bigr]}_{\mathbf{A}} \boldsymbol{\theta} \;=\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}\,\bigr]}_{\mathbf{b}}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical estimates (2 marks):**
$$
\hat{\mathbf{A}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}(S_t)\bigl[\mathbf{x}(S_t) - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\bigr]^T, \quad \hat{\mathbf{b}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Closed-form solution (1 mark):**
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{LSTD} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{A}}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{b}}
$$

**Step 5 — Advantages over semi-gradient TD(0) (1 mark):**
- **No step-size tuning required** — the solution is computed in one shot.
- **Data-efficient** — uses all transitions optimally (in the least-squares sense).
- **Deterministic** — does not depend on the order of samples.
- **Convergent** with linear FA without Robbins-Monro conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Gradient MC and Semi-gradient TD:** Students often write "TD(0) computes a true gradient" — *wrong*. TD(0) is a **semi-gradient** because the target depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the on-policy distribution $\mu(s)$ in $\overline{VE}$:** Some answers write $\sum_s [V_\pi - \hat{V}]^2$ without the weight. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up LSTD and LSPI:** LSTD only learns the value function (no policy improvement). LSPI = LSTD + policy iteration. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Claiming convergence of semi-gradient TD with neural networks:** This is **not guaranteed**. Examiner deducts marks if you claim otherwise. Always say "empirically stable with target net + replay, no formal guarantee".
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU ESE expects every algebraic step. Jumping from $\nabla$ to final update without showing chain rule application = 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam:**

- **Core definition:** VFA approximates $V_\pi(s)$ or $Q_\pi(s, a)$ using a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ to handle large/continuous state spaces.
- **Why VFA:** Tabular RL fails under the curse of dimensionality — VFA **generalizes** across similar states.
- **Objective:** Minimize $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_s \mu(s)[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})]^2$; $\mu(s)$ = on-policy distribution.
- **Linear FA form:** $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}(s)$; gradient is simply $\mathbf{x}(s)$.
- **Gradient MC update:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha [G_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})] \nabla \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ — true gradient, unbiased, high variance.
- **Semi-gradient TD(0) update:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})] \nabla \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ — semi-gradient, biased, low variance.
- **TD fixed point:** Semi-gradient TD(0) does not minimise $\overline{VE}$ — it converges to a different (smaller) error with bias.
- **LSTD:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \hat{\mathbf{A}}^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ — direct solution, no step size, linear FA only.
- **LSTD complexity:** $O(d^2 n + d^3)$ — scales poorly in $d$, motivating recursive variants.
- **n-step & $\lambda$-returns:** Blends reduce TD bias; $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda^{n-1} G_{t:t+n}$.
- **The Deadly Triad:** Off-policy + Bootstrapping + Function Approximation → divergence. Fixes: target network, replay buffer, double estimators, gradient clipping.
- **Deep Q-Network (DQN):** CNN VFA over pixels; $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}[(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta}))^2]$.
- **Convergence:** Robbins-Monro conditions $\sum \alpha_n = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_n^2 < \infty$ required for stochastic approximation; semi-gradient TD(0) needs additional off-policy + linear FA assumptions to formally converge.
- **Robotics relevance:** All continuous control tasks (locomotion, manipulation, drone flight) mandate VFA. Choose linear FA for interpretability/sample efficiency; deep FA for high-dimensional perception (vision, point clouds).
- **Mark-distribution tip (KTU 2024 ESE):** VFA derivation question = 7 marks (derivation), 7 marks (algorithm/discussion). Always include a numerical example or a 2-D linear FA sketch in derivations for partial credit.

> **End of Module 4 — Value Function Approximation Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Prediction Objective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Prediction Objective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Reinforcement Learning (RL), **The Prediction Objective** refers to the problem of **estimating the value function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ (or the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$) for a **fixed, given policy** $\pi$. 

Formally, given:
- A Markov Decision Process (MDP) tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$
- A target policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ that the agent is *forced to follow*

The **prediction problem** asks the agent to compute the expected return when starting from state $s$ (or state-action pair $(s,a)$) and subsequently following $\pi$ forever:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k}\,R_{t+k+1} \,\bigg\vert\, S_t = s\,\right]$$

where the discount factor satisfies $\gamma \in [0,1]$, with $\gamma = 0.9$ being a standard engineering choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prediction ≠ Control.** Prediction *evaluates* a fixed policy. Control *improves* a policy to maximize the return. This distinction is a **guaranteed 2–3 mark question** in KTU Module-4 examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cricket Coach

Imagine you are a cricket coach evaluating your current batsman's technique (the **policy**). Before you decide *how to change* his shots (the control problem), you must first **predict how many runs he is expected to score** against a particular bowler type from his current batting stance. 

- You watch many innings → you build a **mental score predictor** (the value function).
- You do *not* ask him to change his stance yet → you are just measuring.
- Once the prediction is reliable, *then* you start suggesting improvements → control.

That "measuring step" is exactly what the **Prediction Objective** does in RL for robotics. The robot executes its current control policy $\pi$ (e.g., a wall-following controller), and the prediction objective tells us, *before running the experiment physically*, how much cumulative reward the robot is expected to gather.

## 1.3 Why Prediction Matters in Robotics

| Robotics Task | Role of Prediction |
|---|---|
| **Bipedal walking** | Estimate stability margin of current gait policy |
| **Drone navigation** | Forecast energy consumed by current trajectory policy |
| **Manipulator grasping** | Predict success probability of current grasp policy |
| **Autonomous driving** | Evaluate collision-risk of current lane-change policy |

> [!NOTE]
> The discount factor $\gamma$ is a dimensionless scalar in $[0,1]$. Setting $\gamma = 0$ gives a *myopic* one-step predictor. Setting $\gamma \to 1$ makes the agent care about the entire infinite horizon.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Prediction Objective

The Prediction Objective can be broken into **four operational pillars** that any prediction algorithm must satisfy:

### Pillar 1 — Mathematical Target
The true value $V^{\pi}(s)$ is unknown in practice. The agent must build an approximation $\hat{V}(s; \mathbf{w})$, parameterized by a weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ (e.g., linear features or neural-network weights).

### Pillar 2 — Bellman Consistency
Any correct estimate $\hat{V}$ must satisfy the **Bellman equation for $\pi$**:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}} p(s',r \vert s,a)\bigl[\,r + \gamma\,V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$

This is a system of $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert$ linear equations in $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert$ unknowns — solvable in tabular MDPs but intractable for large robotics state spaces.

### Pillar 3 — Sample-Based Approximation
Real robots cannot enumerate $\mathcal{S}$ or $\mathcal{A}$. They must **learn from trajectories** $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, \dots)$ sampled by interaction.

### Pillar 4 — Bootstrapping vs. Sampling Trade-off
- **Monte Carlo (MC)** waits until the episode ends, then averages returns → *unbiased but high variance*.
- **Temporal-Difference (TD)** updates after every step using its own estimate → *biased but low variance*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for The Prediction Objective. Memorise every row before sitting the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

| # | Equation | Name | When Used |
|---|----------|------|-----------|
| 1 | $G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Return definition | Definition of any value |
| 2 | $V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert S_t=s]$ | State-value function | Prediction target |
| 3 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Action-value function | Prediction target (action level) |
| 4 | $V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | Consistency (v↔q) | Link between V and Q |
| 5 | $V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)[r+\gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Bellman Prediction Eqn. | True value satisfies this |
| 6 | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1}+\gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\bigr]$ | TD(0) update | Online, single-step prediction |
| 7 | $G_t^{(n)} \;=\; R_{t+1}+\gamma R_{t+2}+\dots+\gamma^{n-1}R_{t+n}+\gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$ | n-step return | n-step TD prediction |
| 8 | $G_t^{\lambda} \;=\; (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$ | λ-return | TD(λ) eligibility traces |
| 9 | $\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1}+\gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | TD error | Core learning signal |
| 10 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\bigl[G_t - V(s)\bigr]$ | MC update (first-visit) | Episodic prediction only |

> **Notation safeguard:** All absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert$ cardinality) are rendered using `\vert` so they do not collide with the markdown table pipe symbol.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Prediction Objective |
|-------|-------------------------------------|
| **Industrial Robotics** | Pre-deployment safety certification of a pick-and-place policy |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Risk-scoring a planned lane-keep policy before highway entry |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Energy-cost estimation of a walking gait on flat terrain |
| **Drone Swarms** | Decentralised value estimation for cooperative coverage |
| **Soft Robotics** | Predicting manipulation success under material deformation uncertainty |

The Prediction Objective is also the **inner loop** of Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI): every policy-improvement step requires an accurate value-function estimate of the *current* policy. Without a good predictor, the controller is flying blind.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Prediction Equation

We start from the formal definition of the state-value function and expand the expectation one step into the future.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &\stackrel{\text{def}}{=} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\bigl[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\bigl[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\bigr] \quad \text{(by recursive expansion of } G_t)\\[4pt]
&= \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)\,\bigl[\,r + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\,G_{t+1}\mid S_{t+1}=s']\,\bigr]\\[4pt]
&= \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)\,\bigl[\,r + \gamma\,V^{\pi}(s')\,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Bellman Prediction Equation**. Every prediction algorithm (MC, TD(0), TD(λ)) is a *different way of approximately solving* this equation from samples.

## 3.2 Derivation of the TD(0) Update Rule

We seek a stochastic approximation to the Bellman equation that uses only samples observed along a single trajectory. Define the **TD target** $Y_t$ and **TD error** $\delta_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_t &\stackrel{\text{def}}{=} R_{t+1} + \gamma\,V(S_{t+1}) \\[4pt]
\delta_t &\stackrel{\text{def}}{=} Y_t - V(S_t) \;=\; R_{t+1}+\gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

We minimise the squared error $\tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(Y_t - V(S_t)\bigr)^2$ by stochastic gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} &= \mathbf{w}_t - \alpha\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\!\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}(Y_t-\hat{V}(S_t;\mathbf{w}_t))^2\Bigr) \quad\text{(generic)}\\[4pt]
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} &= \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha\bigl(Y_t - \hat{V}(S_t;\mathbf{w}_t)\bigr)\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{V}(S_t;\mathbf{w}_t) \quad\text{(tabular }\hat{V}=V)\\[4pt]
\Rightarrow\quad V(S_t) &\leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the tabular (table-lookup) case used in KTU theory questions, $\hat{V}(s) = V(s)$ directly, so the gradient is just $1$ for the updated entry and $0$ elsewhere. That yields the canonical TD(0) update above.

## 3.3 Derivation of the n-step Return and the λ-Return

The **n-step return** interpolates between the 1-step TD target and the full Monte Carlo return:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t^{(1)} &= R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \\[4pt]
G_t^{(2)} &= R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} V(S_{t+2}) \\[4pt]
&\;\;\vdots \\[4pt]
G_t^{(n)} &= \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})
\end{aligned}
$$

A geometric average over all $n$ gives the **λ-return**, which is the target for TD(λ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t^{\lambda} &= (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} \\[4pt]
\text{(verify normalisation):} \quad (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1} &= (1-\lambda)\cdot\frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Two extreme cases recover the familiar algorithms:

| Limit | Resulting algorithm |
|-------|---------------------|
| $\lambda = 0$ | $G_t^{\lambda} = G_t^{(1)}$ → **TD(0)** |
| $\lambda = 1$ | $G_t^{\lambda} = G_t^{\text{MC}}$ (full return) → **Monte Carlo** |

## 3.4 On-Policy vs Off-Policy Prediction

For prediction under a *behaviour* policy $\mu$ while targeting policy $\pi$, the **importance-sampling correction** is required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{\mu}\!\bigl[G_t\bigr] &= \sum_{\tau} p_{\mu}(\tau)\,G_t(\tau) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{\tau} p_{\pi}(\tau)\,\frac{p_{\mu}(\tau)}{p_{\pi}(\tau)}\,G_t(\tau) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\bigl[\rho_t\,G_t\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **importance-sampling ratio** for an episode of length $T$ is

$$
\rho_t \;=\; \prod_{k=t}^{T-1}\frac{\pi(A_k \vert S_k)}{\mu(A_k \vert S_k)}
$$

For per-step importance sampling used in off-policy TD methods (e.g., Expected SARSA, Tree Backup):

$$
\rho_{t+1} \;=\; \frac{\pi(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1})}{\mu(A_{t+1}\vert S_{t+1})}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem.** A robot vacuum cleaner executes a fixed policy $\pi$ on a 3-state MDP. The reward function gives $r = +1$ on entering "Clean" state, $r = 0$ otherwise. Transitions are deterministic. $\gamma = 0.5$. The episode is infinite-horizon. Compute $V^{\pi}$ by matrix inversion of the Bellman prediction equation.

**States:** $s_1$ (Dirty), $s_2$ (Clean), $s_3$ (Charging). Policy $\pi(s_1)=a_{\text{clean}}$, $\pi(s_2)=a_{\text{stay}}$, $\pi(s_3)=a_{\text{scan}}$.

**Transitions:** $s_1 \xrightarrow{a_{\text{clean}}} s_2$, $s_2 \xrightarrow{a_{\text{stay}}} s_2$, $s_3 \xrightarrow{a_{\text{scan}}} s_1$. Rewards: $r(s_1,a_{\text{clean}}) = 1$, others $= 0$.

**Bellman prediction system (tabular, deterministic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(s_1) &= 1 + \gamma V(s_2) \\[4pt]
V(s_2) &= 0 + \gamma V(s_2) \\[4pt]
V(s_3) &= 0 + \gamma V(s_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving line 2:** $V(s_2) - \gamma V(s_2) = 0 \Rightarrow V(s_2)(1-\gamma) = 0 \Rightarrow V(s_2) = 0$.

**Solving line 1:** $V(s_1) = 1 + \gamma\cdot 0 = 1$.

**Solving line 3:** $V(s_3) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.

**Final Answer:** $\bigl[V(s_1),\;V(s_2),\;V(s_3)\bigr] = [1.0,\; 0.0,\; 0.5]$.

This is exactly the kind of **6-mark derivation** the KTU ESE expects from Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between the Prediction Objective and the Control Objective in Reinforcement Learning. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Prediction Objective:** Given a *fixed* policy $\pi$, estimate the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \vert S_t = s]$. No policy change occurs.
- **Control Objective:** Find the *optimal* policy $\pi^{\star}$ that maximises $V^{\pi}(s)$ for every state $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
- **Relation:** Prediction is the *inner loop*; control is the *outer loop* of Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI). In robotics, prediction is used for *safety verification* of an existing controller, whereas control is used to *design* a new one.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Retest]`
**Define the TD error $\delta_t$ and state its role in the prediction objective. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:** The TD error at time $t$ is defined as
$$\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$
It is the **one-step discrepancy** between the predicted value of $S_t$ and the better-informed bootstrap estimate $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. Its role in the prediction objective is to drive the **iterative update** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$, making $\delta_t$ the *learning signal* that contracts the estimate toward the true Bellman-consistent value $V^{\pi}(S_t)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** Derive the **Bellman Prediction Equation** from the formal definition of the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$. Clearly state each algebraic transition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-state MDP has transition kernel $p$ and a fixed stochastic policy $\pi$. The Bellman prediction equation yields the linear system $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{R} + \gamma \mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}$. Solve for $\mathbf{V}$ when $\mathbf{R} = [1, 0, 2, 0]^{\top}$, $\gamma = 0.5$, and
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2 & 0.8 & 0 & 0\\0 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0\\0 & 0 & 0.3 & 0.7\\0.4 & 0 & 0 & 0.6\end{bmatrix}$$
using matrix inversion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Starting from $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert S_t = s]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\bigr] \quad\text{(recursive expansion)}\\[4pt]
&= \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)\bigl[r + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1}\mid S_{t+1}=s']\bigr]\\[4pt]
&= \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Each line is worth **~2 marks**: the recursive expansion (2M), the law-of-total-expectation substitution (2M), identification of the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$ (2M), and final compact form (1M).

**(b)** We need $(\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P})\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{R}$, so $\mathbf{V} = (\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P})^{-1}\mathbf{R}$.

Compute $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{I} - 0.5\mathbf{P}$:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix}0.9 & -0.4 & 0 & 0\\0 & 0.75 & -0.25 & 0\\0 & 0 & 0.85 & -0.35\\-0.2 & 0 & 0 & 0.7\end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving the triangular system by back-substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9 V_1 - 0.4 V_2 &= 1 \\[2pt]
0.75 V_2 - 0.25 V_3 &= 0 \\[2pt]
0.85 V_3 - 0.35 V_4 &= 2 \\[2pt]
-0.2 V_1 + 0.7 V_4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From row 4: $V_4 = \tfrac{0.2}{0.7} V_1 \approx 0.2857 V_1$.
Substituting iteratively yields $V_1 \approx 1.30,\; V_2 \approx 0.43,\; V_3 \approx 1.30,\; V_4 \approx 0.37$. (Accept any equivalent solution obtained by direct inversion: **2 marks** for setting up matrix equation, **3 marks** for computation, **2 marks** for final numerical answer.)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **TD(0) prediction algorithm** with its update equation. Show that under a stationary distribution, the expected update direction is the negative gradient of the **Mean Squared Value Error** with respect to the tabular estimate $V(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Monte Carlo (MC)** and **Temporal-Difference (TD)** prediction along the axes of bias, variance, sample efficiency, and applicability to continuing (non-episodic) tasks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The TD(0) update rule is
$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\bigr]$$

To show that this is a stochastic-gradient step toward the **MSVE** $\;J(\mathbf{V}) = \sum_{s} d(s)\bigl[V^{\pi}(s) - V(s)\bigr]^2$, where $d(s)$ is the stationary distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\tfrac{1}{2}\nabla_{V(s)} J(\mathbf{V}) &= d(s)\bigl[V(s) - V^{\pi}(s)\bigr] \\[4pt]
\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\delta_t \mid S_t = s\bigr] &= \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(s)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{s',r,a}p(s',r\vert s,a)\pi(a\vert s)[r + \gamma V(s')] - V(s) \\[4pt]
&\stackrel{\text{Bellman}}{=} V^{\pi}(s) - V(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \vert S_t=s] = V^{\pi}(s) - V(s)$, which is **exactly the direction** in which the gradient step should move. Hence the expected TD(0) update is a stochastic semi-gradient step on the MSVE. **(2M for setup, 3M for derivation, 2M for concluding remark.)**

**(b)** Comparison table (each correct row ~1.5 marks):

| Axis | Monte Carlo Prediction | TD(0) Prediction |
|------|------------------------|------------------|
| Bias | **Unbiased** (uses actual return $G_t$) | **Biased** (uses bootstrap estimate $V(S_{t+1})$) |
| Variance | **High** (sum of many random rewards) | **Low** (single reward + estimate) |
| Sample efficiency | Poor in continuing tasks | Excellent (online, after every step) |
| Episodic vs Continuing | Requires episode termination | Works in both regimes |
| Convergence speed | Slow; needs many episodes | Fast; converges to fixed point of Bellman operator |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Prediction Objective questions**
> 1. **Mixing up Prediction vs Control:** Many students write $V^{\star}$ instead of $V^{\pi}$ in prediction questions. Always use $\pi$ explicitly because the policy is *fixed*. Marks are deducted if you write "optimal policy" in a prediction answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the expectation over actions:** The Bellman prediction equation contains $\sum_a \pi(a\vert s)$. Students frequently drop this sum, treating the policy as deterministic — this loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Confusing TD error with reward:** $\delta_t$ is *not* a reward; it is the discrepancy between two value estimates. A surprising number of KTU answer sheets commit this error.
> 4. **Not showing the matrix form** $(\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P})\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{R}$ in the matrix-inversion sub-question loses **at least 3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Prediction Objective**

- **Prediction = evaluate a *fixed* policy $\pi$**; Control = *improve* the policy. Never confuse the two.
- The true prediction target is $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \vert S_t=s]$.
- The **Bellman Prediction Equation** is the consistency condition that all correct estimates must satisfy: $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\vert s,a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- The matrix form is $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{R}_{\pi} + \gamma \mathbf{P}_{\pi}\mathbf{V}$, solvable as $\mathbf{V} = (\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P}_{\pi})^{-1}\mathbf{R}_{\pi}$ when $\gamma < 1$.
- **TD(0):** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$. Biased but low variance, online.
- **Monte Carlo:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [G_t - V(s)]$. Unbiased but high variance, requires episode termination.
- **n-step TD** uses $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$.
- **TD($\lambda$):** geometric blend $G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$; reduces to TD(0) when $\lambda=0$ and to MC when $\lambda=1$.
- **On-policy prediction:** SARSA family — uses actions actually taken by $\pi$.
- **Off-policy prediction:** requires importance-sampling ratio $\rho_t = \prod_{k}\frac{\pi(A_k\vert S_k)}{\mu(A_k\vert S_k)}$.
- **Convergence:** All tabular prediction algorithms converge to the unique fixed point $V^{\pi}$ as long as $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$ (Robbins-Monro conditions).
- **Discount factor range:** $\gamma \in [0,1]$. Use $\gamma = 0.99$ for far-sighted robots, $\gamma = 0.5$ for short-horizon reactive control.
- **Memory hook:** *Predict → Don't touch the policy. Control → Touch the policy.* This single line settles 80% of KTU Module-4 theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic-Gradient and Semi-Gradient Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stochastic-Gradient and Semi-Gradient Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Approximate Reinforcement Learning**, when the state and/or action spaces are too large (or continuous) to store value estimates in a tabular form, the value function is represented as a **parameterized functional approximator**:

$$
\hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w}) \approx v_{\pi}(s)
$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **weight vector** of the approximator (e.g., linear features, neural network parameters). Learning then reduces to finding the optimal $\mathbf{w}$.

A **Stochastic-Gradient (SG) method** performs a true stochastic gradient descent on an objective (typically the Mean Squared Value Error between the approximation and the *true* value function), using a single-sample unbiased estimate of the gradient at each step.

A **Semi-Gradient (SG-variants with bootstrapped targets) method** uses a bootstrapped estimate as the target (e.g., the TD(0) target $U_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)$), and updates $\mathbf{w}$ in the direction of only the *prediction* part of the error. The bootstrapped target is treated as if it were the ground truth, even though it depends on $\mathbf{w}_t$ — hence "semi"-gradient: the gradient with respect to the target is **ignored** (or set to zero).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sutton & Barto's canonical distinction:** A *true* stochastic-gradient method requires the target of the update to be **independent of $\mathbf{w}$**. The moment a bootstrap target ($R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})$) is used, the algorithm is no longer a true gradient descent and is called a **semi-gradient method**. This seemingly minor technicality has dramatic consequences for convergence guarantees, especially under **off-policy** training and **nonlinear** function approximation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are learning to estimate the **height of mountains** in Kerala from photographs, but you only ever see *one mountain at a time* through a telescope:

- **Stochastic-Gradient (True) Setting** — Suppose each mountain has its true height printed on a small sign at its peak, but the sign is sometimes hidden. When the sign is visible, you compare the sign's number (ground truth $v_\pi$) with your visual estimate $\hat{v}$ and adjust your judging skill (weights $\mathbf{w}$) to reduce the disagreement. Since the sign's value does not depend on your skill, your learning direction is *unbiased*. This is the textbook SGD setting.

- **Semi-Gradient Setting** — Now the mountain has **no sign**. Instead, you estimate the height of one mountain using the height of the *next* mountain you see, combined with the elevation difference between them (the reward $R_{t+1}$). Your estimate of the next mountain depends on your current judging skill, so the target itself moves as you learn. You still adjust your skill in the gradient direction of *only your current prediction*, ignoring how your own skill shifted the target. The error signal is therefore *biased* — but in practice, it works surprisingly well (it's the engine behind Deep Q-Networks and TD-Gammon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why semi-gradient methods are so widely used despite their biases?** Because they do not require waiting for the full return $G_t$ to complete an episode (as in Monte-Carlo), nor a model of the environment. They learn **online, sample-by-sample**, which is essential for robotics where trajectories are continuous and episodes cannot be cleanly restarted.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions Used in This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Discount factor | $\gamma = 0.9$ to $0.99$ |
| $\alpha_t$ | Step-size (learning rate) | typically $0.01$ to $0.5$ |
| $\mu(s) \geq 0$ | **On-policy** state-distribution | $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$ |
| $d(s)$ | Stationary distribution under $\pi$ | $\sum_s d(s) = 1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Feature vector for state $s$ | $\|\mathbf{x}\| \leq 1$ |
| $\overline{VE}$ | Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE) | $\geq 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **On-policy distribution $\mu(s)$** is **bold** in KTU exam answers because it is the *only* distribution under which Sutton & Barto's classical convergence theorem for **linear semi-gradient TD(0)** holds. Mixing off-policy data with this theorem is the most common source of exam-time confusion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Value-Error Objective Function

The natural objective for value-function approximation is the **Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE)**:

$$
\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}) \doteq \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \left[ v_{\pi}(s) - \hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w}) \right]^{2}
$$

where $\mu(s)$ is a weighting distribution (often the on-policy stationary distribution $d_\pi$).

### 2.1.1 Why not minimize the Bellman error directly?

A tempting alternative is the **Mean Squared Bellman Error (MSBE)**:

$$
\overline{BE}(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \big( R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \big)^{2} \right]
$$

| Objective | Symbol | Minimizer | Gradient Bias |
|---|---|---|---|
| MSVE | $\overline{VE}$ | $\mathbf{w}^* = \arg\min \overline{VE}$ | **Unbiased** w.r.t. true $v_\pi$ |
| MSBE | $\overline{BE}$ | $\mathbf{w}_{\text{BE}}$ | Biased but TD-fixed-point |
| MSTE | Expected TD-error | — | Unbiased sample |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TD fixed point $\mathbf{w}_{TD}$ (where $\alpha = 0$ in the TD-update rule) is the **unique** solution of $\overline{BE}$ only when $\hat{v}$ is linear in $\mathbf{w}$. With nonlinear approximators (deep nets), the three objectives can have **different minima**.

---

## 2.2 The Stochastic-Gradient (True SG) Update Rule

For a differentiable approximator $\hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w})$ with **true target** $U_t \doteq v_\pi(S_t)$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \doteq \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \left[ U_t - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t) \right] \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)
$$

### Properties

- $\mathbb{E}\!\left[ U_t \mid S_t \right] = v_\pi(S_t)$  ⟹  the update is **unbiased**.
- Converges to a **local optimum** of $\overline{VE}$ under standard SGD assumptions (Robbins-Monro step-sizes: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$).
- **Problem**: We almost never have access to $v_\pi(S_t)$. We only have samples of returns.

---

## 2.3 The Semi-Gradient TD(0) Update Rule

Replace the unknown $v_\pi(S_t)$ with the **bootstrapped target**:

$$
U_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)
$$

The update becomes:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \doteq \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t) \right] \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)
$$

### Why "Semi"?

The true gradient of $\big(U_t - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)\big)^2$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ would differentiate **both** terms. Semi-gradient **ignores the dependency of $U_t$ on $\mathbf{w}$**:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{\text{true}} = \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \cdot (-1) \;+\; \gamma \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) \cdot (\cdots)
$$

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{\text{semi}} = -\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \cdot \big( U_t - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \big)
$$

Hence only **half** the gradient is computed per update.

---

## 2.4 Linear Function Approximation — Special Closed Forms

For **linear approximation** $\hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$, the gradient has the simple form:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{x}(s)
$$

| Quantity | Linear Closed Form |
|---|---|
| Approximator | $\hat{v}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$ |
| Gradient | $\mathbf{x}(s)$ |
| TD fixed point | $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ |
| Matrix $\mathbf{A}$ | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{x}(S_t)\big(\mathbf{x}(S_t) - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\big)^{\top} \right]$ |
| Vector $\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{x}(S_t) R_{t+1} \right]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is guaranteed to be **positive definite** whenever $\gamma < 1$ and the feature vectors span $\mathbb{R}^d$. This is the algebraic reason linear semi-gradient TD(0) **always converges** under on-policy sampling.

---

## 2.5 Convergence Guarantees — The Master Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Sutton, 1988; Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1997):**
> For **linear** semi-gradient TD(0) with on-policy sampling and standard stochastic-approximation step-sizes, the weights converge w.p.1 to the **TD fixed point** $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, and:
>
> $$
> \overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \;\leq\; \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \, \min_{\mathbf{w}} \overline{VE}(\mathbf{w})
> $$
>
> This $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ factor is called the **asymptotic loss** — semi-gradient methods achieve a value error that is *at most* $1/(1-\gamma)$ times the best possible error in the approximation class.

### Interpretation of the $1/(1-\gamma)$ Factor

- $\gamma \to 0$: asymptotic loss = 1 ⟹ near-optimal.
- $\gamma \to 1$: asymptotic loss $\to \infty$ ⟹ the error can be made arbitrarily large.
- **Engineering implication**: For long-horizon robotics tasks (large $\gamma$), use semi-gradient methods **only when necessary**, and prefer Monte-Carlo or gradient-TD methods when asymptotic optimality matters.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> All pipes for absolute value / cardinality are written as `\vert` to protect the markdown table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MSVE objective | $\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_s \mu(s)\big[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w})\big]^{2}$ | $\mu(s) > 0$ on visited states |
| 2 | True SGD update | $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t\big[U_t - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)\big]\nabla\hat{v}$ | Target $U_t$ independent of $\mathbf{w}$ |
| 3 | Semi-gradient TD(0) | $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t\delta_t\,\nabla\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)$ | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)$ |
| 4 | TD error | $\delta_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)$ | Bootstrapping signal |
| 5 | Linear approx. | $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ | $\nabla\hat{v} = \mathbf{x}(s)$ |
| 6 | TD fixed point | $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Linear, on-policy |
| 7 | Matrix $\mathbf{A}$ | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbf{x}_t(\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma\mathbf{x}_{t+1})^{\top}\right]$ | Positive definite if $\gamma<1$ |
| 8 | Vector $\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbf{x}_t R_{t+1}\right]$ | Depends on reward distribution |
| 9 | Asymptotic loss | $\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \le \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\min_{\mathbf{w}}\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w})$ | Bounded factor |
| 10 | Convergence condition | $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty,\quad \sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro |
| 11 | n-step semi-gradient | $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t\big[G_{t:t+n} - \hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)\big]\nabla\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)$ | Reduces to MC when $n = T-t$ |
| 12 | Baird's counterexample | 7-state MDP with linear approx. | Semi-gradient TD **diverges** |

---

## 2.7 Why Semi-Gradient Methods Can Diverge (Off-Policy + Function Approx.)

The convergence guarantee of §2.5 collapses under **three** simultaneous conditions:

1. **Off-policy training** (behaviour $\neq$ target policy) — e.g., Q-learning, experience replay.
2. **Function approximation** (state aggregation, linear, or nonlinear).
3. **Bootstrapping** (semi-gradient, not Monte-Carlo).

This is known as the **"deadly triad"** (Sutton & Barto, Chapter 11). Baird's counterexample (1995) is the canonical KTU reference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: When asked "why does semi-gradient TD converge on-policy but diverge off-policy?", the examiner expects three named ingredients — off-policy sampling, bootstrapping, function approximation — and **not** the vague phrase "the gradient becomes biased." State the triad explicitly.

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility in Robotics

| Robotic Task | Function Approximator | Semi-Gradient Variant |
|---|---|---|
| Quadruped gait control | Deep MLP | Semi-gradient DDPG actor-critic |
| Manipulator grasp value | CNN over visual input | Semi-gradient DQN |
| SLAM value iteration | Tile coding / RBFs | Linear semi-gradient TD($\lambda$) |
| Drone hover | Linear polynomial | On-policy linear semi-gradient TD |
| Autonomous-driving lane-value | CNN + LSTM | Recurrent semi-gradient TD |

In every case above, the **policy-evaluation loop is a semi-gradient TD update**; the only difference is the structure of $\hat{v}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Semi-Gradient TD(0) Update from the Bellman Residual

### Step 1 — Define the per-step TD error

The TD error at time $t$ measures the inconsistency between the current estimate and the one-step Bellman target:

$$
\delta_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The Bellman equation for the value function is $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]$. Replacing the unknown $v_\pi$ by $\hat{v}$ yields the approximate Bellman residual, which we call $\delta_t$. Because $\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)$ depends on $\mathbf{w}_t$, the residual is itself a random function of $\mathbf{w}_t$.

### Step 2 — Square the TD error to form a per-step loss

$$
\mathcal{L}_t(\mathbf{w}) \doteq \tfrac{1}{2}\, \delta_t^{2}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Squaring makes the loss non-negative and gives a smooth, differentiable surface suitable for gradient descent. The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a common convention so that the gradient does not carry a leading 2.

### Step 3 — Take the true gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}_t \;=\; \delta_t \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \delta_t
$$

Substituting $\delta_t$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \delta_t \;=\; \gamma \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) - \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w})
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The reward $R_{t+1}$ does not depend on $\mathbf{w}$, so its derivative is zero. Each $\hat{v}$ term is differentiated by the chain rule.

### Step 4 — Apply the chain rule on the squared loss

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}_t \;=\; \delta_t \left[ \gamma \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) - \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Take expectation to find the expected update direction

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}_t \right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \delta_t \left( \gamma \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) - \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) \right) \right]
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The expectation converts the stochastic sample gradient into the population gradient that we wish to follow.

### Step 6 — Define the semi-gradient by ignoring the target's dependence on $\mathbf{w}$

If we **freeze the target** $\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})$ during the update, we set $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) \equiv 0$. The expression reduces to:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{\text{semi}} \mathcal{L}_t \;=\; -\,\delta_t \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w})
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is the precise sense in which the algorithm uses only half the gradient — the half corresponding to the *prediction*, not the *target*.

### Step 7 — Form the stochastic gradient ascent step

A **descent** step on $\mathcal{L}_t$ in the semi-gradient direction becomes:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t - \alpha_t \left[ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{\text{semi}} \mathcal{L}_t \right] \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t\, \delta_t \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_t)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We descend the loss (subtract the gradient) and use $\mathbf{w}_t$ inside the gradient evaluation (not $\mathbf{w}_{t+1}$) so that the step is causally computable from data available at time $t$. This gives the canonical Sutton-Barto semi-gradient TD(0) update.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the Linear TD Fixed Point

### Step 1 — Substitute the linear approximator into the TD update

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \Big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbf{w}_t^{\top} \mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \mathbf{w}_t^{\top} \mathbf{x}_t \Big] \mathbf{x}_t
$$

> *Conversion logic:* In the linear case, $\hat{v}(S_t, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v} = \mathbf{x}_t$.

### Step 2 — Identify the per-step "delta" coefficient

$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \mathbf{w}_t^{\top}\!\left( \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \mathbf{x}_t \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Rewrite the update compactly

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \Big[ R_{t+1} - \mathbf{x}_t^{\top}\mathbf{w}_t + \gamma \mathbf{x}_t^{\top} \mathbf{w}_t \, (\mathbf{x}_{t+1}/\mathbf{x}_t)^{\top} \Big] \mathbf{x}_t
$$

A cleaner equivalent form (used by Sutton) is:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \left[ R_{t+1}\, \mathbf{x}_t - \mathbf{x}_t \left(\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1}\right)^{\top}\mathbf{w}_t \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Take expectations under the stationary distribution

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{w}_{t+1} - \mathbf{w}_t \right] \;=\; \alpha_t \left( \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}_t \right)
$$

where:

$$
\mathbf{A} \doteq \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{x}_t \left(\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1}\right)^{\top} \right]
$$

$$
\mathbf{b} \doteq \mathbb{E}\!\left[ R_{t+1} \mathbf{x}_t \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Set the expected update to zero (equilibrium)

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}_{\infty} - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}_{TD} \;=\; \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}\;}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* At a fixed point, the expected per-step weight change is zero. Because $\mathbf{A}$ is positive definite (when $\gamma < 1$ and the features span $\mathbb{R}^d$), the inverse exists and the fixed point is unique.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Asymptotic Loss Bound $\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \le \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\min \overline{VE}$

### Step 1 — Define the projection onto the approximation subspace

Let $\mathbf{w}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{w}} \overline{VE}(\mathbf{w})$ be the best in-class weights. Define the projection operator $\Pi$ that minimizes the weighted squared error:

$$
\Pi \, v \;=\; \arg\min_{\mathbf{w}} \sum_s \mu(s)\big[ v(s) - \hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) \big]^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Bellman operator in matrix form

Let $\mathbf{v}_{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ be the vector of true values. The Bellman operator becomes:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\pi} \;=\; \mathbf{R} + \gamma \mathbf{P} \mathbf{v}_{\pi}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}_s = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$, and $\mathbf{P}$ is the state-transition matrix.

### Step 3 — Express the TD fixed point as a fixed-point equation

For linear features with $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_s$, let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \times d}$ stack the feature vectors row-wise. Then $\mathbf{w}_{TD}$ satisfies:

$$
\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}_{TD} \;=\; \Pi \big( \mathbf{R} + \gamma \mathbf{P} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}_{TD} \big)
$$

### Step 4 — Bring all terms to one side and rearrange

$$
\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}_{TD} - \gamma \Pi \mathbf{P} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}_{TD} \;=\; \Pi \mathbf{R}
$$

$$
\big( \mathbf{I} - \gamma \Pi \mathbf{P} \big) \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}_{TD} \;=\; \Pi \mathbf{R}
$$

### Step 5 — Use the contraction property of $\Pi \mathbf{P}$

The composed operator $\Pi \mathbf{P}$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in the $\mu$-weighted norm (because $\Pi$ is non-expansive in this norm and $\mathbf{P}$ contracts by $\gamma$):

$$
\big\Vert \Pi \mathbf{P} \big\Vert_{\mu} \;\le\; \gamma
$$

### Step 6 — Conclude the bound on asymptotic loss

Iteratively applying the operator equation and using the contraction property gives:

$$
\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \;=\; \big\Vert \Pi \mathbf{v}_{\pi} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}_{TD} \big\Vert_{\mu}^{2} \;\le\; \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \, \overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}^*)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The factor $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ arises because each iteration of the operator can multiply the residual by at most $\gamma$, and the geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k} = 1/(1-\gamma)$ bounds the cumulative effect of the mis-specification of $\Pi$.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Linear TD on a 3-State Chain

### Setup

A 3-state Markov chain with states $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3$, terminal at $s_3$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$. Reward $+1$ on every transition.

- True values: $v_\pi(s_1) = 1 + 0.9\, v_\pi(s_2),\; v_\pi(s_2) = 1 + 0.9\, v_\pi(s_3),\; v_\pi(s_3) = 0$
- Solving: $v_\pi = [9.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.0]$ in the convention where each step gives reward $+1$ until the terminal state.

### Feature Vectors

$$
\mathbf{x}(s_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{x}(s_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{x}(s_3) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### TD Fixed-Point Computation

Under on-policy uniform sampling $d(s) = 1/3$:

- $\mathbf{P}_{12} = 1$, $\mathbf{P}_{23} = 1$, all others zero.

Compute $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)]$:

$$
\mathbf{b} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3}\big( 1\cdot \mathbf{x}(s_1) + 1 \cdot \mathbf{x}(s_2) + 1 \cdot \mathbf{x}(s_3) \big) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{x}_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1})^{\top} \right]$:

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3} \sum_{s_t} \mathbf{x}_t (\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1})^{\top}
$$

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3} \left( \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \gamma\cdot 1 \\ 0 - \gamma\cdot 1 \end{bmatrix}^{\top} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \gamma\cdot 0 \\ 1 - \gamma\cdot 1 \end{bmatrix}^{\top} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 - \gamma\cdot 0 \\ 1 - \gamma\cdot 0 \end{bmatrix}^{\top} \right)
$$

Substituting $\gamma = 0.9$ and simplifying term-by-term:

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & -0.9 \\ 1.0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} + \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.1 \\ 1.0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} + \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.8 \\ 2.0 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Solving $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$

$$
\mathbf{A}^{-1} \;=\; 3 \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 & 0.8 \\ -2.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \big/ \det(\mathbf{A})
$$

$\det(\mathbf{A}) = \frac{1}{9}(1.1 \cdot 1.2 - (-0.8)(2.0)) = \frac{1}{9}(1.32 + 1.6) = \frac{2.92}{9}$

$$
\mathbf{A}^{-1} \;=\; \frac{27}{2.92}\begin{bmatrix} 1.2 & 0.8 \\ -2.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 11.10 & 7.40 \\ -18.50 & 10.18 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{w}_{TD} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 11.10 & 7.40 \\ -18.50 & 10.18 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 12.33 \\ -5.54 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Resulting Approximation

$$
\hat{v}(s_1) = 12.33 \cdot 1 + (-5.54)\cdot 0 = 12.33
$$

$$
\hat{v}(s_2) = 12.33 \cdot 1 + (-5.54)\cdot 1 = 6.79
$$

$$
\hat{v}(s_3) = 12.33 \cdot 0 + (-5.54)\cdot 1 = -5.54
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Compare with true values $[9.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.0]$. The approximator overestimates $s_1,s_2$ and makes $s_3$ negative (impossible for an episodic task), illustrating the well-known **bias** of the TD fixed point relative to the MSVE optimum $\mathbf{w}^*$.

---

## 3.5 Baird-Style Counterexample Sketch (Off-Policy Divergence)

In Baird's 7-state MDP, an off-policy distribution gives **higher probability to "bad" states**, and the linear semi-gradient TD update with a bootstrapped target causes the weight vector to grow unboundedly. KTU students should remember only the conclusion:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Baird's conclusion (1995):** There exist MDPs, policies, and linear function approximators for which the off-policy semi-gradient TD(0) update produces $\Vert \mathbf{w}_t \Vert \to \infty$. This single counterexample motivated an entire generation of research, including Gradient-TD (GTD), Emphatic-TD, and the family of **three-operator** Bellman methods.
