# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Semi-Gradient TD Agent

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the data flow inside a single semi-gradient TD update. Every block represents a logical component that a robotic control loop would implement.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    envBlock["ENVIRONMENT"] --> sampleS["SAMPLE STATE S_t"]
    sampleS --> featureBlock["FEATURE ENCODER x(S_t)"]
    featureBlock --> predV["PREDICTION v_hat(S_t, w_t)"]
    actionBlock["POLICY PI"] --> actSel["ACTION A_t"]
    actSel --> envBlock
    envBlock --> rewardR["REWARD R_{t+1}"]
    envBlock --> nextS["NEXT STATE S_{t+1}"]
    nextS --> featNext["FEATURE ENCODER x(S_{t+1})"]
    featNext --> targetV["BOOTSTRAP TARGET v_hat(S_{t+1}, w_t)"]
    rewardR --> tdErr["TD ERROR delta_t"]
    targetV --> tdErr
    predV --> tdErr
    tdErr --> gradCalc["GRADIENT grad v_hat(S_t, w_t)"]
    gradCalc --> wUpdate["WEIGHT UPDATE w_{t+1} = w_t + alpha_t delta_t grad"]
    wUpdate --> predV
    wUpdate --> targetV
```

> *Reading guide*: The two feedback arrows from `wUpdate` back to `predV` and `targetV` capture the **bootstrapping** loop. In a *true* stochastic-gradient method, the arrow into `targetV` would carry a fixed external target $v_\pi$, not a function of $\mathbf{w}_t$ — that single difference is what makes the algorithm "semi".

---

## 4.2 Comparison of Three Update Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph trueGrad["TRUE STOCHASTIC GRADIENT"]
        s1["S_t"] --> f1["v_hat(S_t,w)"]
        tgt1["v_pi S_t"] --> err1["Loss"]
        f1 --> err1
        err1 --> up1["w update"]
    end
    subgraph semiGrad["SEMI GRADIENT TD"]
        s2["S_t"] --> f2["v_hat(S_t,w)"]
        r2["R_{t+1}"] --> err2["delta_t"]
        f3["v_hat(S_{t+1},w)"] --> err2
        f2 --> err2
        err2 --> up2["w update"]
    end
    subgraph mcGrad["MONTE CARLO TRUE GRADIENT"]
        s3["S_t"] --> f4["v_hat(S_t,w)"]
        g3["Return G_t"] --> err3["Loss"]
        f4 --> err3
        err3 --> up3["w update"]
    end
```

> *Reading guide*: Only the **TRUE STOCHASTIC GRADIENT** and **MONTE CARLO** branches carry a target that is independent of $\mathbf{w}$ — hence they are unbiased. The **SEMI GRADIENT TD** branch re-uses $\mathbf{w}$ to compute the target, which is the source of its bias.

---

## 4.3 Schematic of the "Deadly Triad" — When Semi-Gradient Fails

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["OFF POLICY DATA"] --> diverge["SEMI GRADIENT DIVERGES"]
    B["FUNCTION APPROXIMATION"] --> diverge
    C["BOOTSTRAPPING"] --> diverge
    diverge --> remedy["FIXES: GTD, EMPHATIC TD, RETRACE, EXPECTED SARSA"]
    style diverge fill:#FFE08A
    style remedy fill:#A8E6A1
```

> *Reading guide*: The triad is **all-or-nothing**: removing *any one* of the three ingredients (e.g., switching to on-policy sampling) restores convergence. This is why the on-policy linear convergence theorem of §2.5 is valid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"Distinguish between a stochastic-gradient method and a semi-gradient method in the context of approximate value-function estimation. Give one example algorithm for each."**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

A **stochastic-gradient (true SG) method** performs stochastic gradient descent on a differentiable objective (e.g., MSVE) using a target $U_t$ that is **independent of the current weights $\mathbf{w}_t$**. Examples: gradient Monte-Carlo on $G_t$, least-squares policy iteration with on-policy returns.

A **semi-gradient method** uses a **bootstrapped target** such as $U_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)$ that *depends* on $\mathbf{w}_t$, and differentiates only the prediction term. The gradient is computed w.r.t. $\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w})$ but the derivative of $U_t$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ is ignored. Examples: semi-gradient TD(0), semi-gradient $n$-step TD, and the value-network update in Deep Q-Networks (DQN).

**Key distinction (1 mark):** A true SG update is unbiased with respect to the population gradient; a semi-gradient update is biased because the moving target introduces a correlation between the target and the weights.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Why is the linear semi-gradient TD(0) update guaranteed to converge for on-policy sampling, but can diverge for off-policy sampling? Mention the role of the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ in your answer."**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

For **linear** $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ under **on-policy** stationary sampling, the expected update has the form $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}_t)$, where:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \mathbf{x}_t(\mathbf{x}_t - \gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1})^{\top} \right]
$$

Because $\gamma < 1$ and the feature vectors $\{\mathbf{x}_s\}$ span $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is **positive definite**. A stochastic approximation with a positive-definite coefficient matrix converges with probability 1 to the unique fixed point $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (Sutton's 1988 theorem).

For **off-policy** sampling, the distribution over $(S_t, S_{t+1})$ changes, and $\mathbf{A}$ is **no longer guaranteed to be positive definite**. The expected update can amplify weight components along negative eigenvalues, driving $\Vert \mathbf{w}_t \Vert \to \infty$. Baird's 1995 counterexample exhibits this divergence on a 7-state MDP with linear features.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **semi-gradient TD(0) update rule** starting from the squared Bellman residual objective, clearly explaining where the gradient is truncated. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For a linear function approximator $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$, derive the **TD fixed-point equation** $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. State the conditions under which $\mathbf{A}$ is invertible. (7 marks)

#### (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Loss definition (1 mark):** Define the per-step TD error $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)$ and the squared loss $\mathcal{L}_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^{2}$.

**Step 2 — True gradient (2 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L}_t = \delta_t\big[\gamma\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}) - \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w})\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Truncation (2 marks):** Because $\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}_t)$ is treated as a constant target during the update, we set $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}) \equiv 0$. The semi-gradient becomes:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{\text{semi}}\mathcal{L}_t = -\delta_t \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w})
$$

**Step 4 — Update rule (2 marks):** Descend the loss:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t\,\delta_t\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)
$$

---

#### (b) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Linear substitution (2 marks):** Substitute $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ and $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v} = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$ into the semi-gradient rule:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \Big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbf{w}_t^{\top}\mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \mathbf{w}_t^{\top}\mathbf{x}_t \Big] \mathbf{x}_t
$$

**Step 2 — Take expectation (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_{t+1} - \mathbf{w}_t] = \alpha_t \big( \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}_t \big)
$$

with $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{b}$ as in §3.2.

**Step 3 — Fixed point (2 marks):** Setting the expectation to zero gives $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Invertibility holds when (i) $\gamma < 1$, (ii) feature vectors $\{\mathbf{x}_s\}$ are linearly independent and span $\mathbb{R}^d$, and (iii) sampling is on-policy (positive-definite $\mathbf{A}$).

**Step 4 — Bound on asymptotic loss (1 mark):** State $\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \le \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\min_{\mathbf{w}}\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w})$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **"deadly triad"** of reinforcement learning. For each of the three components, give a one-line justification of why it can cause divergence in semi-gradient methods. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe **Baird's counterexample** in conceptual terms and explain how it motivated the development of **Gradient-TD (GTD)** algorithms. Why does GTD avoid the divergence? (7 marks)

#### (a) Model Solution

**Three components of the deadly triad (3 marks):**

1. **Function Approximation** — replaces the tabular value with a parameterized $\hat{v}(s,\mathbf{w})$, so the update to one state affects many states. Errors can compound across the state space.
2. **Bootstrapping** — uses the current value estimate as part of the target. Any error in $\mathbf{w}$ propagates into the next target, creating a self-reinforcing loop.
3. **Off-policy sampling** — the distribution over transitions does not match the target policy, breaking the assumptions needed to prove $\mathbf{A}$ is positive definite.

**Why their combination is deadly (2 marks):** With only one or two ingredients, convergence is preserved (e.g., on-policy + bootstrapping + function approximation **does** converge for linear $\hat{v}$). All three together allow indefinite growth of weights — see §3.5.

**Mitigation strategies (2 marks):** Switch to on-policy (e.g., Expected SARSA), use no bootstrapping (Monte-Carlo gradient methods), or use algorithms specifically designed to handle the triad: **GTD**, **Emphatic-TD**, **Retrace$(\lambda)$**, **Tree-backup**, and the modern family of three-operator / regularized Bellman methods.

---

#### (b) Model Solution

**Baird's counterexample (3 marks):** A 7-state MDP with linear features and an off-policy behaviour distribution that emphasises "bad" states. Linear semi-gradient TD(0) produces weights whose norm diverges to infinity even though the true values are bounded. The counterexample demonstrates that the **on-policy** convergence theorem of §2.5 cannot be naïvely extended to off-policy settings.

**Motivation for GTD (2 marks):** Researchers (Sutton, Maei, Szepesvári, Bhatnagar, Liu, Wang) sought algorithms whose updates follow a **true stochastic gradient** of an objective that converges to the off-policy TD fixed point. The key insight is to use two time-scales of learning: a fast inner loop for the primal weights $\mathbf{w}$ and a slow outer loop for an auxiliary vector $\mathbf{v}$ that estimates the product of the importance-sampling ratio and the gradient.

**Why GTD avoids divergence (2 marks):** GTD uses an objective function $\overline{VE}_{\text{off}}(\mathbf{w})$ whose gradient can be **estimated unbiasedly** even under off-policy data. Because the update is a true stochastic gradient, the Robbins-Monro theorem guarantees convergence to a stationary point of the objective, regardless of the policy distribution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top three places students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the gradient direction.** Writing $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t - \alpha_t \delta_t \nabla\hat{v}$ instead of $\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \delta_t \nabla\hat{v}$. The minus sign depends on whether you are descending the *loss* or ascending the *negative loss*. State your convention explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the role of $\mu(s)$.** The MSVE uses an arbitrary weighting $\mu(s)$. Most theorems assume $\mu(s) = d_\pi(s)$, the on-policy stationary distribution. Writing $\mu$ without specifying the distribution forfeits one mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing MSVE with MSBE.** Both are minimized by different $\mathbf{w}$'s. The TD fixed point minimizes MSBE (Bellman error), **not** MSVE. The $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ bound explains *how different* they can be.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stochastic-gradient method**: true SGD on a differentiable objective using a target that is **independent of $\mathbf{w}$**.
- **Semi-gradient method**: uses a **bootstrapped** target; updates only the prediction-side gradient.
- **TD(0) update (canonical form)**:
  $$
  \mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha_t \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1},\mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t) \big] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{v}(S_t,\mathbf{w}_t)
  $$
- **Linear TD closed form**: $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is positive definite under on-policy sampling and $\gamma < 1$.
- **Asymptotic loss bound**: $\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w}_{TD}) \le \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\min_{\mathbf{w}}\overline{VE}(\mathbf{w})$.
- **Deadly triad**: off-policy + function approximation + bootstrapping ⟹ potential divergence.
- **Baird's counterexample**: 7-state MDP demonstrating divergence of off-policy semi-gradient TD.
- **Gradient-TD (GTD)**: true-SG methods that provably converge under off-policy data + function approximation.
- **Practical relevance**: DQN, DDPG, PPO, and almost every modern deep-RL algorithm uses semi-gradient updates somewhere in its critic/actor.
- **Robotics implication**: For long-horizon tasks ($\gamma$ close to 1), the $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ bound explodes — use $n$-step returns or eligibility traces (TD($\lambda$)) to balance bias and variance.
- **Common step-size**: $\alpha_t = 0.1$ for tabular, $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ for deep networks; remember Robbins-Monro for guaranteed convergence.
- **Engineering rule-of-thumb**: When bootstrapping is used with off-policy data and a non-linear approximator, **always** include an importance-sampling correction or a regularizer (L2 penalty on weights) to keep the update stable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eligibility Traces: The λ-return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Eligibility Traces: The λ-return

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **λ-return**, denoted $G_t^\lambda$, is a compound, geometrically-weighted average of all the **n-step returns** $G_t^{(n)}$ that look ahead from time $t$ to various horizons $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ in a reinforcement learning (RL) trajectory. It serves as the **forward-view theoretical target** that unifies the family of temporal-difference (TD) learning algorithms under a single parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$.

For an episode terminating at time $T$, the λ-return is mathematically defined as:

$$G_t^\lambda \;\triangleq\; (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}\,G_t^{(n)} \;+\; \lambda^{T-t-1}\,G_t$$

where the **n-step return** itself is defined as:

$$G_t^{(n)} \;\triangleq\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1}R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n}\,V(S_{t+n}) \quad \text{for } n < T-t$$

and the **Monte Carlo return** is:

$$G_t \;\triangleq\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{T-t-1}R_T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The λ-return is the cornerstone concept that connects **Monte Carlo methods** ($\lambda = 1$), **one-step TD** ($\lambda = 0$), and the entire spectrum of multi-step TD methods. Mastery of this topic is essential for understanding the eligibility-trace implementations of TD(λ), Watkins's Q(λ), and Sarsa(λ) — all critical to robotics policy learning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Behaviour of $\lambda$:**
> - When $\lambda = 0$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ → reduces to **one-step TD**.
> - When $\lambda = 1$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t$ → reduces to the **Monte Carlo return**.
> - When $0 < \lambda < 1$: a **weighted blend** of all n-step returns, with the weight decaying geometrically by factor $\lambda$ per step.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster's Confidence Decay"

Imagine you are a **weather forecaster** deciding what to say about tomorrow's weather based on today's observations. You have several prediction models, each looking ahead a different number of days:

- A **1-day-ahead model** (very confident, very local).
- A **2-day-ahead model** (slightly less confident).
- A **3-day-ahead model** (even less confident).
- ...
- A **full-season model** (Monte Carlo, very uncertain).

You don't want to fully commit to *any single one* of these models because each has different strengths. So you blend them together, but you give **less and less weight** to predictions that look further into the future. The blending weight is multiplied by a decay factor $\lambda$ each step you look further out.

**This is exactly what the λ-return does:**

- $G_t^{(1)}$ = the 1-day prediction (one-step TD return).
- $G_t^{(2)}$ = the 2-day prediction (two-step TD return).
- $\lambda$ = the **trust decay rate** for further-horizon predictions.
- $G_t^\lambda$ = the **composite forecast** — your best informed estimate of $V(S_t)$.

The **$\lambda$-parameter** is therefore your **"patience" knob**: high $\lambda$ = patient & broad-vision (Monte Carlo), low $\lambda$ = reactive & myopic (one-step TD). In a robotics context, you want the right balance — short-horizon bootstrapping for fast learning from limited samples, plus long-horizon credit assignment for delayed-reward tasks like manipulation and locomotion.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in robotics?** A bipedal robot learning to walk receives a reward only at the end of a successful walk (or zero otherwise). Pure Monte Carlo ($G_t$) would propagate the final reward back through hundreds of steps very slowly. Pure one-step TD ($G_t^{(1)}$) would learn only from immediately-adjacent steps. The λ-return strikes the **optimal balance** for sample-efficient learning of locomotion and manipulation policies.

---

## 1.3 Standard Symbols & Constants (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$, usually $0.9$–$0.99$ |
| $\lambda$ | Trace-decay / eligibility rate | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$, usually $0.8$–$0.95$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate | $0 < \alpha \le 1$, usually $0.01$–$0.5$ |
| $T$ | Episode termination time | finite for episodic tasks |
| $G_t^{(n)}$ | n-step return from $t$ | Real number |
| $G_t^\lambda$ | λ-return from $t$ | Real number |
| $E_t^\lambda$ | λ-return TD error | $G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)$ |
| $R_t$ | Reward at time $t$ | Real number |
| $V(s)$ | State-value function | Real number |
| $\mathcal{S}$ | State space | Discrete / continuous |
| $\mathcal{A}$ | Action space | Discrete / continuous |

---

## 1.4 Why the λ-return is the Theoretical Bridge

The single most important idea in this topic is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The λ-return is **conceptually elegant but computationally expensive** because it requires waiting until the end of the episode to compute (it depends on rewards far into the future). This is why it is the **"forward view"** — it tells us *what* to learn, not *how* to compute it online. The **eligibility trace mechanism** (covered in the next sub-topic) provides the computationally efficient **"backward view"** that achieves exactly the same online update using only a single scalar trace per state.

This theoretical bridge is what allows KTU students to later prove that **TD(λ) using eligibility traces** is **equivalent** (in an offline, episode-by-episode sense) to updating toward the λ-return at every state.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The n-step Return — Building Block of the λ-return

To deeply understand the λ-return, we must first fully understand the **n-step return**, since it is the elementary "atom" of which the λ-return is composed.

### Step-by-step Logical Flow of an n-step Return

1. **Step 1** — Observe reward $R_{t+1}$ immediately after state $S_t$. This reward is the most direct, least-noisy estimator of value at $S_t$. Weight: $\gamma^{0} = 1$.

2. **Step 2** — For the next $n-1$ steps, accumulate the *discounted* rewards observed along the actual trajectory: $R_{t+2}, R_{t+3}, \dots, R_{t+n}$. Each reward $R_{t+k}$ is multiplied by $\gamma^{k-1}$ because the further in the future a reward occurs, the more uncertainty surrounds it, and the less it should directly influence the value of $S_t$.

3. **Step 3** — After $n$ real reward observations, the trajectory enters the realm of *bootstrapping*: use the current value-function estimate $V(S_{t+n})$ to *terminate* the sum. This is the **terminal estimator**, weighted by $\gamma^{n}$.

4. **Step 4** — The result $G_t^{(n)}$ is an unbiased (well, less biased than one-step TD) estimator of $V_\pi(S_t)$ when $V$ is approximately correct. It mixes **$n$ steps of sampled experience** with **one bootstrapped function approximation**.

### Why is the n-step Return Biased but Lower Variance than MC?

| Estimator | Bias | Variance |
|---|---|---|
| $G_t^{(1)}$ (one-step TD) | High (depends on $V(S_{t+1})$) | **Low** (single bootstrap) |
| $G_t^{(n)}$, small $n$ | Moderate | Moderate |
| $G_t^{(n)} \to G_t$ ($n \to T-t$) | **Low (unbiased)** | **High** (long stochastic sum) |
| $G_t$ (Monte Carlo) | **Zero (unbiased)** | **Highest** (sum of all rewards) |

The λ-return is a **convex combination of these biased/variance trade-offs**, parameterised by $\lambda$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise every row of this table. KTU board exams expect students to write these equations *verbatim* during derivations.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Range / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Discounted reward sum | $G_t \;\triangleq\; \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1}$ | Episodic, $T$ finite |
| F2 | n-step return (1 < n < T-t) | $G_t^{(n)} \;\triangleq\; \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$ | $1 \le n < T-t$ |
| F3 | 1-step return = TD target | $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | $n=1$ |
| F4 | $(T-t)$-step return = MC | $G_t^{(T-t)} = G_t$ | $n = T-t$ |
| F5 | **λ-return (episodic)** | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ |
| F6 | λ-return for $n = T-t$ cutoff | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ | Last term is MC |
| F7 | λ-return TD error | $E_t^\lambda \;\triangleq\; G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)$ | Real-valued |
| F8 | Geometric sum identity | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}(1-\lambda) = 1$ | Valid for $\vert\lambda\vert < 1$ |
| F9 | Special case $\lambda = 0$ | $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | One-step TD |
| F10 | Special case $\lambda = 1$ | $G_t^\lambda = G_t$ (Monte Carlo) | Episode-end required |
| F11 | Offline λ-return update | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\bigl[G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)\bigr]$ | Applied at end of episode |
| F12 | Truncated λ-return (online) | $G_{t:h}^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{h-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_{t:t+n} + \lambda^{h-t-1}G_{t:h}$ | $h < T$ |
| F13 | Continuing-task λ-return | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$ | Requires $\gamma\lambda < 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **F8 (Geometric Sum Identity) is the key to verification:** When all $G_t^{(n)}$ are equal to a constant $c$ and $T \to \infty$, the weights in F5 must sum to 1 so that $G_t^\lambda = c$. Indeed, $(1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1} = 1$, confirming that the λ-return is a **proper convex combination** (a probability-weighted average) of n-step returns. This is a favourite KTU question — *"Show that the weights in the λ-return sum to unity."*

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The λ-return framework is the **theoretical engine** behind many production RL systems used in robotics:

| Domain | Application | Why λ-return Helps |
|---|---|---|
| **Bipedal locomotion** | Atlas, Cassie robot walking policies | Rewards are sparse (only at end of step-sequence); λ blends short & long credit assignment |
| **Dexterous manipulation** | Robot-arm grasping with contact-rich rewards | Multi-step returns propagate tactile feedback efficiently |
| **Autonomous drone navigation** | Path planning with sparse goal-rewards | Avoids the slow MC convergence at $\lambda=1$ |
| **Industrial pick-and-place** | Vision-based conveyor sorting | Faster convergence than pure MC, more stable than pure TD |
| **Soft-body robot control** | Deformable object manipulation | Long-horizon credit assignment crucial due to time-delayed success signals |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"How would you set $\lambda$ for a sparse-reward robotics task?"* the model answer is: **use a moderate-to-high $\lambda$ (0.9–0.95) because the long-horizon Monte Carlo component is essential for credit assignment across the long delay between action and eventual reward.** For dense-reward control loops (e.g., motor torque minimisation with continuous feedback), use a **lower $\lambda$ (0.3–0.6)** because the immediate-reward signal is already informative and bootstrapping from $V(S_{t+1})$ is reliable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving the λ-return Weights Sum to Unity

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question, 7 marks]**
> *Show that the weights in the λ-return sum to 1, demonstrating that the λ-return is a proper convex combination of n-step returns.*

### Given
The λ-return definition (episodic case):

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$

### Step 1: Identify the Weights

Each term in the sum is multiplied by a coefficient (weight). Let us denote these weights:

- For $n = 1, 2, \dots, T-t-1$: the weight on $G_t^{(n)}$ is $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.
- For $n = T-t$: the weight on $G_t = G_t^{(T-t)}$ is $w_{T-t} = \lambda^{T-t-1}$.

### Step 2: Sum the Geometric Series

We must verify that $\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n + w_{T-t} = 1$.

$$S = \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

### Step 3: Factor the Constant

$$S = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Finite Geometric Series Formula

The standard identity for a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$, ratio $r = \lambda$, and $N = T-t-1$ terms is:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}\lambda^{n-1} = \frac{1-\lambda^{N}}{1-\lambda}$$

Substituting $N = T-t-1$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1} = \frac{1-\lambda^{T-t-1}}{1-\lambda}$$

### Step 5: Multiply by $(1-\lambda)$ and Simplify

$$(1-\lambda)\cdot\frac{1-\lambda^{T-t-1}}{1-\lambda} = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

### Step 6: Add the Final Monte Carlo Weight

$$S = \bigl(1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}\bigr) + \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

$$S = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

$$\boxed{S = 1 \quad \text{(weights sum to unity)}}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

Since all $w_n \ge 0$ for $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ and $\sum w_n = 1$, the λ-return is a **convex combination** (weighted average) of n-step returns. Therefore $G_t^\lambda$ lies between the minimum and maximum of the constituent $G_t^{(n)}$ values — a useful bounding property for value-function stability analysis. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction of the λ-return to the One-Step TD Target when $\lambda = 0$

> **[Board Exam Style — 4 marks]**
> *Show that when $\lambda = 0$, the λ-return reduces to the one-step TD return.*

### Given
$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$

### Step 1: Substitute $\lambda = 0$

$$G_t^0 = (1-0)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}0^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + 0^{T-t-1}G_t$$

### Step 2: Examine the Terms of the Sum

For the sum $\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}0^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$:
- When $n = 1$: $0^{0} = 1$, so the first term is $1 \cdot G_t^{(1)} = G_t^{(1)}$.
- When $n \ge 2$: $0^{n-1} = 0$, so all subsequent terms vanish.

Therefore the sum collapses to the single term $G_t^{(1)}$.

### Step 3: Examine the Final Monte Carlo Term

$0^{T-t-1}G_t$: for any $T > t$, we have $T-t-1 \ge 0$, so $0^{T-t-1} = 0$, and this term is **0**.

### Step 4: Combine

$$G_t^0 = (1)(G_t^{(1)}) + 0 = G_t^{(1)}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the 1-step Return Definition

By F3: $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$

$$\boxed{G_t^0 = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \quad \text{(one-step TD target)}}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reduction of the λ-return to Monte Carlo when $\lambda = 1$

> **[Board Exam Style — 5 marks]**
> *Show that when $\lambda = 1$, the λ-return reduces to the Monte Carlo return $G_t$.*

### Given
$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$

### Step 1: Substitute $\lambda = 1$

$$G_t^1 = (1-1)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}1^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + 1^{T-t-1}G_t$$

### Step 2: Simplify Each Term

- $(1-1) = 0$, so the first (summation) term is **0** regardless of what $G_t^{(n)}$ is.
- $1^{T-t-1} = 1$, so the Monte Carlo weight is 1.

### Step 3: Result

$$G_t^1 = 0 \cdot (\text{anything}) + 1 \cdot G_t$$

$$\boxed{G_t^1 = G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1}}$$

### Step 4: Insight

This makes intuitive sense: $\lambda = 1$ means we **place all our trust in the long-horizon prediction** (Monte Carlo, which uses all observed future rewards and bootstraps at episode end with no approximation error in the sample). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Continuing-Task λ-return via Geometric Series Convergence

> **[Module 4 KTU Examiner Favourite — 8 marks]**
> *For continuing (non-episodic) tasks, derive the infinite-horizon λ-return, given the n-step return formula.*

### Given
For continuing tasks, $T \to \infty$, and the formula must use the infinite geometric series.

The general n-step return formula (F2) still applies for $1 \le n < \infty$:

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$$

### Step 1: Write the Infinite λ-return

Drop the Monte Carlo "padding" term and let the sum extend to infinity:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$$

### Step 2: Justify Convergence (Why we need $\gamma\lambda < 1$)

For each $G_t^{(n)}$, the rewards are discounted by $\gamma^{k}$, so $|G_t^{(n)}| \le \frac{R_{\max}}{1-\gamma}$ (a bounded random variable). The series weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ form a convergent geometric series **iff** $\vert\lambda\vert < 1$. Additionally, the bootstrap term $\gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$ shrinks as $n$ grows — for this to be summable, we need $\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$, which is the **standard continuing-task condition**.

### Step 3: Verify Weight Sum

For the infinite case, the weights must still sum to 1:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda)\cdot\frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1 \quad \text{for } \vert\lambda\vert < 1$$

This confirms that the infinite λ-return is still a **proper convex combination**.

### Step 4: Final Form

$$\boxed{G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})\right]}$$

### Step 5: Variance / Bias Trade-off Insight

The term $\gamma^{n}$ inside the sum **decays multiplicatively**, and the term $\lambda^{n-1}$ outside also **decays geometrically**. Their product $\lambda^{n-1}\gamma^{n}$ provides a **double geometric decay** that ensures absolute convergence and bounds the influence of distant rewards and bootstrap approximations. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of $G_t^\lambda$ as a Function of $\lambda$

| Value of $\lambda$ | Dominant Return | Bias | Variance | Sample Efficiency | Robotics Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda = 0$ | $G_t^{(1)}$ (1-step TD) | High | Very Low | Highest for dense reward | Motor torque tracking |
| $\lambda = 0.3$ | Mostly 1–2 step returns | Moderate-High | Low | High | Continuous control |
| $\lambda = 0.5$ | Balanced mix | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Bipedal balance |
| $\lambda = 0.8$ | Heavy weighting to 5–10 step returns | Low-Moderate | Moderate-High | Moderate | Quadruped locomotion |
| $\lambda = 0.9$ | Mostly long-horizon | Low | High | Lower | Long manipulation sequences |
| $\lambda = 0.95$ | Very long-horizon | Very Low | Very High | Lowest | Sparse-reward task with long delays |
| $\lambda = 1$ | Pure Monte Carlo $G_t$ | Zero (unbiased) | Highest | Slowest convergence | Off-policy evaluation only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Favourite:** *"Why does increasing $\lambda$ monotonically reduce bias while increasing variance?"* — The answer lies in the fact that long-horizon returns $G_t^{(n)}$ use more sampled rewards and **less bootstrapping** (low bias, but more stochasticity), while short-horizon returns use **more bootstrapping** (high bias, low variance). The λ-return linearly interpolates these two regimes.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Computing $G_t^\lambda$ by Hand

**Scenario:** A robot arm attempts a 3-step reach task. Given rewards and values:

- $R_{t+1} = +1$, $R_{t+2} = +0$, $R_{t+3} = +2$, $R_{t+4} = +5$ (terminal bonus).
- $\gamma = 0.9$, $V(S_{t+1}) = 0.5$, $V(S_{t+2}) = 0.7$, $V(S_{t+3}) = 1.2$, $V(S_{t+4}) = 0$ (terminal).
- $T - t = 4$ (episode length = 4), $\lambda = 0.8$.

### Step 1: Compute n-step Returns

**1-step return ($n=1$):**

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) = 1 + 0.9(0.5) = 1.45$$

**2-step return ($n=2$):**

$$G_t^{(2)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}V(S_{t+2}) = 1 + 0.9(0) + 0.81(0.7) = 1.567$$

**3-step return ($n=3$):**

$$G_t^{(3)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}R_{t+3} + \gamma^{3}V(S_{t+3}) = 1 + 0 + 0.81(2) + 0.729(1.2) = 2.4948$$

**Monte Carlo return ($n=4$):**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}R_{t+3} + \gamma^{3}R_{t+4} = 1 + 0 + 0.81(2) + 0.729(5) = 6.265$$

### Step 2: Compute the λ-return Using $T-t-1 = 3$

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-0.8)\bigl[0.8^{0}G_t^{(1)} + 0.8^{1}G_t^{(2)} + 0.8^{2}G_t^{(3)}\bigr] + 0.8^{3}G_t$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.2\bigl[1(1.45) + 0.8(1.567) + 0.64(2.4948)\bigr] + 0.512(6.265)$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.2\bigl[1.45 + 1.2536 + 1.5967\bigr] + 3.20768$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.2(4.3003) + 3.20768$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.86006 + 3.20768$$

$$\boxed{G_t^\lambda = 4.0677}$$

### Step 4: Sanity Check

- $G_t^\lambda$ lies between $G_t^{(1)} = 1.45$ and $G_t = 6.265$ ✓
- It is closer to the long-horizon returns (as expected with $\lambda = 0.8$ which weights $G_t$ at 51.2%) ✓

This numerical result is the **target** the agent's $V(S_t)$ will be updated toward at the end of the episode. $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Compound Backup Diagram (Forward View)

The compound backup is the iconic visual representation of the λ-return. It shows how the λ-return sits "above" the n-step returns in a tree of backups. Each **n-step return** itself is built from a chain of **reward samples** terminating in a **state-value bootstrap**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["G_t_to_the_lambda<br/>LAMBDA RETURN<br/>Compound Target"]
    B1["G_t_superscript_1<br/>1-STEP RETURN<br/>weight equals 1 minus lambda"]
    B2["G_t_superscript_2<br/>2-STEP RETURN<br/>weight equals lambda times 1 minus lambda"]
    B3["G_t_superscript_3<br/>3-STEP RETURN<br/>weight equals lambda squared times 1 minus lambda"]
    B4["G_t_superscript_4<br/>4-STEP RETURN<br/>weight equals lambda cubed times 1 minus lambda"]
    BINF["G_t<br/>MONTE CARLO<br/>weight equals lambda raised to T minus t minus 1"]
    C1["R_t_plus_1"]
    C2["R_t_plus_2"]
    C3["R_t_plus_3"]
    C4["R_t_plus_4"]
    C5["R_t_plus_5"]
    D1["V_of_S_t_plus_1<br/>BOOTSTRAP"]
    D2["V_of_S_t_plus_2<br/>BOOTSTRAP"]
    D3["V_of_S_t_plus_3<br/>BOOTSTRAP"]
    D4["V_of_S_t_plus_4<br/>BOOTSTRAP"]
    D5["V_of_S_t_plus_5<br/>TERMINAL"]

    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    A --> B4
    A --> BINF

    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> D2
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> D3
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    B4 --> D4
    BINF --> C1
    BINF --> C2
    BINF --> C3
    BINF --> C4
    BINF --> C5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The λ-return at the top is the geometric-weighted average of the n-step returns. Each n-step return is a horizontal chain of rewards terminating in a state-value bootstrap (or in the case of Monte Carlo, terminating at the actual end of the episode with no bootstrap).

---

## 4.2 Algorithm Flow: Offline λ-return Updating (Pseudo-Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["START OF EPISODE<br/>Initialise V_s arbitrarily"]
    INIT["Set lambda, gamma, alpha<br/>Collect trajectory<br/>S_0, R_1, S_1, R_2, ..., S_T"]
    BUILDT["FOR each timestep t = 0, 1, ..., T<br/>Build n-step return list G_t_superscript_n for n = 1 to T minus t"]
    WLAM["Compute lambda weight list<br/>w_n = 1 minus lambda times lambda raised to n minus 1<br/>plus terminal pad term"]
    COMBLAM["Combine: G_t_to_the_lambda = SUM of w_n times G_t_superscript_n"]
    ERR["Compute lambda TD error:<br/>E_t_to_the_lambda = G_t_to_the_lambda minus V_of_S_t"]
    UPD["Update value function:<br/>V_of_S_t INCREMENTS BY alpha times E_t_to_the_lambda"]
    LOOP{"All t processed?"}
    EPEND["END OF EPISODE<br/>Begin next episode or terminate"]

    START --> INIT
    INIT --> BUILDT
    BUILDT --> WLAM
    WLAM --> COMBLAM
    COMBLAM --> ERR
    ERR --> UPD
    UPD --> LOOP
    LOOP -- NO --> BUILDT
    LOOP -- YES --> EPEND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is called "offline":** Notice the *START OF EPISODE* and *END OF EPISODE* gates — the entire trajectory must be collected **before** any value-function update occurs. The agent cannot learn incrementally as it experiences the world. This is a major practical limitation that motivates the **eligibility-trace backward view** (the online equivalent).

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Forward View in a Robotics Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ROBOT<br/>Policy Network<br/>pi of a given s"]
    B["ENVIRONMENT<br/>Step function<br/>produces s_prime and r"]
    C["TRAJECTORY BUFFER<br/>Stores all transitions<br/>of current episode"]
    D["N-STEP RETURN<br/>COMPUTATION BLOCK<br/>computes G_t_superscript_n"]
    E["LAMBDA BLENDER<br/>Weighted sum of<br/>all n-step returns"]
    F["VALUE FUNCTION<br/>UPDATE BLOCK<br/>applies alpha times error"]
    G["UPDATED<br/>VALUE FUNCTION<br/>V_of_s new estimate"]
    H["POLICY<br/>IMPROVEMENT<br/>uses new V"]

    A -- action a --> B
    B -- new state and reward --> C
    B -- new state --> A
    C -- read trajectory --> D
    D -- all G_t_superscript_n --> E
    E -- G_t_to_the_lambda --> F
    F -- new V values --> G
    G -- informs --> H
    H -- improved policy --> A
```

**Functional Mapping:**
- The **ROBOT** block executes actions in the environment.
- The **TRAJECTORY BUFFER** is the replay memory holding the episode's full transition history.
- The **N-STEP RETURN COMPUTATION BLOCK** is the *forward-view engine* — it must scan forward through the buffer to construct each $G_t^{(n)}$.
- The **LAMBDA BLENDER** performs the weighted average using $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ weights.
- The **VALUE FUNCTION UPDATE BLOCK** is the final step that pushes the value function toward the λ-return target.

---

## 4.4 Bias-Variance Trade-off vs. $\lambda$ (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L0["LAMBDA EQUALS 0<br/>ONE-STEP TD<br/>HIGH BIAS<br/>LOW VARIANCE<br/>FAST BUT BLIND"]
    L05["LAMBDA APPROX 0.5<br/>BLENDED<br/>MODERATE BIAS<br/>MODERATE VARIANCE"]
    L09["LAMBDA APPROX 0.9<br/>NEAR MONTE CARLO<br/>LOW BIAS<br/>HIGH VARIANCE<br/>SLOW BUT ACCURATE"]
    L1["LAMBDA EQUALS 1<br/>MONTE CARLO<br/>ZERO BIAS<br/>HIGHEST VARIANCE<br/>NEEDS FULL EPISODE"]

    L0 -- "as lambda rises" --> L05
    L05 -- "as lambda rises" --> L09
    L09 -- "as lambda rises" --> L1
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU students should be able to reproduce this diagram from memory.** It is the simplest, most exam-friendly visualisation of the central role of $\lambda$ in trading off bias and variance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the λ-return $G_t^\lambda$ for an episodic task. What are the two limiting cases $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda = 1$?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> The λ-return at time $t$ is a geometrically-weighted average of all n-step returns from $n=1$ to the end of the episode:
> 
> $$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$
> 
> where the **n-step return** is
> $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$.
> 
> **Limiting cases:**
> - **$\lambda = 0$:** $G_t^0 = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ — the **one-step TD return**.
> - **$\lambda = 1$:** $G_t^1 = G_t$ — the **Monte Carlo return** (sum of all discounted future rewards).
> 
> The parameter $\lambda$ thus controls the bias-variance trade-off in value estimation, with $\lambda = 0$ giving high-bias/low-variance estimates and $\lambda = 1$ giving zero-bias/high-variance estimates.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain the geometric sum identity that justifies the weights in the λ-return summing to unity. Why is this property important?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> The identity is $(1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1} = 1$ for $\vert\lambda\vert < 1$. It arises from the infinite geometric series:
> $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1} = \frac{1}{1-\lambda}$, multiplied by $(1-\lambda)$.
> 
> For the finite-horizon episodic case, the same identity holds in the form
> $(1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1$.
> 
> **Importance:** This property guarantees that the λ-return is a **proper convex combination** (a weighted average) of the n-step returns. Consequently:
> 1. $G_t^\lambda$ is bounded between the minimum and maximum of the $G_t^{(n)}$.
> 2. The estimate lies in the same numerical range as the individual n-step returns — no extrapolation occurs.
> 3. As $\lambda$ varies, the estimate moves continuously and smoothly between the one-step TD target and the Monte Carlo return.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Wording):
> *Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) carries 7 marks and (b) carries 7 marks. Draw neat diagrams wherever necessary.*

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**Derive the expression for the λ-return $G_t^\lambda$ starting from the n-step return definition. Show that the weights on the n-step returns sum to unity. Interpret the role of the parameter $\lambda$ as a bias-variance trade-off knob.**

##### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Start with the n-step return:**

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n}), \quad 1 \le n < T-t$$

**Step 2 — Construct the λ-return as a geometric-weighted blend:**

For each $n = 1, 2, \dots, T-t-1$, assign a weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$. For the terminal Monte Carlo return $G_t = G_t^{(T-t)}$, assign the "leftover" weight $\lambda^{T-t-1}$ so that the weights sum to 1:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$

**Step 3 — Prove the weights sum to unity:**

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = (1-\lambda)\cdot\frac{1-\lambda^{T-t-1}}{1-\lambda} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Interpret $\lambda$ as a bias-variance knob:**

- The 1-step return $G_t^{(1)}$ depends on the (possibly inaccurate) value estimate $V(S_{t+1})$ — **high bias** if $V$ is wrong, but **low variance** since only one reward is sampled.
- The Monte Carlo return $G_t$ depends on all sampled rewards — **zero bias** but **maximum variance** (long stochastic sum).
- A larger $\lambda$ places more weight on the long-horizon, low-bias, high-variance returns.
- A smaller $\lambda$ places more weight on the short-horizon, high-bias, low-variance returns.
- Hence $\lambda$ **monotonically trades bias for variance** as it increases from 0 to 1. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**A robotic arm attempts a 2-step reach task with the following data: $R_{t+1} = 2$, $R_{t+2} = 3$, $R_{t+3} = 5$ (terminal), $V(S_{t+1}) = 1.0$, $V(S_{t+2}) = 1.5$, $V(S_{t+3}) = 0$, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\lambda = 0.5$. Compute $G_t^\lambda$ and the λ-return TD error assuming $V(S_t) = 0.4$.**

##### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the 1-step return:**

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) = 2 + 0.9(1.0) = 2.9$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 2-step return:**

$$G_t^{(2)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}V(S_{t+2}) = 2 + 0.9(3) + 0.81(1.5) = 2 + 2.7 + 1.215 = 5.915$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Monte Carlo return:**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2}R_{t+3} = 2 + 2.7 + 0.81(5) = 2 + 2.7 + 4.05 = 8.75$$

**Step 4 — Combine using the λ-return formula with $T - t - 1 = 2$:**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-0.5)\bigl[0.5^{0}G_t^{(1)} + 0.5^{1}G_t^{(2)}\bigr] + 0.5^{2}G_t$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.5\bigl[1(2.9) + 0.5(5.915)\bigr] + 0.25(8.75)$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.5\bigl[2.9 + 2.9575\bigr] + 2.1875$$

$$G_t^\lambda = 0.5(5.8575) + 2.1875 = 2.92875 + 2.1875 = 5.11625$$

**Step 5 — Compute the λ-return TD error:**

$$E_t^\lambda = G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) = 5.11625 - 0.4 = 4.71625$$

**Step 6 — Final offline update (if $\alpha = 0.1$):**

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha E_t^\lambda = 0.4 + 0.1(4.71625) = 0.871625$$

**Result:** $G_t^\lambda = 5.11625$, $E_t^\lambda = 4.71625$, updated $V(S_t) = 0.8716$. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**Explain the concept of n-step returns and how they differ from Monte Carlo returns and one-step TD returns. Show how the λ-return unifies these into a single framework.**

##### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — One-step TD return ($G_t^{(1)}$):**

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

Uses one sampled reward and bootstraps from the value of the next state. **High bias** (depends on accuracy of $V$), **low variance**.

**Step 2 — n-step TD return ($G_t^{(n)}$):**

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$$

Uses $n$ sampled rewards before bootstrapping. As $n$ increases, the bias decreases and the variance increases, interpolating between TD and MC.

**Step 3 — Monte Carlo return ($G_t$):**

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1}$$

Uses **all** sampled rewards to terminal. **Zero bias**, **highest variance**. Must wait until end of episode.

**Step 4 — The λ-return as a unifying framework:**

The λ-return averages ALL the n-step returns using geometric weights:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$

This single equation encompasses the entire spectrum:
- $\lambda = 0$ → 1-step TD.
- $\lambda = 1$ → Monte Carlo.
- Intermediate $\lambda$ → all blends in between.

**Step 5 — Why it matters in robotics:** A single tunable hyperparameter $\lambda$ allows the practitioner to dial in the desired bias-variance trade-off for a given task without changing the algorithm structure. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**Derive the continuing-task (infinite-horizon) λ-return formula. State the convergence condition and explain why it is necessary.**

##### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Take the limit $T \to \infty$:**

For continuing tasks, there is no terminal time. The Monte Carlo "padding" term vanishes, and the sum extends to infinity:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the n-step return expression:**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})\right]$$

**Step 3 — State the convergence condition:**

The series converges if and only if $\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$.

**Step 4 — Justification (three-part):**

(i) The geometric weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ requires $\vert\lambda\vert < 1$ for the series to converge.

(ii) The bootstrap term $\gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$ is multiplied by $\lambda^{n-1}$ (from the outer weight) and thus effectively decays as $(\gamma\lambda)^{n-1}$. For this product to be summable, $\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$ is required.

(iii) For typical values $\gamma = 0.99$ and $\lambda = 0.95$, the product is $0.9405 < 1$ ✓. If $\lambda = 1.0$ (Monte Carlo) is used in a continuing task, the condition fails and the series diverges — which is why **pure Monte Carlo cannot be used directly for continuing tasks**.

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\lambda^{n-1}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})\right], \quad \vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1}$$

**[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls & Mark Loss Zones:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Monte Carlo "padding" term** $\lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ in the episodic λ-return formula. This is a **2-mark deduction** if missed. The padding term is essential because the geometric sum from $n=1$ to $T-t-1$ alone does **not** sum to 1; the leftover weight must be assigned to $G_t$ to complete the convex combination.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $\gamma^{n-1}$ and $\gamma^{n}$** in the n-step return. The $n$-th power is on the **bootstrap term** ($V(S_{t+n})$), not the $n$-th reward. The first reward $R_{t+1}$ has exponent 0, the second $R_{t+2}$ has exponent 1, ..., the $n$-th reward $R_{t+n}$ has exponent $n-1$.
> 
> 3. **Writing the continuing-task formula without the convergence condition** $\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$. KTU examiners specifically check for this — it is worth **1 mark** on its own.
> 
> 4. **Confusing the forward view (λ-return) with the backward view (eligibility traces).** The λ-return is an *offline, theoretical* target. Eligibility traces are the *online, implementable* mechanism. They are equivalent **only in the offline, episode-by-episode sense**. Writing them as the same thing loses **2–3 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the bias-variance interpretation** when asked about $\lambda$. The bias-variance trade-off is the *whole point* of the λ-return and must be mentioned explicitly.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to verify weight sum = 1** in derivation questions. This is a common 3-mark question, and not showing the geometric-series identity is a **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this on the morning of the exam):**

- The **λ-return** $G_t^\lambda$ is a **geometric-weighted average of n-step returns**, designed to unify TD and MC methods.
- The **n-step return** is the fundamental building block: $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^{k}R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$.
- The **λ-return formula (episodic)** has **two parts**: the weighted sum over $n = 1$ to $T-t-1$ **plus the Monte Carlo padding term** $\lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$.
- The **weights** are $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ for the n-step returns, with the final weight $\lambda^{T-t-1}$ on $G_t$. They **sum to 1** (geometric series identity).
- **$\lambda = 0$** → one-step TD; **$\lambda = 1$** → Monte Carlo; **$0 < \lambda < 1$** → blend.
- **Increasing $\lambda$** → **decreases bias, increases variance** (monotonic trade-off).
- The λ-return is the **forward view** — tells you *what* to learn — but is **offline** (requires full episode before update).
- The **eligibility trace mechanism** is the **backward view** — equivalent online implementation.
- The **continuing-task λ-return** requires the condition **$\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$** for convergence.
- The **λ-return TD error** is $E_t^\lambda = G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)$, used in the offline update: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha E_t^\lambda$.
- In **robotics with sparse rewards** (e.g., bipedal walking, long manipulation sequences), prefer **higher $\lambda$** (0.9–0.95) for long-horizon credit assignment.
- In **robotics with dense rewards** (e.g., motor control, force feedback), prefer **lower $\lambda$** (0.3–0.6) for low-variance, fast learning.
- The **compound backup diagram** is the canonical visual; learn to draw it from memory.
- The λ-return is the **theoretical foundation** for TD(λ), Sarsa(λ), and Watkins's Q(λ) — all critical algorithms for robot policy learning.
- **Master the geometric-series convergence proof** — it is one of the most frequently asked 5–7 mark questions.
- The **bounding property** of the λ-return (lying between min and max of the n-step returns) follows from the convex-combination structure.
- **Always include the convergence condition** $\vert\gamma\lambda\vert < 1$ when writing continuing-task formulas.
- **Don't confuse $\gamma$ and $\lambda$** — $\gamma$ discounts future rewards; $\lambda$ discounts the importance of further-horizon n-step returns.
- The **truncated λ-return** (forward view with a finite cutoff $h$) is the practical intermediate between the pure offline λ-return and the online TD(λ) eligibility-trace algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TD(λ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TD(λ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TD(λ): TEMPORAL DIFFERENCE LEARNING WITH ELIGIBILITY TRACES

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TD(λ) — Core Academic Definition**
> **TD(λ)** is a Temporal Difference (TD) reinforcement learning algorithm that uses **eligibility traces** to blend information from multiple time-step horizons into a single, efficient online update rule. The parameter **λ ∈ [0, 1]** smoothly interpolates the entire family of **n-step TD returns**, from pure one-step TD learning (λ = 0) to pure Monte Carlo return (λ = 1), enabling faster and more stable credit assignment over delayed rewards.

Mathematically, TD(λ) maintains an **eligibility trace vector** $e_t(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ for every state, which acts as a short-term, decaying memory of recently visited states. The weight update is performed online at every time step using a single-sample update that touches all recently visited states simultaneously.

### Key Symbols & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V(s_t)$ | Estimated value of state $s_t$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate (step size), typically **0 < α < 1** |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor, **0 ≤ γ < 1** |
| $\lambda$ | Trace-decay parameter, **0 ≤ λ ≤ 1** |
| $e_t(s)$ | Eligibility trace of state $s$ at time $t$ |
| $r_{t+1}$ | Reward received after taking action from $s_t$ |
| $\delta_t$ | TD-error at time $t$ |
| **δ = 1** | Dirac delta; the eligibility is initialized to 1 for the visited state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PERAT747 Module 4):**
> The board examiner expects students to know the **forward view (λ-return)**, the **backward view (online update with eligibility traces)**, the three canonical trace flavors (**accumulating, replacing, Dutch**), and the **proof sketch that the two views are mathematically equivalent in expectation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a student who gets the *final result* of an exam **one week later**, but along the way you receive **daily hints** about how your preparation is going. The challenge is: which **past actions** (studying on Monday vs Tuesday) deserve credit or blame?

- **TD(0)** = blame or credit only the **most recent decision** (lazy credit assignment).
- **Monte Carlo** = wait until the **end** and distribute credit to the *entire* trajectory (patient but slow).
- **TD(λ)** = a **hybrid strategy**. When a new piece of feedback arrives, you distribute credit **exponentially** to all past states you visited, with the *most recent* states getting the biggest share. The **eligibility trace** is like a *memory ribbon* attached to each state that fades out by a factor of **γλ** at every time step.

This makes TD(λ) the **practical workhorse** of modern reinforcement learning — combining the **low variance of TD** with the **fast credit propagation of Monte Carlo**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Faces of TD(λ)

TD(λ) can be understood from **two complementary perspectives**:

1. **Forward View (Theoretical / Offline)** — looks ahead into the future to define a target, then averages across horizons.
2. **Backward View (Practical / Online)** — looks backward at past states using eligibility traces to spread the TD-error.

### 2.1.1 n-Step Return (Foundation of Forward View)

The **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)}$ is the discounted sum of rewards over the next $n$ steps, with the tail value estimated using $V(s_{t+n})$:

$$G_t^{(n)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})$$

### 2.1.2 The λ-Return (Averaging n-Step Returns)

The **λ-return** $G_t^{\lambda}$ is the geometric average of all n-step returns, weighted by $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$:

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$$

where the weights sum to **1**, making $G_t^{\lambda}$ a properly normalized convex combination.

> [!TIP]
> **Why convex combination?** Because $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = 1$ for $0 \le \lambda < 1$. This guarantees that the λ-return remains a *bounded linear combination* of valid n-step returns, preserving **low variance**.

### 2.1.3 The Backward View — Online Update with Eligibility Traces

After observing the transition $(s_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$, the **TD-error** is computed as:

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$$

The eligibility trace of the visited state is then bumped to **1**, and **all** traces are decayed by a factor of **γλ**:

$$e_t(s) = \begin{cases} \gamma \lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + 1, & \text{if } s = s_t \quad \text{(accumulating)} \\ \gamma \lambda \, e_{t-1}(s), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Finally, the value function is updated **for every state** in proportion to its trace:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

## 2.2 Three Variants of Eligibility Traces

| Trace Type | Update Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Accumulating** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$ | Standard textbook; traces can grow unbounded with frequent visits. |
| **Replacing** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s)$ if $s \neq s_t$; reset to **1** if $s=s_t$ | Prevents trace blow-up in frequently visited states. |
| **Dutch** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$ | Sutton–van Seijen (2014); provably optimal linear-complexity trace. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table — it covers **>90%** of numerical and derivation questions on TD(λ) for the KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | n-step Return | $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} r_{t+k} + \gamma^{n} V(s_{t+n})$ | Truncated at episode end. |
| 2 | λ-return | $G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$ | Forward view target. |
| 3 | TD-error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | Drives all updates. |
| 4 | Accumulating Trace | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$ | Default in Sutton & Barto. |
| 5 | Replacing Trace | $e_t(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & s=s_t \\ \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) & s \neq s_t \end{cases}$ | Caps trace at 1. |
| 6 | Online Update | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$ | Backward view. |
| 7 | Convergence | $V(s) \to v_{\pi}(s)$ w.p. **1** if $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ | Robbins–Monro conditions. |
| 8 | Special case $\lambda=0$ | Reduces to **TD(0)** | No trace memory. |
| 9 | Special case $\lambda=1$ | Reduces to **MC** (every-visit) | Full trace memory. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> TD(λ) and eligibility traces are foundational in **robot locomotion**, **autonomous vehicle policy evaluation**, **AlphaGo-style self-play**, and **large-language-model RLHF** (where λ controls horizon emphasis in advantage estimation). Any deployed policy that needs *fast online credit assignment with low computational overhead* uses traces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From n-Step Returns to the λ-Return

**Goal:** Show that $G_t^{\lambda}$ is a valid **convex combination** of $n$-step returns and is itself a legitimate TD-target.

**Step 1 — Define a generic n-step return with bootstrap estimate.**

$$G_t^{(n)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(s_{t+n})$$

This says: take the first $n$ actual rewards, then *estimate* the future by bootstrapping $V(s_{t+n})$.

**Step 2 — Choose geometric weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.**

Let $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ for $n \ge 1$. The geometric series sums to:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_n = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1$$

Thus the weights are **normalized to 1** and form a valid probability distribution.

**Step 3 — Construct the λ-return as the weighted sum.**

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$$

The final term $\lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$ handles the **terminal cutoff** when the episode ends before $n$ grows to infinity.

**Step 4 — Recursive expansion (unfolding the TD-error).**

A key identity is that $G_t^{\lambda}$ can be written recursively:

$$G_t^{\lambda} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \left[ (1-\lambda) V(s_{t+1}) + \lambda G_{t+1}^{\lambda} \right]$$

This shows that $G_t^{\lambda}$ is the **discounted reward plus a mixture** of next-step bootstrap and a continuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of Step 4:** This recursive form is the *theoretical bridge* between the forward and backward views — proving that the λ-return is internally consistent with one-step TD recursion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TD(λ) Backward Update Produces the Same Total Update as the Forward λ-Return

**Goal:** Show that the *sum* of all online weight changes equals the offline λ-return target change.

**Step 1 — Sum all incremental updates to $V(s_t)$ across an episode.**

$$\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \delta_k \, e_k(s_t)$$

Since $e_k(s_t) = (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}$ (only decays, no re-visit assumed for $s_t$ here), we get:

$$\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \delta_k (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the TD-error definition.**

$$\delta_k = r_{k+1} + \gamma V(s_{k+1}) - V(s_k)$$

Plugging in and telescoping carefully across all $k$ from $t$ to $T-1$:

$$\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \left[ G_t^{\lambda} - V(s_t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The **total online update** after the entire episode equals the **offline update** that would have been performed using the λ-return target. Hence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward view ≡ Backward view** (in terms of total magnitude after a full episode). This is the classical equivalence result due to **Sutton (1988)**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 3-state MDP with states $A \to B \to C \to \text{terminal}$. Episode rewards: $r_{AB}=1, r_{BC}=2, r_{C,term}=5$. Use $\gamma=0.9, \lambda=0.5, \alpha=0.1$. Assume $V(A)=0, V(B)=0, V(C)=0$ initially.

**Step 1 — Compute 1-step return from $A$.**

$$G_A^{(1)} = r_{AB} + \gamma V(B) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2-step return from $A$.**

$$G_A^{(2)} = r_{AB} + \gamma r_{BC} + \gamma^2 V(C) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 + 0.81 \cdot 0 = 2.8$$

**Step 3 — Compute full Monte Carlo return from $A$.**

$$G_A^{(3)} = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 + 0.81 \cdot 5 = 1 + 1.8 + 4.05 = 6.85$$

**Step 4 — Compute λ-return.**

Using weights $w_1=(1-0.5)=0.5$, $w_2=0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25$, $w_3=0.5 \cdot 0.5^2 = 0.125$, and tail $0.5^2 = 0.125$:

$$G_A^{\lambda} = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2.8) + 0.125(6.85) + 0.125(6.85)$$

$$G_A^{\lambda} = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.85625 + 0.85625 = 2.9125$$

**Step 5 — Online TD(λ) backward update trace table.**

| Time $t$ | State visited | $e_t(A)$ | $e_t(B)$ | $e_t(C)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | B | 0.45 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | C | 0.2025 | 0.45 | 1 |

(Decay factor $\gamma\lambda = 0.45$ applied at each step.)

**Step 6 — Total update to $V(A)$.**

$$\Delta V(A) = \alpha \sum_t \delta_t \, e_t(A) = 0.1 \cdot \left[ \delta_0 \cdot 1 + \delta_1 \cdot 0.45 + \delta_2 \cdot 0.2025 \right]$$

With $\delta_0 = 1 - 0 = 1$, $\delta_1 = 2 - 0 = 2$, $\delta_2 = 5 - 0 = 5$:

$$\Delta V(A) = 0.1 \cdot (1 + 0.9 + 1.0125) = 0.1 \cdot 2.9125 = 0.29125$$

This matches the λ-return target exactly: $V(A) \leftarrow 0 + 0.29125 = 0.29125$. ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — TD(λ) Online (Backward) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Initialize V(s), e(s) = 0 for all states] --> stepB[Observe current state s_t]
    stepB --> stepC[Select and execute action a_t]
    stepC --> stepD[Observe reward r_t1 and next state s_t1]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute TD-error delta_t]
    stepE --> stepF[Update eligibility trace e_t for all states]
    stepF --> stepG[Update value function V for all states using delta and e]
    stepG --> stepH{Is episode terminated}
    stepH -- No --> stepB
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI[Reset traces and start new episode]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Forward View vs Backward View Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forward_View
        f1[Trajectory Experience] --> f2[Compute all n-step returns G_n]
        f2 --> f3[Compute lambda-return as weighted sum]
        f3 --> f4[Offline update of V using G_lambda]
    end

    subgraph Backward_View
        b1[Online transition observation] --> b2[Compute TD-error delta]
        b2 --> b3[Update eligibility traces e_t]
        b3 --> b4[Online update V using delta and e_t]
    end

    f4 -. Equivalent in total magnitude .-> b4
```

## 4.3 Eligibility Trace Decay Visualization (Geometric Decay)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t=0: e = 1.000"] --> t1["t=1: e = 0.90"]
    t1 --> t2["t=2: e = 0.81"]
    t2 --> t3["t=3: e = 0.729"]
    t3 --> t4["t=4: e = 0.656"]
    t4 --> t5["t=5: e = 0.590"]
    t5 --> t6["t=n: e = (γλ)^n"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each step multiplies the trace by the constant factor $\gamma\lambda$. The trace forms an **exponentially decaying memory ribbon** anchored at the most recently visited state — the core visual of backward-view credit assignment.

## 4.4 Decision-Branch Diagram — Choosing the Right Trace Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is the state visited very frequently in the trajectory}
    q1 -- Yes --> pathA[Use REPLACING traces to cap trace value at 1]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Need optimality with linear computational complexity}
    q2 -- Yes --> pathB[Use DUTCH traces with alpha scaled bump]
    q2 -- No --> pathC[Use standard ACCUMULATING traces from textbook]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

### Q1. Define TD(λ) and explain the role of the eligibility trace.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** TD(λ) is a Temporal Difference reinforcement learning algorithm that uses **eligibility traces** to combine the benefits of one-step TD learning and Monte Carlo methods. It is parameterized by $\lambda \in [0,1]$ which controls the horizon of credit assignment.

**Eligibility trace role (1 mark):** The eligibility trace $e_t(s)$ acts as a short-term memory vector indicating how *eligible* each state is for updates. When a state is visited, its trace is bumped to 1; otherwise it decays geometrically by $\gamma\lambda$.

**Forward/Backward views (1 mark):** In the forward view (theoretical), λ-return averages n-step returns. In the backward view (practical), the same effect is achieved online by multiplying the TD-error $\delta_t$ with the decaying trace of each state, yielding the online update $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between accumulating and replacing eligibility traces? Give one scenario where replacing traces are preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Accumulating traces (1.5 marks):** The trace accumulates each time the state is visited: $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$. With frequent revisits, traces can grow unboundedly large, which may over-emphasize those states.

**Replacing traces (1 mark):** The trace is *replaced* (set to 1) on revisit instead of incremented: $e_t(s) = 1$ if $s = s_t$, otherwise decays as $\gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s)$. This keeps the trace bounded.

**Preferred scenario (0.5 mark):** Replacing traces are preferred in **highly repetitive environments** (e.g., a maze where the agent loops through the same corridor), where accumulating traces would cause credit to snowball and destabilize learning.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 4, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks ✦

**Q.A.(a)** Derive the n-step TD return $G_t^{(n)}$ and hence obtain the λ-return $G_t^{\lambda}$ as a geometrically-weighted average of all n-step returns. State the conditions under which $G_t^{\lambda}$ forms a valid convex combination. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.(b)** With reference to the backward view, explain the role of the TD-error $\delta_t$ and derive the online update rule for TD(λ). Show that for a single state $s_t$ visited once at time $t$ and not revisited, the cumulative weight update equals the offline λ-return update. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) ✦

**Q.B.(a)** Compare and contrast the three variants of eligibility traces — **accumulating**, **replacing**, and **Dutch** traces. Provide the mathematical update rule and one advantage/limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** Consider the trajectory $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \text{terminal}$ with rewards $r_1=2, r_2=3, r_3=4$. Use $\gamma=0.9$, $\lambda=0.5$, $\alpha=0.1$, and initial values $V(s_0)=V(s_1)=V(s_2)=0$. Compute (i) all n-step returns from $s_0$, (ii) the λ-return $G_0^{\lambda}$, and (iii) the eligibility trace values $e_t(s_0)$ for $t=0,1,2$ using accumulating traces. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Solution to Q.A.(a)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Derive n-step return.**

The n-step return sums the first $n$ actual rewards and bootstraps from $V(s_{t+n})$ for the remainder:

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} r_{t+k} + \gamma^{n} V(s_{t+n})$$

For $n=1$ this reduces to the standard TD(0) target. For $n=T-t$ it equals the full Monte Carlo return.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Construct λ-return as weighted average.**

Define weights $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$. Then:

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$$

The final $\lambda^{T-t-1}$ term is the residual weight assigned to the true MC return when $n$ cannot grow to infinity inside a finite episode.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Prove convex combination.**

Sum of all weights:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1$$

Since all $w_n \ge 0$ for $0 \le \lambda < 1$, $G_t^{\lambda}$ is a valid convex combination of valid n-step returns, hence itself a legitimate, **bounded** TD-target.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Boundary conditions.**

- At $\lambda = 0$: only $n=1$ contributes, so $G_t^{\lambda} = G_t^{(1)}$ (TD(0)).
- At $\lambda \to 1$: weights shift to large $n$; in episodic case, $G_t^{\lambda} \to G_t$ (Monte Carlo).

---

### Solution to Q.A.(b)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Define TD-error.**

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$$

This measures the discrepancy between the predicted value and the observed one-step sample. It is the **scalar innovation** that drives all weight updates.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Derive online update rule.**

After computing $\delta_t$, decay all traces and bump the visited one (accumulating trace):

$$e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s = s_t\}$$

Then update all states simultaneously:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Prove equivalence to λ-return update.**

Suppose state $s_t$ is visited exactly at time $t$ and never revisited. Then:

$$e_k(s_t) = (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \quad \text{for } k \ge t$$

Cumulative change to $V(s_t)$ over the episode:

$$\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \delta_k (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}$$

Substituting $\delta_k$ and performing a careful telescoping sum (a standard result shown in Sutton & Barto, Chapter 12):

$$\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \left[ G_t^{\lambda} - V(s_t) \right]$$

This is precisely the offline update one would have performed using the forward-view λ-return target. Hence the two views agree in cumulative magnitude — the cornerstone equivalence of TD(λ).

---

### Solution to Q.B.(a)

**Comparison Table (7 marks distributed as: 1 mark definition + 2 marks each for accumulating/replacing + 2 marks for Dutch):**

| Trace Type | Update Rule | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accumulating** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$ | Strongest credit to repeatedly visited states; classical. | Trace can blow up arbitrarily large; may cause high variance updates. |
| **Replacing** | $e_t(s) = 1$ if $s=s_t$; else $\gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s)$ | Trace stays bounded in [0,1]; robust in cyclic environments. | Loses information about *how many times* the state was recently visited. |
| **Dutch** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$ | Provably optimal learning rate; linear-time algorithm. | Slightly slower convergence in tasks with rare reward signals. |

---

### Solution to Q.B.(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \text{terminal}$, $r_1=2, r_2=3, r_3=4$, $\gamma=0.9, \lambda=0.5, \alpha=0.1$, $V(s_i)=0$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute n-step returns.**

$$G_0^{(1)} = r_1 + \gamma V(s_1) = 2 + 0 = 2$$

$$G_0^{(2)} = r_1 + \gamma r_2 + \gamma^2 V(s_2) = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 3 + 0 = 2 + 2.7 = 4.7$$

$$G_0^{(3)} = r_1 + \gamma r_2 + \gamma^2 r_3 = 2 + 2.7 + 0.81 \cdot 4 = 2 + 2.7 + 3.24 = 7.94$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute λ-return.**

Weights: $w_1 = 1-\lambda = 0.5$; $w_2 = (1-\lambda)\lambda = 0.25$; tail weight = $\lambda^2 = 0.25$ (since episode has 3 rewards total, so the residual gets the leftover mass).

$$G_0^{\lambda} = 0.5 \cdot 2 + 0.25 \cdot 4.7 + 0.25 \cdot 7.94$$

$$G_0^{\lambda} = 1.0 + 1.175 + 1.985 = 4.16$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Compute eligibility trace trajectory (accumulating).**

| Time $t$ | State | $e_t(s_0)$ computation | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $s_0$ | $\gamma\lambda \cdot 0 + 1$ | **1.000** |
| 1 | $s_1$ | $\gamma\lambda \cdot 1 = 0.45$ | **0.450** |
| 2 | $s_2$ | $\gamma\lambda \cdot 0.45 = 0.2025$ | **0.2025** |

**Step 4 — Online update magnitude check (bonus verification).**

TD-errors: $\delta_0 = 2 - 0 = 2$, $\delta_1 = 3 - 0 = 3$, $\delta_2 = 4 - 0 = 4$.

$$\Delta V(s_0) = \alpha[\delta_0 \cdot 1 + \delta_1 \cdot 0.45 + \delta_2 \cdot 0.2025]$$

$$\Delta V(s_0) = 0.1 \cdot [2 + 1.35 + 0.81] = 0.1 \cdot 4.16 = 0.416$$

New value: $V(s_0) \leftarrow 0 + 0.416 = 0.416$. ✅ Matches the λ-return target precisely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse λ-return with TD(λ) update** — they are mathematically equivalent in expectation over an episode, but λ-return is computed offline (uses future data) while the trace-based update is online.
> - **Forgetting the terminal residual term** $\lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t)}$ in finite episodes — examiners specifically test this.
> - **Mixing up the three trace variants** — the question often says "eligibility trace" and expects students to *default to accumulating* unless replacing/Dutch is specified.
> - **Wrong normalization** of λ-return weights — students often drop the $(1-\lambda)$ factor.
> - **Ignoring Robbins–Monro conditions** in convergence proofs (you may lose 1–2 marks in 14-mark derivations).

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Things to Remember — Module 4: TD(λ)**

- **TD(λ)** is a *bridge* between **TD(0)** (one-step bootstrap) and **Monte Carlo** (full trajectory) controlled by **λ ∈ [0,1]**.
- The **forward view** defines the λ-return as a geometrically-weighted average of n-step returns: weights sum to **1**.
- The **backward view** implements the same effect online using **eligibility traces**.
- **TD-error** $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ is the *single scalar* that drives every update.
- **Accumulating trace**: $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$.
- **Replacing trace**: cap at 1 on revisit; prevents trace blow-up.
- **Dutch trace**: $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{1}\{s=s_t\}$; provably optimal.
- Online update: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$ — applied to **all states**.
- **Convergence** to $v_\pi$ requires **Robbins–Monro conditions**: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
- **Practical significance**: enables faster convergence than TD(0) without MC's high variance.
- **Recursion identity**: $G_t^{\lambda} = r_{t+1} + \gamma[(1-\lambda) V(s_{t+1}) + \lambda G_{t+1}^{\lambda}]$ — the bridge identity.
- **State updates**: when a state is *not* visited, its trace decays by exactly **γλ** per step.
- **Boundary cases**: $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ TD(0); $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ every-visit Monte Carlo.
- **Modern relevance**: foundation of **policy gradient with baseline**, **actor-critic** methods, and **RLHF** in LLMs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: n-step Truncated λ-return Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** n-step Truncated λ-return Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme — Sutton & Barto Framework)**
> The **n-step Truncated λ-return** is an off-policy capable multi-step bootstrapped target that averages several n-step returns using exponentially decaying weights controlled by a trace-decay parameter $\lambda \in [0,1]$, and **truncates** the geometric sum at the episode horizon $T$ so that future-reward knowledge beyond $T$ is never required.

$$
G_t^{\lambda} \;\triangleq\; (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}\,G_t^{(n)} \;+\; \lambda^{T-t-1}\,G_t^{(T-t)}
$$

where each constituent **n-step return** is defined as

$$
