<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**State the REINFORCE update rule and identify the role of the score function in the rule.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (point-wise, as expected by KTU evaluators):**

1. The REINFORCE update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha\, G_t\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A_t \vert S_t)$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate, $G_t$ the discounted Monte-Carlo return and $\pi_{\theta}$ the policy.

2. **Role of the score function** $\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A_t\vert S_t)$: it indicates the *direction in parameter space* in which the log-probability of the selected action increases. Multiplying by $G_t$ ensures that parameters move to make **high-return actions more probable** and low-return actions less probable.

3. It is the result of the **log-derivative (likelihood-ratio) trick** which removes the need to differentiate the state-distribution — a key contribution of the Policy Gradient Theorem.

*(Expected length: 3 crisp lines + 1 equation. Award 1 mark for the update rule, 1 mark for identifying the role, 1 mark for connecting to PG theorem/likelihood-ratio trick.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Why is REINFORCE said to have high variance, and how does the introduction of a baseline help without introducing bias?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **High variance cause:** REINFORCE estimates the gradient using the *raw stochastic return* $G_t$, which is the sum of many random rewards; consequently, two episodes with identical policies can produce very different gradient estimates.

2. **Baseline reduces variance:** Subtracting a state-dependent quantity $b(S_t)$ (typically $\hat V^{\pi}(S_t)$) centres the return — effectively, actions are reinforced *relative to expected performance* under that state, reducing the magnitude of fluctuations.

3. **No bias introduced:** For any $b(s)$ independent of $a$,

$$\mathbb{E}_{a\sim\pi_{\theta}}\!\bigl[b(s)\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s)\bigr] = 0,$$

so subtracting the baseline leaves the expected gradient unchanged. Hence the estimator remains **unbiased** but with strictly lower variance when $b(s)\approx\mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t=s]$.

*(Mark split: 1 + 1 + 1.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write**
> - **Skipping the policy-gradient derivation** even when only a 7-mark sub-part asks for it is the most common reason for losing 3–4 marks. Always derive the log-derivative step **explicitly**.
> - Students often **forget the discounted-state-distribution term** $d^{\pi}(s)$ when stating the policy-gradient theorem. Examiners treat this as a conceptual gap and deduct 1–2 marks.
> - Confusing **baseline** with **bootstrapping**: a baseline is *not* a TD target. Saying "REINFORCE with baseline bootstraps from $V(s)$" is a *fatal error* — full mark cancellation for that sub-part.
> - **Numerical updates:** forgetting the sign convention (REINFORCE uses gradient *ascent*; many students write descent) loses 1 mark even when all algebra is correct.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Derive the Policy Gradient Theorem starting from* $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\theta}}[G(\tau)]$. *Show clearly where the likelihood-ratio trick is used.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 3-action, single-state bandit has policy* $\pi_{\theta}(a_i \vert s) = \dfrac{e^{\theta_i}}{\sum_j e^{\theta_j}}$ *with* $\theta = [0.2, 0.0, -0.1]$. *In one episode, the agent samples action* $a_2$ *and receives reward* $R=2$. *Assume a constant baseline* $b(s)=1$ *and learning rate* $\alpha=0.5$. *Compute the parameter update* $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha (R-b)\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(a_2 \vert s)$ *numerically.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Express $J$ as an expectation.**

$$J(\theta) = \int P(\tau\vert\theta)\,G(\tau)\,d\tau.$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate.**

$$\nabla_{\theta} J = \int \nabla_{\theta}P(\tau\vert\theta)\,G(\tau)\,d\tau.$$

**Step 3 — Apply the log-derivative identity.**

$$\nabla_{\theta}P(\tau\vert\theta)=P(\tau\vert\theta)\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln P(\tau\vert\theta).$$

Hence

$$\nabla_{\theta} J = \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\bigl[G(\tau)\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln P(\tau\vert\theta)\bigr].$$

*(This is the **likelihood-ratio trick**: we now have a gradient inside an expectation that can be estimated by sampling.)*

**Step 4 — Factorise $P(\tau\vert\theta)$.**

$$P(\tau\vert\theta)=P(s_0)\prod_{t}\pi_{\theta}(a_t\vert s_t)P(s_{t+1}\vert s_t,a_t).$$

**Step 5 — Take the log and differentiate.**

$$\nabla_{\theta}\ln P(\tau\vert\theta)=\sum_{t}\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(a_t\vert s_t).$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite the gradient in per-time-step form.**

$$\nabla_{\theta}J = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{t}\gamma^{t}\,Q^{\pi}(S_t,A_t)\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A_t\vert S_t)\right]$$

where $Q^{\pi}(S_t,A_t)=\mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t,A_t]$ is the action-value function. (Derivation of the $Q^{\pi}$ appearance uses the *Markov property* and removes earlier-reward terms that do not depend on $A_t$.)

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

**Mark-split rationale:** (i) Likelihood-ratio trick + log-derivative — *2 marks*; (ii) Factorisation and gradient of log-trajectory — *2 marks*; (iii) Final PG theorem statement with $Q^{\pi}$ — *3 marks*.

##### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the softmax probabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= e^{0.2}+e^{0.0}+e^{-0.1} = 1.2214+1.0000+0.9048 = 3.1262,\\
\pi(a_1) &= 1.2214/3.1262 = 0.3907,\\
\pi(a_2) &= 1.0000/3.1262 = 0.3199,\\
\pi(a_3) &= 0.9048/3.1262 = 0.2894.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Score function for softmax.** For action $a_i$:

$$\nabla_{\theta_j}\ln\pi_{\theta}(a_i)=\mathbb{1}\{i=j\}-\pi_{\theta}(a_j).$$

For $i=2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta_1}\ln\pi(a_2) &= 0 - \pi(a_1) = -0.3907,\\
\nabla_{\theta_2}\ln\pi(a_2) &= 1 - \pi(a_2) = +0.6801,\\
\nabla_{\theta_3}\ln\pi(a_2) &= 0 - \pi(a_3) = -0.2894.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Advantage (return minus baseline).**

$$\delta = R - b(s) = 2 - 1 = 1.$$

**Step 4 — Update.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1^{\text{new}} &= 0.2 + 0.5\times 1 \times (-0.3907) = 0.2 - 0.1954 = 0.0046,\\
\theta_2^{\text{new}} &= 0.0 + 0.5\times 1 \times 0.6801 = 0.3400,\\
\theta_3^{\text{new}} &= -0.1 + 0.5\times 1 \times (-0.2894) = -0.1 - 0.1447 = -0.2447.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\theta_2$ increased sharply (the chosen action becomes more likely), while $\theta_1$ and $\theta_3$ decreased (the unchosen actions become less likely) — exactly the *reinforcement* behaviour promised by REINFORCE.

**Mark-split rationale:** (i) Correct softmax probabilities — *2 marks*; (ii) Correct score function with indicator formula — *3 marks*; (iii) Final update with arithmetic — *2 marks*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** *Explain the REINFORCE algorithm with a baseline. State its update rule, prove that the baseline does not bias the gradient estimator, and discuss why it reduces variance.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare REINFORCE (Monte Carlo Policy Gradient) with the Actor-Critic method along the following axes: bias, variance, sample efficiency, on/off-policy nature, and whether bootstrapping is used. Present your answer in a comparison table.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Update rule with baseline:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha\,(G_t - b(S_t))\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A_t \vert S_t)$$

where $b(S_t)$ is any function only of the state (typically an estimate $\hat V^{\pi}(S_t)$).

**Proof of zero bias contribution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{A\sim\pi_{\theta}}\!\left[b(s)\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A\vert s)\right] 
&= b(s)\,\sum_{a}\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s)\,\frac{\nabla_{\theta}\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s)}{\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s)}\\
&= b(s)\,\nabla_{\theta}\sum_{a}\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s) \\
&= b(s)\,\nabla_{\theta}(1) = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the expected value of the baseline term is zero, the *mean* of the new estimator equals the mean of the original REINFORCE estimator — **unbiasedness is preserved**.

**Why variance drops:** The variance of the centred estimator is

$$\mathrm{Var}\!\bigl[(G_t - b(S_t))\nabla\bigr] = \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(G_t - b(S_t))^{2}\Vert\nabla\Vert^{2}\bigr] - \bigl(\mathbb{E}[(G_t-b(S_t))\nabla]\bigr)^{2}.$$

If $b(s)$ is close to $\mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t=s]$, then the conditional variance of $G_t - b(S_t)$ is small and the *overall* variance of the estimator is reduced. The optimal baseline is $b^{*}(s)=\mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t=s]$ which minimises the trace of the estimator's covariance.

**Mark-split:** Update rule + statement — *2 marks*; bias proof — *3 marks*; variance argument — *2 marks*.

##### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

| Axis | REINFORCE (MC-PG) | Actor-Critic |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Bias** | Unbiased (true PG) | Biased (approximation error from learned $V$ or $Q$) |
| **Variance** | High | Much lower (TD target substitutes for full return) |
| **Sample Efficiency** | Poor — needs complete episode per update | High — online, per-step updates |
| **On/Off-policy** | On-policy | Typically on-policy (A2C), but off-policy variants exist |
| **Bootstrapping** | **No** (uses $G_t$) | **Yes** (uses $\hat V(S_{t+1})$ or TD-error) |
| **Bias–Variance Trade-off** | Favours unbiasedness | Trades a small bias for big variance reduction |

**Mark-split rationale:** Filling in 5 rows correctly — *5 marks* (1 each); explicit trade-off discussion — *2 marks*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (commit to memory before the exam)**

- [ ] REINFORCE = **Monte Carlo Policy Gradient**, sample-based, no model, no bootstrapping.
- [ ] Update rule: $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha\,G_t\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A_t\vert S_t)$.
- [ ] The **score function** $\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}$ is the *direction* in parameter space that makes the chosen action more likely.
- [ ] The **Policy Gradient Theorem** expresses $\nabla_{\theta}J$ as $\mathbb{E}[Q^{\pi}(S,A)\,\nabla_{\theta}\ln\pi_{\theta}(A\vert S)]$ — eliminating the need to differentiate the state distribution.
- [ ] The estimator is **unbiased but high variance** — a classic bias-variance trade-off.
- [ ] A **baseline** $b(s)$ (independent of $a$) subtracts from $G_t$ to reduce variance without biasing the estimator (proved by the fact that $b(s)\nabla_{\theta}\sum_a\pi_{\theta}(a\vert s)=0$).
- [ ] Common baselines: $\hat V^{\pi}(s)$, moving average of returns, constant mean.
- [ ] For **softmax policies**, $\nabla_{\theta_j}\ln\pi_{\theta}(a_i)=\mathbb{1}\{i=j\}-\pi_{\theta}(a_j)$.
- [ ] For **Gaussian policies**, $\nabla_{\mu}\ln\pi(a\vert s)=(a-\mu(s))/\sigma^{2}$.
- [ ] REINFORCE is **on-policy** — it must sample actions from the *current* policy.
- [ ] REINFORCE handles **continuous action spaces natively**, unlike value-based methods.
- [ ] The discounted state distribution $d^{\pi}(s)=\sum_{t}\gamma^{t}P(s_t=s\vert\pi)$ is part of the policy-gradient expectation — examiners test this.
- [ ] The likelihood-ratio / log-derivative trick is the *mathematical engine* of all policy-gradient methods.
- [ ] Convergence is **slower than Actor-Critic** because of high variance.
- [ ] REINFORCE with **eligibility traces** (REINFORCE($\lambda$)) combines multi-step credit assignment with policy gradient.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SLAAG"**
> **S**core function × **L**ikelihood-ratio trick × **A**dvantage (G − baseline) × **A**symptotic unbiasedness × **G**radient ascent. If you remember SLAAG, you remember REINFORCE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actor-Critic Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT747 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Actor-Critic Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal taxonomy of **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** algorithms, **Actor-Critic Methods** constitute a class of **policy-gradient algorithms that simultaneously learn a parameterized policy (the actor)** and **a value function used to evaluate that policy (the critic)**. Mathematically, the actor is a parametric function

$$\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow [0,1]$$

parameterized by a vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\theta}$, while the critic estimates a state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ or an action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$, parameterized by weights $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d_w}$. The critic's estimate is used to compute a **low-variance baseline or advantage signal** that bootstraps the actor's gradient update, thereby reducing the variance inherent in the classical REINFORCE estimator while preserving the convergence guarantees of policy-gradient theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Actor-Critic methods bridge the divide between **value-based** methods (Q-Learning, SARSA) and **policy-based** methods (REINFORCE). They are considered the workhorse algorithm family for **continuous-action robotic control** because they can learn stochastic policies while still leveraging bootstrapped value estimates for sample efficiency.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a young chess apprentice learning to play. The apprentice is the **Actor** — every move they make on the board is governed by a learned strategy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$. After each move, a coach stands beside them. The coach is the **Critic** — instead of telling the apprentice *what* to play, the coach whispers a single sentence: *"That last move was brilliant"* (positive feedback) or *"That last move was terrible"* (negative feedback).

The coach does not need to prescribe actions; they only need to assess how good the position became **after** the apprentice's move. This scalar judgment — called the **TD error** $\delta$ — is the signal that updates both:
- The apprentice's strategy (actor update), and
- The coach's own internal evaluation model (critic update).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two networks?** In robotics, the action space is often continuous (e.g., joint torques, end-effector velocities). Pure value-based methods like DQN require a max over actions, which is intractable for continuous spaces. Pure policy-gradient methods like REINFORCE have **high variance** and require many samples. Actor-Critic solves both problems: the critic provides a **learned baseline** that drastically reduces gradient variance, while the actor naturally handles continuous actions by directly outputting $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$.

---

## 1.3 Architectural Components — At a Glance

The two core learnable components are defined as follows:

| Component | Mathematical Symbol | Role | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actor** | $\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)$ | Policy selector — chooses actions | Action $a \in \mathcal{A}$ |
| **Critic** | $V_w(s)$ or $Q_w(s,a)$ | Value estimator — evaluates state/action quality | Scalar value estimate |
| **Advantage** | $A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)$ | How much better was $a$ than average? | Scalar advantage |
| **TD Error** | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | One-step bootstrapped error | Scalar critic loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Advantage Function** $A^{\pi}(s,a) = Q^{\pi}(s,a) - V^{\pi}(s)$ measures the relative improvement of taking action $a$ over the average action. It is the canonical scaling factor applied to the actor's policy gradient.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Policy Gradient with Baseline

The fundamental result that Actor-Critic methods are built upon is the **Policy Gradient Theorem with Baseline**. For any baseline $b(s)$ that does not depend on $a$, the expected gradient of the objective $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}[G_0]$ is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot \left(Q^{\pi}(s,a) - b(s)\right)\right]$$

Choosing the baseline $b(s) = V^{\pi}(s)$ minimizes the variance of the gradient estimator without introducing bias, because:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot V^\pi(s)\right] = V^\pi(s) \cdot \nabla_\theta \sum_a \pi_\theta(a \mid s) = V^\pi(s) \cdot \nabla_\theta 1 = 0$$

The quantity $Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s)$ is precisely the **Advantage Function** $A^\pi(s,a)$. The actor is therefore updated along:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha_\theta \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot A(s_t, a_t)$$

---

## 2.2 Critic's Learning Objective — Temporal Difference

The critic is itself a **function approximator** that minimizes the mean-squared TD error:

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[\left(r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)\right)^2\right]$$

Its stochastic gradient descent update is:

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha_w \, \delta_t \, \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$$

where $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)$ is the **one-step TD error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The TD error $\delta_t$ is an **unbiased sample of the advantage** $A^\pi(s_t, a_t)$. Therefore, when the critic has converged sufficiently, the actor can be updated using $\delta_t$ in place of the true advantage — this is the **practical** Actor-Critic update rule.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Actor-Critic Essentials

The following table consolidates every critical equation you must memorize for the KTU board examination:

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Update |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Policy Gradient Theorem** | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, Q^\pi(s,a)\right]$ | Foundation of actor update |
| 2 | **Gradient with Baseline** | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, (Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s))\right]$ | Variance reduction |
| 3 | **Advantage Function** | $A^\pi(s,a) = Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s)$ | Scaling factor for actor |
| 4 | **TD Error (1-step)** | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)$ | Critic target / advantage estimate |
| 5 | **TD Error (n-step)** | $G_t^{(n)} - V(s_t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n}) - V(s_t)$ | Reduced bias |
| 6 | **Critic Loss** | $\mathcal{L}(w) = \mathbb{E}\left[\delta_t^2\right]$ | MSE between prediction and TD target |
| 7 | **Critic Update** | $w \leftarrow w + \alpha_w \delta_t \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$ | Gradient descent on critic loss |
| 8 | **Actor Update** | $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\theta \delta_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ | Policy gradient ascent |
| 9 | **Eligibility Trace (TD-$\lambda$)** | $e_t = \gamma \lambda\, e_{t-1} + \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$ | Credit assignment over time |
| 10 | **Compatible Function Approx.** | $\nabla_w f_w(s,a) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | Guarantees unbiased gradient |
| 11 | **Discount Factor** | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Standard trade-off parameter |
| 12 | **Trace Decay** | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Bias-variance trade-off in traces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In robotics, Actor-Critic enables **model-free continuous control** for tasks such as robotic arm reaching, legged locomotion, and drone flight. The actor outputs continuous torques or velocities; the critic learns how well the robot is doing without needing a hand-engineered reward shaping for every state.

---

## 2.4 Why Actor-Critic? — The Variance-Bias Trade-off

The progression of RL algorithms can be understood as a march toward **lower variance gradient estimates**:

| Algorithm | Gradient Estimate | Variance | Bias |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monte-Carlo REINFORCE | $\nabla_\theta \log \pi \cdot G_t$ | **Very High** | Unbiased |
| REINFORCE with Baseline | $\nabla_\theta \log \pi \cdot (G_t - b(s_t))$ | High | Unbiased |
| **Actor-Critic (1-step TD)** | $\nabla_\theta \log \pi \cdot \delta_t$ | **Moderate** | Slightly Biased |
| **Actor-Critic (TD-$\lambda$)** | $\nabla_\theta \log \pi \cdot (G_t^\lambda - V(s_t))$ | Low | Slightly Biased |

> [!NOTE]
> The **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})$ generalizes between the extremes. As $n \to \infty$, we recover Monte-Carlo (unbiased but high variance). As $n \to 1$, we recover 1-step TD (biased but low variance). The parameter $\lambda$ in $G_t^\lambda$ smoothly interpolates this spectrum.

---

## 2.5 Algorithmic Variants — The Actor-Critic Family

Several variants of Actor-Critic are listed in the KTU syllabus:

### 2.5.1 Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)
Uses $n$-step returns to estimate the advantage: $A(s_t, a_t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \gamma^i r_{t+i+1} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n}) - V(s_t)$. Synchronous, on-policy.

### 2.5.2 Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C)
Multiple parallel agents (workers) explore diverse parts of the state space. Each worker maintains its own copy of actor-critic parameters and asynchronously pushes gradients to a global network. Improves exploration and decorrelates samples.

### 2.5.3 Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)
Adds an **entropy bonus** $\mathcal{H}(\pi_\theta)$ to the reward, encouraging stochasticity and exploration:

$$J_{\text{SAC}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a)\sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) - Q_\phi(s,a) + \alpha \mathcal{H}(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s))\right]$$

### 2.5.4 Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)
A deterministic actor $\mu_\theta(s)$ paired with a Q-critic $Q_\phi(s,a)$, using **replay buffers** and **target networks** to stabilize training. A standard algorithm for robotic continuous control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Policy Gradient Theorem with Baseline

We begin with the standard RL objective:

$$J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s_0 \sim \rho_0,\, a_t \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

### Step 1 — Express the gradient of $J(\theta)$ under the trajectory distribution

Let $\tau = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots)$ denote a trajectory. The probability of a trajectory under policy $\pi_\theta$ is:

$$P(\tau \mid \theta) = \rho_0(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^{\infty} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

The objective can be rewritten as:

$$J(\theta) = \sum_\tau P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau), \quad \text{where } R(\tau) = \sum_t \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_\tau \nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau)$$

Using the **log-derivative trick** $\nabla P = P \cdot \nabla \log P$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_\tau P(\tau \mid \theta)\, \nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau)\right]$$

### Step 3 — Expand $\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)$

$$\log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \log \rho_0(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\left[\log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) + \log P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)\right]$$

Only $\log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ depends on $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back into the gradient

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\tau\left[\left(\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\right)\left(\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t)\right)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Simplify to a sum of per-timestep expectations

By exchanging expectation and sum, and noting that $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ does not depend on $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \mathbb{E}_{s_t, a_t}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, \mathbb{E}\left[G_t \mid s_t, a_t\right]\right]$$

Because $\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid s_t, a_t] = Q^\pi(s_t, a_t)$:

$$\boxed{\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \mathbb{E}_{s_t \sim d^\pi,\, a_t \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, Q^\pi(s_t, a_t)\right]}$$

This is the **Policy Gradient Theorem**.

### Step 6 — Introduce a baseline $b(s_t)$ that is independent of $a_t$

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\,(Q^\pi(s_t, a_t) - b(s_t))\right]$$

because:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a_t \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, b(s_t)\right] = b(s_t) \sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

Since $\pi_\theta \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta = \nabla_\theta \pi_\theta$ and $\sum_{a_t} \nabla_\theta \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = \nabla_\theta \sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = \nabla_\theta 1 = 0$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a_t}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, b(s_t)\right] = 0$$

Thus the baseline does not change the expected gradient — **it only reduces variance**.

### Step 7 — Choose the optimal baseline $b^*(s) = V^\pi(s)$

The optimal baseline that minimizes variance is the state-value function. The resulting scaled quantity is:

$$A^\pi(s_t, a_t) = Q^\pi(s_t, a_t) - V^\pi(s_t)$$

yielding the canonical Actor-Critic gradient:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s_t, a_t}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, A^\pi(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Compatible Function Approximation Theorem

The critic's value approximation $f_w(s,a)$ is called **compatible** with the actor if:

$$\nabla_w f_w(s,a) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$$

**Claim:** If $f_w$ satisfies the compatibility condition and minimizes the squared error:

$$\varepsilon(w) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\left(f_w(s,a) - A^\pi(s,a)\right)^2\right]$$

then $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, f_w(s,a)\right]$.

**Proof Sketch:** Because $\nabla_w \varepsilon(w) = 0$ at the optimum:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_w f_w(s,a) \cdot (f_w(s,a) - A^\pi(s,a))\right] = 0$$

Using compatibility $\nabla_w f_w(s,a) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, f_w(s,a)\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, A^\pi(s,a)\right] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$

Hence the critic's linear-in-features approximation can be plugged in directly into the actor's gradient without bias. This is the **theoretical foundation** of the A2C algorithm.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Single-Step Actor-Critic Update

**Problem:** A 6-DOF robot arm has reached state $s_t$ with critic estimate $V_w(s_t) = 1.20$. The actor selects action $a_t$ with policy $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = 0.40$ and $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = [0.5,\; -0.3,\; 0.8]^T$. The environment returns reward $r_{t+1} = 1.5$ and next state with $V_w(s_{t+1}) = 1.05$. Given $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha_\theta = 0.05$, $\alpha_w = 0.1$, and $\nabla_w V_w(s_t) = [0.2,\; 0.4]^T$, compute the actor and critic updates.

### Step A — Compute the TD error

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)$$

$$\delta_t = 1.5 + 0.9 \times 1.05 - 1.20$$

$$\delta_t = 1.5 + 0.945 - 1.20 = 1.245$$

### Step B — Compute the critic parameter update

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha_w \, \delta_t \, \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$$

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + 0.1 \times 1.245 \times [0.2,\; 0.4]^T = w_t + [0.0249,\; 0.0498]^T$$

### Step C — Compute the actor parameter update

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha_\theta \, \delta_t \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + 0.05 \times 1.245 \times [0.5,\; -0.3,\; 0.8]^T$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + [0.031125,\; -0.018675,\; 0.049800]^T$$

### Step D — Interpretation

The positive TD error ($\delta_t = 1.245$) indicates the critic believes the action led to a better-than-expected state. The actor's weights are updated in the direction that made this action more likely — which is correct behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever you see an Actor-Critic computation problem, **always compute $\delta_t$ first**, then apply it independently to both the actor and critic updates. Examiners award step-marks for separating these two computations clearly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Actor-Critic Variants

| Feature | A2C | A3C | SAC | DDPG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Policy Type** | Stochastic | Stochastic | Stochastic (max-entropy) | Deterministic |
| **Exploration** | Entropy / noise | Asynchronous workers | Entropy bonus | Ornstein-Uhlenbeck / Gaussian |
| **Replay Buffer** | Optional | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Target Networks** | No | No | Yes (Polyak) | Yes (Polyak) |
| **On/Off-Policy** | On-policy | On-policy | Off-policy | Off-policy |
| **Sample Efficiency** | Low | Low | High | High |
| **Stability** | Moderate | Moderate | High | High (with care) |
| **Robotic Use Case** | Locomotion | Multi-robot | Dexterous manipulation | Reaching, grasping |

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Problem — Variance Reduction with Baseline

**Problem:** Show that adding a baseline $b(s)$ reduces the variance of the REINFORCE gradient estimator.

### Solution

The REINFORCE estimator with baseline is:

$$g(\tau) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\,(G_t - b(s_t))$$

Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(g) = \mathbb{E}\left[\,\left\vert\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\,\right\vert^2 \,(G_t - b(s_t))^2\right] - \left(\mathbb{E}[g]\right)^2$$

The optimal baseline minimizes $\mathbb{E}[(G_t - b(s_t))^2 \cdot \vert\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\vert^2]$ for every component of $\theta$. Differentiating with respect to $b(s_t)$ and setting to zero:

$$b^*(s_t) = \frac{\mathbb{E}\left[G_t \,\vert\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\vert^2 \,\vert\, s_t\right]}{\mathbb{E}\left[\vert\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\vert^2 \,\vert\, s_t\right]}$$

This is **not exactly** $V^\pi(s_t)$, but it is well-approximated by $V^\pi(s_t)$ for small policy changes. Using $V^\pi$ as the baseline is the **practical** choice that the Actor-Critic algorithm adopts.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Actor-Critic Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Environment State s_t] --> ACTOR[Actor Network pi_theta]
    ACTOR --> A[Action a_t]
    A --> ENV[Robotic Environment]
    ENV --> R[Reward r_tp1]
    ENV --> NS[Next State s_tp1]
    R --> CRITIC[Critic Network V_w]
    NS --> CRITIC
    CRITIC --> TD[TD Error delta_t = r + gamma V_s_next - V_s]
    TD --> CU[Critic Update w += alpha_w delta grad V_w]
    TD --> AU[Actor Update theta += alpha_theta delta grad log pi]
    CU --> CRITIC
    AU --> ACTOR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Information flows from the environment → actor (decision) → environment (action) → critic (evaluation). The TD error $\delta_t$ is the dual-output signal that drives both learning loops. This dual feedback is the defining characteristic of Actor-Critic methods.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Information Flow — On-Policy Actor-Critic Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize actor params theta and critic params w] --> B[Observe state s_t from environment]
    B --> C[Actor samples action a_t from pi_theta]
    C --> D[Execute a_t in environment]
    D --> E[Receive reward r_tp1 and next state s_tp1]
    E --> F[Critic computes V_w s_t and V_w s_tp1]
    F --> G[Compute TD error delta_t = r_tp1 + gamma V_w s_tp1 minus V_w s_t]
    G --> H[Update critic weights w using MSE loss on delta_t squared]
    G --> I[Update actor weights theta using policy gradient scaled by delta_t]
    H --> J[Set s_t to s_tp1]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Episode Done?}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> L[End of Episode]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Algorithm Flow — A2C vs A3C vs DDPG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A2C[Algorithm A2C]
        A2C1[Sync rollout from workers] --> A2C2[Compute n-step returns]
        A2C2 --> A2C3[Update actor and critic synchronously]
    end
    subgraph A3C[Algorithm A3C]
        A3C1[Async rollout from worker i] --> A3C2[Compute local gradients]
        A3C2 --> A3C3[Push gradients to global network]
        A3C3 --> A3C4[Pull updated weights from global]
    end
    subgraph DDPG[Algorithm DDPG]
        DDPG1[Sample mini-batch from replay buffer] --> DDPG2[Compute Q target using target networks]
        DDPG2 --> DDPG3[Update critic by minimizing Bellman error]
        DDPG3 --> DDPG4[Update actor by maximizing Q]
        DDPG4 --> DDPG5[Soft update target networks]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Eligibility Trace Architecture (TD-lambda Actor-Critic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ET[Eligibility Trace e_t] --> SUM[Trace Accumulator]
    SUM --> PROD[Multiply with TD error delta_t]
    PROD --> CRITICW[Critic Weights Update]
    PROD --> ACTORW[Actor Weights Update]
    SIG[State s_t arrives] --> GRAD[Compute gradients of V_w and log pi]
    GRAD --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on Eligibility Traces:** Eligibility traces accumulate credit assignment over multiple timesteps using an exponential decay $\lambda$. For robotics tasks with sparse rewards (e.g., a robot arm receives +1 only upon reaching a goal), eligibility traces are critical because they propagate the sparse reward signal back through every preceding (state, action) pair that contributed to the success.

---

## 4.5 Continuous Control Robot Pipeline — Actor-Critic in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAM[Camera / Sensors] --> OBS[Observation Encoder CNN or MLP]
    OBS --> STATE[State Vector s_t]
    STATE --> ACTNN[Actor Neural Network]
    ACTNN --> TORQUE[Joint Torques a_t]
    TORQUE --> ROBOT[Robot Arm or Quadruped]
    ROBOT --> CAM
    ROBOT --> REW[Reward Function r_t]
    REW --> CRITNN[Critic Neural Network]
    STATE --> CRITNN
    CRITNN --> VAL[Value Estimate V_w s_t]
    VAL --> LOSS[TD Loss Computation]
    REW --> LOSS
    LOSS --> OPT[Optimizer Adam or RMSProp]
    OPT --> ACTNN
    OPT --> CRITNN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implementation Insight:** In modern robotic Actor-Critic systems, the actor and critic networks often share the early convolutional/encoder layers (to reduce parameter count) and diverge only in the final fully-connected layers. The actor head outputs continuous actions (typically through a `tanh` squashing function), while the critic head outputs a scalar value estimate.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the critic in an Actor-Critic algorithm. Why is the TD error used as the advantage estimate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded for clarity + technical accuracy):**

The critic in an Actor-Critic algorithm acts as a **learned value-function approximator** that evaluates how good the current state (or state-action pair) is under the actor's current policy. Its role is twofold:

1. **Variance Reduction:** The critic provides a state-dependent baseline $V^\pi(s_t)$, which is subtracted from the return to form the advantage $A(s_t, a_t)$. This drastically reduces the variance of the policy gradient without introducing bias.

2. **Online Learning:** Because the critic learns online via temporal-difference bootstrapping, it does not require waiting until the end of an episode to evaluate actions — this is critical for robotic tasks with long episodes.

The **TD error** $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)$ is used as the advantage estimate because it is an **unbiased single-sample estimator of $A^\pi(s_t, a_t)$** under the on-policy distribution. When the critic has converged, $\delta_t \approx A^\pi(s_t, a_t)$, and the actor's policy gradient update becomes a low-variance, online learning rule.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between on-policy and off-policy Actor-Critic methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | On-Policy Actor-Critic | Off-Policy Actor-Critic |
|---|---|---|
| Data used for learning | Generated by the current policy $\pi_\theta$ | Stored in a replay buffer from any policy |
| Sample efficiency | Low (data discarded after each update) | High (data reused many times) |
| Exploration | Policy stochasticity / parallel workers | Noise added to actions + replay buffer |
| Stability | Stable but slow | Faster but requires target networks |
| Example | **A2C, A3C** | **DDPG, SAC, TD3** |

**On-policy example:** A2C — uses samples generated by the current actor to compute $n$-step returns and update both networks synchronously.

**Off-policy example:** DDPG — maintains a replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$ of past transitions and uses them to train the critic via Bellman backup, decoupling data collection from policy updates.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **Policy Gradient Theorem** from the standard RL objective, clearly showing all intermediate steps. Explain how introducing a baseline $b(s)$ preserves unbiasedness of the gradient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Actor-Critic architecture** with a block diagram. Derive the update equations for both the actor and the critic, and discuss the role of the **advantage function** in variance reduction. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the objective:
$$J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Rewrite using trajectory probability:
$$J(\theta) = \sum_\tau P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Differentiate and apply the log-derivative trick:
$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_\tau \nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau) = \mathbb{E}_\tau\left[\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, R(\tau)\right]$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Show that only the policy term depends on $\theta$:
$$\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \sum_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute and rearrange to obtain the Policy Gradient Theorem:
$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, Q^\pi(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** Introduce the baseline:
$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\,(Q^\pi(s_t, a_t) - b(s_t))\right]$$

Show that the added term has zero expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}_{a_t}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, b(s_t)\right] = b(s_t) \cdot \nabla_\theta \sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = b(s_t) \cdot \nabla_\theta 1 = 0$$

Hence the gradient expectation is **unchanged** (unbiased), but the **variance is reduced**.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Describe the architecture:
- The **Actor** is a policy network $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ that maps states to action probabilities (or continuous actions via Gaussian head).
- The **Critic** is a value network $V_w(s)$ that estimates expected return from state $s$.
- The TD error $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t)$ is the dual signal that updates both.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Actor update derivation:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha_\theta\, \delta_t\, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$
This is policy gradient ascent, scaled by the scalar advantage estimate $\delta_t$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Critic update derivation:
Minimize the MSE TD loss:
$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \mathbb{E}\left[(r_{t+1} + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t))^2\right]$$
Taking the gradient:
$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha_w\, \delta_t\, \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Discuss the role of the advantage function:
- $A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)$ measures how much better an action is compared to the average action in state $s$.
- Using $\delta_t \approx A(s_t, a_t)$ scales the policy gradient appropriately: actions that beat the baseline are reinforced, actions worse than baseline are suppressed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)** algorithm in detail. Derive the $n$-step advantage estimate and write the pseudocode for the synchronous A2C update loop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **A3C** and **DDPG** in terms of architecture, exploration strategy, on/off-policy nature, and applicability to robotic continuous control. Justify which is more sample-efficient and why. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Define A2C: A synchronous, on-policy Actor-Critic that uses $n$-step bootstrapped returns to estimate the advantage, decoupling trajectory horizon from update frequency.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Derive the $n$-step advantage estimate:
The $n$-step return from time $t$ is:
$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V_w(s_{t+n})$$
The advantage is therefore:
$$A(s_t, a_t) = G_t^{(n)} - V_w(s_t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n V_w(s_{t+n}) - V_w(s_t)$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Pseudocode for synchronous A2C:

```
Initialize actor parameters theta and critic parameters w
Initialize shared step counter t = 0
Repeat until convergence:
    Reset gradients: d_theta = 0, d_w = 0
    Synchronize workers and collect trajectories of T steps
    For t = 1 to T:
        Compute TD error: delta_t = r_t + gamma * V_w(s_t+1) - V_w(s_t)
        Accumulate critic gradient: d_w += grad_w V_w(s_t) * delta_t
        Accumulate actor gradient: d_theta += grad_theta log pi_theta(a_t|s_t) * delta_t
    Apply updates: theta += alpha_theta * d_theta, w += alpha_w * d_w
```

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Architectural comparison:
- **A3C** uses multiple parallel actor-learners each with their own copy of parameters. Gradients are pushed asynchronously to a global parameter server.
- **DDPG** uses a single deterministic actor $\mu_\theta(s)$, a Q-critic $Q_\phi(s,a)$, a replay buffer, and target networks for stability.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Exploration and on/off-policy:
- **A3C** explores through parallel on-policy rollouts — workers independently collect uncorrelated trajectories, improving exploration diversity. Strictly on-policy.
- **DDPG** explores by adding noise (Ornstein-Uhlenbeck for time-correlated noise, or Gaussian for simplicity) to the deterministic action. Strictly off-policy via replay buffer.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Robotic applicability:
- **A3C** is suited for discrete-action or short-horizon continuous tasks; suffers from low sample efficiency because data is discarded after each gradient push.
- **DDPG** is widely used for low-dimensional continuous control (robotic arm reaching, drone hovering) because the replay buffer allows reuse of every transition, drastically improving sample efficiency.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclusion:
DDPG is **more sample-efficient** than A3C due to its off-policy nature and replay buffer. However, A3C is more stable on highly stochastic tasks due to parallel exploration.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Actor-Critic Exam Questions — and How They Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show the log-derivative trick explicitly (–2 marks):** Examiners expect $\nabla P = P \cdot \nabla \log P$ to appear by name in derivations. Simply writing the final form without this intermediate step loses significant partial credit.
>
> 2. **Conflating TD error with reward (–1 mark):** $\delta_t \neq r_{t+1}$. Always include the bootstrapped value term $\gamma V(s_{t+1})$ in your equation.
>
> 3. **Not stating that the baseline preserves unbiasedness (–2 marks):** Whenever you write $\nabla_\theta J = \mathbb{E}[\nabla \log \pi (Q - b)]$, you must explicitly show the proof that $\mathbb{E}[\nabla \log \pi \cdot b] = 0$.
>
> 4. **Confusing actor and critic update directions (–2 marks):** Critic uses gradient **descent** on MSE loss, which results in $w \mathrel{+}= \alpha \delta \nabla V$ (because the loss gradient is $-\delta \nabla V$). Actor uses gradient **ascent** on expected return, $\theta \mathrel{+}= \alpha \delta \nabla \log \pi$. Writing them backwards is a fatal error.
>
> 5. **Omitting the discount factor $\gamma$ in pseudocode (–1 mark):** Every update must include $\gamma$.
>
> 6. **Not labelling the diagram (–2 marks):** When asked for a block diagram, every box MUST be labelled (Actor, Critic, Environment, TD Error, Reward, Next State). Unlabelled boxes receive zero credit in KTU valuations.
>
> 7. **Writing DDPG/SAC/A3C without distinguishing on/off-policy (–1 mark):** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always classify the algorithm first before describing it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Actor-Critic** combines a parameterized **policy** (actor) with a parameterized **value function** (critic) learned simultaneously.
- The **policy gradient theorem** is the theoretical bedrock:
  $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^\pi(s,a)\right]$$
- A **baseline** $b(s)$ preserves unbiasedness of the gradient but reduces variance. The optimal choice is $V^\pi(s)$, giving rise to the **advantage function** $A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)$.
- The **TD error** $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ is an unbiased single-sample estimator of the advantage and is used as the practical scaling factor.
- **Actor update** (gradient ascent): $\theta \mathrel{+}= \alpha_\theta \delta_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$.
- **Critic update** (gradient descent on MSE): $w \mathrel{+}= \alpha_w \delta_t \nabla_w V_w(s_t)$.
- **Compatible function approximation**: $\nabla_w f_w(s,a) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ guarantees unbiased gradient even with approximate critic.
- **TD-$\lambda$ traces** interpolate between Monte-Carlo (high variance, unbiased) and 1-step TD (low variance, biased).
- **On-policy variants** (A2C, A3C): Stable, simpler, but sample-inefficient.
- **Off-policy variants** (DDPG, SAC, TD3): Use replay buffers and target networks for sample-efficient robotic control.
- **Robotics applications**: continuous torque control, legged locomotion, dexterous manipulation, drone flight — anywhere the action space is continuous or high-dimensional.
- **Sample efficiency ranking (best to worst)**: SAC > TD3 > DDPG > A2C > A3C > REINFORCE.
- **Variance ranking (highest to lowest)**: REINFORCE > REINFORCE + baseline > 1-step Actor-Critic > n-step Actor-Critic > TD-$\lambda$ Actor-Critic.
- **Always remember** to include the entropy regularization term when describing modern algorithms like SAC.
- **Always remember** target networks and replay buffers when describing DDPG/TD3.
- The **discount factor** $\gamma$ must be present in every TD update equation — do not omit it under any circumstances.
